## 6. SDL transcription discipline

Critical to the project. Read [§2.1](#21-trust-the-figure), [§2.2](#22-encode-then-verify-not-infer-then-encode), [§2.3](#23-pin-implementation-evidence) and [docs/sdl-primer.md](sdl-primer.md) before touching any `*.sdl.yaml`.

### 6.1 The pipeline

```
spec-sdl/<machine>/<state>.sdl.yaml      ← human-authored transcription
              │
              ▼  dotnet run --project codegen/src/Packet.Sdl.CodeGen   (in packet-net/ax25sdl)
spec/csharp/<Machine>_<State>.g.cs                                  ← generated, checked in to packet-net/ax25sdl
spec/csharp/tests/<Machine>_<State>.g.Tests.cs                      ← generated, checked in to packet-net/ax25sdl
```

CI in `packet-net/ax25sdl` runs the codegen and `git diff --exit-code`. Drift fails CI. The generated C# library is published as the `Packet.Ax25.Sdl` NuGet; the generated tests run alongside in ax25sdl's own CI (per the 2026-05-21 wire-up — see §17). Packet.NET consumes the lib via NuGet and inherits the test coverage upstream rather than maintaining a local copy.

### 6.2 The DSL

Schema: [`/spec-sdl/schema/sdl-machine.schema.json`](../spec-sdl/schema/sdl-machine.schema.json). Event catalog: [`/spec-sdl/events.yaml`](../spec-sdl/events.yaml).

Worked example: [`/spec-sdl/data-link/connected.sdl.yaml`](../spec-sdl/data-link/connected.sdl.yaml) — see especially the header comment which documents variable definitions and implementation cross-references.

A transition entry looks like:

```yaml
- id: t01_dl_flow_off_when_own_receiver_busy
  on: DL_FLOW_OFF_request
  guard: "own_receiver_busy"
  actions:
    - set_own_receiver_busy
    - "RNR response"
    - clear_acknowledge_pending
  next: Connected
  notes: "figc4.4a col 5, Yes branch of 'Own Receiver Busy?'. …"
```

### 6.3 Workflow

1. Human reads the SDL figure. Translates each path through the diagram into a plain-text bullet list (`event → decision → actions → next state`).
2. Agent receives the plain-text description and encodes it into the YAML. Agent does *not* read the PNG to infer paths.
3. Agent runs `dotnet run --project codegen/src/Packet.Sdl.CodeGen` *in packet-net/ax25sdl* against the page's `.sdl.yaml`. (The codegen and the C# tests both live in ax25sdl since the 2026-05-17 5-repo split; see §17.)
4. `dotnet build` + `dotnet test` should pass. CI re-runs the codegen and fails on diff.
5. PR review pairs the YAML diff with the source figure. The PR description embeds or links to the figure.
6. On non-obvious transitions, the PR also cites at least one implementation reference per [§2.3](#23-pin-implementation-evidence).
7. Any `verification_pending` note added to the YAML must also be added to [`docs/spec-issues.md`](spec-issues.md) — the central tracker for candidate upstream-spec issues. Cross-link in both directions (YAML transition notes reference the SI-NN id; the tracker cites the YAML transition id).

### 6.4 SDL inventory & status

> Non-figc4 transcription work (Simplex Physical, Duplex Physical, Link Multiplexer, Management Data-Link, Segmenter, Reassembler — 18 pages) tracked at [`packet-net/ax25sdl#14`](https://github.com/packet-net/ax25sdl/issues/14). The figc4.x redraw effort lives at [`packet-net/ax25sdl#13`](https://github.com/packet-net/ax25sdl/issues/13).

Source: `https://github.com/packethacking/ax25spec/blob/main/doc/ax.25.2.2.4_Oct_25.md`.

| Machine | Figure(s) | Pages | State name(s) | Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simplex Physical | C2a.1–C2a.7 | 7 | Ready, Receiving, TX Suppression, TX Start, Transmitting, Digipeating, RX Start | ⬜ none |
| Duplex Physical | C2b.1–C2b.5 | 5 | RX Ready, Receiving, TX Ready, TX Start, Transmitting | ⬜ none |
| Link Multiplexer | C3.1–C3.3 (+ C3.4 subroutines) | 3 (+1) | Idle, Seize Pending, Seized | ⬜ none |
| Data-Link | C4.1, C4.2, C4.3, C4.4a–c, C4.5a–e, C4.6a–b, C4.7a–b (subs) | 12 (+2 subs) | Disconnected, Awaiting Connection, Awaiting Release, Connected, Timer Recovery, Awaiting V2.2 Connection | 🟡 figc4.1, figc4.2, figc4.3, figc4.4, figc4.6 done; figc4.5/4.7 remaining |
| Management Data-Link | C5.1, C5.2 (+ C5.3–C5.8 subroutines) | 2 | Ready, Negotiating | 🟡 **prose-bootstrap** (`verification_pending`) — figc5.1/figc5.2 hand-authored from §6.3.2 + App-C5.3 *prose* (Tom-directed exception; figc5.x never redrawn, PNGs all-black), shipped in `Packet.Ax25.Sdl` 0.9.0 and driven by `Ax25ManagementDataLink` (§5.Z V3 part 2, §17 2026-06-02) and behaviourally exercised on the transition-coverage ledger since V5a (Ready 2/2 + Negotiating 5/5; §17 2026-06-03). The figc5.3–5.8 per-parameter subroutines are collapsed to one `Apply Negotiated Parameters` placeholder; the *responder* XID-command path + error A are un-transcribed figure detail (runtime helper). figc5.x graphml redraw + figure-faithful transcription is the pending backfill. |
| Segmenter / Reassembler | C6.1–C6.2 | 2 | (Segmenter, Reassembler) | ⬜ none |

Total ≈ 28 pages (was 27 before figc4.6 turned out to span pages a + b).
Phase 0 covered ~2 columns of one page.

---

## 7. Test pyramid + interop CI

| Layer | Tooling | When it runs |
|---|---|---|
| Unit | xUnit | every PR (CI) |
| Property | FsCheck.Xunit v3 | every PR |
| Mutation | Stryker.NET | nightly + release branches |
| Conformance (SDL) | xUnit, generated | every PR; `git diff --exit-code` guard |
| Component | xUnit + `WebApplicationFactory<Program>` + mock KISS | every PR |
| Interop | xUnit + Testcontainers.NET (LinBPQ, Xrouter, net-sim, test-net) | subset on PR, full on nightly |
| Hardware loop | xUnit + USB-attached NinoTNC pair (`[Trait("Category","HardwareLoop")]` + `SkippableFact`) | self-hosted runner job, every PR touching `Packet.Kiss*` or `Packet.Ax25` |
| E2E UI | Playwright (.NET) | every PR (once UI exists) |
| Fuzz | SharpFuzz | nightly, 1 h per target |
| Soak | bespoke harness | nightly, 72 h on tagged release |

CI filter convention: default jobs run with `--filter "Category!=HardwareLoop&Category!=Interop"`. Hardware-loop and interop jobs use the opposite filter.

### 7.0 Interop tiers — where each kind of interop test lives, and why

Real-peer interop comes in three tiers, distinguished by *what is under test* and *how the frames cross to the peer*. The guiding principle: **protocol-correctness coverage belongs on a frame-perfect, load-insensitive transport; only modem/channel-dependent coverage belongs on the load-sensitive software-AFSK path.** Shift protocol coverage left.

| Tier | Transport | What it tests | Load sensitivity | Lives in |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1 — in-process harness** | `TwoStationHarness` / `CircuitPairHarness` (pdn↔pdn, in-memory bus) | pure AX.25 / NET/ROM protocol, deterministic, no real peer | none (no I/O, fake clock) | `tests/Packet.*.Tests` unit/integration |
| **2 — frame-perfect over AXUDP** | pdn ↔ **real LinBPQ** over BPQAXIP/UDP (`AxudpKissModem` → real `Ax25Listener`); **NO modem/audio** | real-peer **protocol** interop — AX.25 connected mode, NET/ROM L3 NODES + L4 circuits, Connect-Request framing — against a genuine third-party stack | **insensitive** (AX.25-frames-over-UDP: no AFSK encode/decode to glitch under CPU load) | `tests/Packet.Interop.Tests/Linbpq/*ViaAxudp*`, `LinbpqViaAxudpConnectedMode` |
| **3 — over net-sim (software-AFSK + simulated channel)** | pdn ↔ real BPQ/XRouter/rax25 over net-sim's AFSK1200 + lossy half-duplex channel | the **modem/channel layer**: TXDELAY/FCS-over-AFSK, loss → SREJ recovery, version-negotiation on the wire, half-duplex contention (#292), framing across a real demodulator | **sensitive** (a CPU glitch → audio-decode failure → a *fake* "loss"); §7.2 fences + §7.1 "re-run a lone red in isolation" | `tests/Packet.Interop.Tests/Netsim/*`, `Linbpq/*ViaNetsim*`, `Xrouter/*`, `Rax25/*` |

**Why Tier 2 exists (the AXUDP shift-left).** net-sim's software modem (samoyed AFSK1200) is the only way to test the modem/channel layer end-to-end, but it is inherently load-sensitive: under host-CPU contention the demodulator drops a frame and the AX.25 layer sees a phantom loss, which manifests as an interop flake (the #47/#310 contention dynamic, here at the audio layer). **AXUDP (BPQAXIP-over-UDP) is AX.25-frames-over-UDP** — the same real LinBPQ peer, the same `Ax25Listener` + `NetRomService` production pipeline, but the frames cross as UDP datagrams with no AFSK encode/decode. So it sheds net-sim's flakiness while staying a genuine real-BPQ interop. We validated AXUDP↔LinBPQ end-to-end first (#301/#306; `AxudpKissModem`), then re-homed the *protocol* interop onto it.

**What moved to Tier 2 (protocol correctness):**
- **NET/ROM L3 NODES both ways + L4 circuits both ways + Connect-Request framing** — `NetRomL4CircuitViaAxudp` (the re-home of the load-sensitive net-sim `NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim`, now removed). pdn originates NODES → BPQ learns it; pdn hears BPQ's NODES; an L4 circuit pdn→BPQ carries Information both ways + disconnects cleanly; BPQ→pdn originates a reverse circuit that reaches pdn's console + round-trips data. Deterministic: **green across many consecutive runs, including under sustained CPU saturation (load avg ~36) where it held ~12 s with zero flakes** — the load-insensitivity the move buys.
- **NET/ROM NODES ingest** — `NetRomNodesIngestViaAxudp` (the AXUDP analog of `NetRomNodesIngestViaNetsim`). pdn ingests real BPQ's NODES over UDP in ~1 s vs the net-sim version's 90 s channel-loss budget.
- **A representative AX.25 connected-mode interop** (SABM/UA + I-frame round-trip + DISC) — already covered frame-perfectly by `LinbpqViaAxudpConnectedMode` (#301), both directions, plus the FCS-mandatory regression guard.

**What stays a thin net-sim *protocol* smoke (catch modem-interaction surprises):**
- one **NODES-over-AFSK** — `NetRomNodesIngestViaNetsim` (kept; proves NET/ROM NODES survive a real demodulator, and is where XRouter's ambient NODES is observed).
- one **connected-mode-over-AFSK** — `LinbpqViaNetsimConnectedMode` (kept; SABM/UA + I-frame round-trip across the AFSK channel).

**What stays Tier 3 because the modem/channel *is* the subject:** the AFSK1200/lossy-channel scenarios — `NetsimConnectedModeScenarios` (I-frame V(s)/V(r)/V(a) over the half-duplex sim), `LinbpqViaNetsimExtendedMode` (SABME→FRMR v2.0 fallback on the wire), `Rax25ViaNetsimConnectedMode` + `XrouterViaNetsimConnectedMode` (multi-stack handshake over AFSK), the lossy 10 kB / SREJ-recovery transfers, the listener/UI-frame scenarios, and the mod-128 `DirewolfMod128Interop` rig (its own audio-loopback). These are inherently load-sensitive and run behind the §7.2 clean-stack fences.

**How BPQ delivers NODES over a point-to-point AXIP port (the Tier-2-enabling finding).** Unlike a broadcast RF/KISS port, BPQAXIP only delivers a frame to MAP entries whose callsign matches the frame's *destination* (`bpqaxip.c` `AXIP_TX` → `SendFrame`); a NET/ROM NODES UI frame is addressed to the pseudo-destination `NODES`, which matches no per-station MAP, so a stock AXIP port silently drops its own NODES (verified: zero NODES datagrams reach a peer). BPQAXIP gates broadcast delivery behind a `BROADCAST NODES` line + the per-MAP `B` (broadcast-recipient) flag. The fixture's AXIP port therefore carries `BROADCAST NODES` + `B`-flagged MAP entries for the NET/ROM test callsigns — the AXUDP counterpart of the net-sim port's `QUALITY=192` NODES-enablement (a per-port broadcast-delivery config, not an AX.25 protocol change). Crucially `AUTOADDMAP` is replaced with **`AUTOADDQUIET`**: both auto-learn an inbound peer's reply route, but `AUTOADDMAP` *also* flags the auto-added entry a broadcast recipient (`bpqaxip.c` L2205), which — once `BROADCAST NODES` is on — makes BPQ blast NODES at every transient AXIP peer and breaks the connected-mode tests' "no-reply" assertions; `AUTOADDQUIET` (L2212) clears that flag, so only the explicit `B`-flagged NET/ROM MAPs receive NODES.

**The second, subtler half — `BROADCAST NODES` + a `B`-MAP *authorise* delivery but do not *trigger* it; BPQ must first have *heard* that peer's own NODES (verified on the wire).** A `B`-flagged MAP with `BROADCAST NODES` on is necessary but **not sufficient**: BPQ delivers NODES to a `B`-MAP entry only once it has heard *that peer's own NODES* on the port — i.e. once it treats the peer as a live NET/ROM neighbour. A peer that BPQ has never heard from receives nothing, even with the `B` flag set. The consequence for the Tier-2 NODES-ingest tests is asymmetric by peer design:
- The C# `NetRom*ViaAxudp` peers run a full transmitting `NetRomService` whose NODES broadcast loop *also* satisfies BPQ's heard-first requirement, so the heard-first gate is met as a side effect and never surfaces — the `B`-MAP appears sufficient on its own (it isn't).
- A **read-only / TX-free** AXUDP NODES-ingest peer (the design point of the C# read-only slice, the ax25-ts netrom module, and the Rust read-only leg) transmits nothing, so BPQ never hears it, never treats it as a live neighbour, and the `B`-flagged MAP authorises a delivery that is never triggered — the listener hears **silence**. The fix is to **warm BPQ first**: the peer emits at least one NODES of its own through the same AXUDP transport (the exact seam a transmitting netrom layer would use) so BPQ reciprocates. So the `B`-flagged MAP is what *authorises* the NODES delivery; the warming NODES is what *triggers* it — both are required for a read-only ingest peer. (Source of the finding: the on-the-wire behaviour pinned bringing up `docker/linbpq/bpq32.cfg`'s ax25-ts `PNTSAX-1` MAP — see the cfg comment there — and §17 2026-06-05 ax25-ts Tier-2.)

**Follow-up — ax25-ts Tier 2 over AXUDP (RESOLVED 2026-06-05).** The TypeScript runtime (`@packet-net/ax25`, `packet-net/ax25-ts`) now has its own AXUDP/BPQAXIP transport (`AxudpTransport`, FCS-always, the parity leg of `Packet.Axudp`/`AxudpKissModem`) and its protocol interop has been re-homed onto it, mirroring this PR. ax25-ts's `tests/integration/` now carries `linbpq-via-axudp.test.ts` (an `Ax25Stack` connected-mode round-trip — SABM/UA + `P\r` I-frame round-trip + disconnect, over BPQAXIP/UDP) and `netrom-nodes-ingest-via-axudp.test.ts` (its read-only NET/ROM module ingesting real LinBPQ's NODES over UDP, deterministic ~2 s vs the net-sim 90 s budget, load-insensitive). The net-sim protocol smokes (`linbpq-via-netsim`, `netrom-nodes-ingest-via-netsim`) are kept as the thin Tier-3 modem/channel smoke. These run in this repo's `interop.yml` phase B (the sibling-clone step) against the same docker stack — the AXUDP tests dial BPQ's BPQAXIP/UDP port (8093), which needs a `B`-flagged `MAP` for the TS callsign in the fixture (added below). See §17 2026-06-05 (ax25-ts Tier-2) + ax25-ts's own CHANGELOG.

### 7.1 Interop scenarios — staged growth

| Phase added | Scenario | Assertion |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | UI frame KISS-TCP → LinBPQ | LinBPQ log contains exact AX.25 hex |
| 1 | UI frame across back-to-back NinoTNCs | both ends decode identical bytes |
| 2 | SABM/UA cycle (mod-8) vs LinBPQ over net-sim AFSK1200 | our session reaches Connected after UA; DISC/UA returns it to Disconnected (`LinbpqViaNetsimConnectedMode`) |
| 2 | SABM/UA cycle (mod-8) vs XRouter over net-sim AFSK1200 | our session reaches Connected after UA; DISC/UA returns it to Disconnected (`XrouterViaNetsimConnectedMode`) |
| 2 | SABM/UA cycle (mod-8) vs rax25 (Habets, Rust) over net-sim AFSK1200 | our session reaches Connected after UA; DISC/UA returns it to Disconnected (`Rax25ViaNetsimConnectedMode`) — handshake only; REJ/SREJ + segmentation untested in rax25 |
| 2 | ax25.ts (TypeScript, Node TCP) vs LinBPQ over net-sim AFSK1200 | `Ax25Stack` + `TcpKissTransport` reach Connected after UA, exchange I-frames (banner from BPQ + `P\r` ports-command round-trip), DISC/UA cleanly returns to Disconnected (`packet-net/ax25-ts: tests/integration/linbpq-via-netsim.test.ts`, exercised by this repo's `interop.yml` step that clones the sibling repo) |
| 2 | 30 % scripted loss net-sim afsk1200, 10 kB transfer | completes; retries observed; no FRMR |
| 4 | connected-mode over **AXUDP**: pdn → **LinBPQ** (BPQAXIP UDP) | ✅ `LinbpqViaAxudpConnectedMode.Pdn_Connects_Out_To_Linbpq_Over_Axudp_And_Exchanges_IFrames` — the pdn node host (`PortSupervisor` + `TransportFactory` → `AxudpKissModem`) dials BPQ's NODECALL through its own outbound connector; SABM/UA crosses the UDP tunnel, an `I`-frame round-trip (`P`-ports command → BPQ's ports list) flows, BPQ's RR S-frame acks are accepted (an unstripped FCS tail would break them), DISC/UA tears down. **FCS is mandatory on BPQAXIP/UDP** and AXUDP carries it unconditionally (source-verified `bpqaxip.c`; see §17 2026-06-04). |
| 4 | connected-mode over **AXUDP**: **LinBPQ** → pdn (BPQ originates) | ✅ `LinbpqViaAxudpConnectedMode.Linbpq_Connects_In_To_Pdn_Over_Axudp_And_Reaches_The_Node_Prompt` — a pdn node host listens as PNAX25-1 (static `MAP` target); we drive BPQ's telnet to `C 2 PNAX25-1`, BPQ originates SABM over its AXIP port, pdn accepts (its `SessionAccepted` fires + the supervisor runs the node console), and pdn's banner+prompt relay back to the BPQ user. |
| 4 | **AXUDP FCS regression guard** vs **LinBPQ** | ✅ `LinbpqViaAxudpConnectedMode.FcsLess_Sabm_IsDropped_FcsBearing_Sabm_GetsUa` — on the wire: an FCS-less SABM (sent raw via `AxudpSocket.SendRawAsync`) is silently dropped by BPQAXIP/UDP ("Invalid CRC") → no reply; the same SABM with the 2-octet CRC-16/X.25 FCS → BPQ answers UA (`AxudpSocket.ReceiveAsync` strips+validates it). Locks in the FCS-mandatory-for-BPQ requirement so a regression (AXUDP emitting FCS-less again) is caught (§17 2026-06-04). |
| 3 | ACKMODE echo via LinBPQ | ack-bytes returned within 5 s |
| 3 | ACKMODE via NinoTNC pair | ack-bytes correlate to RX on partner TNC |
| 6 | rhp2lib-net client → Packet.Rhp2.Server | full connect / send / recv / close |
| 6 | agw_client.py (ported) | R/G/g/X/V/D pass; non-loopback refused |
| 5.Z V2 🚫 | SABME (mod-128) connect vs **LinBPQ** over net-sim AFSK1200 | **BLOCKED — LinBPQ (6.0.25.23) cannot do mod-128.** Source-verified + on the wire: an inbound SABME is answered with FRMR (`L2Code.c` ~687: *"Reject until we support Mod 128"*); BPQ only ever sends SABM outbound; its XID is clamped to "Mod 8 and 256 Byte frames". No config knob enables it (`MAXFRAME=1..127` caps only the mod-8 window). Needs a genuinely mod-128 peer → **direwolf (#177)**. (V5b §17 2026-06-03) |
| 5.Z V2 ✅ | SABME (mod-128) connect vs **direwolf** | ✅ `DirewolfMod128Interop.ExtendedConnect_DirewolfAnswersUaAtMod128_ThenDisconnects` — our extended-preferred `DL-CONNECT` emits SABME, real Dire Wolf 1.8.1 answers **UA at modulo-128** (no FRMR, no DM, no config knob — the opposite of LinBPQ), we reach Connected-extended; UA asserted on the wire; DISC/UA tears down. **Headline finding of #177: Dire Wolf, as responder, accepts an inbound SABME at mod-128 unconditionally** (`sabm_e_frame` state 0: `if (extended) set_version_2_2` → modulo 128 + UA; `maxv22` gates only the *outgoing* SABME path). **Topology:** Dire Wolf is NOT on net-sim — its connected-mode engine is AGW-only on a real radio channel, and net-sim exposes no external audio ingress, so the `docker/direwolf` container runs the two-direwolf audio-loopback path (two Dire Wolf radio channels share a PulseAudio null sink as the AFSK1200 RF; a gateway instance is the KISS modem our session dials, a responder instance runs `ax25_link.c` driven by an in-container AGW echo helper). (V5b §17 2026-06-03) |
| 5.Z V2 ✅ | Version-negotiation **fallback**: SABME → a v2.0-only peer (**LinBPQ**) | ✅ `LinbpqViaNetsimExtendedMode.ExtendedConnect_FallsBackToMod8_OnLinbpqFrmr_ThenDisconnects` — our extended-preferred `DL-CONNECT` emits SABME, the real LinBPQ answers FRMR (§975, its documented mod-128 rejection), our figc4.6 `t14_frmr_received` fallback (Ax25Spec45) sets version 2.0 + re-establishes with SABM → **mod-8 Connected**; FRMR asserted on the wire; DISC/UA tears down. A companion test proves the fallback link carries data (banner + `P\r` round-trip). LinBPQ *is* the "BPQ port pinned v2.0" the row anticipated. rax25 (`fn sabme()` TODO) remains an alternative fallback peer for a future row. (V5b §17 2026-06-03) |
| 5.Z V4 ✅ | mod-128 **windowed data transfer** vs **direwolf** | ✅ `DirewolfMod128Interop.BidirectionalTransfer_Mod128_PayloadRoundTrips_With7BitSequencing` — after the mod-128 connect, a connected-data payload round-trips via the AGW echo helper; asserts Dire Wolf's echo I-frame uses a **two-octet extended control field with 7-bit N(S)/N(R)** (the on-the-wire proof the link is genuinely modulo-128, not mod-8). (V5b §17 2026-06-03) — mod-128 windowed transfer also proven in-process (`Mod128*ConformanceTests`, V5a ledger). |
| 5.Z V4 ✅ | mod-128 **SREJ loss recovery** vs **direwolf** | ✅ `DirewolfMod128Interop.SrejRecovery_Mod128_DirewolfSrejsDroppedIFrame_WeSelectivelyRetransmit` — the rig drops our outbound I-frame N(S)=1 once (simulating loss on Dire Wolf's RX); Dire Wolf (`srej_enable = srej_single`, on by default for a v2.2 responder) sends an **SREJ** on the wire, our figc4.5 SREJ-received path selectively retransmits the single frame, and all payloads round-trip. SREJ-from-Dire-Wolf asserted on the wire. (V5b §17 2026-06-03) — also proven in-process (`Mod128LossRecoveryConformanceTests`). |
| 5.Z V4 ✅ | mod-128 **segmentation/reassembly** (> N1 payload) vs **direwolf** | ✅ `DirewolfMod128Interop.Segmentation_Mod128_RoundTripsBothDirections_ViaDirewolfInnerPidQuirk` — a >N1 payload now segments + reassembles **round-trip both directions** behind the default-on de-facto-interop quirk `SegmentFirstCarriesL3Pid`. §6.6 / Figure 6.2 draws a segment as `[F/X][data]` with no field for the original L3 PID (figure-literal reassembly loses it → `PidNoLayer3`); Dire Wolf — the only known v2.2 segmenter — reads §6.6's "two-octet header" prose to put the **original L3 PID as an inner octet on the first segment** (`ax25_link.c` `dl_data_request` ~L1330–1410 `[F/X][original PID][data]`, reassembler ~L2010–2030 `ra_buff->pid = data[1]`; the inner octet counts toward the budget via `DIVROUNDUP(len+1, N1−1)`). The repo now **defaults to Dire Wolf's format** (off only under `StrictlyFaithful`, which keeps the figure-literal `[F/X][data]`/`PidNoLayer3` form), so both legs are wire-compatible: forward (us → Dire Wolf, our N1 forces several segments) Dire Wolf reassembles the exact 220 bytes + echoes; reverse (Dire Wolf → us, its PACLEN=128 re-segments the echo) our reassembler reconstructs the exact payload + recovers the inner PID — asserted on the wire via our own receive-side `SegmentationLayer`. Bonus: the inner-PID octet **fixes the documented `PidNoLayer3`-on-reassembly limitation** (the original L3 PID now survives a segmented series). Wire subtlety: N1 must be symmetric — our receiver N1 was raised to match Dire Wolf's PACLEN=128 (a smaller N1 trips the in-frame "info ≤ N1" check → DL-ERROR(O)). Per repo policy this is a **named de-facto-interop quirk** (match the only known v2.2 segmenter), not a silent default; the spec is still canonical (`StrictlyFaithful` reproduces it). The §6.6 / Figure 6.2 gap (two-octet header drops the L3 PID; Dire Wolf fills it non-standardly) is a candidate ax25spec clarification, deliberately not filed. mod-128 segmentation also proven in-process (`SegmentationIntegrationConformanceTests`, incl. default-preserves-L3-PID + StrictlyFaithful-figure-literal cases). (§17 2026-06-04) |
| 5.Z V3 ✅ | **XID negotiation** vs **direwolf** | ✅ `DirewolfMod128Interop.XidNegotiation_Mod128_DirewolfAnswersXidCommand` — after the mod-128 connect we send an XID command; Dire Wolf answers with an **XID response** (U-frame base 0xAF) on the wire, link survives. (Dire Wolf *initiates* XID only as the connection initiator — `ax25_link.c` L4904; as responder it stays Connected and only answers an XID command, which is exactly what we drive.) (V5b §17 2026-06-03) — XID-vs-LinBPQ remains mod-8-only (BPQ's `ProcessXIDCommand` clamp); not implemented this leg. |
| 5.Z V5 ⬜ | **ax25-ts mod-128 parity** vs **LinBPQ** over net-sim | the TS runtime (`@packet-net/ax25`) connects mod-128, exchanges extended I-frames, recovers loss, and tears down — mirroring the C# rows (run from `packet-net/ax25-ts`, driven by this repo's `interop.yml` sibling-clone step) |
| 9 | read-only **NET/ROM NODES ingest** vs **XRouter + LinBPQ** over net-sim (**Tier 3 — the thin NODES-over-AFSK smoke**) | ✅ `NetRomNodesIngestViaNetsim.Pdn_hears_both_reference_nodes_NODES_broadcasts_and_learns_them_as_neighbours` — pdn (a real `Ax25Listener` + `NetRomService`, no TX) ingests the on-the-wire NODES (UI/PID-0xCF/dest `NODES`) of **both** reference peers and learns each as a directly-heard neighbour with an assumed direct route: `PNXRT:PN0XRT` (XRouter, ambient ~75 s cadence) and `PNTST:PN0TST` (LinBPQ, **provoked** — the source-verified positional-challenge `PASSWORD` → `SENDNODES` sysop handshake forces an immediate broadcast). **Headline finding: BPQ advertises NODES out a port only if that port's `QUALITY` ≠ 0, and the per-`PORT` default is 0** (`L3Code.c`) — so the netsim port needs `QUALITY=192` to emit at all; "empty table" was never the reason it was silent (#303). **Retained as the thin Tier-3 NODES smoke** (catches modem-interaction surprises + observes XRouter's ambient NODES through a real demodulator); the *detailed* NODES + L4 protocol interop now lives in Tier 2 — see §7.0 (§17 2026-06-05). (§17 2026-06-04, PR #305) |
| 9 | **ax25-ts NET/ROM NODES ingest** vs **XRouter + LinBPQ** over net-sim (TS parity) | ✅ `packet-net/ax25-ts: tests/integration/netrom-nodes-ingest-via-netsim.test.ts` — the TS `@packet-net/ax25` netrom module (a real `Ax25Listener` + `NetRomService`, no TX) ingests the on-the-wire NODES of **both** reference peers and learns each as a directly-heard neighbour with an assumed direct route, mirroring the C# row above: `PNXRT:PN0XRT` (XRouter, ambient) and `PNTST:PN0TST` (LinBPQ, **provoked** via the same positional-challenge `PASSWORD` → `SENDNODES` handshake, the C# `BpqSysop` driver ported to a `node:net` IAC-aware telnet client). Run from `packet-net/ax25-ts`, driven by this repo's `interop.yml` sibling-clone step (`npm run test:integration` against the same docker stack; `describe.skipIf` self-skips when 127.0.0.1:8100 is down). Closes the #37 follow-up. (§17 2026-06-04, ax25-ts #38) |
| 9 ✅ | **NET/ROM NODES ingest** vs **LinBPQ** over **AXUDP** (**Tier 2 — frame-perfect**) | ✅ `NetRomNodesIngestViaAxudp.Pdn_hears_real_linbpq_NODES_over_axudp_and_learns_it_as_a_neighbour` — the AXUDP analog of the net-sim NODES-ingest row: pdn (a real `Ax25Listener` over `AxudpKissModem` + `NetRomService`, no TX) ingests real LinBPQ's `PNTST:PN0TST` NODES over the BPQAXIP/UDP tunnel and learns it as a directly-heard neighbour — same protocol assertion, modem-less. Provoked via the same `PASSWORD` → `SENDNODES` sysop driver; **lands in ~1 s** (no AFSK channel loss) vs the net-sim version's 90 s budget. **Enabling finding:** a point-to-point AXIP port drops its own NODES UI (addressed to the pseudo-call `NODES`) unless the recipient MAP is broadcast-flagged — the fixture adds `BROADCAST NODES` + a `B`-flagged MAP (the AXUDP counterpart of the netsim port's `QUALITY=192`), and uses `AUTOADDQUIET` so the connected-mode tests' AUTOADD peers don't also get NODES. (§17 2026-06-05) |
| 9 ✅ | **NET/ROM L3 origination + L4 circuits** vs **LinBPQ** over **AXUDP** (**Tier 2 — frame-perfect; re-home of the load-sensitive net-sim test**) | ✅ `NetRomL4CircuitViaAxudp.Pdn_originates_NODES_and_runs_L4_circuits_both_ways_with_real_linbpq_over_axudp` — the transmitting NET/ROM stack validated against a **real LinBPQ 6.0.25.23**, frame-perfectly over a BPQAXIP/UDP tunnel (no modem/audio). **L3 both ways:** pdn (`broadcast`, alias `PNL4B`) originates NODES → BPQ learns it (`NODES`→`PNL4B:PNL4BX-1`, `ROUTES`→`PNL4BX-1 200 1`, queried via the sysop session), and pdn hears BPQ's `PNTST:PN0TST`. **L4 pdn→BPQ (core):** `ConnectCircuitAsync` opens an interlink AX.25 PID-0xCF session over UDP + originates an L4 circuit (`NetRomNodeConnection`, `TransportKind=NetRom`) → a command round-trips to BPQ's node prompt over the circuit (BPQ's `PN0TST` identity relays back — Information both ways) → clean Disconnect Request/Acknowledge. **L4 BPQ→pdn (reverse):** BPQ's `C PNL4B` originates the circuit to pdn (`IncomingCircuit` fires with the correct originating user `PN0TST` + window 4 — the proof the **Connect-Request info-field framing** `[window][user][node]` via `ConnectRequestInfo` reads correctly), pdn accepts + bridges an echo console, a relayed line round-trips. **The re-home of the removed `NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim`** (its protocol assertions, minus the AFSK): **deterministic across many consecutive runs incl. under sustained CPU saturation (load avg ~36), holding ~12 s with zero flakes** — exactly the load-insensitivity the net-sim version lacked (§7.2 flagged it ~1-in-3 under load). The `ConnectRequestInfo` codec (#308/#309, the canonical `[window][user][node]` appendix layout; spec-faithful default, not a BPQ-ism) is now asserted frame-perfectly here. (§17 2026-06-05) **Isolation:** tagged `[Trait("Group", "NetRom")]` so it runs in the clean-stack-fenced NET/ROM phase of `interop.yml` (a fresh BPQ → no learned-state carryover), isolated from the timing-sensitive AX.25 tests — see §7.2. `NetRomService` is `await using` so its `DisposeAsync` cleanly DISCs the interlink before teardown (the #309 BPQ-state hygiene). |
| 9 ✅ | **ax25-ts NET/ROM NODES ingest** vs **LinBPQ** over **AXUDP** (**Tier 2 — frame-perfect; TS parity**) | ✅ `packet-net/ax25-ts: tests/integration/netrom-nodes-ingest-via-axudp.test.ts` — the AXUDP analog of the ax25-ts net-sim NODES-ingest row + the TS parity of `NetRomNodesIngestViaAxudp`: the TS `@packet-net/ax25` netrom module (a read-only `Ax25Listener` over the new `AxudpTransport` + `NetRomService`, no TX) ingests real LinBPQ's `PNTST:PN0TST` NODES over the BPQAXIP/UDP tunnel and learns it as a directly-heard neighbour with an assumed direct route — same protocol assertion, modem-less. Provoked via the same `PASSWORD` → `SENDNODES` handshake; **deterministic in ~2 s** (vs the net-sim version's 90 s budget) and **load-insensitive** (green under sustained CPU saturation, load avg > 40). Runs from `packet-net/ax25-ts`, driven by this repo's `interop.yml` phase-B sibling-clone step against the same stack. Uses a DISTINCT callsign `PNTSAX-1` + fixed port 8196 with a `B`-flagged `MAP` in the fixture (added this PR); since the TS netrom module is read-only/TX-free by design, the test warms BPQ by sending its own NODES through the shared `AxudpTransport` (the seam a transmitting netrom layer would use) so BPQ treats it as a live neighbour and reciprocates. (§17 2026-06-05 ax25-ts Tier-2) |
| 9 ✅ | **ax25-ts connected-mode** vs **LinBPQ** over **AXUDP** (**Tier 2 — frame-perfect; TS parity**) | ✅ `packet-net/ax25-ts: tests/integration/linbpq-via-axudp.test.ts` — the AXUDP analog of the ax25-ts net-sim connected-mode row + the TS parity of `LinbpqViaAxudpConnectedMode` (Direction A + the FCS guard): `Ax25Stack` over `AxudpTransport` dials BPQ's NODECALL over BPQAXIP/UDP (SABM/UA via BPQ's `AUTOADDQUIET` — no static MAP needed for dial-out), reaches Connected, exchanges I-frames (BPQ's CTEXT banner + a `P\r` ports-command round-trip — exercising BPQ's RR S-frame acks, which an unstripped FCS tail would break), DISC/UA disconnects. **FCS is mandatory on BPQAXIP/UDP** and `AxudpTransport` carries it unconditionally (CRC-16/X.25, low byte first, FCS-always, no FCS-less mode); a successful FCS-bearing handshake is itself the FCS proof (the C# `FcsLess_Sabm_IsDropped` guard locks the negative case). Distinct callsign `PNTSCX-1` + fixed port 8197. (§17 2026-06-05 ax25-ts Tier-2) |

---

### 7.2 Interop matrix isolation — cross-scenario contamination + the clean-stack fence

**The problem (Tom's diagnosis, confirmed on the wire 2026-06-05).** The interop stack is **one shared LinBPQ daemon + one net-sim half-duplex afsk1200 channel** (`collision_mode: silence` — any concurrent TX silences *both*). The single `interop matrix` job ran *all* `Category=Interop` C# tests + the cloned-ax25-ts suite against that one stack, so scenarios contaminated each other through **LinBPQ learned-state** (NODES/routes/open AX.25 sessions) and **channel contention**. The heavyweight NET/ROM L4 scenario (`NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim` — NODES origination + bidirectional L4 circuits + a broadcasting pdn) left the channel busy and LinBPQ warm, and the timing-sensitive AX.25 tests that share net-sim port 8100 (`Rax25ViaNetsimConnectedMode`, `NetsimConnectedModeScenarios`, the ax25-ts `IFrame_RoundTrip`) then flaked intermittently. The interop analog of #47 (the unit-test contention flake, fixed with `ci.yml` `max-parallel:4`).

**The #309 leak — real, and a node runtime bug (not just a test artefact).** Probing LinBPQ's sysop interface (`LINKS`/`NODES`/`ROUTES`) immediately after the `NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim` object was disposed showed LinBPQ still holding a **half-open connected-mode AX.25 link to `PNL4ND`** (`LINKS: PNL4ND PN0TST S=5` — connected, T1 running) plus the learned `PNL4:PNL4ND` node + route. Root cause: `NetRomService.Dispose()`/`DetachPort()` dropped the interlink from its dictionary but **never sent a DISC** on the underlying interlink AX.25 session, and `Ax25Listener.DisposeAsync()` abandons sessions locally without DISC — so the neighbour was left a session it thinks is alive and **polls (RR P/F + T1 retransmits) onto the shared channel** for ~10–20 s until its own N2 timeout. A focused harness reproduced the flake deterministically: with a leaked interlink present, fresh AX.25 connects on the shared channel degraded from 6/6 @ ~2 s to as low as 3/6 with 25–30 s timeouts; a clean channel was always 6/6 @ ~2 s. **The same `Dispose`/`DetachPort` path runs in the live node host** (on shutdown and on port reconfigure/detach), so a real pdn that opened a NET/ROM interlink left the neighbour a half-open link → **the fix is lab-deployable.**

**The runtime fix (lab-deployable).** `NetRomService` now implements `IAsyncDisposable`: `DisposeAsync()` tears the L4 circuits down (their Disconnect Requests ride the still-live interlinks), then **cleanly DISCs each interlink AX.25 session** — posting `DlDisconnectRequest` and waiting (bounded, 8 s grace) for the DISC/UA to settle on the wire — *before* detaching the ports / disposing the listeners. `DetachPort` posts a best-effort DISC; the new `DetachPortAsync` does the graceful waited DISC. `NodeHostedService` now disposes `NetRomService` **before** the `PortSupervisor` (the DISC needs the ports' listeners alive), and `PortSupervisor.TearDown{,All}Async` call `DetachPortAsync` before disposing each port. In-process regression: `NetRomL3L4IntegrationTests.Disposing_the_service_cleanly_DISCs_its_interlink_…` (two nodes, open an interlink A→B, dispose A's service, assert B's AX.25 session returns to Disconnected — deterministic, no docker). On the wire the LinBPQ link now drops in ~5 s (a real DISC) instead of timing out N2.

**The isolation (Tom's call — split the NET/ROM scenarios off).** The runtime fix is necessary but not sufficient: the contamination is cumulative (heavy NET/ROM channel traffic + LinBPQ warm state, broader than the one leaked link), so the C# NET/ROM scenarios are tagged `[Trait("Group", "NetRom")]` and `interop.yml` runs **three clean-stack-fenced phases**, each preceded by a fresh `down -v --remove-orphans` + `up --wait` (`scripts/interop-recreate-stack.sh`): **(A)** the AX.25 + general interop group (`Category=Interop&Group!=NetRom`) — the timing-sensitive group, run **first** on a pristine stack so no NET/ROM residue can precede it; **(B)** the cloned-ax25-ts integration suite (its own fresh stack — its internal NET/ROM-ingest test can't contaminate the C# AX.25 timing tests); **(C)** the C# NET/ROM group (`Category=Interop&Group=NetRom`) — run **last + isolated**, where nothing follows it. The `Category=Interop` filter is unchanged (CLAUDE.md's `dotnet test --filter "Category=Interop"` still runs everything locally); the job stays `[self-hosted, Linux, X64]` and keeps the `group: interop` concurrency singleton. Cost: 2 extra `up --wait` (~3 min) per run — bought reliability. **Evidence:** the timing-sensitive AX.25 group (phase A) ran **33/33 every iteration** across many fenced runs and ax25-ts (phase B) green every time — no AX.25 timing test flaked from cross-scenario residue, the contamination is gone. Per §2.5 / the brief: no AX.25 assertion was weakened and no timeout blanket-bumped — the fix is isolation + a real teardown, not masking. **Caveat — the heavyweight `NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim` test is load-sensitive** (independent of contamination): on a quiet host it is reliable (6/6 isolated on a fresh stack), but under sustained CPU saturation (a back-to-back fresh-stack soak loop) it flakes ~1-in-3 — the pdn→BPQ data round-trip times out when the contended host starves its real-time L4 handshakes / the lossy half-duplex sim eats BPQ's reply I-frame inside the read window (catching only XRouter L3RTT transit chatter). That is the exact §7.1 "environmentally flaky, non-blocking, re-run a lone red in isolation" class the test's own remarks already flag (the #47 contention dynamic, here at the interop layer), NOT the cross-scenario contamination this PR targets; deeper hardening of that single test's L4 round-trip (or pinning it nightly-only) is a separate follow-up. Isolating it in phase C means a lone red there cannot drag a timing-sensitive AX.25 test red with it.

**RESOLVED 2026-06-05 (the Tier-2 re-home — §7.0).** That "separate follow-up" is done: `NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim` has been **removed and re-homed to `NetRomL4CircuitViaAxudp`** — the same protocol assertions (NODES both ways, L4 circuits both ways, Connect-Request framing) against the same real LinBPQ, but over a frame-perfect BPQAXIP/UDP tunnel with no software-AFSK. The root cause was never the protocol or the L4 round-trip — it was net-sim's load-sensitive modem (a CPU glitch → audio-decode failure → phantom loss); over AXUDP the test is deterministic, holding ~12 s with **zero flakes across many consecutive runs under sustained CPU saturation (load avg ~36)** where the net-sim version flaked ~1-in-3. Phase C now runs the thin net-sim NODES smoke (`NetRomNodesIngestViaNetsim`) + the two AXUDP NET/ROM tests (`NetRom*ViaAxudp`); the AXUDP tests carry no channel contention (they touch only BPQ's UDP port + telnet, never net-sim port 8100), and `NetRomService.DisposeAsync` DISCs their interlink so BPQ-state hygiene holds within the group. See §7.0 for the full tiering. (§17 2026-06-05)

#### 7.2.1 Teardown reliability — the resource-leak ledger (two CI layers + in-test guaranteed dispose)

The §7.2 *isolation* fence keeps scenarios from contaminating each other; this subsection is the orthogonal concern — **every CPU/RAM-heavy interop resource must be torn down on PASS *and* FAIL/throw/timeout**, because the self-hosted runner persists between runs and the dev box is a small LXC, so a leaked docker stack or background task accumulates → disk/RAM pressure → cascading flakes. The teardown map (resource → mechanism → guaranteed-on-fail):

| Resource | Teardown mechanism | Guaranteed on fail/throw/timeout? |
|---|---|---|
| The docker interop stack (net-sim, LinBPQ, XRouter, rax25, direwolf + the rfnet network + linbpq-data volume) | **Two layers.** (1) `interop.yml` final `tear down` step `if: always()` → `down -v --remove-orphans` (runs even on a FAILED/cancelled phase or job-timeout). (2) **Backstop:** `scripts/interop-recreate-stack.sh` `down -v --remove-orphans` runs *before each* phase (A/B/C), so leftovers from a prior **hard-killed** run (where `always()` was skipped) are reaped on the next run's first docker step. | ✓ both layers — the `always()` step is best-effort + `|| true`; the recreate-before-each is the backstop for a crash that skips it. Containers/orphans/named volume + network are reaped (validated: `down -v --remove-orphans` reaps a service removed from the compose file as a true orphan). |
| In-container processes (direwolf's two Dire Wolf instances + PulseAudio + AGW echo, net-sim's modems) | `docker/direwolf/entrypoint.sh` `set -eu` + `trap term TERM INT` (kills all child PIDs) + a supervise loop; and they live *inside* the container, so `down -v` reaps them regardless. | ✓ |
| In-test KISS-TCP sockets / AXUDP modem / `Ax25Listener` / `NetRomService` (the per-test resources the connected-mode + NET/ROM interop tests open against the stack) | `await using` (runs on the throw path) — `NetRomService` declared *after* its listener so `DisposeAsync` DISCs the interlink while the listener is still alive (the #309 discipline). Production `DisposeAsync` cancels its own internal pump + disposes sockets. | ✓ |
| In-test **background pump tasks** (`Task.Run(() => InboundPump(rig, ct))` in the connected-mode scenarios) | **`InboundPumpScope` (`tests/Packet.Interop.Tests/InboundPumpScope.cs`, new):** an `IAsyncDisposable` that owns the pumps' lifetime and **cancels + awaits them in `DisposeAsync` on every exit path**, declared `await using` *after* the test's `using var cts` so it disposes first. | ✓ **— gap fixed.** Previously these were cancelled+awaited with a bare `cts.Cancel(); await Task.WhenAll(pumps)` only at the *happy-path end*; an assertion throwing before that line abandoned the pumps (racing the socket + cts disposal → unobserved task exception, background work into the next test). |
| In-test spawned OS processes | none — the interop tests *attach* to the already-running stack (they never `Process.Start` a peer/daemon), so there is nothing to orphan. (The vestigial `Testcontainers` package reference is unused — no per-test container lifecycle.) | ✓ (n/a) |
| The nightly fuzzer (`fuzz.yml`) | in-process `dotnet run -- --smoke` (a single foreground process the job awaits); no docker, no daemon, no detached work — dies with the job. | ✓ (nothing persistent to clean) |

**Validation (proven, not reasoned).** With the stack up, a representative interop test was forced to (a) **throw** and (b) **hang past a shortened in-test budget**; in both cases the test failed/cancelled fast (no teardown deadlock) and left **zero orphaned test processes** and the docker stack untouched (the test owns no docker) — confirming `InboundPumpScope.DisposeAsync` + the `await using` sockets dispose on the failure path. The CI backstop was validated directly: `down -v --remove-orphans` reaps the full defined stack + named volume + a service removed from the compose file (a true Compose orphan). The temporary fault was reverted. See §17 2026-06-05 (teardown-reliability audit).

#### 7.2.2 Heavy-workflow serialization — a reader/writer host flock against CPU oversubscription

The self-hosted CI runs **4 runners (`pdn-runner-1..4`) on one physical box**. Each workflow has its own `concurrency` group, so the box is oversubscribed beyond `ci.yml`'s `max-parallel: 4` whenever a CPU-heavy workflow runs *alongside* `ci` — most often `interop` (its net-sim AFSK modems burn CPU continuously while the stack is up). The flaky-test campaign (§17 2026-06-05) root-caused the recurring `Packet.Node.Tests` flake to exactly this: under oversubscription the integration suites' real-time AX.25 inbound pump (a thread-pool `Task.Run` over a `Channel`) and retransmit timer get scheduling-starved, so a delivered UA sits unconsumed and `ConnectAsync` burns its budget. The work is correct — it is being *starved*, not raced — so the §2.5 fixes (bump the budget / retry / skip) are all forbidden masking, and the campaign proved the tempting code fix (a dedicated pump thread) does **not** help, because `Channel` read completions are themselves pool-scheduled. The real lever is reducing oversubscription.

**The mechanism (in-repo, no host access):** a box-wide **reader/writer `flock`** on `/tmp/pdn-ci-heavy.lock`. `ci.yml`'s matrix legs take it **shared** (`flock -s`); the heavy exclusive workflows — `interop` (each test phase), `fuzz`, `deb-smoke` (the crossgen publish) — take it **exclusive** (`flock -x`). Shared holders coexist, so `ci`'s legs still run 4-up among themselves; an exclusive holder excludes all shared holders and vice versa, so a heavy workflow never executes concurrently with `ci`'s test phase. Every site is **command-wrapped** (`flock <mode> <lockfile> <cmd>`), so the lock is released the instant the wrapped process exits — pass, fail, or kill — with **no holder process to leak** and therefore no deadlock risk; and it **harmlessly no-ops if the runners don't actually share `/tmp`** (each locks its own file → every acquire succeeds immediately → status quo), which is the safe failure mode. Deliberately **not** a GitHub `concurrency:` group: that allows only one in-progress + one pending run per group and **cancels** older pending runs (which would silently drop a queued `main`-push CI). Coverage is the test-execution overlap (where both sides are hottest); a brief residual overlap is possible during `interop`'s stack-recreate windows, which is acceptable. The durable fix remains host-side (per-runner cgroup CPU isolation, or a dedicated runner for `Packet.Node.Tests`) and stays Tom's call.

---

## 8. Hardware-in-the-loop development cycle

The durability backbone. Two NinoTNCs are connected:

- **USB serial** to dev host. KISS over `/dev/ttyACM*` (Linux) or COM ports (Windows).
- **Audio TX↔RX** cross-wired between the two TNCs, emulating an FM RF link.

Use cases:

- Phase 1 onward: every PR touching `Packet.Kiss*` or `Packet.Ax25` runs the self-hosted hardware-loop job, exercising a representative interop scenario over the physical loop.
- Phase 3: ACKMODE echo verified end-to-end across real firmware.
- Phase 2: lossy-channel behaviour reproduced physically by attenuating the audio path.
- Local dev iteration: `packetnet sdlgen` + `dotnet test --filter "Category=HardwareLoop"` against the live TNC pair.

The probe at [`tests/Packet.Interop.Tests/Hardware/NinoTncEnumeration.cs`](../tests/Packet.Interop.Tests/Hardware/NinoTncEnumeration.cs) is the entry point. Currently skips when fewer than 2 ACM-class devices are found.

---

## 9. Packaging & onboarding

**Status (2026-06-04):** the first real packaging path is shipped — pdn builds as a self-contained `.deb` for amd64/arm64/armhf (`scripts/build-deb.sh`, hardened systemd unit + conffile in `packaging/`), released via `publish-node.yml` on `node-v*` tags and validated end-to-end on the box (see §17); every release is now gated by an automated install smoke (`scripts/deb-install-smoke.sh`) that proves the amd64 `.deb` installs, boots + answers `/healthz`, and purges cleanly on a pristine Debian/Ubuntu container, with a shift-left copy (`deb-smoke.yml`) on packaging PRs. The signed-tarball installer, cosign verification, multi-arch Docker image, `packetnet ctl`, and the channel-aware in-app self-update below remain the eventual onboarding vision (self-update design: [`docs/node-self-update-design.md`](node-self-update-design.md)). **The apt repo is maintainer-owned and out of packet.net's scope** — the Debian maintainer (hibby) runs + signs the OARC reprepro repo; packet.net just ships a well-behaved `.deb` (never self-mutating) for it to package.

- `curl -sSL https://packet.net/install.sh | sudo bash` → detect distro/arch → fetch signed tar → cosign-verify → install systemd unit → generate admin bootstrap URL → print URL.
- `systemctl enable --now packetnet` left to the installer; service runs as unprivileged `packetnet` user; stateful dirs `/var/lib/packetnet`, `/etc/packetnet`.
- Docker image multi-arch via `buildx`; healthcheck on `/api/system/health`.
- TLS on by default. Self-signed cert generated on first start. LE/ACME opt-in from web UI.
- `packetnet ctl` subcommands for ops (reset admin password, dump diag bundle, regenerate cert, run migrations).
- `POST /api/system/update` — **channel-aware** in-app update with auto-rollback on health failure: self-update (versioned dirs + `current` symlink + cosign-verify + atomic flip) on the self-contained channel; an **Apply** button driving a targeted `apt-get install --only-upgrade packetnet` on the packaged channel — so the node never overwrites dpkg-owned files. Design: [`docs/node-self-update-design.md`](node-self-update-design.md).

Targets: linux-x64, linux-arm64, linux-arm (v7), win-x64, osx-arm64, osx-x64. Self-contained binaries unless armhf size > 80 MB (fallback FDD) — measured: the linux-* `.deb`s are all self-contained, armhf ~40 MB (comfortably under), so no FDD fallback is needed.

---

## 10. Security threat model

| Surface | Default bind | Auth | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Web UI (HTTPS) | `0.0.0.0:8443` | session cookie + passkey | TLS by default; SameSite=strict; CSRF token |
| REST API | same | JWT scopes | rate-limited |
| WS monitor/console/update | same | JWT in cookie | scope-gated |
| RHPv2 TCP | `127.0.0.1:8050` | auth msg required | `--listen-public` opt-in |
| RHPv2 WS | piggyback web port `/rhp` | auth msg (or relaxed under `--linbpq-compat`) | |
| AGW TCP | `127.0.0.1:8000` | none (legacy) | non-loopback requires `--listen-public --i-understand-agw-is-unauthenticated` |
| MCP stdio | n/a | local user | `pdn mcp` subcommand; bridges to loopback |
| MCP SSE | `127.0.0.1:8051` | bearer; `read` (read tools) / `operate` (write tools) | in-process; pass-through when auth off |
| KISS-TCP outbound | n/a | n/a | TLS not in scope (TNC vendors don't speak it) |
| AXUDP | configurable | none | startup banner warning |

All write endpoints audit-logged (actor, IP, scope, payload hash) into `config.db`.

The RHPv2 TCP front-end is resource-bounded against a hostile/buggy peer (defaults, all configurable in the `rhp:` block): a concurrent-connection cap (`maxConnections`, 64), a per-client live-handle cap (`maxHandlesPerClient`, 256 — refused with errCode 4), and an in-frame read timeout (`inFrameTimeoutSeconds`, 30 — drops a peer that stalls part-way through a frame, while leaving idle-between-frames unbounded). See the 2026-06-13 amendment-log entry.

JWT scopes (shipped): the hierarchical **`read` ⊂ `operate` ⊂ `admin`** model (`AuthScopes.cs`) — *not* the granular `frames:read`/`ports:write`/`sessions:write`/`mcp:invoke` list this section once penciled in (never built). All surfaces, REST and MCP, gate on the shipped three: read tools/endpoints `read`, writes `operate`, user-management `admin`; the gate passes through when `management.auth.enabled` is off. See [`docs/mcp-design.md`](mcp-design.md) §"Auth & audit".

### 10.1 On-air authentication — TOTP (future direction)

Amateur radio forbids *encryption* (obscuring the meaning of a transmission) but **permits — and needs — authentication**; the two are separable, and TOTP (RFC 6238) sits squarely on the allowed side. A TOTP code is a short, time-bound, single-use value derived from a pre-shared seed via HMAC: it travels in cleartext on the air, yet proves liveness + possession of the seed *without ever transmitting a reusable secret*, and can't be replayed after its time step. That makes it a near-ideal authenticator for a cleartext-mandated medium — and a primitive worth weaving through the whole ecosystem (pdn, pico-node, ax25-ts), not only the IP-side management surfaces in the table above. Where it could earn its place:

1. **Privileged on-air commands.** The console already exposes destructive verbs over a cleartext link — pico-node's `SET`/`SAVE`/`REBOOT` (flash config + reboot) are unauthenticated today, and a sysop-mode / remote-config story is coming for pdn. A TOTP challenge would gate those so a passer-by who can merely *reach* the node can't reconfigure or reboot it. This is the modern replacement for BPQ's positional-challenge `PASSWORD` (which we already drive for `SENDNODES` — see the NET/ROM interop notes): same "prove you hold the secret" goal, but standard, non-replayable, and authenticator-app-friendly.
2. **Trusted peer / interlink authentication.** A node could require a valid TOTP from a neighbour before accepting privileged routing input — a `SENDNODES`-style push, an INP3 peering (see [`netrom-inp3-plan.md`](netrom-inp3-plan.md)), or a trusted interlink — so a rogue station can't inject false routes or impersonate a known node. A per-neighbour shared seed; verification is local and offline.
3. **Web / REST / MCP second factor.** The conventional use: a TOTP factor alongside the passkey/JWT auth in the table above for the Phase-4 admin surfaces (§5.4 Slice-3). Slots in with no on-air implications.

Design notes / open questions: TOTP needs a roughly-synced clock at both ends — pdn has system time; pico-node has no RTC and would lean on NTP-over-WiFi (or a wider validation window). The step/skew window trades clock tolerance against the replay opportunity (a code is live for its whole step). Seed provisioning + rotation, and the per-node vs per-user vs per-neighbour seed model, are the substantive design decisions. Implementation is small and portable (HMAC-SHA1/SHA-256 + a time counter — fits `no_std` pico-node). **Captured as a direction, not scheduled.**

---

## 11. Out of scope for v1

- NET/ROM (Phase 9).
- BBS / chat / mailbox / DAPPS (out-of-tree plugins).
- HF waveforms (VARA, ARDOP) — KISS modems only.
- Multi-node clustering / Postgres backend.
- AOT publish (revisit when Fido2NetLib + Konscious are AOT-trim-safe).
- Localisation (English only in v1).
- **AX.25 digipeating** (an antipattern for nodes). *(GB7RDG-parity decision 2026-06-16 — see §5.G.)*
- **Public multi-user telnet login** (the console stays a sysop/admin interface). *(§5.G.)*
- **BPQmon-style MQTT node telemetry** (we're not a BPQ port — replaced by a Prometheus item in §5.G). *(§5.G.)*
- **IP-over-AX.25 gateway** and **SNMP** (monitoring is REST + SSE + Prometheus). *(§5.G.)*
- **CMS / Winlink RMS gateway** (separate product decision; also deferred in pdn-bbs). *(§5.G.)*

---

## 13. Reference shelf

### 13.1 Spec

- **AX.25 v2.2 rev 4 (Oct 2025)** — Markdown rewrite: [`packethacking/ax25spec`](https://github.com/packethacking/ax25spec) · raw URL: `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/packethacking/ax25spec/main/doc/ax.25.2.2.4_Oct_25.md` · Local cache: `/tmp/ax25-spec.md` if pulled.
- **KISS TNC protocol**: `https://github.com/packethacking/ax25spec/blob/main/doc/kiss-tnc-protocol.md`.
- **Multi-drop KISS / ACKMODE (Karl Medcalf WK5M, John Wiseman G8BPQ)**: `https://github.com/packethacking/ax25spec/blob/main/doc/multi-drop-kiss-operation.md`.

#### APRS (for SP-008 + direwolf-reference pipeline)

The original [`APRS101.pdf`](http://www.ui-view.net/files/APRS101.pdf) from 1998 is **explicitly marked obsolete** by [how.aprs.works/aprs101-pdf-is-obsolete](https://how.aprs.works/aprs101-pdf-is-obsolete/). Canonical updated specs live in [`wb2osz/aprsspec`](https://github.com/wb2osz/aprsspec) (maintained by the direwolf author):

- [`APRS12c.pdf`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wb2osz/aprsspec/main/APRS12c.pdf) — primary spec (v1.2 draft C). Replaces APRS101 for new work. ~130 pp.
- [`Understanding-APRS-Packets.pdf`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wb2osz/aprsspec/main/Understanding-APRS-Packets.pdf) — implementer-friendly companion + **catalogued common bugs** (lowercase callsigns, Kenwood 0xFF bursts, etc.).
- [`APRS-Symbols.pdf`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wb2osz/aprsspec/main/APRS-Symbols.pdf) — full symbol table.
- [`APRS-Digipeater-Algorithm.pdf`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wb2osz/aprsspec/main/APRS-Digipeater-Algorithm.pdf) — digipeater behaviour (when we ever digipeat).

When APRS101 and APRS12c disagree, **APRS12c wins** for our purposes.

### 13.2 Reference implementations

- **LinBPQ** (John Wiseman G8BPQ) — *the* canonical implementation. Local clone: [`/home/tf/src/linbpq/`](/home/tf/src/linbpq/). Notable files:
  - `L2Code.c` — data-link layer (frame handling, RR/RNR decisions in `RR_OR_RNR()` at L4143).
  - `AGWAPI.c:37-67` — AGW 36-byte header struct.
  - `tests/integration/helpers/{agw_client.py, linbpq_instance.py, telnet_client.py}` — test harness we will port patterns from.
  - `docs/protocols/{kiss,rhp,bpqtoagw,axip}.md` — LinBPQ-specific configurables.
- **ax25ms** (Thomas Habets) — protocol-faithful modern C++ implementation. Clone: [`/tmp/impl-survey/ax25ms/`](/tmp/impl-survey/ax25ms/). `src/seqpacket_con.{h,cc}` carries `own_receiver_busy` / `peer_receiver_busy` flags as defined; flag-set paths are dead code.
- **direwolf** (WB2OSZ) — full v2.2 SDL state machine in `src/ax25_link.c`. Useful for cross-checking SDL transitions; the author flags errata in comments (search for `Erratum`).
- **rhp2lib-net** (M0LTE) — RHPv2 .NET client. Local clone: [`/home/tf/src/rhp2lib-net/`](/home/tf/src/rhp2lib-net/). We mirror `src/RhpV2.Client/Protocol/{RhpFraming,RhpConstants,Messages,RhpJson}.cs` into `Packet.Rhp2` verbatim; `Testing/MockRhpServer.cs` is the blueprint for `Packet.Rhp2.Server`.

### 13.3 Test peers

- **test-net** — synthetic BPQ + XRouter network: [`github.com/packet-net/test-net`](https://github.com/packet-net/test-net). 14 BPQ + 2 XRouter nodes, 5 towns + backbones, external KISS attach at `localhost:8100`.
- **net-sim** — audio-domain network simulator: [`github.com/packethacking/net-sim`](https://github.com/packethacking/net-sim). afsk1200 / gfsk9600 / il2p modems, FM capture mixer, per-link `loss_db`. **Does not support ACKMODE**.
- **Xrouter** — closed-source NetRom node: [`github.com/packethacking/xrouter-container`](https://github.com/packethacking/xrouter-container). AXIP/AXUDP only (no KISS).

### 13.4 Hardware

- **NinoTNC** (N9600A) — kissproxy mode catalog reused under MIT. Source: [`packet-net/kissproxy@web-interface`](https://github.com/packet-net/kissproxy/tree/web-interface), files `KissFrameBuilder.cs:45-196` and `ModemState.cs:111-141`.

### 13.5 Other docs

- [DAPPS](https://m0lte.github.io/dapps/) — reference plugin target.
- [LinBPQ docs (M0LTE fork)](https://m0lte.github.io/linbpq/).

## 14. Glossary

Domain shorthand collected here as we hit it. Keep current.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **ACKMODE** | G8BPQ KISS extension: command `0x{port}C` with 2 ack-bytes prefix; TNC echoes them back when the frame actually transmits. Lets the host time TX-completion. Not supported by net-sim. |
| **AGW** | TCP wire protocol exposed by SV2AGW's "AGW Packet Engine"; widely adopted as a host-to-TNC API. 36-byte fixed header + variable payload. No auth in v1. |
| **AX.25** | The AX.25 v2.2 link-layer protocol — Amateur Packet-Radio's HDLC variant. |
| **AXUDP** | UDP encapsulation of AX.25 frames. Used for inter-node links without a real RF modem. |
| **DL primitive** | A service primitive exchanged between Layer 3 and the Data-Link state machine. `DL-CONNECT`, `DL-DATA`, `DL-FLOW-OFF`, etc. See §5.3 of the spec. |
| **DXE** | Data Exchange Equipment — spec term for the AX.25 endpoint. |
| **FCS** | Frame Check Sequence — 16-bit CRC-CCITT (poly 0x1021), MSB-first. |
| **HDLC** | High-level Data Link Control — the ISO link-layer family AX.25 derives from. |
| **KISS** | Keep It Simple Stupid — the de-facto host-TNC framing protocol. SLIP-style escape (`FEND=0xC0`, `FESC=0xDB`). |
| **L2 / L3** | Link layer (AX.25 itself) / network layer (NET/ROM, IP, etc.). |
| **mod-8 / mod-128** | Sequence-number width. mod-8 frames have 3-bit `N(S)` / `N(R)`; mod-128 have 7-bit. Negotiated via XID. Mod-128 needs SABME, not SABM. |
| **N(R), N(S), V(A), V(R), V(S)** | Acknowledge sequence in received frame / send sequence in received frame / our acknowledge state variable / our receive state variable / our send state variable. |
| **NET/ROM** | A widely-used layer 3 routing protocol over AX.25. Out of v1; planned as `Packet.NetRom` in Phase 9. |
| **NinoTNC** (N9600A) | KISS modem hardware. Operating mode set via KISS SETHW command; mode + 16 means "don't write to flash". |
| **Own Receiver Busy** | A flag in the data-link state machine defined by the spec as *"Layer 3 is busy and cannot receive I frames."* See §C4.3 and the col 5 amendment-log entry. |
| **P/F bit** | Poll/Final bit in the AX.25 control field. Set in commands (poll), echoed in responses (final). |
| **PID** | Protocol ID byte in the AX.25 header; identifies the encapsulated L3 protocol (`0xCF` = NET/ROM, `0xF0` = "none", `0x08` = segmented frame). |
| **RHPv2** | Routing Hub Protocol v2 — a JSON-over-TCP/WS API for external apps to use a node's data-link. Spec: [rhp2lib.pages.dev](https://rhp2lib.pages.dev/). |
| **SDL** | Specification and Description Language (ITU-T Z.100). The diagram language the AX.25 v2.2 spec uses for its state machines. See [docs/sdl-primer.md](sdl-primer.md). |
| **SREJ** | Selective Reject — supervisory frame requesting retransmission of a single I-frame by `N(R)`. v2.2 only. |
| **SSID** | Secondary Station Identifier — 4-bit suffix in an AX.25 callsign (e.g. `G7XYZ-7`). |
| **T1 / T2 / T3** | Acknowledge / response-delay / inactivity timers. T1 ≈ 3000 ms default; T3 is a keep-alive. |
| **TNC** | Terminal Node Controller — historical name for the KISS modem box. |
| **UI frame** | Unnumbered Information frame — connectionless AX.25 datagram. Used by APRS, beacons, broadcast. |
| **XID** | Exchange Identification frame — used to negotiate mod-8/mod-128, window size `k`, T1, N2, etc. |

---

Tracked here so they don't get lost. Once resolved, move the resolution into the relevant section and add an amendment-log entry.

| ID | Question | Stage | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|
| OQ-001 ([#186](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/186)) | Provenance / spec-deviation field — promote `notes:` prefix to a structured `verification:` block? Deferred until we have ≥3 examples to validate the schema against. | ✅ tracked + closed as completed (see [#186](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/186)) | Tom |
| OQ-002 ([#187](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/187)) | Hardware-loop runner — should we expose it via GitHub Actions self-hosted runner labels (`hardware-loop-runner`)? Tom is providing the rig. Will sort once kit lands on the bench. | Open | Tom |
| OQ-003 ([#188](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/188)) | Cosign key management for one-click update — where does the trusted public key live in the installer? Probably `installers/sigstore-pubkey.pem` baked into the install script, but needs a key-rotation story. | Open | Phase 7 |
| OQ-004 ([#189](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/189)) | NuGet org name — is `Packet.*` claimable on nuget.org or do we need `PacketNET.*` / `Packethacking.*`? Tom to check before first NuGet publish (Phase 6+). | ✅ tracked + closed as completed (see [#189](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/189)) | Tom |
| OQ-005 ([#190](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/190)) | OIDC pluggability — what's the abstraction shape so we don't paint ourselves into a corner? Affects Phase 4 user model. | ✅ tracked + closed as completed (see [#190](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/190)) | Phase 4 |
| OQ-006 ([#191](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/191)) | yEd / GraphML SDL workflow — Tom tried Mermaid and reported the rendering looks too unlike the original SDL to be useful for visual comparison. Mermaid output is shipped anyway (cheap, catches transcription bugs the YAML diff might miss) but does NOT solve the input-side problem. yEd spike (one figure, agreed shape mapping, parse the .graphml back to our YAML) still on the table whenever Tom has big-screen time. | ✅ tracked + closed as completed (see [#191](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/191)) | Tom |
| ~~OQ-007~~ | ~~Stryker mutation score for `Packet.Kiss` is dragged below 70 % by `KissTcpClient`…~~ | ✅ Resolved 2026-05-12 — excluded via `stryker-config.json`; score 67.07→73.33. Fake-socket harness left for whenever Phase 6/7 wants tighter coverage. |
| OQ-008 ([`packet-net/ax25sdl#15`](https://github.com/packet-net/ax25sdl/issues/15)) | Publish `/spec-sdl/` as a community-canonical AX.25 v2.2 state-machine artifact, separate from this repo? The YAML is language-agnostic by design — a Rust/Python/Go/TS codegen against the same files would produce the same transitions, and our C# codegen becomes the reference implementation rather than the source of truth. Three things would need to firm up before "authoritative" is defensible: (a) the guard mini-DSL needs a real grammar (today `GuardEvaluator` parses by ad-hoc string splitting — fine for one consumer, not for many); (b) action verbs need a stable catalog with documented semantics (today they're free-form strings like `"RNR response"`, `"start_T1"`); (c) the schema + events catalog need semver. Realistic move: finish the 27 pages, stabilise the schema, then split `/spec-sdl/` + schema + events into a sibling repo (likely under `packethacking/`). What makes this credibly authoritative rather than just *another* transcription is the encode-then-verify discipline + collaboration with the spec author — both already in place. Revisit at the end of Phase 2. | Open | Tom |
| OQ-009 ([#192](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/192)) | NinoTNC mode-change handshake — does the firmware support runtime mode switching without a write-to-flash cycle? `SETHW(mode + 16)` is the "don't write to flash" form; is the actual mode change immediate, or does it require power-cycle? Affects feasibility of Phase 10's mode-agility workstream. Probe once hardware is on the bench. | ✅ Answered 2026-07-02 on the bench rig (firmware 3.44): `SETHW(mode+16)` takes effect immediately — no re-init, no power cycle. Both TNCs were sitting in different modes, were SETHW'd to mode 6 live, and passed RF traffic first try (see §17 2026-07-02). Flash-persist variant not exercised (deliberately — spares the flash). | Phase 10 |
| OQ-010 ([#193](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/193)) | NinoTNC bootloader / firmware-update protocol — `flashtnc` is the canonical tool; what's the wire protocol? Best to read [`ninocarrillo/flashtnc`](https://github.com/ninocarrillo/flashtnc) source rather than reinvent. Affects feasibility + risk of `packetnet ctl flash-tnc`. | ✅ Answered 2026-07-03 — protocol read from flashtnc.py, validated by 7 upstream flashes on the rig, then reimplemented natively: `BootloaderNinoTncFirmwareFlasher` + `packet-tune flash-tnc`, hardware-validated (zero-change 3.41 reflash of `/dev/ttyACM1`, 17 535 lines/193 s). Wire protocol documented in [`docs/research/tait-ccdi-spike.md`](research/tait-ccdi-spike.md) §flash-tnc; feasibility of `packetnet ctl flash-tnc` = proven (the node-host wiring is the remaining, now low-risk, step). | Phase 10 |
| OQ-011 ([#194](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/194)) | Radio-control abstraction shape — what's the right `IRadioControl` API? Tait CCDI gives us SNR / RSSI / busy / channel / TX-keying. Yaesu CAT and ICOM CI-V have different feature sets. Common subset is probably {frequency-set, frequency-get, RSSI-get, busy-get, PTT-set} — anything radio-specific (Tait's SNR is unusually rich) goes behind a feature-probe. Decide before locking the Tait implementation. 2026-07-02: v0 shipped in `Packet.Radio` exactly as proposed — {RSSI-get, busy-get (property + carrier-sense events), PTT-set} with `RadioCapabilities` feature flags and reserved flags for channel/frequency/TX-power; frequency members deliberately deferred. Still open until a second implementation (Yaesu CAT / ICOM CI-V) pressure-tests the shape. | Open (v0 shipped) | Phase 10 |
| OQ-012 | Native DCD seam — hardware carrier-sense (radio DCD via `IRadioControl`, and Nino's coming KISS DCD extension) should feed the AX.25 stack's *native* channel-busy machinery (the spec's physical/link-multiplexer layer models a real DCD input driving p-persistence and seize/release), i.e. emulate a TNC that exposes DCD — NOT a parallel transport-level deferral. `Packet.Radio.CarrierSenseTxGate` (2026-07-02) is explicitly the interim/degenerate form; retire or demote it once the real seam exists. Open spec-side question for [`packet-net/ax25sdl`](https://github.com/packet-net/ax25sdl): are the physical/LM state machines transcribed, or is that a prerequisite? Both DCD sources (radio control channel + KISS extension) must land in the same seam. **2026-07-04: confirmed feasible in-repo** — `Packet.Ax25.Session.LinkMultiplexerSignal` is exactly the native medium-access seam (LM_SEIZE/RELEASE, "the arbiter that owns the radio"). **2026-07-04: landed.** Architectural finding along the way: in this implementation the LM_SEIZE→confirm round-trip drives *only* the §6.7.1.2 delayed-ack RR timing — actual frame emission (I/S/U/UI, SABM) drains directly to the listener's `SendBytes` sink (the code that literally owns the modem and serialises frames), bypassing the LM. So the carrier-sense gate sits at that transmit chokepoint (the multiplexer's radio-serialisation point), not at the seize-confirm (which would defer acks only). New injectable `ICarrierSense` (in `Packet.Ax25.Transport.Abstractions`) is consulted by a new `Packet.Ax25.Session.CarrierSenseGate` (slot-time wait-for-clear, fail-open) at every `Ax25Listener` keyup; default = no source = always-clear = byte-for-byte prior behaviour, so the SDL battery is untouched. **2026-07-04 (parity rework):** the source is now supplied on a **first-class, parity-tracked** `Ax25ListenerOptions.CarrierSense` member — carrier-sense is a general engine capability (any AX.25 stack should defer keyup while the channel is busy), not a Tait detail, so it belongs on the parity surface, not hidden on a ctor param. The ax25-ts counterpart (`Ax25ListenerOptions.carrierSense` + a `CarrierSense`/`CarrierSenseGate` seam) landed alongside, so `scripts/parity-check.mjs` sees the seam mirrored on both inventories (no new exception, no gap). The node bridges a radio-attached port's `IRadioControl` DCD via `RadioCarrierSense` in `PortSupervisor` bring-up, passed on that option. The interim transport-level `Packet.Radio.CarrierSenseTxGate` (2026-07-02) is now **deleted** (unreleased — never in a `lib-v*` tag — and fully superseded by the native seam). **Residual:** Nino's KISS DCD extension lands in the same gate (a KISS transport implementing `ICarrierSense`), and routing *all* emissions through the LM (so seize/release genuinely gates the medium) stays future spec-side work. | ✅ Landed 2026-07-04 (parity-symmetric; residual: KISS DCD source) | Phase 11 |

---

## 16. Risks

| # | Risk | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| R-01 | SDL transcription is laborious and error-prone (27 pages, subtle semantics). | Per-PR review · trust-the-figure discipline · implementation cross-references · codegen + conformance tests catch structural breakage. |
| R-02 | LinBPQ interop drift between releases. | Pin LinBPQ commit in `docker/linbpq/`; nightly job pulls latest and reports diff. |
| R-03 | ACKMODE only verifiable against LinBPQ container or hardware loop (net-sim doesn't support it). | Hardware-loop runner with the NinoTNC pair + LinBPQ container covers all real-world cases. |
| R-04 | .NET 10 armhf self-contained binary size. | Fallback to framework-dependent + bundled runtime for armhf only; document. |
| R-05 | Passkey UX on LAN HTTPS with self-signed cert (WebAuthn requires secure origin). | `localhost` is treated as secure by browsers; mkcert-style helper for LAN; documented. |
| R-06 | Spec figures may contain genuine errata that aren't caught until the conformance tests fail against a real peer. | Implementation cross-references give us a fallback truth source; PR reviewer checks at least one impl when transition is non-obvious. |
| R-07 | Direwolf / ax25ms / LinBPQ disagree on a subtle behaviour. | Document the divergence in the transition's `notes:`; pick the spec interpretation unless a real peer demonstrably needs otherwise. |

---

## 17. Amendment log

Most recent first. Format:

```
### YYYY-MM-DD — short title
What changed, why, where to look for details.
```


### 2026-07-08 — Head-end: mDNS-under-systemd fix + notify/watchdog + /statusz + stale-input drain ([#577] [#583] [#586])

The Go head-end cluster from the 2026-07-08 arc review, in one PR (head-end Go + packaging/scripts only — no .NET):

- **[#577] (critical, confirmed empirically): mDNS was broken under the shipped systemd unit.** `RestrictAddressFamilies=AF_INET AF_INET6` blocked the `AF_NETLINK` socket Go's `net.Interfaces()` needs, so `zeroconf.Register` failed with *"Could not determine host IP addresses"* on **every `.deb` install** — zero-config discovery silently dead, masked by PDN's manual-address fallback. Fix: `headend/packetnet-headend.service` now sets `RestrictAddressFamilies=AF_UNIX AF_INET AF_INET6 AF_NETLINK` with a comment mirroring the node unit's (which allows AF_NETLINK for tsnet for the same reason); the dangling hardening comment is resolved by documenting **why no `SocketBind*` allow-list exists** (ports are operator-configurable + bridges bind sequentially on hot-plug — any static list would rot). **Verified empirically via transient units on the bench box**: the broken set reproduces the failure signature; the fixed set logs `mDNS advertising _pdnhead._tcp …`. The install smoke (`scripts/headend-deb-install-smoke.sh`) now asserts the unit's AF list AND greps the boot log for `mDNS advertising` / fails on the #577 signature (registration succeeds on a default docker bridge, so the positive assert holds in-container).
- **[#583] (Go half): head-end self-observability.** New `GET /statusz` → `{instanceId, bridgeCount, bridges:[{id, tcpPort, clientConnected}]}` so external monitoring can watch the Pi directly; `/healthz` stays a pinned bare `ok` (backward compat — PDN's health check + the smoke). And the unit is now `Type=notify` + `WatchdogSec=30`: a hand-rolled, dep-free `sd_notify` writer (`headend/notify.go`, ~40 lines) sends `READY=1` once the listeners are up (the HTTP listener is now pre-bound so READY is accurate), `WATCHDOG=1` at half the armed `WATCHDOG_USEC` (with the `WATCHDOG_PID` guard), `STOPPING=1` on shutdown — all no-ops when `NOTIFY_SOCKET` is unset (direct runs unchanged). **Verified empirically**: a transient `Type=notify` + `WatchdogSec=5` unit went `active`, survived 3+ watchdog periods with `NRestarts=0`, served `/healthz` + `/statusz`, stopped clean. (The .NET half — `pdn_headend_*` metrics + background poll — remains open on [#583].)
- **[#586] item 3: stale serial-buffer drain.** A new client no longer receives bytes the device emitted while no client was attached: the bridge flushes the serial input buffer on client connect via a new `SerialPort.DrainInput()` (real hardware: `ResetInputBuffer` = `tcflush(TCIFLUSH)` — instantaneous and non-blocking, so unlike a read-with-deadline drain it cannot swallow bytes arriving *after* the connect; no pump is running yet, so nothing races). Items 1/2/4/5 of [#586] are .NET-side and remain open.
- **Tests.** `TestBridge_DrainsStaleInputOnConnect` (pre-buffered stale bytes flushed, post-connect bytes intact, incl. the between-clients case), `TestStatuszShape`/`TestStatusz_EmptyRegistry` (contract pin), `notify_test.go` (datagram against a fake `NOTIFY_SOCKET` unixgram, env guard, `watchdogInterval` parsing, heartbeat + cancel). `go test ./...`(+`-race`) / `go vet` / `gofmt` green; `make arm64` static build intact; `scripts/build-headend-deb.sh linux-amd64 0.1.4` builds with the new unit staged and the install smoke passes on debian:stable-slim + ubuntu:24.04.
- **Docs.** `headend/README.md`: `/statusz` API contract, `/healthz` pinned-liveness note, connect-time flush under *What it does*, Install section's notify/watchdog + AF_NETLINK/AF_UNIX hardening note. Ship as `headend-v0.1.4` (release cascade separate, per `docs/releasing.md`).

[#577]: https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/577
[#583]: https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/583
[#586]: https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/586

### 2026-07-06 — RELEASE: headend-v0.1.3 (hot-plug + by-path device ids)

Cut `headend-v0.1.3` carrying two head-end improvements, both **hardware-validated on the 2×NinoTNC + 2×Tait bench**:

- **Hot-plug** ([#572]) — a poll-based re-enumerate + diff (`--rescan-interval`, default 3s, `0` disables) dynamically adds/removes device bridges without a restart, leaving existing bridges + connected clients untouched. **Validated on-rig** by reversibly de-/re-authorizing a device via sysfs: a NinoTNC removed in ~3s (`bridge removed … tcp freed`) and re-added in ~2s (`bridge added …`, same id, port reused); a connected client on another device undisturbed.
- **by-path device ids** ([#569]/[#574]) — the device id is now the **physical USB socket** (`/dev/serial/by-path` basename), collision-proof by construction and stable across reboot + same-port replug; `/dev` basename only as an unstable last resort; the by-id string retained as an informational `byId` hint. This **supersedes the by-id-first chain** and closes the shared-serial by-id-flip edge ([#574]) by design rather than patching it (Tom: "by-path is the specific socket, it can't collide"). **Validated on-rig**: the two CP2102 dongles that share USB serial `0001` now both enumerate `idSource:"by-path"` with distinct socket ids, and a CP2102 hot-replug re-adds cleanly (the case that snagged under the by-id chain). One documented consequence: moving a device to a *different* USB port changes its id (re-adopt) — correct for a physical reconfiguration.

Go-only (no lib/node) → `publish-headend.yml` builds the arm64/arm/amd64 `.deb`s + raw binaries + `SHA256SUMS`. Closes [#572] + [#574]. The shared-serial-replug finding was itself surfaced by the hot-plug hardware test.

### 2026-07-06 — Head-end: by-path is the primary device id (shared-serial by-id flip fix) ([#574])

Fixed [#574] — found validating hot-plug ([#572]) on the bench, where the two CP2102 Tait CCDI dongles
**share USB serial `0001`**. udev creates the shared `by-id` symlink (`usb-…CP2102…0001-if00-port0`) for only
ONE of them, and on a **hot-replug** can **flip** it onto the *returned* device, stealing it from the still-present
sibling. Under [#569]'s **by-id-first** id chain the returned device then enumerated with the **same id** as the
still-running sibling's bridge, so the rescan diff (keyed by id + path) saw that id already bridged and **did not
re-add** it (`/inventory` stuck at 3), and the id→device mapping was ambiguous. Root cause: **by-id is only
stable-unique when the USB serial is unique** — a shared serial makes it collide and flip.

- **Fix — `headend/enumerate.go`.** The id chain is now **`/dev/serial/by-path` → `/dev` basename**, dropping
  by-id as an id source:
  - **`by-path` basename** is the **primary id for every device** — it names the physical USB socket, so it is
    **unique by construction** (two devices can't share a socket, so it can never collide the way a shared serial's
    by-id does) and **stable across reboot + same-port replug**. `idSource:"by-path"`, `idStable:true`.
  - **`/dev` basename** is the sole **last resort** (no by-path link — a minimal udev config lacking
    `60-serial.rules`), logged as unstable, `idSource:"dev"`, `idStable:false`.
  - The **`/dev/serial/by-id` string is retained** in the inventory's `byId` field as an informational hint (device
    serial/model + the basename the allow/deny filter matches) but is **never** the id. `idSource` values become
    `by-path` | `dev` (never `by-id`). Net effect on the bench: both `0001` dongles get their distinct by-path ids
    (`…-usb-0:1:1.0-port0` vs `…-usb-0:2:1.0-port0`), stable, **no flip, no collision** — regardless of which one
