### 2026-06-17 — Web UI: accessibility (Radix) pass for overlays and menus (#478)

The control panel's overlay/menu surfaces are now built on the **Radix primitives that were already in the dependency set but unused** — so they get real focus management, ARIA, keyboard navigation, and dismiss semantics, with **no visual change**. Web-UI-only; no backend/runtime change.

The bespoke implementations and what they were missing: `Sheet` (drawer) and `Modal` were hand-rolled `fixed`-overlay divs with a manual `window` keydown listener for Escape and an overlay-click handler — no focus trapping, no focus restore to the opener, no `role="dialog"`/`aria-modal`/labelled-by wiring (a screen reader couldn't tell the modal apart from the page behind it, and Tab could escape the dialog). `Tooltip` was a portaled `<span role="tooltip">` shown on hover/focus but with no described-by association to its trigger. `UserMenu` (in the shell topbar) was a `useState` toggle + a `document` mousedown listener with no menu semantics — no `aria-haspopup`/`aria-expanded`, no `role="menu"`/`menuitem`, no arrow-key navigation or type-ahead, and Escape didn't close it.

The swaps, done once at the shared-primitive layer so every consumer benefits: `Sheet` + `Modal` → **`@radix-ui/react-dialog`** (`Dialog.Root/Portal/Overlay/Content/Title/Description/Close`) — focus trapped while open and restored on close, Escape + overlay-click dismiss, `role="dialog"` + `aria-modal` with the title as the accessible name. `Tooltip` → **`@radix-ui/react-tooltip`** (Provider/Root/Trigger/Portal/Content) — hover **and** keyboard-focus triggered, described-by wired, Escape-dismissable, still portaled so it can't be clipped by an `overflow-hidden` ancestor. `UserMenu` → **`@radix-ui/react-dropdown-menu`** — a proper `aria-haspopup` trigger and an arrow-key-navigable `role="menu"` with Escape/outside-click dismiss and focus restore. The **controlled `open`/`onClose` prop API of `Sheet`/`Modal` is unchanged** (Radix's `onOpenChange(false)` is bridged onto `onClose`), so none of the ~13 call sites across screens needed edits — the accessibility upgrade is entirely inside `components/ui/index.tsx` + the shell's `UserMenu`. Visuals (slide-in drawer, centred card, popover styling, header/body/footer) preserved via the same Tailwind classes, with the slide transition now riding Radix's `data-[state]` attributes. Web gate green locally (typecheck + 86 tests + vite build; the per-run parallel-test flakes are the documented runner-oversubscription infra issue — green single-fork and at bounded concurrency). Closes #478.

### 2026-06-17 — Web UI: per-link T1/T3 link-troubleshoot view (#467)

The control panel now has a **Link troubleshoot** view (`/links`, in the left nav below Monitor) that surfaces the per-link timing/recovery telemetry monitor-v2 already exposes but which nothing in the UI consumed — closing the last frontend gap §5.8 called out ("the **T1/T3 graphs** web link-troubleshoot *view* — the data is now exposed"). Web-UI-only: it **consumes** the already-exposed data, no backend/runtime change.

What it reads, and from where: live **SRTT** (the engine's `SmoothedRttMs`) and **retries** (the live `RC`), plus the **REJ/SREJ** tallies and frame counts, come per-(port, peer) from `GET /api/v1/links` (`LinkStats`) — the monitor-v2 seam #173 wired to read the connected-mode session's T1/SRTT timer state. The configured **T1/T3** come from each port's `ax25.t1Ms`/`ax25.t3Ms` via `GET /api/v1/config` (these are configuration, not live timer instances — the node has no per-link running-timer projection, and the issue's "T1/T3" is exactly the configured timers SRTT/retries are tuned against). No new endpoint or field was needed; the data was all already on the wire.

The view polls `/links` every 2s, keeps a small per-link history ring, and renders both a per-link detail card (T1/T3/SRTT/retries headline + dependency-free inline-SVG **SRTT and retry trend sparklines** + REJ/SREJ + frame counts) and a compact comparison table across every link. SRTT is colour-graded against the port's configured T1 (green well under, amber past T1/2, red at/over T1 — since T1 should be ≥ ~2·SRTT). Links appearing/disappearing are handled by keying on `port:peer`: a link absent from a poll is marked **idle** and kept for a few cycles' grace (so a flapping link doesn't flicker away) before being dropped; a Pause control freezes polling. `LinkStats` was already typed in the UI and `api.linkStats()` already existed (the Monitor's stat strip uses it), so this adds only the screen + route + nav entry + an `Icon` glyph; a smoke test asserts it renders the per-link T1/T3/SRTT/retries. Web gate green locally (typecheck + 86 tests + vite build). Closes #467.

### 2026-06-17 — Bake standard-port mail into shipped node defaults: CapabilityBoundingSet + smoke-test gate (#469)

A fresh `.deb` install can now serve standard-port mail (IMAP 993 / SMTP 465/587) with **zero manual systemd surgery** — the packet.net half of #469 (the pdn-bbs default-port half — `ImapConfig` 1143→993, SMTP submission →465 — ships in a companion `packet-net/pdn-bbs` PR). The `AmbientCapabilities=CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` grant that lets the non-root `packetnet` service (and the pdn-supervised app daemons that inherit its ambient caps) bind privileged ports already landed in `0db30b6` (proven on the lab as a drop-in, then baked into the packaged unit). This change closes the rest: it pins `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` on the unit so the ceiling is exactly that one capability — every other cap is dropped from the bounding set, so even a compromised supervised app cannot raise (e.g.) `CAP_NET_ADMIN`. Ambient ⊆ bounding, so both directives now list the cap; verified well-formed with `systemd-analyze verify` (rc=0). It also adds a packaging-test assertion: `scripts/deb-install-smoke.sh` now greps the *installed* `/lib/systemd/system/packetnet.service` for both the `AmbientCapabilities` and `CapabilityBoundingSet` lines, so a regression that drops the privileged-bind grant fails the cross-distro smoke run instead of silently shipping a unit that can only serve high ports.

`build-deb.sh` copies the unit verbatim, so the staged `.deb` carries the change with no script edit needed. **Packaging-only — zero `Packet.Ax25` / library / node-host behavioural change**, so the parity inventory and `parity-check.mjs` are untouched. Closes #469.

### 2026-06-17 — Fix: kiss-tcp self-heals a half-open TNC/modem reboot (#464)

A kiss-tcp port now recovers from a transient upstream reboot — a net-sim restart, a softmodem/TNC bounce, a pulled cable — **without an operator restart of the service**. The reconnect machinery itself already existed (`ReconnectingKissModem`, #50: re-dial with capped exponential backoff + KISS-param replay, wired into `PortSupervisor` for `KissTcpTransport` ports), but it only fired when the inner frame stream **ended** — i.e. when the peer sent a graceful FIN. The actual field failure is a **half-open** TCP connection: the peer vanishes with no FIN (reboot/cable-yank), so `KissTcpClient`'s `ReadAsync` blocks forever, the stream never ends, the reconnect never triggers, and the port silently dies — exactly the "requires a manual service restart" symptom #464 (and the netsim-rf-lab memory note) describe.

The fix closes that gap at its root: `KissTcpClient` gains a **read-idle liveness timeout** (`DefaultReadIdleTimeout`, 5 min; deterministically driven by an injected `TimeProvider`) — a read that stalls past the idle budget is presumed dead and throws `IOException`, the **same** end-of-stream signal a graceful close produces, so the existing `ReconnectingKissModem` re-dial path takes over. As a faster, belt-and-braces backstop on real sockets, OS **TCP keepalive** is enabled (30 s idle / 10 s interval / 5 probes; best-effort, unsupported knobs swallowed) so a truly dead peer surfaces a read error well before the 5-min idle backstop. The idle window is intentionally generous because a healthy packet link is often quiet for long stretches; `Timeout.InfiniteTimeSpan`/`Zero` disables it (the pre-fix block-forever behaviour, used by the interop/loopback tests). The supervisor threads its own `TimeProvider` through a new `TransportFactory.CreateAsync(transport, timeProvider, ct)` so initial connect **and** every re-dial get the timeout and component tests stay deterministic.

**In-flight AX.25 sessions across the bounce:** they reset, by design, and the node recovers to a working *listening* state rather than wedging. The `Ax25Listener` is built once over the reconnecting wrapper and is never torn down across a re-dial (the wrapper's `ReadFramesAsync` loops internally and never yields end-of-stream during reconnect), so the accept loop survives. But the far end (TNC + the RF peer beyond it) is gone, so any connected-mode session keyed to it sees no acks, exhausts T1/N2, and disconnects via the normal state-machine path; there is no AX.25 facility to resume a session across a transport identity change, and faking one would leave a wedged half-open session. New connects work as soon as the link is back and the KISS params are replayed. This is documented on the `ReconnectingKissModem` class.

**Node-host/transport change only — zero `Packet.Ax25` surface change**, so the parity inventory and `parity-check.mjs` are untouched. Tests: `KissTcpClientReadIdleTests` (a half-open loopback link ends the stream once the fake clock passes the idle window; inbound data resets the idle window so a busy-but-quiet link is **not** dropped; idle-detection-disabled blocks forever like the pre-fix path) and `KissTcpReconnectIntegrationTests` (the real `KissTcpClient`-over-loopback + `ReconnectingKissModem` chain: a half-open first link self-heals, replays TXDELAY/persist onto the reconnected modem, resumes delivering frames with exactly one re-dial; plus a refused-dial-twice-then-recover case proving the bounded-backoff retry loop). Full solution suite green (1266 Node + 83 Kiss + 952 Ax25, no regressions). Closes #464.

### 2026-06-17 — MHeard `MH` console verb + persisted heard log (#454)

The node now has an **`MH`** console verb (`MH` node-wide / `MH <port>` per-port) listing recently heard stations the way operators expect from BPQ / Linux-AX.25 nodes, backed by a **persisted heard log** that — unlike the live `/api/v1/links` telemetry — **survives port teardown AND node restart**. The §5.G MHeard item.

The heard log is a derivation of the **same** frame-trace stream that feeds `/api/v1/links`, not a parallel tap: `NodeTelemetry.Observe` calls the new (optional) `HeardLog.Record(port, source, at)` on every *received* frame, recording the transmitting station. "Heard" = a frame was received from that callsign on that port; TX frames are ours and never a hearing. So one source of truth, consistent with the metrics-exporter discipline above.

`src/Packet.Node.Core/Heard/`: `HeardEntry` (the per-(port, callsign) row — first/last-heard + count), `HeardLog` (the hot-dictionary service; per-port `ForPort` rows + a node-wide `NodeWide` merge that folds a callsign across the ports it was heard on, summing the count and counting distinct ports), over a resilient `SqliteHeardStore` (a `heard_log` table in the consolidated pdn.db, WAL, degrade-not-throw, `INSERT..ON CONFLICT` upsert — mirrors `SqlitePeerCapabilityStore`). **Survival semantics differ from `NodeTelemetry`** on purpose: telemetry counters are dropped on `DetachPort` (a bounced port should count from zero) but the heard log is *history* — it is only cleared by the retention policy or an explicit operator reset, never by teardown. **Retention** bounds growth: a 30-day age-out window + a 500-stations-per-port cap, re-applied timer-free every 2048 hearings and once on construction (so a restart re-applies the policy to the hydrated rows). Optional store ⇒ in-memory-only when no pdn.db (tests / embedders unaffected), mirroring the capability cache.

**Surfaces:** the `MH` console verb (read-only, no elevation — it leaks only which stations were on the air, the same data the monitor shows; columns callsign / last-heard-ago / count / port[s]; help updated) and, cheaply and consistently with the existing read API, **`GET /api/v1/heard`** (node-wide) + `?port=<id>` (per-port), projecting the two instants relative-ago like the NetRom/Capabilities rows. Wired: `HeardLog` + `SqliteHeardStore` registered in `Program.cs` (eager) and `NodeServiceCollectionExtensions` (embedder); `NodeHostedService` threads it into `NodeTelemetry` + the console env and exposes a `Heard` handle; `NodeConsoleEnvironment.Heard`; `MhCommand` + parser (`MH`/`MHE`/`MHEARD` from the ≥2-char stem so a bare `M` doesn't trigger it) + `MhText` dispatch.

**No `Packet.Ax25` change** — node-host only; the parity inventory is untouched (`parity-check.mjs` unaffected). Tests: `SqliteHeardStoreTests` (round-trip, upsert-on-conflict, per-(port, call) keying, Clear/ClearAll, **Prune age-out + per-port cap**, persist-across-reopen, degrade-not-throw), `HeardLogTests` (record first/last/count, **per-port vs node-wide aggregation + dedup/merge**, **survives a simulated restart**, retention/pruning, construction re-prune, forget/clear), `MhConsoleTests` (node-wide + per-port output, not-available, nothing-heard, help), `NodeCommandParserTests` MH cases, a `NodeTelemetry` feed test (RX source recorded, TX not), and `HeardApiTests` (`/api/v1/heard[?port=]` reachable as arrays). Full `Packet.Node.Tests` green (1264). Closes #454.

### 2026-06-17 — Prometheus `/metrics` exporter (#457)

The node now serves a Prometheus-compatible scrape endpoint at **`GET /metrics`** (Prometheus text exposition format), so operators can graph/alert on node health, per-port behaviour, and NET/ROM forwarding throughput with standard tooling (Prometheus + Grafana) — the §5.G item that replaced the dropped BPQmon ask. The acceptance hinge (first-class): **one source of truth**. Every value is read from the SAME live counters that feed the REST/SSE `/api/v1/*` telemetry — the `NodeTelemetry` frame tap (per-port frame totals, per-(port, peer) byte/REJ/SREJ rollups), the live `Ax25Session` timer state (RC / queue depth / outstanding I-frames), the new `NetRomService.ForwardingStats`, and the existing `PdnReadApi.BuildStatus`/`BuildPorts` projection helpers — so a `/metrics` number and the corresponding `/api/v1` JSON number can never diverge.

**Bounded label cardinality** is the other load-bearing decision: the only label is `port` (one per *configured* port, a closed operator-controlled set) plus a 3-value `reason` on the forward-drops series. There is deliberately **no `peer`/`callsign` label** anywhere — a per-(port, peer) link is keyed by the remote callsign, which is unbounded as random stations are heard, so per-link counters are **aggregated up to the port** before export. A `Label_cardinality_is_bounded_to_the_port_even_with_many_peers` test pins this (50 distinct peers on one port → one series, no peer label, document series count stays a small constant).

`pdn_*` namespace, correct Prometheus types (counter vs gauge), three buckets: node health (`pdn_build_info` info-gauge, `pdn_uptime_seconds`, ports up/total, sessions, NET/ROM neighbour/dest counts, process RSS/CPU/threads/GC-heap/start-time, traffic-log drops), per-port (`pdn_port_up`, sessions, frames in/out, info bytes in/out, REJ/SREJ, retries, tx-queue-depth, outstanding-iframes), and forwarding (`pdn_netrom_forwarded_frames_total`/`_bytes_total`, `pdn_netrom_forward_drops_total{reason}`). **Same listener** as the REST API, **same `read`-scope gate** (`PdnAuthPolicies.Read`) — unauthenticated when `management.auth.enabled` is off (the node binds 127.0.0.1 by default → the standard localhost-scrape posture), `read`-token once auth is on. **In-process, no Prometheus client dependency**: a hand-rolled `PrometheusTextWriter` (~70 lines) emits HELP/TYPE/sample lines with exposition-spec label escaping.

New: `src/Packet.Node/Api/PdnMetricsApi.cs` (formatter + `Render` + `MapPdnMetrics`), `NetRomService.ForwardingStats` (+ `NetRomForwardingStats` record; counters bumped on the `ForwardDatagram` path), `docs/observability.md` (the full metric reference + exposure posture). `PdnReadApi.BuildStatus` widened `private`→`internal` so the exporter reuses it. Wired in `Program.cs` beside `MapPdnReadApi`. **No `Packet.Ax25` change** — the exporter only *reads* public session/context properties (`RC`, `IFrameQueue`, `SentIFrames`); parity inventory untouched (`parity-check.mjs` unaffected). Tests: `PrometheusTextWriterTests` (line shapes, integral-vs-float formatting, non-finite→0, label escaping), `PrometheusExporterTests` (well-formed exposition, node-health values track config, per-port frame/byte counters track the tap, **bounded cardinality**, forwarding all-zero/by-reason), `MetricsEndpointTests` (boots the real composition root → `/metrics` reachable, content type, every line `pdn_*` + parses), and a `ForwardingStats_start_at_zero` assertion. Closes #457. Node-host change only.

### 2026-06-17 — Two quick node-config wins: per-port NET/ROM QUALITY (#455) + per-port PACLEN/N1 (#458)

Two §5.G GB7RDG-parity gaps closed in one PR — both per-port config knobs on the existing config seam, both with the web Ports editor wired (the rest of the §5.G CONSIDER items still need their own design passes).

**Per-port NET/ROM QUALITY (#455).** A new `PortConfig.NetRomQuality` (`int?`, 0..255, null ⇒ inherit) overrides the node-wide `netRom.defaultNeighbourQuality` for routes a port hears, so a mixed-grade node advertises an accurate quality on each leg (the GB7RDG 191/192 case) instead of one uniform value. The override threads config → `PortSupervisor.BringUp` → `NetRomService.AttachPort(…, neighbourQuality)` (stored on the per-port `Attachment`) → `NetRomRoutingTable.Ingest(…, neighbourQuality)`, which now refreshes the cached neighbour `PathQuality` every ingest (so a hot edit / a neighbour newly heard on a different-grade port reflects on the next NODES broadcast). Null preserves today's behaviour byte-for-byte (the table's `DefaultNeighbourQuality`, canonical 192). It is the lightest hot class — a `NetRomQualityChanged` reconcile bucket applies it live via `NetRomService.UpdatePortQuality` (NET/ROM awareness is read-only; it can never disturb a session), no port restart.

**Per-port PACLEN / N1 (#458).** A new `Ax25PortParams.N1` (`int?`, 16..256, null ⇒ the 256 default) seeds the per-session `Ax25SessionContext.N1` so a port can run a small frame-length cap on a slow/lossy medium (an HF port ~80) while VHF/UHF stays at 256. Precedence: the configured value is the *offered* N1; XID negotiation can only lower it (the negotiator takes the min), never raise it past the cap. **Parity-safe library threading (the load-bearing decision):** N1 rides the live-reseed `Ax25SessionParameters` record — which is NOT one of the five surfaces `ax25-ts/scripts/parity-check.mjs` compares — and is seeded in `Ax25Listener.BuildSession`; it was deliberately NOT added to `Ax25ListenerOptions` (that IS parity-tracked, so a member there would fail interop). The node host carries it past construction with a post-construct `UpdateSessionParameters` reseed in `BringUp`. Verified: `parity-check.mjs` passes (4 pre-existing documented exceptions, zero new gaps); zero new library flags. An N1 edit is the same HOT AX.25 reseed as any timer/window change (new sessions only).

UI: both fields in the web Ports editor (`web/packetnet-ui/`) — N1 in the advanced "Link timing" grid, NET/ROM quality in a new always-visible "NET/ROM" block (a clearable input — blank ⇒ inherit) — matching the existing `Ax25PortParams`/`PortConfig` read-and-write pattern (System.Text.Json camelCase, hand-mirrored TS types; no DTO layer). Validation rejects out-of-range (16..256 for N1, 0..255 for QUALITY) rather than clamping, per the per-port discipline. YAML template + the `defaultNeighbourQuality` comment document both. Tests: validator range pairs, YAML round-trip, the routing-table per-port-quality unit suite (override / fallback / the mixed-grade-node case / hot refresh), an N1-via-session-parameters segmentation proof in `Ax25.Tests`, ReconcilePlanner + preview-builder classification, a `NetRomAware` end-to-end "191 honoured through the real supervisor" integration test, and a UI render test asserting both fields appear in the editor. Suites green: Ax25 952, NetRom 280, Node 1216, web 85. No library flag ⇒ no ax25-ts leg needed; node-host config only.

### 2026-06-17 — Tracker reconciliation + downstream release ledger + CI capacity + plan/memory gap audit

**(a) Tracker reconciliation (§5.X spikes + §15 open questions).** A plan+memory gap audit found the §5.X spike-backlog and §15 open-questions prose was stale: several items still read as OPEN/undone in the prose, but their GitHub issues are in fact CLOSED-as-completed. Verified each against the live tracker (`gh issue view … --json state`) and updated the prose to match. The spike items now marked tracked + closed as completed: **SP-001** ([#176](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/176)), **SP-004** ([#179](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/179)), **SP-005** ([#180](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/180)), **SP-006** ([#181](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/181)), **SP-009** ([#184](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/184) — static Mermaid rung shipped; the live-UI rung stays a deferred future-option), **SP-010** ([#260](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/260)). The §15 open questions now marked tracked + closed as completed: **OQ-001** ([#186](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/186)), **OQ-004** ([#189](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/189)), **OQ-005** ([#190](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/190)), **OQ-006** ([#191](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/191)). Genuinely-open items left as-is — **OQ-008** ([`packet-net/ax25sdl#15`](https://github.com/packet-net/ax25sdl/issues/15), still open), plus SP-002/003/007/008 and OQ-002/003/009/010/011. The §5.X header box moved ⬜ → 🟡 to reflect the mixed state. Prose-only reconciliation; no completion detail invented beyond "tracked + closed as completed (see #N)".

**(b) Downstream release ledger (the lib-v0.10.0 / node-v0.15.0 cascade's downstream fan-out).** The downstream legs of the capability-cache release (entry below) shipped this session — this is the `docs/releasing.md` Step-5 ledger entry for that cascade: **axcall v0.2.11** and **packet-term-tui v0.2.11** both bumped to the `Packet.*` libraries 0.10.0; **packet-term-web** repinned to **@packet-net/ax25 0.14.0** (auto-deployed via its publish workflow). Completes the downstream fan-out for the libraries-0.10.0 / node-0.15.0 / ax25-ts-0.15.0 coordinated release.

**(c) CI capacity.** A **5th self-hosted runner (studybox)** was added on a separate box to relieve the runner-host oversubscription noted across recent entries (~5 GB/job × 4 runners > 16 GB on the single host — the OOM/swap-thrash that has been re-running interop jobs). The extra runner on its own box removes that single-host memory contention for the interop/crossgen-heavy jobs.

**(d) Plan + memory gap audit (2026-06-17).** A plan+memory gap audit walked the plan and the working memory against the trackers and identified ~20 undone-but-untracked items; tracking issues were filed for them across `packet-net/packet.net` and `pdn-bbs` so the backlog is visible in the trackers rather than living only in prose. (This reconciliation entry is part (a)'s output of that same audit.)

### 2026-06-17 — Per-peer AX.25 capability cache (adaptive interlink + user-CONNECT dialing)

Goal set by Tom. Turns the hard-coded mod-8 interlinks (the regression-fix below) into adaptive per-neighbour dials: the node now remembers, per (port, peer), whether a neighbour does v2.2/SABME (`SupportsExtended`) and whether it answers a pre-session XID (`SupportsSrejViaXid`), so a dial skips the probes a known non-answerer would only stall on, and re-probes a negative after ~30 days. New `src/Packet.Node.Core/Capabilities/`: `PeerCapabilityCache` (`PlanDial(port, peer, policy) → PeerDialPlan{Extended, PreConnectXid}` + a **plan-aware** `RecordOutcome`) over a resilient `SqlitePeerCapabilityStore` (pdn.db, WAL, degrade-not-throw, mirrors the routing/refresh-token stores). Two policies: `Interlink` (miss ⇒ mod-8 — preserves the conservative default against the BPQ/XRouter neighbour population) and `UserConnect` (miss ⇒ optimistic SABME, today's listener default).

**The correctness hinge** (first-class asserted): `RecordOutcome` is *plan-aware* and only writes on a dial that RETURNED — `SupportsExtended` is updated only when we actually dialled SABME (a mod-8 dial proves nothing about extended capability), `SupportsSrejViaXid` only when a pre-connect XID actually ran, and a dial that THREW (timeout / DM = no link of either version) writes nothing. Wired at all three outbound sites (`NetRomService.EnsureInterlinkAsync` + `PortSupervisor.OpenInterlinkAsync` interlinks, `Ax25OutboundConnector` user CONNECT) via a new per-call `ConnectAsync(remote, local, extended, preConnectXidNegotiatesSrej, ct)` overload (mirrored in ax25-ts `connect()`; parity-invisible — a name-deduped overload, no new flag). Operator surfaces: console **`CAP`/`CAPS`** (read) + **`CAP CLEAR <port:peer>`** (Operate-gated), and **`GET`/`DELETE /api/v1/capabilities`** + a Routes-style SPA panel; unknown (`bool?` null) renders as `v2.2?`/`SREJ?`, never false. Persisted, so the learned table survives restart. Tests: cache core + store round-trip + plan-aware/no-write-on-throw, interlink + user-CONNECT wiring, the overload, console + REST + SPA — full unit suites green (Node.Tests 1200, Ax25.Tests 951, web 84) + interop on a quiet box. Lib (`Packet.Ax25` overload) + node change → lib-v0.10.0 / node-v0.15.0 / ax25-ts 0.15.0. Addresses the §5.G capability-cache item.

### 2026-06-17 — Released: lib-v0.9.0 + node-v0.14.0 (v2.2-preferred CONNECT + working SREJ to BPQ + figc4.6 fallbacks)

Release cascade for the two arcs below (the v2.2-first CONNECT/SREJ work + the interlink-mod-8/pre-session-XID regression fix). Tagged `lib-v0.9.0` + `node-v0.14.0` on green `main` (merge 95da708). Published + verified: NuGet `Packet.Ax25` 0.9.0 (+ the 6-package set) indexed; `node-v0.14.0` GitHub release non-draft with amd64/arm64/armhf `.deb`+`.tar.gz`+`SHA256SUMS`; GHCR docker image. ax25-ts parity landed in #65 (SREJ port) + #66 (interlink/responder/budget); its npm release + the downstream `axcall`/`packet-term-tui` pin bumps + the lab deploy are batched into the capability-cache release (above) to avoid double churn. Runner infra: runner-2 had been OOM-killed (memory oversubscription — ~5 GB/job × 4 runners > 16 GB) and a concurrent ci+interop run swap-thrashed into a 27-min hang; cancelled, restarted runner-2 (all 4 back), re-ran clean. Systemic mitigation (a concurrency group for interop/crossgen) recommended to Tom, not yet applied.

### 2026-06-17 — Fix: NET/ROM interlinks dial mod-8 + PDN answers a pre-session XID (regression from the prefer-v2.2 default)

The `PreferExtendedConnect=true` default (the prefer-v2.2 connect arc below) regressed NET/ROM interlink establishment: `NetRomService`/`PortSupervisor` dialled neighbours via the 2-arg `ConnectAsync(neighbour, ct)`, which then attempted **SABME**. LinBPQ's AXUDP NET/ROM port silently ignores SABME (it does not FRMR it on that path), so the dial exhausted N2 and threw instead of degrading — breaking L4 circuit origination (reproduced: `NetRomL4CircuitViaAxudp` timed out ~38 s; with the fix, ~13 s). Three coordinated fixes (Tom's call: do it completely — "C"):

1. **Interlinks dial v2.0/mod-8 explicitly.** `NetRomService.OpenInterlink` fallback + `PortSupervisor.OpenInterlinkAsync` now pass `extended: false`. Interlinks are mod-8 infrastructure (the neighbour population is overwhelmingly v2.0 BPQ/XRouter); prefer-extended is for the user's point-to-point CONNECT, not the L3/L4 plumbing.

2. **PDN answers a pre-session XID command as responder** (`Ax25Listener.DispatchInbound`). The initiator side (`PreConnectXidNegotiatesSrej`) already existed, but PDN never answered an inbound XID command with no active session — so PDN↔PDN mod-8 interlinks stalled (the initiator's pre-SABM XID went unanswered → figc4.1 t05 → DM). Now, on a cache-miss XID **command** with `AcceptIncoming`, PDN builds+caches a real session, seeds it SREJ-capable (so `DefaultOfferFor` advertises SREJ; the §6.3.2 lesser-of merge in `RespondToXidCommand` reverts it if the peer didn't offer SREJ), and sends the F=1 XID response. The cached session is the staging mechanism: the subsequent inbound SABM reuses it, and figc4.1 t14 `Set Version 2.0` clears only `IsExtended` (not `SrejEnabled` — verified by reflection-probing the pinned `Packet.Ax25.Sdl` 0.10.0 transition table), so the negotiated SREJ survives into Connected. This is **plain spec-compliant MDL behaviour, NOT a named flag** — §4.3.3.7 ("a station receiving an XID command returns an XID response" unless a UA is pending / FRMR condition exists — unconditional), §6.3.2 (negotiation precedes the connection; the connection adopts the negotiated params), Annex C5.3 (the MDL is a connection-independent machine). Parity inventory unchanged.

3. **The pre-connect XID probe is an optimistic short wait** (`NegotiateSrejBeforeConnectAsync`): `min(3.5 s, max(1.5 s, 2·T1V))` instead of up to 12 s. A peer that does pre-session XID answers on the FIRST frame (BPQ; now also PDN per #2); a peer that doesn't (a dumb v2.0 TNC) shouldn't stall a mod-8 dial before the SABM fallback. Adaptive per-neighbour reuse (SABME to v2.2-capable neighbours; skip the probe for known non-answerers) is the per-peer capability cache (§5.G).

Tests: new `Ax25ListenerPreSessionXidTests` (inbound XID command → XID response, not DM; then SABM → Connected with `SrejEnabled`); full Ax25 unit suite green (949). Interop verified contention-free on a quiet box (studybox): the NET/ROM L4 circuit tests, `SrejXidViaNetsim`, LinBPQ, Direwolf all green. Mirrored in ax25-ts ([#66](https://github.com/packet-net/ax25-ts/pull/66) — `connector.ts` mod-8 interlink, `listener.ts` responder + budget; the stalling `connector.test.ts`/`connector-transit.test.ts` now pass). Lib + node change.

### 2026-06-17 — Working SREJ on the mod-8 link to LinBPQ (XID-before-SABM + HDLC-Opt-Functions octet-order fix), proven on the wire

The user priority "ensure this gets us working SREJ, that's important" + "BPQ definitely can do SREJ". Empirically probed the live LinBPQ (6.0.25.23) over net-sim and source-verified against the running image's `L2Code.c`, then fixed two real defects and proved SREJ recovery on the wire.

**Empirical mechanism (captured frames, not inferred).** LinBPQ *does* do mod-8 SREJ — the earlier "BPQ = go-back-N only" was wrong. Two hard requirements, both proven live: (1) **XID must precede the SABM.** BPQ's `ProcessXIDCommand` runs only on the no-active-link path and sets `LINK->Ver2point2`, which flips its reject scheme from REJ to SREJ (`L2Code.c` ~L4230 `if (LINK->Ver2point2) return SREJ`). An XID on an already-established link is ignored — so the AX.25-figure model (XID *after* the UA, figc4.6 MDL-NEGOTIATE) never reaches BPQ's responder. (2) **The 3-octet HDLC Optional Functions PV must be transmitted MSB-octet-first.** BPQ (`xidval>>16` first / parse `value=(value<<8)+*p`) and direwolf (`xid.c` same) both serialise MSB-first, as AX.25 v2.2 **§3.8** ("high-order octet first") mandates; our codec emitted it **LSB-first** — a real bug. (Figure 4.6 *prints* the PV `82 A8 22` = LSB-first, contradicting §3.8 — a figure-rendering error, not the authority.) With LSB order BPQ silently drops the XID (fails its `OPMustHave`/`OPSREJMult` check) and never negotiates SREJ; with MSB order BPQ answers XID selecting SREJ-multi+mod-8, then SREJs a dropped I-frame. Wire proof: with XID-before-SABM, dropping our N(S)=1 made BPQ emit `SREJ nr=1`; with a plain SABM it emitted `REJ nr=1` (go-back-N).

**Fixes.** (a) **`HdlcOptionalFunctions.ToOctets()`/`FromOctets()` now MSB-octet-first** (the spec-correct, all-impls order) — a conformance bug fix, default-correct, with a `lsbOctetFirst` param retained only for regression study. This also makes the existing direwolf XID/SREJ path negotiate correctly. (b) **`Ax25ListenerOptions.PreConnectXidNegotiatesSrej`** (default on, mirrored on `Ax25SessionParameters`): on a mod-8 dial, `ConnectAsync` runs an XID command/response **before** the SABM (`NegotiateSrejBeforeConnectAsync` — reuses the per-session `Ax25ManagementDataLink` + the existing inbound XID routing), advertising SREJ + SREJ-multiframe at mod-8; the peer's XID response sets `SrejEnabled` to the mutual result. A peer that never answers XID reverts to go-back-N and proceeds to a plain SABM, so it is always safe to leave on. The MDL's default offer (`DefaultOfferFor`) now sets `SrejMultiframe` when offering SREJ (BPQ's XID responder *requires* the OPSREJMult bit). Note: PDN's SREJ-*received* honoring path was already ungated — the figc4.x `SREJReceived` transitions are not gated on `SrejEnabled`, so PDN already selectively retransmits on any incoming SREJ; the gap was purely in getting BPQ to *send* SREJ (the XID negotiation above).

**Tests.** Codec: existing XID unit + property suites updated for MSB-first (Figure46Info HDLC PV `82 A8 22`→`22 A8 82`, the always-1-bits reconstruction, the fuzz seed) — all green (Packet.Ax25.Tests 947, Properties 84). Connect: `Ax25ListenerPreferV22ConnectTests` updated (mod-8 version-choice tests set `PreConnectXidNegotiatesSrej=false` to isolate SABM-vs-SABME) + a new test asserting a default mod-8 dial leads with XID. Interop (`Category=Interop`, live BPQ): new **`SrejXidViaNetsim`** — `Mod8_XidNegotiatesSrej_DroppedIFrameRecoveredViaBpqSrej` (XID-before-SABM → BPQ XID response selects SREJ-multi+mod-8 → dropped I-frame recovered via BPQ SREJ on the wire, asserts no REJ) and the contrast `Mod8_NoXid_DroppedIFrameRecoveredViaBpqRej_GoBackN`.

**Lead integration (this pass).** (1) **ax25-ts parity** — in progress: the HDLC-Optional-Functions octet order is a *behaviour* change (byte-reversed wire output), so `parity-check.mjs` (which compares flag inventories) will NOT catch it; ax25-ts needs the identical MSB-first fix or the two stacks emit byte-reversed XID and only one negotiates SREJ. The new `PreConnectXidNegotiatesSrej` listener option also needs a TS counterpart or a recorded parity exception. (2) The octet-order change is a codec conformance fix (no quirk), but Figure 4.6's LSB-first PV rendering *does* contradict the normative §3.8 ("high-order octet first") — a Fig-4.6 figure-errata candidate (alongside the pre-existing ABM-byte one); flagged for Tom in the audit doc, not filed. (3) lib→node release cascade — pending. Lib change (`Packet.Ax25`) + audit + plan.

### 2026-06-17 — Outbound CONNECT prefers v2.2 (SABME/mod-128) by default, degrading to v2.0 for non-v2.2 peers

`Ax25Listener.ConnectAsync` now initiates an **extended (SABME / mod-128)** connect on every dial by default (new `Ax25ListenerOptions.PreferExtendedConnect`, default `true`, mirrored on `Ax25SessionParameters`), so a default dial negotiates SREJ + window (XID) against capable peers (direwolf: real SREJ-128 on the wire) and degrades cleanly to v2.0/SABM for peers that can't — the FRMR fallback (`Ax25Spec45`, LinBPQ) and the **new DM fallback** (`Ax25Spec48`, XRouter) both drop the link to mod-8 so a dial NEVER fails against them. A per-call override (`ConnectAsync(remote, local, extended, ct)`) and the listener default knob both let a caller force v2.0. The inbound answerer is untouched (it adopts the peer's version from the SABM/SABME it receives). New named quirk **`Ax25Spec48DmRejectionDegradesToV20`** (default on; off under `StrictlyFaithful`): figc4.6's AwaitingV22Connection DM-received handler routes a DM(F=1) (`t11_dm_received_yes`) to Disconnected as a §975 refusal with no fallback — but a peer DM-ing our *polled* SABME (P=1) answers F=1, so XRouter's "I can't do mod-128" DM killed the connect with `IsExtended` stuck true (verified on the wire). The quirk makes a DM in AwaitingV22Connection (either F-branch) run figc4.6's own `t14_frmr_received` v2.0-fallback chain (force v2.0 → Establish_Data_Link → SABM → AwaitingConnection) — the DM analogue of the FRMR fallback. Implemented in `Ax25Session` (`ResolveDmDegradeMatch` substitutes the DM match for the FRMR transition; `ApplyPreExecutionQuirks` forces `IsExtended=false` so Establish emits SABM), scoped to the extended-connect state. Tests: conformance (`DataLinkIntegrationTests` ×4 + `Mod128EstablishmentConformanceTests` DM-degrade + Spec48-off teardown + `TransitionCoverageTests` floor preserved via a Spec48-off coverage block), prefer-v2.2 default (`Ax25ListenerPreferV22ConnectTests` ×4), and interop (`XrouterViaNetsimExtendedMode` — live SABME → DM → degrade → mod-8 link carrying data; LinBPQ FRMR + direwolf SREJ-128 still green). **Lead integration (this pass):** (1) the **ax25-ts parity leg** — `Ax25Spec48DmRejectionDegradesToV20` + the `PreferExtendedConnect` listener option + the `ConnectAsync(extended)` knob need the TS counterpart or a recorded parity exception (in progress, with the SREJ leg's MSB-octet + `PreConnectXidNegotiatesSrej` changes); (2) the upstream **ax25spec figure-errata issue** for the figc4.6 DM-F=1-no-degrade gap is **filed as [packethacking/ax25spec#48](https://github.com/packethacking/ax25spec/issues/48)** — the quirk was renamed `Ax25Spec46`→**`Ax25Spec48`** to match (the original `Spec46` collided with the unrelated upstream #46 segmentation proposal); (3) the lib→node release cascade — pending. Lib + node change.

### 2026-06-17 — Console: split the radio-port listing out of NODES into its own PORTS command

`NODES` was conflating two things — the node identity + NET/ROM table AND the radio-port list. Split the ports into a dedicated **`PORTS`** verb (read-only, no elevation), matching BPQ ergonomics. `NODES` now shows just the node line + the NET/ROM neighbours/routes; `PORTS` shows `Ports:` + each `<id> [up|down] <endpoint>`. New `PortsCommand` + `NodeCommandService.PortsText()` (the section lifted out of `NodesText()`), each row led by the **1-indexed config-order port number** — the same one `CONNECT` takes (`C <n> <call>` → `config.Current.Ports[n-1]`, PortSupervisor's router), so the listing tells you what to type to dial out a specific port. Parser: `PORTS` (and any prefix of it — which subsumes `PORT`) with **no args** → `PortsCommand`; the sysop `PORT <id> UP|DOWN` form (i.e. with args) still routes to the power command, so a bare `P`/`PORT` now lists rather than erroring. Help text updated (added `P[orts]`; `N[odes]` reworded). Tests: parser (`PORTS`/`PORT`/`P`/`PO` → list; `PORT <id> UP|DOWN` → power; bad-args → malformed), and the telnet/AX.25/NET-ROM console integration tests now assert `Node ` from `NODES` + `Ports:` from `PORTS`. Node-only console change.

### 2026-06-17 — Application packet-identity registration: slice 4 (app migration) + release wave

Closed the arc: migrated the downstream apps to the node-owned-callsign contract and cut the release wave (one release per repo, no intermediate node releases). Each app now binds **`PDN_APP_CALLSIGN`** verbatim when the node injects it (no self-derivation, no SSID probe) and falls back to its legacy `<node>-N` derivation when absent (standalone / older node), and each manifest declares `packet.command`: **pdn-bbs v0.2.33** (BBS — `ResolveCallsign` prefers the env, `BBS` service alias kept), **pdn-bpqchat v0.1.1** (CHAT — `BoundCallsign()`/`NodeOwnsCallsign()`, no-walk-when-owned), **pdn-convers v0.1.3** (CONVERS — `ResolveBinding`/`ExactBind`). **DAPPS** migrated on `master` (DbStartup prefers `PDN_APP_CALLSIGN`, never probe-walks when owned, `packet.command: DAPPS`) and **released v0.34.2** — dapps has no release automation (manual multi-arch self-contained net8.0 binaries, `kind: assets`), so the three RIDs (linux-x64/arm64/arm) were built by hand with `-p:Version=0.34.2` (verified the x64 binary reports a clean non-dev 0.34.2), uploaded with the manifest via `gh release create`, and a CA2017 log-template mismatch the migration introduced was fixed first. Catalog bumped for all four apps (`catalog/apps.yaml` + `AppCatalogYamlTests`: bbs 0.2.33, bpqchat 0.1.1, convers 0.1.3, dapps 0.34.2). Released as **node-v0.13.23** (slices 1–3 + the catalog bump) and deployed to the lab; bbs v0.2.33 + bpqchat v0.1.1 re-staged there and slices 1/2/4 wire-verified (PDN_APP_CALLSIGN bound — bbs auto `M9YYY-1`, bpqchat pinned `M9YYY-9`; the `BBS` command verb connects to the mailbox). Slice-3 alias advert is staged (`apps[].netrom.alias` M9YBBS/M9YCHT set) but gated off on the lab (`netRom.broadcast: false` — enabling it would inject into GB7RDG's real network). Built by a five-way agent fan-out (one node + four app repos), integrated + verified here. **All releases cut.**

### 2026-06-17 — Application packet-identity registration: built (slices 1–3)

Implemented the app packet-identity surface designed in the spec entry below (§5.G row → "built (slices 1–3)"). **Slice 1 (manifest verb + node-owned callsign):** added `AppPacketSpec { string? Command }` on `AppPackageManifest.Packet` and **clean-removed `session.match`** (no back-compat parse — the bundled WALL/LOBBY manifests moved to `packet.command` in the same change); the verb for a session app is now `packet.command`, owner-overridable. `AppOverrideConfig` lost `Match`, gained `Command` (verb override) + `Callsign` (pin) + `Netrom { Alias, Quality }`. `ApplicationConfig`'s verb `Match` → `Command`; its executable `Command` → `Executable` (disambiguating the BPQ-`APPLICATION`-line shape: an inline app has both a verb and an exec); gained the same `Callsign` + `Netrom`. The node is now the **callsign authority** via the pure, unit-tested `AppCallsignResolver`: a pin (full callsign or `-N` suffix on the node base) wins, else `<node-base>-<lowest free SSID>` deterministically (pins reserved first; node SSID + other apps' callsigns excluded), injected as `PDN_APP_CALLSIGN` in `AppServiceSupervisor.BuildSpec`. `PDN_NODE_CALLSIGN` stays (un-migrated apps still self-derive — additive back-compat). **Slice 2 (command verb at the prompt):** an enabled app's `command` registers as a bare node-prompt verb after the built-ins, exact case-insensitive — for a service app (`IApplicationHost.ResolveServiceCommandCallsign`) a loopback connect to its resolved callsign (the same path `C <callsign>` takes via the `ConnectRouter`), for a session app the existing per-connection attachment; collisions (built-in / inline / cross-package) reuse the catalog's session-verb-collision flagging. **Slice 3 (opt-in NET/ROM alias advert):** `AppNetromAdvert.Build` projects each enabled app whose owner set `netrom.alias` to a NODES entry (alias → resolved callsign, best-neighbour = node, configured quality, default 255), fed to `NetRomService.AppAdvertSource` and appended to `BroadcastNodes` after the learned routes; absent alias ⇒ nothing extra (off by default). `NodeConfigValidator` gained pinned-callsign + netrom-alias uniqueness across the inline + package union + the node's own identity. Web API + SPA surface the new `apps[]` fields. **Known back-compat gap:** the bare command verb for a service app loopback-connects only if the app actually `RegisterAppCallsign`s the *resolved* callsign — an un-migrated app that self-derives a different SSID won't be reachable by verb until it binds `PDN_APP_CALLSIGN`. **Not yet done:** the per-app migration in pdn-bbs / DAPPS / pdn-bpqchat / pdn-convers (they bind their own derived callsign today; switching them to `PDN_APP_CALLSIGN` is downstream work in those repos).

### 2026-06-17 — Application packet-identity registration: spec agreed (command / callsign / opt-in NET/ROM alias)

Designed the app-registration surface raised under §5.G (un-parks "mesh app reachability" as an opt-in piece of a coherent model). Three reachability dimensions, mirroring BPQ's `APPLICATION n,CMD,Call,Alias,Quality`, split by who owns each: **command** (the node-prompt verb / application name) is app-authored in the manifest (`packet.command`, optional, owner-overridable — it **replaces** `session.match` outright, **no back-compat parsing**: pre-1.0/single-deployment, the bundled manifests move over in the same change); **callsign** and **NET/ROM alias** are *wholly the node's* (they encode this node's identity + location, which a portable manifest can't know) and live in the owner's `packetnet.yaml` per-app `apps[]` entry (`callsign`, `netrom.alias`/`quality`), which hot-reloads + is web-UI-editable. The node becomes the **callsign authority**: it resolves each enabled packet app's callsign (pin, else `<base>-<free SSID>`) and injects **`PDN_APP_CALLSIGN`** — apps bind that instead of deriving their own. Migration touches **every app that self-derives `<node>-N` today**: pdn-bbs (`bbs.yaml callsign:`), DAPPS (`DAPPS_CALLSIGN` env), pdn-bpqchat (`SSID`/derive, default -4), pdn-convers (same). The **NET/ROM alias is opt-in, off by default** (no alias ⇒ nothing extra on the mesh — the anti-noise default Tom asked for). Inline `applications:` entries gain the same `command`/`callsign`/`netrom` fields, making them the exact analog of a BPQ `APPLICATION` line. Collisions (verb vs built-ins/other apps; callsign/alias uniqueness) reuse the existing session-verb-collision flagging across the inline+package union. **Spec written** in `docs/app-packages.md` (manifest `packet` block + owner `apps[]`/`applications[]` identity fields + the `PDN_APP_CALLSIGN` contract + § Application packet identity); §5.G row updated to CONSIDER—designed. **No implementation yet** — held for Tom's sign-off on the written spec. Callsign-binding largely exists (`RegisterAppCallsign`/`AddLocalAlias`); the new pieces are the bare command verb and the opt-in NODES alias advert (`NetRomService.BroadcastNodes`).

### 2026-06-16 — GB7RDG (LinBPQ) replacement gap analysis + scoping decisions (new §5.G)

A full subsystem audit of PDN vs the live GB7RDG node (the cutover target), to answer "what's missing to replace LinBPQ at GB7RDG." Four parallel read-only capability audits (node/RF/NET-ROM, BBS-vs-LINMAIL, APRS/servers/integrations, chat/apps) cross-referenced against the 2026-06-11 snapshot (`gb7rdg-config/bpq32.cfg` + the `/opt/oarc/bpq` tree); the consolidated, file-cited findings are in **`gb7rdg-config/GAP-ANALYSIS.md`** (forwarding-specific detail already in `gb7rdg-config/ANALYSIS.md`). Bottom line: the protocol core (NET/ROM L3+L4+INP3, AX.25 mod-8/128, multi-port KISS-TCP+ACKMODE, BBS B1F/B2F/LZHUF/BID/routing) is present and live-wired; the gaps are breadth, not depth. Recorded the outcomes as a roadmap table in **§5.G** with Tom's dispositions (2026-06-16): **DROP** — AX.25 digipeating (antipattern for nodes), public multi-user telnet login (the console is an admin interface now), BPQmon/MQTT telemetry (we're not a BPQ port), AGW emulator (apps use RHPv2), IP gateway, SNMP, CMS/Winlink; **PARK** — APRS (secondary), mesh app-alias-in-NODES (noisy; opt-in only if ever); **CONSIDER** (each needs its own design pass before building) — BBS content filtering (the highest-impact gap), a new **Prometheus** metrics/exporter (replacing the dropped bpqmon ask), chat on-air federation + bpqchat release + peer allow-list, M0LTEMap reporting, OARC API, per-port NET/ROM QUALITY + PACLEN/N1 knobs, housekeeping lifetime defaults + MaxMsgno, L4Compress, connect-script fidelity (`!`/`NC`/INTERLOCK), receiver-side restart granting, inbound FBB-over-internet-TCP, the MHeard `MH` verb, and WP consume. The DROP set is also reflected in §11. **No build authorised by this entry** — it's the roadmap of record so each item gets considered individually. A GitHub issue sweep to file/triage the CONSIDER items is wanted "at some point" (not yet done). The BBS-owned CONSIDER items are mirrored into pdn-bbs's roadmap (`forwarding.md`).

### 2026-06-15 — Remove the unagreed RHP `hello`/`helloReply` capability-discovery extension (#449)

The RHP `hello`/`helloReply` handshake (a pdn extension letting a client ask the server its `maxData`/`enc`/`pfams`) was implemented in the RHP server but **never agreed** — it originated as a *proposal* in `rhp2lib-net/docs/protocol-field-notes.md` and shipped without ratification. An org-wide audit (issue #449) confirmed nothing actually uses it: no client sends a `hello` and depends on the reply (the only would-be sender, pdn-bpqchat's `Client.Hello()`, was dead code with zero callers; the `rhp2lib-net` client never modelled it at all). We don't ship unagreed protocol surface, so it's removed. Deleted the `HelloMessage`/`HelloReplyMessage` DTOs (`RhpMessages.cs`), the `RhpMessageType.Hello`/`HelloReply` constants (`RhpConstants.cs`), the deserialize cases (`RhpJson.cs`), and the server handler + the `HelloMessage→HelloReplyMessage` error-path arm + the now-unused `Implementation` advert stamp/`System.Reflection` using (`RhpServer.cs`). **Post-removal wire behaviour (verified):** an incoming `hello` no longer matches a known type → falls to the `UnknownMessage` arm → the server answers the generic `{"type":"helloReply","errCode":2,"errText":"Bad or missing type"}` (id echoed; errCode 14 if pre-auth) — i.e. pdn now looks like a server that simply doesn't support `hello`, **identical to real XRouter**, which is the whole point. Tests were rewritten (not just deleted) to assert the removal: `WireSerializationTests` (hello deserializes as `UnknownMessage`), `XRouterWireConformanceTests` (hello answered with the unknown-type fallback, no capability fields, pre- and post-auth), the `RhpServerHardeningTests` liveness helper switched off hello to a `socket` ping, and the Interop `XRouterRhpWireDiffTests` now asserts XRouter and pdn answer **identically**. `docs/rhp2-server.md` drops the `## pdn extensions` section and lists hello under "Deferred by name". The **research is kept**: `rhp2lib-net/docs/protocol-field-notes.md` (the proposal) and issue #449 are untouched — if capability discovery is wanted later it gets designed + agreed first. Not an `Ax25ParseOptions`/quirk/listener change, so no ax25-ts parity leg. `Packet.Rhp2.Tests` 192/192, full default suite green (~2997 across 13 projects). Paired removal of the dead `Hello()`/`TypeHello` in [`packet-net/pdn-bpqchat`](https://github.com/packet-net/pdn-bpqchat).

### 2026-06-15 — NET/ROM config: collapse `connect` + `forward` into a single `routing` mode

Removed a long-standing config wart: `netrom.forward` defaulted **true** but was inert unless `netrom.connect` was also on (forwarding reuses the connected-mode interlink machinery `connect` gated), so the common default state `connect:false, forward:true` misleadingly read as "forwarding" while doing nothing. Replaced the two bools with a single 3-state `NetRomRouting` enum (`none` = passive listen + maintain the table only; `endpoint` = open interlinks for our own `connect <alias>` but no transit; `transit` = full router — interlinks + relay third-party transit), keeping `enabled` (hearing) and `broadcast` (advertising) as the genuinely-orthogonal escalations. The new knob is `netRom.routing` (default `none`). **Back-compat is total:** `connect`/`forward` are retained as nullable legacy inputs (`bool?`, null = absent) feeding a pure `NetRomConfig.ResolveRouting()` → `(routing, warnings)` resolver — every deployed config (incl. the shipped default `node.yaml` template) keeps parsing + behaving identically. Mapping: `connect:true & forward!=false` → transit; `connect:true & forward:false` → endpoint; `connect!=true & forward!=true` → none; the contradictory `connect:false, forward:true` → none **plus a warning** that `forward:true` was inert and is now `routing:none`; an explicit `routing:` wins over any legacy keys (with a warning if both are present). Unset-vs-explicit-`none` is distinguished by binding `Routing` as a nullable enum (null ⇒ fall back to legacy ⇒ default none). The resolver's warnings surface on the **boot log** via `FileConfigProvider` (the existing config-concern channel — no new wire/API surface). `NetRomService`/`NodeConfigValidator` read the resolved `EffectiveRouting` (`ConnectEnabled` ⟺ endpoint|transit, `ForwardEnabled` ⟺ transit), never the raw fields. Updated: `NodeConfig.cs` (enum + resolver + `[YamlIgnore]` on the derived `EffectiveRouting`), `NetRomService.cs`, `NodeConfigValidator.cs`, `FileConfigProvider.cs`, the SPA NET/ROM config screen (a single routing select replacing the connect/forward toggles — `config.tsx` + `types.ts` + `mock.ts`), `docs/node-api.yaml` (routing enum; legacy keys marked deprecated-but-accepted), `NodeConfigTemplate`, and `docs/strict-vs-pragmatic-audit.md`. Tests: per-mode `NetRomService` behaviour (none = no interlinks/transit; endpoint = interlinks only; transit = both), the full back-compat mapping matrix (incl. the inert combo → none+warning), routing-wins-over-legacy + its warning, validator + YAML round-trip of the new knob, and the boot-log warning surfacing. **Node-only** — this is node host config, NOT an `Ax25ParseOptions`/quirk/listener flag, so no ax25-ts parity leg. 1082 Node + 68 web green (the 1 unrelated `AppCatalogYamlTests` bbs-0.2.8-vs-0.2.9 failure predates this branch).

### 2026-06-15 — Seamless slot reauth: login-redirect for /apps/* browser navigations + SPA silent/proactive refresh

Fixed the "blank screen + empty `bbs.txt` download" a phone tab to `/apps/<id>` showed when resumed after the session expired. **Root cause:** the slot iframe authenticates via the `pdn_at` cookie (it can't set an `Authorization` header), and on expiry the JwtBearer pipeline emitted a bare `401 + WWW-Authenticate: Bearer` with an empty body — unrenderable in a browser frame, so iOS Safari saved it to disk. **Server** (`Program.cs` `OnChallenge` + new `AppGatewayChallenge`): for a `/apps/*` request that is a real browser navigation (`Sec-Fetch-Dest` document/iframe/frame or `Sec-Fetch-Mode: navigate`, `Accept: text/html` fallback) emit a renderable re-auth instead of the bare 401 — a top-level nav gets a `302` to `/login?next=/apps/<id>` (the SPA route, server-derived + same-site, NOT the raw gateway path); a sub-frame gets a `200 text/html` that breaks the slot out to the top-level login (`window.top.location`, JSON-encoded URL). XHR/API 401s (`Accept: application/json`, fetch/empty `Sec-Fetch-Dest`) are unchanged — the SPA's `on401` owns those. **No auth bypass** (still rejects + requires login); **no open-redirect** (`next` is server-derived `/apps/<id>` + the SPA `safeNext` guard rejects `//`, scheme, backslash). **SPA** (`lib/api.ts` + `app/auth.tsx`): on a 401, one refresh-token rotation + single retry before logout (already present); added a **proactive refresh on tab-focus** when the access token is near/at expiry — the exact "flip back to the tab" trigger — reusing the single in-flight, cross-tab-locked rotation so the one-time-use refresh token is never double-spent (`/auth/refresh` already re-sets the `pdn_at` cookie, so the slot recovers silently). **AppFrame** remounts the slot iframe on an actual session rotation (`SESSION_REFRESHED_EVENT`) so it re-requests with the fresh cookie; `login.tsx` honours a validated same-site `next` post-login. Tests: `AppGatewayChallengeTests` (nav→302 / iframe→break-out / XHR→bare-401 / non-`/apps` untouched) + `api.auth` web tests (401→refresh→retry, failed-refresh→logout, concurrent-401 single-refresh, proactive-near-expiry). 1054 Node + 68 web green. **Node-only** — no lib/downstream.

### 2026-06-15 — App-platform UX bundle: apps-in-nav, the `packet` capability, slot UI mode (BBS), sidecar slim, docker→GHCR, and the m0lte→packet-net transfer sweep

A bundle of app-platform UX + ecosystem-tidy work that shipped together (all deployed to the lab + green before this commit). **Apps in the left nav** ([`docs/app-packages.md`](app-packages.md)): enabled web-capable apps are first-class nav entries (manifest icon+name) rendered in `shell.tsx`'s `AppNav`; `GET /api/v1/apps` (the launcher feed, `PdnAppGateway`) gained a supervisor `state` so the nav shows a **warning glyph for an enabled-but-not-running app** (mirrored on the management row); the Apps page dropped its launcher grid → pure management (Available + installed). **`network`→`packet` capability rename**: the capability meant *full packet-radio network access* (callsign bind + AX.25/NET-ROM), not LAN/internet — renamed across the 4 app manifests + `catalog/apps.yaml`, with a display-normalize + `network` back-compat alias (`AppCapabilities`) so old manifests still render `packet`. **App UI-surface modes** (`ui.mode`: standalone | embedded | slot; `AppUiConfig.Mode`): a `slot` app renders inside the panel chrome via a borderless in-panel iframe (`app-frame.tsx`, src `?pdn_embed=1`); pdn-bbs adopted slot — headless render that drops its own outer chrome (threaded `pdn_embed` through links/forms), restyled its webmail CSS to PDN's design tokens, and follows the panel's manual dark/light toggle (panel threads `&theme=`, the BBS honours it via a same-origin `pdn_theme` cookie + `<html class>`), plus `Cache-Control: no-store` so updates render without a hard refresh (shipped as pdn-bbs **v0.2.5→v0.2.8**, catalog repointed). **Sidecar slim**: `build-deb.sh` UPX-compresses the tsnet sidecar (~21.9→~6.4 MB amd64, ~70% off the *installed* footprint; the `.deb` download is ~unchanged — zstd already compresses it; guarded so a missing `upx` just warns — **the release runner needs `upx` installed**). **Docker → GHCR**: `publish-docker.yml` + `docker-image.sh` + the docker/docs refs now build/push `ghcr.io/packet-net/packet.net` via the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` (`packages: write`) instead of Docker Hub `m0lte/packet.net`. **m0lte→packet-net sweep**: the GitHub repos were transferred from the `m0lte`/`M0LTE` org to **`packet-net`**; renamed 346 stale `m0lte/<repo>`→`packet-net/<repo>` refs across ~67 files (verified per-owner via `gh`) + repointed the local clones' git remotes; left the not-moved repos (`linbpq`, `pskr2mqtt`), the `m0lte.uk` domains, and the `M0LTE` callsign. Also fixed the node self-update github client default owner (`m0lte`→`packet-net`, the 301-redirect was read as "no update") and `deploy-node.sh`'s dev-version base (derive from the latest `node-v*` tag so a dev build sorts at/above the latest release).

### 2026-06-14 — Node self-update Phase 7 slice 3: the github channel + runtime channel detection + the Apply UI

Slice 3 of Phase 7 node self-update ([`docs/node-self-update-design.md`](node-self-update-design.md)) — on top of the already-shipped `apt` + self-contained channels, this adds the **`github`** channel, **runtime** channel detection, and the previously-deferred **web-UI Apply** surface. (Out of scope, maintainer-owned: the OARC/hibby apt repo; cosign/minisign hardening; the self-contained `install.sh` of slice 2c.) **Runtime channel detection (revised § Channel detection):** the build stamp now encodes only what the build genuinely knows — `deb` vs `selfcontained` (`build-deb.sh` stamps `deb`, was `apt`) — because the *same* `.deb` can be apt-managed or `dpkg -i`'d from a Release, which is an install-time fact, not a build fact. `RuntimeInstallChannelProvider` resolves once at boot: stamp `selfcontained` → `SelfContained` with **zero process calls** (so a non-Debian / dpkg-less / Windows host never shells out); stamp `deb`/absent → **dpkg ownership of the running binary** (`dpkg-query -S` on the resolved `/proc/self/exe`, *not* repo presence) → not-owned / dpkg-absent → `SelfContained`; owned → **`apt-cache policy packetnet`** → a real repo origin (not just `/var/lib/dpkg/status`) → `Apt`, else / apt-cache-absent → `Github`. Every probe goes through an injectable `IProcessRunner` (production `SystemProcessRunner` turns a missing-executable `Win32Exception` into a safe not-launched result, never throws); `PDN_INSTALL_CHANNEL` overrides. This **supersedes the earlier `InstallChannel=apt` marker decision** (§ Decisions #2) — baking `apt` into every `.deb` is exactly why a GitHub-installed node (the lab) mislabelled itself. `InstallChannel` gained `Github`; the `/info` channel wire token is now `selfcontained` (was `self-contained`). **`github` apply:** a `packetnet-github-update` helper behind a new `packetnet-update` dispatcher (the single `packetnet-update.service` oneshot now execs the per-channel helper, keyed on `/run/packetnet/update.channel`); the C# launcher writes a `/run/packetnet/github-update.json` request (`{targetVersion,arch,debUrl,sha256}`) which the helper **re-validates every field of** (arch == `dpkg --print-architecture`; HTTPS GitHub host; 64-hex sha re-verified against the download; `targetVersion` `dpkg --compare-versions gt` installed — no downgrade) then `dpkg -i` (detached, survives the postinst restart) → `/healthz`-gate → rollback via `dpkg -i` of the retained prior `.deb`. `packetnet.service` gained `RuntimeDirectoryPreserve=yes` so the spool survives the restart. **Per-channel "update available?" check:** `apt` = `apt-cache policy` candidate; `github` = latest `node-v*` via the GitHub Releases API; self-contained = the `latest.json` feed — all total/guarded (offline / missing tool / API error → `updateAvailable:false`), behind `SystemVersionService` (TTL cache + single-flight) so `GET /api/v1/system/info` is an in-memory read; `NodeVersion.IsUpdateOver` compares numeric bases so a `0.1.0+dev…` build is never offered a same-base "downgrade". `/info` now returns `{ version, channel: apt|github|selfcontained|unknown, updateMechanism, updateAvailable, latestVersion }`. **Web UI:** an "About this node" panel in Config→Management shows the running version + channel, an "update available · vX → vY" banner, and an admin **Apply** button that POSTs `/system/update` then polls `/healthz` + `/info` until the version changes (the fire-and-acknowledge reconnect, 180s deadline), disabled when channel is `unknown`. **Tests:** 19 detection + 74 self-update backend + 1023 full filtered Node suite + 50 web (incl. the poll-reconnect), all green; both builds clean under warnings-as-errors. Built by a background multi-agent workflow (foundation → parallel backend+frontend → integration), verified here. Working-tree/uncommitted; lab deploy pending.

### 2026-06-14 — App catalog: auto-restart an enabled app on update so the new binary runs

Bug fix in the "Available apps" install/update path ([`docs/app-catalog.md`](app-catalog.md) § Install flow item 4). **The bug:** `POST /api/v1/apps/available/{id}/install` re-laid an app's payload but did not restart it, so for an already-**enabled**, running, pdn-managed app the supervisor kept running the **old** binary after an update. The supervisor only restarts a service when its *spawn fingerprint* (resolved command + args + working dir + merged environment) changes, and a version bump changes none of those — so a plain reconcile leaves the old process alive and "Update" in the UI had no effect until a manual restart. **The fix** (`PdnAvailableAppsApi.TryRestartIfEnabledAsync`): after a successful `InstallFromCatalogAsync`, re-discover the package and — only if it is currently enabled, pdn-managed (`service.managed == pdn`), and error-free — drive `IAppServiceSupervisor.RestartAsync(id)` synchronously within the request (RestartAsync respawns from the *currently discovered* package, so it picks up the freshly-laid on-disk binary, not a stale cached spec; the UI shows a spinner during install, so the couple extra seconds is fine). A **fresh** install lands disabled (install ≠ enable) → nothing is restarted. The restart is **best-effort and never demotes a committed install to a failure**: a missing supervisor (degraded boot), a disabled/unmanaged/broken package, or any restart throw yields `restarted: false` in the response (alongside `ok: true`) plus an audit line, never a 422 — the payload is already on disk. The install response gained a `restarted` boolean; a `restart_app` audit entry is recorded on the post-install bounce. **Backend-only** — the web UI already reloads after install, so no UI change. Tests: extended `AvailableAppsApiTests` with six cases (enabled-managed → restart fires + audited; disabled-managed → no restart; enabled-but-no-service → no restart; restart-throws → still 200 + restarted=false + error audit; no-supervisor → still 200; restart-ok audit), using the existing real-discovery + faked-catalog/installer harness plus a recording `FakeSupervisor` mirroring `AppPackagesApiTests`. Node suite green; both builds clean (no warnings).

### 2026-06-14 — Network-access S4: app-declared tailnet port forwarding (the IMAP path)

Generalises the embedded sidecar from "the web UI" to **any TCP service an app declares** — the mechanism that lets the BBS's IMAP/SMTP reach iPhone Mail over Tailscale with a real cert. **Manifest:** `pdn-app.yaml` gains a `forward:` block — `[{ listen, target, tls: terminate|raw }]` (`AppForwardSpec`, default `terminate`). **Validation** (`AppPackageCatalog`, total): `listen` 1..65535 and **≠ 443** (reserved for the web reverse-proxy); `target` must be **loopback** host:port (127.0.0.1/::1/localhost — pdn must never proxy the tailnet to arbitrary hosts); a `listen` claimed by two discovered packages flags both (the session-verb-collision pattern). **Sidecar (Go):** new `--forwards-file <path>` (a JSON array the supervisor writes) → per entry, on the **tsnet node**: `terminate`=`ListenTLS(":listen")` with the node's auto LE cert → dial the loopback `target` (plaintext) + bidi copy; `raw`=`Listen(":listen")` (relies on the tailnet's WireGuard encryption). Implicit-TLS only (993/465) — STARTTLS is out of scope (would need a protocol-aware sidecar). The byte-pump is factored + unit-tested without a tailnet; UDP deferred (the `ListenPacket` shape differs; TCP is the need). **Supervisor:** `TailscaleSidecarHostedService` collects forwards from **enabled, error-free** packages → writes `<stateDir>/forwards.json` → passes `--forwards-file` → folds the set into the spawn fingerprint (enable/disable/forward-change restarts the sidecar); forwards only when tailscale is enabled; the web `--target` is unchanged. **The division:** apps serve **plaintext on loopback**; pdn owns the TLS edge + the trusted `<callsign>-pdn.<tailnet>.ts.net` name — exactly the web app-gateway model, for raw TCP. Tailnet-only (Funnel can't carry raw IMAP); each forward is a capability shown at enable. **`GET /api/v1/apps/packages`** entries gain `forwards`; the enable-confirm lists the tailnet exposure. Docs: `app-packages.md` + `network-access.md` (IMAP promoted from doc-only to **built**). **pdn-bbs adapted** (PR #30 / v0.2.0): under `PDN_APP_ID` the first-run `bbs.yaml` serves IMAP + SMTP-submission **plaintext on loopback** (127.0.0.1:11430/11465) and the manifest declares `forward: 993→11430, 465→11465 tls:terminate`; standalone unchanged. 957 Node + 40 web + the sidecar's Go tests green. **Remaining:** add a `bbs` catalog entry (from the v0.2.0 release), deploy, lab end-to-end with iPhone Mail.

### 2026-06-14 — Network-access S3 + Release node-v0.11.0 — the lab on Tailscale, DNS-01 retired

S3 of the network-access pivot ([`network-access.md`](network-access.md)): the lab migrated to the embedded Tailscale path and the fragile DNS-01 dance is gone. **Two bugs the bringup surfaced (both fixed in #443):** (1) the sidecar inherits `packetnet.service`'s sandbox, whose `RestrictAddressFamilies` lacked **`AF_NETLINK`** — tsnet's userspace network monitor needs a netlink socket, so the sidecar crash-looped on the lab (fine outside the sandbox); added `AF_NETLINK` (Tailscale's own unit does the same), verified via `systemd-run` mirroring the unit. (2) **The node hostname now defaults to `<callsign>-pdn`** (`TailscaleHostname.Resolve` — lowercased base callsign, SSID dropped; e.g. `GB7RDG`→`gb7rdg-pdn`) so multiple nodes on one tailnet don't collide on a bare `pdn` (which Tailscale would suffix `-1`/`-2`, making the FQDN — and the passkey RP ID — unpredictable); empty hostname ⇒ derive, explicit wins. Also surfaced the sidecar's parsed `error` reason in the supervisor log. **Lab bringup:** enabled `tailscale: { enabled: true }` (interactive login — Tom clicked the surfaced `login.tailscale.com` URL), the node joined as **`m9yyy-pdn.tailef2e8.ts.net`**; repointed `relyingPartyId`/`allowedOrigins` to it; **retired DNS-01** — removed the acme.sh cron + the `pdn.m0lte.uk` cert material, dropped `certificatePath` so the LAN `:8443` listener self-signs. **Verified:** an on-tailnet iPhone loaded `https://m9yyy-pdn.tailef2e8.ts.net` with the real LE cert and passkeys work — the whole point of the pivot, now with zero public DNS / port-forward / ACME. (The MagicDNS host is **tailnet-private** — only on-tailnet devices resolve/reach it; Funnel is the opt-in public mode, deliberately off for the sysop panel.) **Released as node-v0.11.0** (`gh workflow run publish-node.yml -f version=0.11.0`): 3-arch debs incl. the go-built sidecar, install-smoked, on the GitHub Release. Node-only — no lib/TS/downstream. The acme.sh+Cloudflare DNS-01 approach (the old `passkeys-lan-trust-pattern.md`) is now fully superseded.

### 2026-06-14 — Network-access S2: the embedded tsnet sidecar (Go enters the build)

The Tier-C slice ([`network-access.md`](network-access.md)). **The sidecar** `sidecar/tsnet/` (Go, `tailscale.com` v1.100.0, static `CGO_ENABLED=0`, ~20 MB/arch): joins the operator's tailnet via `tsnet` (userspace — no `tailscaled`/root/TUN), surfaces the interactive **login URL** off the IPN bus, reads back the **FQDN** (`Self.DNSName`), `ListenTLS(":443")` (auto LE cert) — or `ListenFunnel` when `--funnel` — and reverse-proxies to pdn's loopback `--target`, stamping `X-Forwarded-Proto: https` + `X-Forwarded-Host: <fqdn>` so pdn's S1 loopback-trusted `ForwardedHeaders` see the request as HTTPS at the `.ts.net` host (the mechanism that makes passkeys work remotely). CLI `--hostname/--state-dir/--target/--authkey-file/--funnel`; **stdout = a JSON status stream** (`starting`/`needs-login`+authURL/`running`+fqdn/`error`); SIGTERM → exit 0; tags come from the auth key (not a flag). `build-deb.sh` cross-builds it per-arch (RID→GOARCH, GOARM=7 for armhf; `go` at `/usr/bin/go` on the runner) → `/usr/lib/packetnet/packetnet-tsnet`; **PDN_FAST deb 40.7→47.6 MB** (the ~20 MB binary, ~7 MB compressed) — still far under the old ~73 MB. **The consumer side:** `ITailscaleStatus` (thread-safe snapshot holder) + `TailscaleSidecarHostedService` (subscribes `OnChange`; idempotent gated reconcile launches/stops/restarts the one child by a spawn-fingerprint; inline `authKey` → a 0600 temp file deleted on exit, or `authKeyFile` passed through; stdout JSON → the holder, stderr → log; exponential backoff on `TimeProvider`; `setsid` group + SIGTERM→grace→SIGKILL teardown mirroring `AppServiceSupervisor`; a missing binary when enabled → status `error`, never a crash; a disabled/default node launches nothing); `GET /api/v1/system/tailscale` (read scope) → `{enabled,state,fqdn,authUrl,funnel}`; the web Management-tab **"Remote access (Tailscale)"** panel (polls status; `needs-login` → an "Authorize this node →" link, `running` → "Reachable at `https://<fqdn>`" + funnel badge, and an admin-gated "Use `<fqdn>` for passkeys" button that sets `relyingPartyId`+`allowedOrigins` via the config-write seam, operator-initiated with an "invalidates existing passkeys" warning). `CLAUDE.md` "What lives where" gains `sidecar/tsnet/`. 929 Node + 38 web tests green; `build-deb` end-to-end stages the sidecar. **Remaining: S3** — enable it on the lab, do the interactive login, verify passkeys, retire the `pdn.m0lte.uk` DNS-01 cert.

### 2026-06-14 — Network-access S1: HTTP-first foundation (no Go)

First slice of the network-access pivot ([`network-access.md`](network-access.md)), the pure-.NET groundwork; nothing Tailscale-functional yet. **ForwardedHeaders:** `app.UseForwardedHeaders()` now runs **first** in the pipeline, trusting `X-Forwarded-For/Proto/Host` **only from loopback** (`KnownProxies` = loopback v4+v6, `KnownIPNetworks` cleared — the S2 sidecar runs on loopback; a non-loopback client can't spoof scheme/host). So behind the sidecar's TLS, `Request.IsHttps`/`Scheme`/`Host` reflect the public `https` origin → `PdnAppGateway`'s `pdn_at` `Secure = Request.IsHttps` cookie is correct and any request-derived WebAuthn origin is `https://`. With no proxy it's a no-op. **Passkey UI gate:** new `web/.../lib/secureContext.ts` `passkeysAvailable()` (= `window.isSecureContext && PublicKeyCredential`); login/setup/users **hide** the passkey affordances + show a "passkeys need HTTPS — use Tailscale or localhost" hint when false, with password (+over-RF TOTP) login fully intact — so a plain-HTTP LAN deployment degrades gracefully instead of offering a button that errors. **`tailscale:` config block** added to `NodeConfig` (`enabled/authKey/authKeyFile/hostname/tags/stateDir/target/funnel`) with `TailscaleConfigValidator` (when enabled: hostname `[a-z0-9-]`, target host:port, stateDir set; always: authKey/authKeyFile mutually exclusive) + YAML map + round-trip/arbitrary tests — **parsed + validated but inert** (S2's `TailscaleSidecarHostedService` consumes it). **Docs:** `passkeys-lan-trust-pattern.md` gets a SUPERSEDED banner (body preserved as analysis); `packaging/packetnet.yaml` `management.https` comment rewritten (Tailscale = blessed remote/passkey path, no built-in ACME/DNS-01) + a commented `tailscale:` example. 916 Node + 31 web tests green; both builds clean.

### 2026-06-14 — Network-access ADR: HTTP-first, TLS at the edge, Tailscale (embedded) for passkeys, DNS-01 retired

Design/ADR at [`docs/network-access.md`](network-access.md); execution as slices S1–S3. **Why:** making passkeys work over the WLAN currently leans on a publicly-trusted cert for `pdn.m0lte.uk` via acme.sh + Cloudflare **DNS-01** — fragile and operator-hostile (needs a public domain + CF token + the DNS-01 dance + renewal). **The hard constraint:** WebAuthn needs a *secure context* (trusted-cert HTTPS, or `localhost`), and the RP ID can't be an IP — so bare-LAN HTTP can't do passkeys (accepted). **The pivot:** pdn is **HTTP-first; TLS is the edge's job** (it already matches — `management.https` is opt-in self-signed/BYO, no ACME inside pdn). The blessed remote+passkey path becomes **Tailscale, embedded (Tier C)**: a small **`tsnet` Go sidecar** (`sidecar/tsnet/`, static CGO-off, cross-compiled per arch, staged at `/usr/lib/packetnet/packetnet-tsnet`, supervised by a `TailscaleSidecarHostedService`) joins the operator's tailnet, gets a **free real Let's Encrypt cert** for `pdn.<tailnet>.ts.net` via `ListenTLS`, and reverse-proxies to pdn's loopback HTTP — trusted HTTPS → **passkeys work remotely**, no public DNS / port-forward / ACME. **Tom's decisions (2026-06-14):** Tier C (embedded sidecar — accepts Go entering the build; `tsnet` is Go-only, no managed client, P/Invoke `libtailscale` rejected in favour of the cleaner sidecar); **keep** the optional self-signed/BYO HTTPS; IMAP = self-signed LAN / Tailscale-trusted remote (doc-only — the BBS arc; retiring DNS-01 keeps trusted-cert mail for tailnet devices, loses only internet-public no-VPN mail since Funnel can't carry raw IMAP); **DNS-01/Cloudflare fully retired** (no "advanced" footnote — own-edge is the operator's job); the self-signed CA + `ca.crt`/`.mobileconfig` smoothing **parked** ([#439](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/439)). pdn code: add `UseForwardedHeaders` (loopback-only) so the proxied scheme/Secure-cookie are right; gate the passkey UI on `window.isSecureContext`; the `tailscale:` config block; status surfacing (FQDN + login URL + an RP-ID *suggestion* — pdn never silently changes the RP ID, which would invalidate passkeys). Free tier covers it all (MagicDNS + LE cert + Serve/Funnel + tsnet). **Slices: S1** HTTP-first foundation (no Go) **→ S2** the sidecar (Go in the build) **→ S3** migrate the lab to Tailscale + stop the `pdn.m0lte.uk` DNS-01 cert. Go 1.26 already on the runner.

### 2026-06-14 — Release node-v0.10.0 — the app catalog ("Available apps") arc

Shipped Slices 6a + 6b + 6c (the whole app-catalog feature — [`docs/app-catalog.md`](app-catalog.md)) on green `main` (`ebf1ad9`). Cut via `gh workflow run publish-node.yml --ref main -f version=0.10.0` (the dispatch path — the release job creates the tag itself, so a failed build leaves nothing dangling); `publish-node.yml` built the **amd64 / arm64 / armhf** `.deb`s (arm cross-published via crossgen2 R2R, serial), install-smoked the amd64 one on Debian-stable + Ubuntu-LTS, and published the GitHub Release with the `.deb`s + self-contained `.tar.gz`s + `latest.json` + `SHA256SUMS`. **The headline: the release `.deb`s are 48 / 43 / 47 MB (amd64/arm64/armhf), down from ~73 MB+** — DAPPS's ~33 MB self-contained binary no longer rides in the package; it (and bpqchat/convers) install on demand from the embedded catalog. **Node-only** — Slice 6 lives entirely in `Packet.Node*` (no `Packet.Ax25`/library change), so no `lib-v*` tag, no axcall/tui downstream, no ax25-ts/TS leg. **Lab** (`packetdotnet`): deployed and **tidied to a clean slate** — the prior inline-enabled apps (bbs/convers/dapps) removed from disk + config; only wall/lobby remain (bundled, disabled); the catalog (`/usr/share/packetnet/catalog/apps.yaml`) serves dapps/bpqchat/convers as installable; `/api/v1/apps/available` + `/apps/packages` wired (401 auth-gated). CI note: the PR's shift-left `build-and-smoke` wedged ~45 min on self-hosted-runner oversubscription (the known infra flake) and was cancelled — the build-deb change was validated by a local build + a real lab deploy, and the authoritative build+smoke ran in this release pipeline.

### 2026-06-13 — App platform Slice 6c: DAPPS out of the deb, catalog staged instead

The size payoff. `scripts/build-deb.sh` no longer fetches + stages the DAPPS per-RID binary + manifest (the `dapps_version` + four sha256 pins + the `dapps_fetch`/cache machinery are deleted); it now stages the committed `catalog/apps.yaml` to `/usr/share/packetnet/catalog/apps.yaml`. **Only WALL + LOBBY remain bundled** in the deb; DAPPS, bpqchat and convers install on demand from the catalog (Slice 6a/6b). Measured: the PDN_FAST dev `.deb` dropped **73.6 → 40.7 MB** (the ~33 MB DAPPS self-contained binary, gone). `ShippedManifestsTests` lost its three DAPPS `SkippableFact`s + the full-shipped-layout leg + the build-deb-pin helpers (the catalog file is parsed/validated by `AppCatalogYamlTests`; WALL/LOBBY + the examples-discovery test stay). `docs/app-packages.md` § Distribution rewritten so the **app catalog is the primary distribution path** and bundling is just the two reference apps; the implementation-map line points at `catalog/apps.yaml` staging. Release as a `node-v*` cut + lab deploy follow.

### 2026-06-13 — App platform Slice 6b: Available-apps API + UI
