Surfaces the 6a catalog + installer in the control panel. **API:** `PdnAvailableAppsApi` — `GET /api/v1/apps/available` (read scope) projects the catalog (`IAppCatalog.List()`) left-joined with the discovered packages into `{id, name, version, …, kind, installed, installedVersion, updateAvailable, installable}`, and `POST /api/v1/apps/available/{id}/install` (admin, audited) drives `IAppInstaller.InstallFromCatalogAsync` (404 unknown id, 409 not-installable for this RID, 422 on installer failure). `installedVersion` is read from the `.pdn-install.json` marker (`IAppInstaller.GetInstalled`, new) — the catalog version pinned at install time — so bpqchat/convers don't show a spurious update badge against their lagging in-repo manifest version (O4). **install ≠ enable**: install lands a *disabled* package; the owner's enable grant runs it. `PdnAppPackagesApi` gains `POST /{id}/uninstall` (admin, audited — refuses while enabled, strips the `apps:` override, then removes only marker-recorded payload, leaving app state) and `POST /upload` (admin, multipart `.pdnapp`, `DisableAntiforgery` + 512 MB request limit). **UI** (`web/packetnet-ui`): an "Available apps" section — Install/Update behind the capability `ConfirmDialog` (reused from enable), an admin-gated "Upload a .pdnapp" affordance, Install disabled-with-tooltip when no artifact exists for this node's arch, and a per-row Uninstall on installed packages (disabled while enabled to pre-empt the 409). +19 backend + 8 web tests; full Node suite green (889), vitest 30; both builds clean. Still DI-surfaced only against the *embedded* catalog — DAPPS stays bundled until 6c.

### 2026-06-13 — App platform Slice 6a: catalog + installer foundation

The read-and-install half of the app catalog (`docs/app-catalog.md`), additive and not yet surfaced via API/UI. `catalog/apps.yaml` is the curated index (dapps = `kind: assets`; bpqchat + convers = `kind: deb`; every artifact sha256-pinned from GitHub's recorded digests). New `Packet.Node.Core/Applications/Catalog/`: the record tree + YamlDotNet parser/validator; `IAppCatalog`/`EmbeddedAppCatalog` (reads the embedded `/usr/share/packetnet/catalog/apps.yaml`, total + non-throwing, distinct from the installed-package `IAppPackageCatalog`); `IAppInstaller`/`AppInstaller` over `IArtifactFetcher` (https-only + 512 MB cap), `IDebExtractor` (`dpkg-deb -x`, unprivileged — extract, not dpkg-install), `Sha256`, `RuntimeIds`. Three artifact kinds (assets / deb / pdnapp; the last also the upload format, path-traversal-guarded untar). Every fetched artifact is sha256-verified against the catalog pin — a mismatch is a hard refusal, nothing staged. **O1 resolved with a `.pdn-install.json` payload marker** (not the dir split the design floated): each install records exactly the files placed, so update/uninstall delete only those and never app-created state, a marker-less (hand-sideloaded) dir is refused, and discovery + `PDN_APP_STATE` are unchanged. `Program.cs` adds `AddHttpClient` + the four DI registrations; no API mapping yet. +27 catalog tests (no live network — fake fetcher + fixtures; the deb test builds a real `.deb` and extracts via real `dpkg-deb`); full `Packet.Node.Tests` green.

### 2026-06-13 — App platform Slice 6 design: the app catalog ("Available apps") — ship the index, not the payloads

Design/ADR landed at [`docs/app-catalog.md`](app-catalog.md); execution to follow as slices 6a–6c (6d = the A2 network refresh, deferred). The driver: bundling app payloads into the node `.deb` doesn't scale — DAPPS's per-arch self-contained binary alone took the deb **39.8→73.6 MB**, and bpqchat/convers/BBS/whatspac are all coming. The fix ships the **index** instead of the payloads.

- **The model:** a curated `catalog/apps.yaml` (committed here, baked into the deb at `/usr/share/packetnet/catalog/apps.yaml`) lists **available-but-not-installed** apps. The control panel's **"Available apps"** view (not a "store" — Tom's wording) lets the owner install one in two ways: **URL-fetch** of a sha256-pinned artifact, or **upload** of a `.pdnapp`. Install stages a package into the owner root `/var/lib/packetnet/apps/<id>/`, where the existing discovery picks it up as **discovered-but-disabled** — enable stays the separate, deliberate run-grant. This is mostly *relocation*: the fetch→verify→stage logic already exists in `build-deb.sh`'s DAPPS block, moving from build-time bash to install-time C# behind an `IAppInstaller`/`IAppCatalog` seam; everything downstream of "a package dir exists" is already built (Slice 5a/5b).
- **Trust = apt's `Release→Packages→.deb` chain:** the embedded catalog is the root (anchored by the deb's own integrity in v1); every artifact is sha256-pinned (pins are GitHub's recorded per-asset digests) → tamper-evident even over plain HTTPS from third-party releases; **install ≠ enable** so a forged catalog still can't auto-run code; **no new privilege** — the install root is `packetnet`-writable, so unlike self-update there's no polkit/root helper.
- **Three artifact kinds** for the three real publishing shapes: `assets` (per-RID raw binary + manifest asset — DAPPS), `deb` (per-arch `.deb`, **extracted** via `dpkg-deb -x` and relocated — *not* dpkg-installed — so bpqchat/convers install unprivileged from their existing `.deb`s), `pdnapp` (tar.gz of the package dir, the go-forward + upload format). Initial vetted set: **dapps** (assets), **bpqchat**, **convers** (deb).
- **Decisions (Tom, 2026-06-13):** A1 (embedded catalog) now, designed so A2 (signed network refresh — the self-updater's cosign seam) is a drop-in; **move DAPPS out of the deb** (reverts ~73→~40 MB); ship **both** install faces (URL + upload) in v1. O1 (resolved in 6a): a `.pdn-install.json` payload marker — the installer records exactly the files it placed, so update/uninstall touch only those and never app-created state; discovery + `PDN_APP_STATE` unchanged (lower-risk than a payload/state dir split). O2: `apt install` of the same `.deb`s stays a complementary CLI channel, not v1. This refines the 5b "DAPPS ships *with* pdn" decision — still the flagship one-click app, just not bytes in the deb.

### 2026-06-13 — Release node-v0.9.3 (the local-app crossconnect teardown fix)

Shipped the crossconnect-teardown fix (the entry below, #430) on green `main` (`40f0c9a`; ci + interop verified on the merge commit — the rax25/netsim `Rax25ViaNetsimConnectedMode` leg flaked twice under the day's heavy runner contention and passed on a third run on the now-idle runner, a lossy-channel timing flake unrelated to this node-console change). `publish-node.yml` → the 8-asset GitHub Release (<https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/releases/tag/node-v0.9.3>); `publish-docker.yml` → **`ghcr.io/packet-net/packet.net:0.9.3`** (linux/amd64 + arm64, index `sha256:eca2682a…`) with **`:latest` moved onto it** (off 0.9.2's `a7f82f07…`). **Node-only, no library change** — the fix is wholly in `Packet.Node.Core.Console` (`LocalAppConnector`), so the six published NuGet packages stay at `lib-v0.8.0`, no downstream/TS. **Open follow-up (separate from this bug):** the reporter's repro app base64-encoded its RHP `send.data` and the node forwarded it literally to the console — worth confirming whether RHP `send.data` is base64 or raw on the wire (a protocol-encoding question, not a teardown question).

### 2026-06-13 — Fix: local-app crossconnect torn down the instant ACCEPT was pushed (Connected→Disconnected, no data)

A node-prompt connect to a **local** app SSID (`C <call>-SSID` where an RHP client has bound+listened on that callsign — the chat-app-over-RHP path) connected and **immediately disconnected**, bridging no data: the app received the `accept` push but the console session was torn down before the app's banner could cross. Root cause in `LocalAppConnector.RunAppAsync` (the crossconnect added earlier this day): it `await`ed the app's accept handler and disposed the loopback's app-end in a `finally`. But the RHPv2 server's accept handler (`OnInboundAcceptedAsync`) pushes ACCEPT and then pumps the connection in the **background**, **returning immediately** — so the `finally` disposed the app-end the instant ACCEPT was sent, EOFing the loopback and ending the console relay (Connected→Disconnected). The over-the-air inbound path (`PortSupervisor.OnAppSessionAccepted`) gets this right — *"the handler owns the connection from here"*, it never disposes on the handler's return — and the crossconnect must mirror it. **Fix:** don't dispose the app-end on the handler's normal return (the app's background pump owns it and disposes it when the child handle closes; teardown flows through the shared loopback instead — caller drop → userEnd dispose → appEnd EOF → pump closes; app close → pump disposes appEnd → userEnd EOF → console relay ends); dispose only if the handler **throws** before taking ownership (idempotent, so a refused accept that already disposed is harmless). The earlier crossconnect tests missed it because their fake handlers *await a read loop* (return only when the link ends), masking the real server's immediate-return-and-pump shape. **Tests:** a new gateway crossconnect test whose handler returns immediately after starting a background pump (verified **red→green**: the app's banner never arrived before, arrives after, and caller→app data flows), full crossconnect set (21) green. **Validated end-to-end** with the reporter's exact one-node repro on a locally-built image (RHP app binds GB7BBB-4, telnet `C GB7BBB-4` → the app's payload now crosses; previously Connected→Disconnected). Node-host console only; no library change, no parity leg. (Side observation worth a follow-up: the repro app base64-encoded its `send.data` and the node forwarded it literally — whether RHP `send.data` is base64 or raw on the wire is a separate question from this teardown bug.)

### 2026-06-13 — Release node-v0.9.2 (the connect-banner fix)

Shipped the connect-banner fix (the entry below, #425) the moment it merged. **Tag `node-v0.9.2`** on green `main` (`456411b`; ci + interop both verified on the merge commit — interop's ax25-ts/LinBPQ "peer refused connection" leg re-run once, the usual netsim flake) → `publish-node.yml` GitHub Release with the 8 assets (per-arch `.deb`s + self-contained `.tar.gz`s + `latest.json` + `SHA256SUMS`, <https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/releases/tag/node-v0.9.2>) and `publish-docker.yml` pushed **`ghcr.io/packet-net/packet.net:0.9.2`** (linux/amd64 + arm64, index `sha256:a7f82f07…`) with **`:latest` moved onto it** (off 0.9.1's `0b8baa62…`), so a fresh `docker run … :latest` now gets the banner fix. **Node-only again** — the fix touches `Packet.Ax25` (`Ax25Session`) + `Packet.Node.Core`, but the **six published NuGet packages are still byte-identical to `lib-v0.8.0`** (Ax25Session's additive replay seam ships to users via the node `.deb`/image; no NuGet consumer needs it yet), so no `lib-v` tag, no axcall/tui downstream, no ax25-ts npm. **Outstanding:** the ax25-ts `SessionRelay` TS-parity follow-up (same connect/subscribe replay seam) — tracked in the fix entry below; a future `lib-v` sweep + the TS leg can land together.

### 2026-06-13 — Design: pdn-whatspac-client — a persistent WhatsPac client as a (separate-repo) pdn app

New design record [`docs/whatspac-client-design.md`](whatspac-client-design.md) (ADR + execution plan; Tom + Claude), reached by iterating from "how could a pdn app connect to WhatsPac?". **Decision:** build **`m0lte/pdn-whatspac-client`** — a separate-repo, out-of-process **DAPPS-shaped** app (the platform's not-first app; DAPPS is the network-plane precedent, pdn-bpqchat/pdn-convers in progress) — *not* a feature in `Packet.Node*` and *not* a re-host of the WhatsPac SPA. It fixes WhatsPac's defining flaw (the over-the-air session to WPS `MB7NPW-9` dies with the browser tab, no persistence) by running a **persistent agent** that holds the WPS link via **RHPv2** (`open(Active)` over the *already-shipped* TCP/9000 server — pdn drives the modem; the legitimate app↔node boundary DAPPS uses), runs WhatsPac's `{hop,cmd,val}` connect-script, speaks the **WPS application protocol**, persists all state to SQLite, and presents it through **two heads**: a LAN/phone HTTP+SSE UI behind the app-gateway, and a line-based **`C WHATSPAC`** RF terminal on the `pdn-app/1` wire. **The WPS application protocol was reverse-engineered from the production SPA bundle** and is documented in the design doc: framing = `base64(zlib-deflate(JSON))` delimited `\r` outbound / `0xC0` inbound (the client reads RHP's Latin-1 `recv.data` so it dodges the browser's high-byte mangling), a `t`-discriminated message catalogue (~30 types: `c`/`k` session, `u`/`o`/`uc`/`ud`/`ue`/`he`/`s` users, `m`/`mb`/`med`/`mr`/`mem` DMs, `cs`/`pch`/`cp`/`cpb`/`cpe`/`cpr`/`cpem` channels+posts, `a`/`ae`/`ar` WhatsPic), and the connect handshake. **Roadmap consequence:** the RHP **WebSocket transport (PWP-0245)** — the one piece deferred by name in [`docs/rhp2-server.md`](rhp2-server.md) — is **not needed** for this design (it would only matter for backing the unmodified WhatsPac *browser*, the rejected alternative), so plan §6's "TCP + WS listeners" can drop the WS half from its critical path. **Scope:** single WPS user for v1; multi-user multiplexing + efficient-binary framing are deferred to the **packet-net/dapps federation** path (Tom + Kevin). No pdn-library surface change → **no ax25-ts parity leg** (it's an app consuming existing seams; the only pdn-repo artefact is the design doc). Sliced 0–6 (on-air capture → protocol lib → agent → RF head → LAN head → packaging → federation). Design only — no code in this repo.

### 2026-06-13 — Fix: a node's connect banner was dropped on an outbound link (RHP `open`/console `C` to a node)

An RHP `open` (or console `C`) to a node's **own callsign** connected — `openReply errCode:0`, `status Connected` — then delivered **nothing**: the node never presented its console banner over that session, so a client waiting for the prompt stalled (the "expect timed out with no data" report). **Root cause, reproduced on the 0.9.1 docker image** (two AXUDP-linked nodes, `python` RHP client): `Ax25NodeConnection` subscribed to the session's `DataLinkSignalEmitted` stream only in its constructor — which, on the **outbound** path, runs *after* `Ax25Listener.ConnectAsync` has already returned a connected link. The accepting node emits its banner the instant it accepts (it does **not** wait), so that I-frame's data-indication was emitted into the subscribe gap and dropped; everything the client sent afterwards worked (a CR returned `GB7BBB> `, `INFO` returned the banner) — proving the session was a correct user circuit, only the *unsolicited* connect-data was lost. The code's own comment had assumed "the remote waits for our banner before sending," false for node-to-node. **Fix:** `Ax25Session` now buffers early inbound DL-DATA indications (bounded, 32) and exposes `AttachConsumerWithReplay`, which—atomically under the session's dispatch gate—replays the buffer to a late-attaching consumer then subscribes it (no loss, no duplication); `Ax25NodeConnection` uses it. Existing event subscribers (NET/ROM tap, axcall `SessionRelay`) are unaffected. **Tests:** a new AXUDP node-to-node test asserts the dialer receives B's banner **without sending first** (verified red→green: hung-then-empty before, passes after), the existing node-to-node test's stale "banner can be missed" workaround comment removed, plus 3 `Ax25Session` replay unit tests. Full Ax25 937 + Node 844 green. The change is in `Packet.Ax25` (`Ax25Session`) + `Packet.Node.Core`; `Ax25Listener` surface/options are untouched, so the parity guard isn't tripped — but the `Ax25Session.AttachConsumerWithReplay` seam + early-replay behaviour is a **TS-parity follow-up** for ax25-ts (its `SessionRelay` has the same connect/subscribe window). Shipped as part of the node-only release below (the six published NuGet libs still need no tag — `Ax25Session` ships to users via the node `.deb`/image; a `lib-v` sweep can pick it up later).

### 2026-06-13 — Re-release node-v0.9.1 (the security fix #421 missed the 0.9.0 cut)

`node-v0.9.0` was tagged on `8056433` while the MCP OAuth/token security remediation (#421, `d98294b` — JWT/MCP token **audience segregation**, the OAuth-without-`management.auth` **misconfiguration guard**, the Argon2 username-enumeration **timing-oracle fix**, RFC 9207 `iss`) was still in review; it merged to `main` *immediately after* the cut, so 0.9.0's `.deb` and image shipped **without** it. Caught on review of the release. Re-cut **`node-v0.9.1`** on `f682688` (current `main`, which includes `d98294b`; ci + interop both green on that commit) → `publish-node.yml` GitHub Release with the usual 8 assets (<https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/releases/tag/node-v0.9.1>) and `publish-docker.yml` pushed **`ghcr.io/packet-net/packet.net:0.9.1`** (linux/amd64 + arm64, index `sha256:0b8baa62…`); **`:latest` moved off 0.9.0 (`sha256:0be37f5a…`) onto 0.9.1**, so the security remediation is in every artifact a `:latest` / fresh-install user gets. `node-v0.9.0` is left published but **superseded** (don't pull `:0.9.0` or that specific tag for a node that will enable `mcp.oauth`). Still node-only — the fix is in `Packet.Node*`, not the published libraries, so the lib/downstream/TS legs stay skipped exactly as for 0.9.0. Process note: tag the release commit only *after* confirming no in-flight security PR is about to land — a `node-v*` tag captures `main` at that instant, and a fix merging minutes later silently misses the artifact.

### 2026-06-13 — Release node-v0.9.0 (+ multi-arch docker image)

Cut the release of everything merged since `node-v0.8.0` (2026-06-11): Phase 8 MCP (in-process server, `pdn mcp` bridge, monitor-v2, persisted audit, OAuth AS behind the default-off flag), Phase 7 in-app self-update (apt + self-contained channels), RHPv2 server hardening (handle/connection-exhaustion, slowloris, per-IP auth throttle), the fuzz extension + NuGet vuln-scan gate, `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` for the BBS mail ports, the multi-arch docker image, and the console connect router + RHP-gateway local-app crossconnect. **Tag `node-v0.9.0`** on green `main` (`8056433`; ci + interop both verified green on the merge commit) → `publish-node.yml` produced the GitHub Release with **8 assets** — per-arch `.deb`s (amd64/arm64/armhf) + per-arch self-contained `.tar.gz`s + `latest.json` + `SHA256SUMS` (<https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/releases/tag/node-v0.9.0>) — and `publish-docker.yml` pushed the **multi-arch image `ghcr.io/packet-net/packet.net:0.9.0` + `:latest`** (linux/amd64 + linux/arm64; `:latest` and `:0.9.0` share index digest `sha256:0be37f5a…`).

**Deliberately a node-only release — no `lib-v0.9.0`, no downstream, no TS leg.** Diffing `node-v0.8.0..main` over `src/`: only `Packet.Node`, `Packet.Node.Core`, `Packet.Mcp`, and `Packet.Agw` changed — **none of the six published NuGet packages** (`Packet.Core`, `Packet.Ax25`, `Packet.Kiss`, `Packet.Kiss.Abstractions`, `Packet.Kiss.Serial`, `Packet.Kiss.NinoTnc`) has a single `.cs` change since `lib-v0.8.0`, so they are byte-identical. Tagging `lib-v0.9.0` would republish identical packages and force pointless `packet-net/axcall` / `packet-net/packet-term-tui` pin-bump rebuilds, so **Step 1 (NuGet) + Step 3 (downstream consumers) were skipped**. No new named flag / parser change landed either (the parity drift-guard is clean), so **Step 4 (ax25-ts npm + packet-term-web) was skipped** too. The libs stay at `lib-v0.8.0`; the node moves to `node-v0.9.0` — the tags are independent by design (`docs/releasing.md`). The `Packet.Agw` fix ships to users via the node `.deb`/image (Agw isn't on the NuGet publish set anyway). Cascade interruption of note: `publish-docker` serialised for ~35 min behind the heavy-build `flock` held by a concurrent security-review CI run — expected (the lock prevents concurrent builds OOMing the shared runner), not a fault.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 8 MCP: OAuth/MCP security-review remediation (build step 10)

Tom's security review of the step-9 authorize/token core (the gate set in step 6). Remediated the two priority findings plus two cheap hardening wins; all on `claude/mcp-oauth-security-review-wtpn83`, still entirely behind `mcp.oauth.enabled` (master unaffected). **(H1) Token audience segregation — the step-9 follow-up, now closed:** MCP credentials (the OAuth-issued connector token *and* the static `/api/v1/mcp/token` bearer) are minted on a dedicated `JwtTokenService.McpAudience` (`packet.net-mcp`), distinct from the control-API audience. The JwtBearer middleware authenticates both audiences (`ValidAudiences`), but each authorization gate now pins exactly one: the `read`/`operate`/`admin` policies require the control-API audience, a new `pdn-mcp` policy (read scope) gates `/mcp` on the MCP audience — so an MCP token can no longer drive the wider REST control API, and a control-API token can't hit `/mcp`. Enforced centrally in `ScopeRequirementHandler` (an `aud` check beside the scope check; still pass-through when auth is off). **(H2) Misconfiguration guard:** `NodeConfigValidator` now refuses `mcp.oauth.enabled` without `management.auth.enabled` — previously that combination stood up a working login/consent/token flow whose tokens were never enforced (the scope gate passes through when auth is off), leaving `/mcp` + REST open behind a false sense of security. **(L1) Username-enumeration timing oracle:** the authorize POST now spends an equivalent Argon2 derivation (`PasswordHasher.VerifyDummy`) on the no-such-user path, so a probe can't distinguish a real username by response timing. **(L4) RFC 9207:** the authorize success redirect now carries `iss`. +5 tests (JWT audience overloads ×2, validator guard theory ×1 covering 4 combos, OAuth full-flow now asserts the MCP audience on the minted token); the `OauthApiTests` config gained `management.auth.enabled: true` to satisfy H2. No ax25-ts parity leg (no parser flag / listener-surface change). Deferred (documented, lower severity): unauthenticated/unthrottled DCR + consent-screen client-name spoofing (M1), `Host`-header trust in discovery/redirect base (M2), stateless-JWT non-revocation (L3 — needs a jti denylist).

### 2026-06-13 — RHPv2 gateway shares the connect router: an app can `open` a local app (in-process crossconnect)

Extended the previous entry's routing to the RHPv2 gateway (`SupervisorRhpGateway.OpenAx25StreamAsync`) — the path a client app's outbound `open` takes. It used to always dial a port (null `port` → first port). Now, **with no explicit port**, an `open.remote` that is a callsign the node is locally registered for (an app bound on its own SSID) bridges in-process over the loopback instead of dialling RF — so one local app can `open` another, app-to-app, with no second SABM, and the target app's `accept` sees the originating `open.local` (or the node) as the caller. An **explicit** port stays a direct RF dial (skips the local-app check) and still validates (`No such port '2' (1..1)`). The local-app bridge is now a single shared seam — `PortSupervisor.TryResolveLocalAppConnector(target, callerPeerId, callerKind)` — used by **both** the console connect router and the gateway (the console router refactored onto it; no behaviour change there). NET/ROM stays out of scope (aliases later). +2 gateway integration tests (in-process crossconnect happy path with originating-callsign + bidirectional data; explicit-port skips local-app and validates); full Node suite 837 + Rhp2 196 green. Doc: `docs/rhp2-server.md` §V1-scope port semantics updated. Node-host/gateway only — no library parser flag/quirk, no listener-surface change → no ax25-ts parity leg.

### 2026-06-13 — Console connect router: local app crossconnect + port-scoped dial (the missing connect layer)

The node console's `Connect` was "dial the one default port" and nothing else — it never consulted the app-callsign registry (so `C GB7RDG-4` from the prompt tried to SABM `GB7RDG-4` over the air instead of bridging to the locally-registered app on that SSID) and couldn't target a specific port. Added the resolver layer the connect command was missing. **Grammar:** `NodeCommandParser` now reads an optional leading 1-indexed port — `C 1 G0ABC-2` (XRouter/BPQ convention); `ConnectCommand` carries `int? Port` (a bare digit token is a port only when a callsign follows; a single digit-leading token like `2E0ABC` stays a callsign; a trailing via-path is still ignored). **Routing:** `PortSupervisor.CreateConnectRouter(default)` returns an `IConnectRouter` the console uses — no port + a registered app SSID → a **loopback crossconnect** to the app (new `LoopbackNodeConnection` duplex pair + `LocalAppConnector`: hand the app end to the registration's accept handler — the very path an over-the-air connect straight to that SSID takes — and relay the user against the other end, presenting the *real caller's* callsign to the app, no RF, no second SABM); an explicit port → a **direct dial out that port** (`ResolveConnector(portId)`); a plain `C <call>` → the session's existing default connector (unchanged). `NodeConsoleEnvironment` gained an optional `ConnectRouter` wired at all three console sites (AX.25 inbound, NET/ROM L4, telnet); when absent (older call sites / tests) the old single-connector behaviour holds and an explicit port is politely refused. **NET/ROM is deliberately out of scope here** (an explicit port is a direct dial; aliases/routed-connect come later — cf. the RHP-gateway `ResolveConnector` asymmetry noted below). +18 tests: parser grammar, the loopback pair (both directions + shared completion + EOF-on-dispose), the real router's four resolutions (local-app / port→p1 / out-of-range / default-fallthrough / unavailable), and the keystone end-to-end — a caller at the node prompt issuing `C DAPPS-7` lands inside the registered app (greeting crosses back, caller→app data flows), with the app seeing the caller's callsign and a `local` arrival label. Node-host console only; no library parser flag / quirk, no listener-surface change → no ax25-ts parity leg.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 8 MCP: OAuth 2.1 authorization server, behind a default-off flag (build step 9)

Built the hosted-claude.ai connector path designed in step 6 — the node as its own OAuth 2.1 AS+RS — **all behind `mcp.oauth.enabled` (default-off), so master is unaffected until an operator opts in** (every route 404s when off). `PdnOauthApi` + `Packet.Node.Core.Auth.Oauth.*`: **discovery** (RFC 9728 protected-resource + RFC 8414 AS metadata, derived from the request host) + a `WWW-Authenticate: …resource_metadata=…` hint on a `/mcp` 401 (added to the JwtBearer `OnChallenge`, only when OAuth is on); **DCR** (RFC 7591 `POST /oauth/register` → persisted `oauth_client` store, public clients, exact-redirect-URI registration); **authorize** (`GET /oauth/authorize` validates client+redirect+S256+scope then renders a server-rendered login/consent page; `POST` verifies credentials via `IUserStore`/`PasswordHasher` + `LoginThrottle`, enforces the user holds the requested node scope, mints a single-use TTL-60s `oauth_code` bound to client+redirect+challenge+user+scope, 302s back with the code); **token** (`POST /oauth/token` code+PKCE-S256→a node JWT via the existing `JwtTokenService.Issue`). Stores follow the resilient `SqliteAuditLog` pattern; codes are single-use via atomic `DELETE … RETURNING`. **Two documented deviations (follow-ups):** access-token-only (no refresh; connector re-authorizes on expiry — `mcp.oauth.accessTokenLifetimeMinutes` default 60) and the MCP token carries the **control-API audience** (same as step 7's Claude Code bearer, so `/mcp` validates it unchanged) — per-resource audience segregation is a hardening pass over both token types. +8 integration tests (`OauthApiTests`: discovery, DCR+reject, consent+unknown-client, full login→code→token, PKCE-mismatch→invalid_grant, single-use replay, bad-credentials, disabled→404). **Security-critical: merged dormant; review before enabling in production** (cf. WebAuthn). No ax25-ts parity leg (no parser flag / listener-surface change). [`docs/mcp-oauth-design.md`](mcp-oauth-design.md) status updated to "implemented behind the flag".

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 8 MCP: REST write-audit completed (build step 8)

Closed the §6 gap step 5 flagged ("the REST write endpoints can adopt the same `IAuditLog` seam — a follow-up"). A new `AuditHttpExtensions.RecordRest(ctx, clock, action, target, outcome, detail)` derives the actor from the request principal (name → `sub` → `owner` when auth is off, matching `HttpContextMcpCallerAccessor`), source `rest`, client-IP from the connection, and records to the same persisted `IAuditLog` (pdn.db) the MCP writes use. Wired into every operational write endpoint, with the **network/transmit** ones first-class (Tom's emphasis — MCP-driven RF must stay transparent to the owner): `connect_session` / `disconnect_session` / `send_session` / `ping_station` (`PdnSessionsApi` — each transmits), `port_lifecycle` + `add_port`/`edit_port`/`delete_port` (`PdnPortsApi`), `PUT /config` + `/config/raw` (`PdnConfigApi`, on apply), `system_update` (`PdnSystemApi`, alongside its existing structured log), `enable_app`/`disable_app`/`restart_app` (`PdnAppPackagesApi`), and `create_user`/`delete_user` (`PdnAuthApi`). Auth-credential events (login/refresh/passkey/TOTP) keep their dedicated `AuthLog` structured-log surface. Send payloads are summarised (length, never the line text). +1 integration test (`PortsApiTests` — a port add + lifecycle land in the audit log as `rest`-sourced rows). So now **both** MCP and REST writes — and the RF/network actions either can drive — are attributable in the durable audit log.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 8 MCP: scope narrowed + the Claude Code bearer token (build step 7)

Tom narrowed the deployment target to **Claude Code (bearer) over LAN-direct or Tailscale** — so the hosted **claude.ai connector / OAuth is parked** (the [design](mcp-oauth-design.md) stays as a record; not built), and public-HTTPS is "works, not the focus". Built the one missing piece that makes the chosen path usable: a durable **MCP bearer token**. `JwtTokenService` gained an `Issue(user, scope, lifetime)` overload (existing call sites unchanged); `McpConfig.TokenLifetimeDays` (default 90); admin-gated + audited `POST /api/v1/mcp/token` (`PdnMcpApi`) mints a long-lived JWT scoped `read` (default) or `operate`, validating through the existing JwtBearer middleware unchanged. Login JWTs (≤60 min) are too short for a static `Authorization` header; this isn't. **Revocation caveat (documented):** a minted token is a stateless JWT → not individually revocable (rotate the signing key to invalidate all); `read` default + bounded lifetime bound the blast radius on a LAN/Tailscale boundary; a jti-denylist is a hardening follow-up. [`docs/mcp-design.md` § Deployment](mcp-design.md) now carries the blessed-path recipe (`mcp/token` → `claude mcp add --transport http … --header "Authorization: Bearer …"`, LAN + Tailscale). +2 `JwtTokenServiceTests` (long-lived validates; non-positive lifetime rejected). Node + Mcp suites green; solution builds clean.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 8 MCP: OAuth design for the hosted claude.ai connector (build step 6 — design)

With all four reachability paths in scope (Tom), the hosted **claude.ai custom connector** is the one needing more than config: the MCP auth spec wants OAuth 2.1. Design written: **[`docs/mcp-oauth-design.md`](mcp-oauth-design.md)**. Decision: the **node is its own Authorization Server + Resource Server** — single process, already owns identities (Argon2id users + passkeys), token minting (`JwtTokenService`), refresh (`RefreshTokenService`), and a consent surface (the panel), so OAuth reuses all of it. Access tokens stay HS256 JWTs but are **audience-segregated** (an MCP token's `aud` is the resource URL, not the control-API audience — neither replays at the other); OAuth scopes `mcp:read`/`mcp:operate` map to the existing `read`/`operate` (no admin-over-MCP). Endpoints (default-off behind `mcp.oauth.enabled`, on the existing web listener): RFC 9728 + RFC 8414 discovery, RFC 7591 DCR, OAuth 2.1 authorize (code+PKCE, reusing panel login/consent) + token + RFC 7009 revoke. Slices: (1) discovery + `401`/`WWW-Authenticate` → (2) DCR + client store → (3) authorize/consent + code store → (4) token + audience-bound JWT + refresh → (5) revoke + panel "connected apps" + hardening. **Security-critical: slices 3–5 (the authorize/token core) want Tom's review before merge, like the WebAuthn work** — slices 1–2 carry no token-issuance risk. No code yet — this is the design + review gate.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 8 MCP: persisted audit log + deployment model decided (build step 5)

**Persisted audit log (a §6 promise, now real).** A node-wide `IAuditLog` / `AuditEntry` + `SqliteAuditLog` (`Packet.Node.Core/Audit/`) persists privileged actions to the consolidated `pdn.db`, mirroring the resilient store pattern (raw SQL + Dapper, WAL, fresh connection per call; a store fault logs + degrades to "not persisted", **never** faults the node — auditing can't take an action path down). Row-capped (20k default, pruned on insert; cap is a ctor param for tests). Each record also emits a structured log line, so auditing shows in normal logs too. Wired into the MCP write tools: `LiveNodeMcpBackend.Audit` records actor / source (`mcp:stdio`|`mcp:sse`) / action / target / detail / client-IP at invocation (`McpCaller` gained an optional `ClientIp`, filled by the SSE accessor from the request). New admin-gated read endpoint `GET /api/v1/audit` (`PdnAuditApi`; pass-through when auth off, like the rest). +4 `SqliteAuditLogTests` (round-trip newest-first, null-IP, cap-prune, bad-path-degrades-without-throwing). The REST write endpoints (sessions/ports/config/system) can adopt the same `IAuditLog` seam — a follow-up to fully satisfy §6's "all write endpoints".

**MCP deployment model decided (with Tom).** Captured in [`docs/mcp-design.md` § Deployment & reachability](mcp-design.md): the **SSE/Streamable-HTTP `/mcp` endpoint is the network transport**, piggybacking the web listener so **MCP reachability == web-panel reachability** (same TLS, same JWT bearer, same path the operator already uses) — **no hard Tailscale dependency**. stdio (`pdn mcp`) is a co-located convenience, not the remote path. All four reachability paths are in scope: LAN-direct, Tailscale/WireGuard overlay, public-HTTPS domain (all three: config + docs + minor hardening on shipped code, Claude Code authenticating with a node JWT bearer header), and the **hosted claude.ai custom connector — which needs an OAuth 2.1 build** (MCP auth spec: the node as an OAuth resource server + AS). OAuth is the long pole and the next unit (its own design pass + security review, like the WebAuthn work). Corrected the design doc's stale `mcp.sse` config (it's `path: /mcp` on the web listener, not a separate `8051` socket).

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 8 MCP: monitor-v2 — live SRT/retries surfaced (build step 4)

Retired the two hard-coded zeros. `PdnReadApi.SessionTimers(supervisor, port, peer)` reads the live connected-mode session's timer state — **smoothed round-trip time (SRT)** and **retry count (RC)** — straight off `Ax25SessionContext` (`Srt`/`RC`, already public), keyed on the (port, peer) link; a link with no live session (UI-only traffic, or a closed session) honestly keeps 0. It feeds **both** consumers: the REST `/links` projection (`BuildLinks` — `SmoothedRttMs`/`Retries` were stubbed 0) and the MCP `link_quality` tool. **Pull, not push** — read at projection time, so zero perturbation of the AX.25 hot path (no new tap, no plumbing through the frame path). The DTO/tool descriptions lost their "0 until the seam lands" caveats. Verified against a **real connected AXUDP link** (the marquee two-node test): node A's SRT for the peer is a positive value post-connect. While there, de-flaked that test's pre-existing eager-banner read race (the `ReadUntilAsync` needle was the callsign, which the connect banner also contains → it could return before the Info reply; switched to a needle unique to the Info reply — now 5/5 stable). 798 Node (+5 from step 3's bridge tests; the link test now also asserts SRT) + 26 Mcp green. **Not done (deliberately):** the **T1/T3 graphs** are a web-UI link-troubleshoot view (a frontend follow-up, not data plumbing — the data's now there); the **link-tuner** stays parked; a DB-persisted write audit is the cross-cutting §6 aspiration (today's MCP write audit is a structured `LoggerMessage` line, matching the codebase norm).

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 8 MCP: the `pdn mcp` stdio bridge (build step 3)

The stdio transport is in. `pdn mcp` (a `Program.cs` arg short-circuit before the web host builds — the first subcommand the node binary has) runs an MCP server over **stdio** for local clients (Claude Code). Because a subcommand is a *separate process* and can't share the running node's in-proc state, it bridges to the node's **loopback REST API** via `RestNodeMcpBackend` (`--node-url`/`PDN_NODE_URL` override the default `http://127.0.0.1:8080`). Coverage: `decode_frame` is pure (works with no node); the read tools + `reset_port` (→ `POST /ports/{id}/lifecycle`) + `disconnect_session` (→ `DELETE /sessions/{id}`) map onto existing `/api/v1` endpoints; `send_ui_frame`/`set_kiss_param` have no REST endpoint and return an honest "SSE-transport-only" message (the full surface remains available over the in-process SSE transport). The caller is the OS-trusted local user (`McpCaller.LocalStdio`, all scopes); logs go to **stderr** (stdout is the MCP stream). **Verified end-to-end**: piped a real `initialize`→`tools/list`→`tools/call` handshake into `packetnet mcp` — server identifies as `packetnet`, lists all 10 tools with schemas, and `decode_frame` round-trips a UI frame (source M0LTE-9, dest APRS, UI/command, pid 240, info "hello"). +5 `RestNodeMcpBackendTests` (stub-handler mapping + status-code + the two SSE-only write gaps). **Remaining:** full write-audit via `AuthLog` (today it's an `ILogger` line), and the monitor-v2 timer-state instrumentation (SRTT/retries/T1-T3) feeding `link_quality` + retiring the REST `/links` zeros.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 8 MCP: in-process server wired into the node host (build step 2)

The MCP server now runs **in-process** in the node host. `LiveNodeMcpBackend` (`Packet.Node/Mcp/`) implements `INodeMcpBackend` over `NodeHostedService`, reusing `PdnReadApi`'s projection builders (bumped to `internal`: `BuildPorts`/`BuildSessions`/`BuildLinks`) so the MCP tools can't drift from `/api/v1`: read tools map straight from those; `recent_frames` filters the telemetry ring (port/peer/kind/since, ≤250); `link_quality` reads the per-link counters (SRTT/retries stay 0 — the monitor-v2 seam, still pending — with an honest `unknown` flag when no link exists); `network_topology` maps the NET/ROM snapshot. Write actions run through the host's **exclusive gate** (same serialisation as the REST writes): `send_ui_frame` → `Ax25Listener.SendUiAsync` (strict factory; digi-path returns not-yet-supported), `reset_port` → `PortSupervisor.RestartPortAsync`, `disconnect_session` → `DlDisconnectRequest` on the matched session; `set_kiss_param` returns an **honest not-yet-wired** result (no KISS-modem param path exists yet — a named remaining step). Each write is audit-logged (actor/transport/tool/detail). **Auth:** the `/mcp` endpoint is gated `read`; write tools additionally require `operate`, enforced in `WriteTools` against the caller's expanded scope set — `HttpContextMcpCallerAccessor` derives it from the request principal and **grants all scopes when `management.auth.enabled` is off**, so the default-unauthenticated loopback behaviour matches the REST surface. New `mcp:` config block (`McpConfig`/`McpSseConfig`, both default-off): the SSE/Streamable-HTTP transport **piggybacks the web listener at `/mcp`** (like RHPv2-WS at `/rhp` — the §6 "8051" note is superseded, recorded there). DI + mount wired in `Program.cs` via `AddPdnMcp`/`MapPdnMcp`. SDK: `ModelContextProtocol.AspNetCore` 1.4.0 (forced `Hosting.Abstractions` 10.0.0→10.0.7, same NU1109 reason as the Logging bump). +2 operate-gating tests (Packet.Mcp.Tests 26 green); 793 Node tests green; solution builds clean. **Next:** the `pdn mcp` stdio subcommand + `RestNodeMcpBackend`, then full write-audit (AuthLog) + the monitor-v2 timer-state instrumentation.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 8 MCP: `Packet.Mcp` tool surface + `decode_frame` (build step 1)

First code on the arc. `Packet.Mcp` (was an empty stub) is now the transport-agnostic tool surface: the `INodeMcpBackend` seam (read + write methods), the Packet.Mcp DTOs (`McpPortStatus`/`McpSessionInfo`/`McpMonitorFrame`/`McpLinkQuality`/`McpNetworkTopology` + the write requests/results + `McpCaller`), an `IMcpCallerAccessor` (default `LocalStdioCallerAccessor`) for write-tool audit attribution, and the four MCP `[McpServerToolType]` classes — `DiagnosticTools.decode_frame` (pure), `ReadTools` (list_ports/list_sessions/recent_frames/link_quality/network_topology), `WriteTools` (send_ui_frame/reset_port/disconnect_session/set_kiss_param). **`decode_frame` is fully built + golden-tested**: hex (with separator/0x tolerance) → KISS-unwrap-if-framed → `Ax25Frame.TryParse(Lenient)` → a `DecodedFrame` (addresses with SSID, digi path with the H-bit `*`, command/response, I/S/U class + RR/RNR/REJ/SREJ/SABM/SABME/DISC/UA/DM/FRMR/UI/XID/TEST type off the control octet, P/F, N(R)/N(S), PID + name, info hex + printable text). SDK: official `ModelContextProtocol.Core` 1.4.0 (the node host will take `.AspNetCore` for the SSE mount); both pinned in `Directory.Packages.props`, which forced a bump of `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions` 10.0.0→10.0.7 (the SDK floors it; CPM transitive pinning made the old pin an NU1109 downgrade). New `tests/Packet.Mcp.Tests` (24 green) covers decode goldens + the fake-backed read/write tool wiring incl. caller attribution. Full solution builds clean. **Next build steps:** `LiveNodeMcpBackend` (over `NodeHostedService`, reusing `PdnReadApi`'s projections) → SSE mount (`MapMcp`, `read`-gated) → the `pdn mcp` stdio subcommand + `RestNodeMcpBackend` → write-action wiring + audit → monitor-v2 timer-state instrumentation.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 8 (MCP) kickoff: design agreed, plan/§6 reconciled

Started the MCP endpoint arc ([#173](https://github.com/packet-net/packet.net/issues/173)). Full design in [`docs/mcp-design.md`](mcp-design.md); Phase 8 flipped ⬜ → 🟡; §5.8 rewritten to point at it. Kickoff decisions taken with Tom: **(1) in-process** — `Packet.Mcp` is the transport-agnostic tool surface behind an `INodeMcpBackend` seam; a live in-proc backend (reusing `PdnReadApi`'s projections) serves the SSE transport mounted in the node pipeline, and a loopback-REST backend serves the `pdn mcp` stdio subcommand (resolving the separate-process reality of a subcommand vs shared live state). **(2) Auth = the shipped `read`/`operate`/`admin` model** — read tools `read`, write tools `operate` + audited; pass-through when auth is off, exactly like `/api/v1`. This corrects §6, which once listed a granular `frames:read`/`ports:write`/`mcp:invoke` scheme **that was never built** — the §6 scope line + the MCP-SSE table row are updated to the shipped model. **(3) Full §5.8 surface in one slice** (read + write tools + stdio + SSE), with an internal build order: `decode_frame` (pure, golden-test anchor) → read tools on existing data → SSE mount → stdio bridge → write tools + audit → **monitor-v2 instrumentation last**. Monitor-v2 is the one part needing new code in the AX.25 engine: SRTT/retries aren't derivable from the frame tap (`PdnReadApi.BuildLinks` honestly stubs them to 0), so it surfaces per-session T1-SRTT/retries/T3 to feed `link_quality`, the REST `/links` stubs, and the T1/T3 troubleshoot view — one seam, three consumers. SDK: official `ModelContextProtocol` (+ `.AspNetCore`); the modern Streamable-HTTP transport subsumes the deprecated raw-SSE the §5.8/§6 prose names. No ax25-ts parity leg (MCP adds no parser flag and doesn't widen the `Ax25Listener` surface). The deferred link-tuner stays parked in Phase 8. No code yet — this entry covers the design + plan reconciliation.

### 2026-06-13 — systemd unit grants CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE (standard mail ports for the BBS app)

`packaging/packetnet.service` now sets `AmbientCapabilities=CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE`, so the non-root `packetnet` service (and the pdn-supervised app daemons that inherit its ambient caps) can bind privileged ports (<1024). Driver: the packet-net/pdn-bbs BBS app serves IMAP on **993** and SMTP submission on **465/587** — the standard ports iPhone Mail's port-less "Add Mail Account" flow auto-discovers; without the capability a fresh `.deb` install could only use high ports (1143/etc.), forcing manual per-client port entry. Ambient caps survive `NoNewPrivileges=true` (granted by systemd at exec, not acquired via setuid). Inert unless an app is configured on a privileged port — a node running nothing on <1024 is unaffected. Proven on the lab (root@packetdotnet) as a hand-added drop-in before baking it into the packaged unit; the companion pdn-bbs change defaults its IMAP port to 993 (packet-net/pdn-bbs#28). Packaging-only; no node behavioural/library change, no `Ax25ParseOptions`/quirks flag, no ax25-ts parity obligation.

### 2026-06-13 — CodeQL moved to scheduled-only (weekly) + dispatch, off per-push/PR

Dropped the `push`/`pull_request` triggers from `codeql.yml`; it now runs on the weekly cron (Mon 03:37 UTC) and `workflow_dispatch` only. Even buildless, scanning two languages on every push and PR is more load than a small project wants on the shared self-hosted box; a weekly sweep of main catches regressions + newly-shipped CodeQL queries, and on-demand dispatch covers "scan this now." Trade-off (named): PRs no longer get pre-merge code-scanning feedback. `cancel-in-progress` now unconditional (a manual run supersedes a scheduled one). Nightly is a one-line cron swap if weekly proves too sparse.

### 2026-06-13 — Docker image (ghcr.io/packet-net/packet.net) — the third distribution path

Added a container image for the node host, alongside the `.deb` (apt) and the self-contained tarball. `docker/node/Dockerfile` builds from the self-contained app tree `scripts/build-deb.sh` already stages (`artifacts/deb/<rid>/opt/packetnet/app` — binary + dlls + wwwroot), so the image carries no separate .NET runtime; **debian-slim base** (not `dotnet/runtime-deps` — there's no net10 runtime-deps tag, and self-contained + `InvariantGlobalization` only needs `libc6/libgcc-s1/libstdc++6/libssl3/zlib1g` + `ca-certificates`), unprivileged `packetnet` user, `tini` PID 1, `/etc/packetnet` + `/var/lib/packetnet` volumes, `HEALTHCHECK` on `/healthz`. A baked `packetnet.container.yaml` flips the HTTP bind to `0.0.0.0` (so `-p` reaches it) with a prominent auth-off-exposure warning. **Multi-arch (amd64 + arm64)** via buildx: `scripts/docker-image.sh` publishes the per-arch self-contained trees with **PDN_FAST (no R2R/single-file → no crossgen, so the arm64 build can't OOM)**, stages them as `pdn-app/<arch>/`, and buildx picks per `TARGETARCH`; arm64's `apt-get` runs under QEMU. Published by a standalone **`publish-docker.yml`** (`workflow_dispatch` *and* `node-v*`), decoupled from `publish-node.yml` so the `.deb` release never waits on it and the image can be (re)published for an existing version without cutting a release; the heavy build is `flock`-guarded against concurrent CI; **skips cleanly when `DOCKERHUB_USERNAME`/`DOCKERHUB_TOKEN` aren't set**. **Validated locally (amd64):** `docker run -p 18080:8080 ghcr.io/packet-net/packet.net` → `/healthz` 200, "listening on http://0.0.0.0:8080", clean start. Image name `ghcr.io/packet-net/packet.net` + creds = Tom's call (creds added 2026-06-13); armhf stays `.deb`-only for now.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 7 slice 2b: the self-contained update helper + the channel-agnostic API

Built the heart of the self-contained channel. **`packaging/packetnet-selfupdate`** is the helper the self-contained install ships as `/usr/lib/packetnet/packetnet-update` (the same `packetnet-update.service` unit as apt, different body): fetch `latest.json` from the configured feed → pick this arch's tarball → download → **sha256-verify** (checksum-only; a marked seam for cosign later) → unpack into `releases/<new>` via a temp dir + atomic rename → **flip the `current` symlink** (the unit's ExecStart target) → restart → poll `/healthz` → on failure **flip back to `$prev` and restart** → GC keeping exactly `current`+`$prev`. Parses the manifest without a jq dependency; paths/restart/health are `PDN_*`-overridable so it's testable without systemd. A fixture smoke (`scripts/selfupdate-smoke.sh`, wired into `deb-smoke`) proves happy-path/checksum-mismatch-refused/rollback — and caught a real bug mid-build: GC by mtime deleted the **rollback target** (it's `$prev`, not necessarily the 2nd-newest), fixed to protect `current`+`$prev` explicitly. **API generalized:** `ISystemUpdateLauncher.StartAptUpdateAsync` → `StartUpdateAsync` (channel-agnostic — starts the one oneshot whatever the install's helper does), and `POST /api/v1/system/update` now launches for **both** apt and self-contained with a channel-aware `via`/audit; only `unknown` → 409 (self-contained stopped returning 501). `SystemUpdateApiTests` updated; full Node suite green. **Remaining (slice 2c):** `installers/install.sh` (lay out `releases`/`current` + the self-contained unit + marker + the helper + polkit + `/etc/packetnet/update.conf` + user/dirs + bootstrap), the feed URL in node config, and the available-version check feeding the UI.
### 2026-06-13 — CodeQL reworked to buildless (C# + web TS), dropping the traced Release build

Refined the `codeql.yml` from the entry below before it ever ran. Switched both languages to **`build-mode: none`** (buildless extraction) and matrixed two SHIPPED codebases — `csharp` (the .NET product) and `javascript-typescript` (`web/packetnet-ui`, the served SPA). Buildless C# skips the full Release compile entirely, so the workflow no longer needs the exclusive `flock -x /tmp/pdn-ci-heavy.lock` (it isn't the CPU-heavy compile that lock guards) — much lighter on the contended self-hosted box, at a small completeness cost vs a traced build. Kept `[self-hosted, Linux, X64]`; added `packages: read` (private query packs); bumped the action to `@v4`. The python/rust/c spec dirs stay out of scope (reference artifacts, not product). GitHub's own "Advanced setup" template was declined — it targets `ubuntu-latest`/`macos-latest` (violates the no-hosted-runners rule) and would have clobbered this file. **Correction to the caveat in the entry below:** the repo is *public* (confirmed via the API — `visibility: public`), so code scanning is free + available with no setup; the SARIF upload just works and there is no GHAS/enable-it step (that caveat only applies to private repos).

### 2026-06-13 — CodeQL static analysis (C#) added; WebAuthn ceremony review (no findings)

Two follow-on security tracks after the #405 hardening pass. **(1) CodeQL** — new `.github/workflows/codeql.yml` runs the C# CodeQL analysis on `[self-hosted, Linux, X64]` (zero hosted-runner minutes, per the project rule) on push/PR to main + a weekly cron + dispatch; `build-mode: manual` (CPM + the `.slnx` solution defeat autobuild) with the Release build wrapped in the exclusive `flock -x /tmp/pdn-ci-heavy.lock` like `fuzz.yml`. (Reworked to buildless in the entry above; this manual-build form never ran.) SARIF upload surfaces in the Security > Code scanning tab — the repo is public, so that's free + available with no setup (no GHAS step; that caveat is private-repo-only). **(2) WebAuthn / passkey ceremony review** — read the registration + assertion path (`PdnWebAuthnApi`, `WebAuthnFido2Builder`, `WebAuthnChallengeCache`, the credential store) end to end: **no exploitable findings.** Challenge is single-use (atomic `TryRemove`) + TTL'd off the injected clock + user-bound; RP-id/origins are pinned from config (a spoofed Host can't widen them); sign-count clone-detection is checked and persisted; credential↔user binding is enforced with a constant-time compare and global credential-id uniqueness; everything fails closed behind a single generic 401. Four non-defect observations recorded (UV=`Preferred` vs `Required` is a policy knob; the username-scoped `assert/begin` is an inherent passkey-existence oracle in the non-discoverable flow; an unused store method; clone-vs-generic log classification keys off an exception substring). Full write-up in [`docs/webauthn-review-2026-06-13.md`](webauthn-review-2026-06-13.md); the session's earlier tracks are in [`docs/security-review-2026-06-13.md`](security-review-2026-06-13.md).

### 2026-06-13 — Web auth core security review (no findings) — written up

Read the security-critical web-auth components end to end (`JwtTokenService`, `RefreshTokenService`, `TotpService`, `PasswordHasher`, `ScopeRequirementHandler` / `AuthScopes.Satisfies`, and the `PdnAuthApi` login flow). **No exploitable issues** — the surface is well-built: HS256 with `ValidAlgorithms` pinned (blocks alg-confusion), zero clock skew; opaque hashed one-time-use refresh tokens with reuse-detection; the TOTP strictly-greater replay guard with constant-time compare; Argon2id at OWASP params with fixed-time verify; correct rank-based scope implication with no fail-open; and a login flow that throttles before the Argon2 verify, pays full Argon2 cost on unknown users (no enumeration timing oracle), and returns a generic 401. Two non-bug observations recorded (TOTP high-water-mark persistence is the caller's responsibility; RHP auth is cleartext-by-protocol so network exposure remains the real control). Full write-up plus the whole session's security work in [`docs/security-review-2026-06-13.md`](security-review-2026-06-13.md).

### 2026-06-13 — CI: NuGet vulnerability-scan gate; fuzz PR trigger widened to the new parsers

Added a `dependency-scan` job to `ci.yml` (self-hosted, per the no-hosted-runners rule): `dotnet list package --vulnerable --include-transitive` over `Packet.NET.slnx`, failing the build if any direct or transitive package has a known advisory (detected by the table's `>` row marker, since the command always exits 0). Currently clean. Also widened `fuzz.yml`'s PR `paths` trigger to `src/Packet.Aprs/**`, `src/Packet.Agw/**`, `src/Packet.NetRom/**` so a change to a newly-fuzzed parser gets an immediate smoke signal (the nightly `--smoke` already exercises all nine targets).

### 2026-06-13 — Fuzzer extended to APRS / AGW / NET/ROM; fixed an AGW `Try*`-throws finding

Extended `tools/Packet.Fuzz` past the AX.25/KISS/node-command surface to the three higher-layer wire parsers that also ingest untrusted bytes: the APRS info-field decoders (all public `Aprs*Decoder.TryDecode`, both presets), AGW length-prefixed framing (`AgwFrame.Parse` + `TryReadDataLength`), and the NET/ROM network-layer parsers (`NetRomPacket`/`NetRomNetworkHeader`/`NetRomTransportHeader`/`NodesBroadcast`). Three new smoke targets + AFL subcommands (`aprs`/`agw`/`netrom`), seed corpora, and structured generators. **Found and fixed a real bug:** `AgwFrame.TryReadDataLength` — a `Try*` method documented to return `false` — *threw* `InvalidDataException` when the advertised little-endian data-length field overflowed Int32, and that throw is wire-reachable (`AgwFrameStream`'s read loop calls it un-guarded on every header off the socket → catch-all → the whole AGW stream tears down). A peer sending a header with a length field ≥ `0x7FFFFFDC` could kill the AGW transport (default loopback; `--listen-public` exposes it). Fix: return `false` on the overflow case (honours the `Try*` contract; `AgwFrame.Parse` keeps its one-shot throwing contract); the streaming caller's false branch now gives an honest "unusable length; stream desynced" message. Regression pinned in `AgwFrameTests`; logged in `tools/Packet.Fuzz/FINDINGS.md` (2026-06-13). 50k-iteration smoke across all nine targets is clean. Additive tooling + a localized bug fix — no parser named-flag, no ax25-ts parity impact.

### 2026-06-13 — RHPv2 server hardening: connection cap, per-client handle cap, in-frame read timeout (slowloris)

The RHPv2 TCP front-end (`src/Packet.Rhp2.Server/RhpServer.cs`) accepted unbounded connections, let a single client allocate unbounded handles (`socket`/`open` in a loop), and awaited each frame forever — so a peer that connected and dribbled (or never finished) a frame pinned the connection indefinitely (slowloris). Added three bounds, all configurable via the `rhp:` block and on by default: **`MaxConnections`** (default 64 — the overflow is closed on accept, not absorbed by the OS backlog); **`MaxHandlesPerClient`** (default 256 — a request past it is refused with errCode 4 "No memory"; the reservation is atomic and freed on handle teardown); and **`InFrameTimeout`** (default 30s — bounds how long the *rest* of a frame may take once its first byte arrives, leaving idle-between-frames unbounded so a legitimately-idle multiplexed connection is never dropped). The slowloris fix lives at the framing layer: a new `RhpFraming.ReadFrameAsync(stream, inFrameTimeout, ct)` overload reads the first header byte un-timed, then bounds the remainder, throwing `TimeoutException` on a stall. Config plumbed through `RhpConfig` (`maxConnections`/`maxHandlesPerClient`/`inFrameTimeoutSeconds`, validated) → `RhpServerHostedService`. Tests: `RhpServerHardeningTests` (caps + reservation-free-on-close + per-connection isolation + stall-drop + idle-survives over real TCP) and framing-level timeout tests in `FramingTests`. No parser named-flag and not in the ax25-ts parity inventory (RhpServerOptions isn't mirrored), so no TS leg required.

Also added a **per-source-IP brute-force throttle on the cleartext `auth` message** (`RhpServerOptions.AuthThrottle`), reusing the web panel's `LoginThrottle` (sliding window, success resets, bounded memory): when `requireAuth` is on, a locked-out IP is refused before the password verify is reached — closing the unlimited-guessing gap on a `--listen-public` port. Wired on in `RhpServerHostedService` whenever auth is required; tested in `RhpServerHardeningTests` (lockout short-circuits the verify; a success clears the counter).

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 7 slice 2a: the self-contained channel's release artifacts + the decisions

Started the self-contained update channel by having the release produce what a self-contained node downloads. `publish-node.yml` now, alongside the three `.deb`s, tars each arch's published self-contained tree to **`packetnet_<ver>_<arch>.tar.gz`** (gzip, not zst, so a minimal Pi target extracts with no extra tool), writes a **`latest.json`** manifest (`{ version, artifacts: { amd64|arm64|armhf: { file, sha256 } } }`, hand-rolled to avoid a jq dep), extends **`SHA256SUMS`** to cover the tarballs too, and attaches all of it to the `node-v*` release. Three decisions recorded (design doc + above): **(1) checksum-only** integrity for the first cut — the trust root is the release `SHA256SUMS`/`latest.json` digest; cosign is a planned hardening with a clear verify seam left in the helper. **(2) Migrations stay additive** — a load-bearing rule, because a binary rollback (symlink flip-back) does NOT roll back `pdn.db`; an older binary must tolerate a newer schema (additive columns/tables it ignores, like `revoked_utc`), so we never ship a destructive migration. **(3) Public feed host** — packet.net is private, so a self-contained node can't anonymously pull its release assets; the tarballs + `latest.json` need a public home (operator domain / OARC), the node's feed URL being config — a deployment dependency, flagged, doesn't block the build. Still to come (slice 2b): the node-side helper (download → sha256-verify → unpack `releases/<new>` → flip `current` → restart → `/healthz` rollback), `installers/install.sh`, and the API generalization (self-contained channel stops returning 501 → triggers the helper). Docs/plan + `publish-node.yml` only — no node code yet; the artifacts land on the next `node-v*` tag.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 7 slice 1: the apt-channel in-app update path (API + helper + polkit + tests)

Built the first self-update slice — the apt-channel Apply path end to end (design: [`docs/node-self-update-design.md`](node-self-update-design.md)). **API** (`PdnSystemApi`): `GET /api/v1/system/info` (read scope → `{version, channel, updateMechanism}`) and `POST /api/v1/system/update` (admin scope, audited through `SystemLog`/`Packet.Node.System`) — on the `apt` channel it dispatches the privileged helper and returns **202** (fire-and-acknowledge, since the update restarts the node); `self-contained` → **501** (next slice), `unknown` → **409**, launch failure → **503**. **Seams** in new `src/Packet.Node.Core/SelfUpdate/`: `IInstallChannelProvider` (`FileInstallChannelProvider` reads the build-stamped `/usr/lib/packetnet/install-channel` marker + a `PDN_INSTALL_CHANNEL` test override, fail-safe to `Unknown`) and `ISystemUpdateLauncher` (`SystemctlUpdateLauncher` runs `systemctl start --no-block packetnet-update.service`; `NotSupported` when systemd is absent — never the node running apt itself). NB: the namespace is `Core.SelfUpdate`, not `Core.System` — the latter shadows the BCL `System` namespace and breaks every `System.*` reference in the assembly. **Packaging** (`packaging/` + `build-deb.sh`): the `install-channel` marker (`apt`), the `packetnet-update.service` oneshot (root, no `[Install]` — on-demand only, detached so it survives the restart it causes), the `packetnet-apt-update` helper (`apt-get update` → targeted `apt-get install --only-upgrade -y packetnet` → `is-active` health-gate → `--allow-downgrades` rollback to the prior version), and the `49-packetnet-update.rules` polkit rule (lets the unprivileged `packetnet` user start *only* that one unit); `control.in` gains `polkitd | policykit-1`. **Tests:** `InstallChannelProviderTests` (marker/env/parse/fail-safe) + `SystemUpdateApiTests` (channel branches, 202/501/409/503, admin-gated-when-auth-on, launcher invoked/not). 797 Node tests green (+17); the apt/systemd/polkit mechanism itself is packaging-side (deb-install-smoke / lab). Deferred to later slices: the self-contained channel (cosign + versioned-dir swap), the web UI Apply button, a `/healthz`-deep health gate, the available-version check, the `dpkg-query` channel fallback.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 7 self-update: the three open decisions settled (active apt Apply, channel marker, restart contract)

Tom resolved the three open questions from the self-update design ([`docs/node-self-update-design.md`](node-self-update-design.md)). (1) **The apt channel gets an active Apply button**, not notify-only: the privileged helper runs a *targeted* `apt-get install --only-upgrade -y packetnet` (never a dist-upgrade). Trust on this channel is **apt's repo signature** (hibby's GPG-signed reprepro), not cosign (cosign stays the self-contained channel's trust root) — two channels, two trust roots. Because an active self-upgrade replaces + restarts the very process that triggered it, the node does **not** run apt as a child: it starts the detached **`packetnet-update.service` systemd oneshot** (over D-Bus, authorized by a polkit rule scoped to that one unit for the `packetnet` user), returns immediately, and the web UI polls `/api/system/version` + `/healthz` until the node reappears on the new version (fire-and-acknowledge, not in-band). Rollback = `apt-get install --allow-downgrades packetnet=<prev>` (needs the repo/cache to retain the prior `.deb` — flagged for the maintainer). Apply is admin-scoped + audit-logged. (2) **The package ships the `InstallChannel=apt` marker** (`/usr/lib/packetnet/install-channel`); the `dpkg-query` sniff stays as fallback only. (3) **The self-contained channel matches the packaged unit's `Restart=` contract** so update/restart behaviour is identical across install methods. Design doc updated (apt section rewritten, privilege model = polkit-scoped detached oneshot, "Open decisions" → "Decisions (settled)"); §5.7 + §9 trued up. Still docs/plan only — no code.

### 2026-06-13 — Phase 7 re-scoped: apt repo dropped (maintainer-owned), in-app self-update designed channel-aware

Tom is working with the Debian maintainer (hibby, who already runs + signs the OARC reprepro repo) on distro packaging, so **the apt repo is dropped from packet.net's scope** — our job on the packaged side is just to be a well-behaved `.deb` (never self-mutating) and a polite update notifier. New design note [`docs/node-self-update-design.md`](node-self-update-design.md) works out how the in-app updater (`POST /api/system/update`, Phase 7) dovetails with system package managers without fighting them. Core decision: **one owner per file** — a self-updater that overwrites dpkg-owned files violates Debian Policy, breaks `dpkg -V`, and gets clobbered by the next `apt upgrade`. So the build stamps an **install channel** and `POST /api/system/update` branches on it: `selfcontained` (tarball/installer path) gets a real atomic self-update — versioned `releases/<ver>` dirs + a `current` symlink the systemd unit points at, cosign-verify → unpack alongside → flip symlink → restart, with health-check rollback by flipping back; `apt` gets notify-only (*"run `sudo apt upgrade`"*) or, maintainer-opt-in, a `defer-to-apt` apply (`apt-get install --only-upgrade`), never an in-place overwrite. Both go through an unprivileged-node + small privileged-helper (`packetnet-update` systemd oneshot / polkit) seam; config + state dirs are never in the swap set. §5.7 re-titled "Packaging + self-update" 🟡, the §5 roadmap row + status header + §9 packaging status trued up. Three open decisions flagged for the maintainer (apt-channel apply affordance y/n; ship the channel marker; match the systemd `Restart=` contract). Docs/plan only — no code.

### 2026-06-12 — Release v0.8.0 cascade + a written release procedure (and a runner-OOM incident)

Cut the **v0.8.0** release for the substantive work stacked on `main` since 0.7.0, and — because the cascade keeps getting rediscovered from scratch — wrote it down. New **[`docs/releasing.md`](releasing.md)** is the canonical procedure (tag-driven; preconditions; what each tag publishes; the downstream fan-out; the known interop/OOM flakes), linked from `CLAUDE.md`. The arc: **`lib-v0.8.0`** → `publish-libs.yml` packed the 6 NuGet packages (carries #394 the SREJ window-wrap clamp / silent-corruption fix, and the library half of #390 TX-complete→T1: `Ax25Listener.RestartT1OnTxComplete` + `PacingKissModem` queue-routing). **`node-v0.8.0`** → `publish-node.yml` built the amd64/arm64/armhf `.deb` GitHub Release (the whole node arc since 0.7.0: #399 auth refresh-rotation fix, #396 over-N1 byte-stream chunking to v2.0 peers, #390 `kiss.t1FromTxComplete`). **Downstream .NET**: `axcall` and `packet-term-tui` bumped their four `Packet.*` pins 0.7.0→0.8.0 (axcall#10 / packet-term-tui#15, built+tested green against the published NuGet — additive, no break) and both released **v0.2.10** (six-platform binaries). **TS leg**: `ax25-ts` had three parity legs unreleased since 0.12.0 — `ax25Spec13ClampSrejWindowToHalfModulus` (#62, mirrors #394), `ax25Spec9AckProgressResetsRc` (#60), and the recorded `RestartT1OnTxComplete` exception (#61) — promoted to **`@packet-net/ax25` 0.13.0** (ax25-ts#63, 1193 tests green) → npm, and `packet-term-web`'s esm.sh pin bumped to match.

**Incident worth recording:** the `node-v0.8.0` `.deb` build was *cancelled* (not failed) twice — the crossgen2 R2R arm cross-publish is memory-hungry and, run while the interop job + three downstream CIs were also queued, OOM'd the constrained runner LXC; the kernel OOM-killer took the **runner agents** down with it (all of `pdn-runner-1..4` + the downstream repos' runners went offline ~45 min until a host reboot + manual `actions.runner.*` service start). Lesson (now in `docs/releasing.md`): serialize the `.deb` cross-build away from other heavy jobs — fire `node-v*` when the runner box is quiet. Also corrected here: the §5 status lines that still called **#142** (the C=Response-SABM strict guard) a "residual follow-up" — it shipped in the 2026-06-10 sweep (`Ax25ParseOptions.AllowCommandFrameAsResponse`) and released in lib-v0.7.0; there is no residual.

### 2026-06-12 — Web auth: stop the refresh-token rotation race logging the sysop out every ~hour (server reuse-leeway + cross-tab client lock)

The pdn web UI was forcing a fresh login roughly every access-token lifetime (~60 min). The node's own auth audit (`Packet.Node.Auth`) was the smoking gun: **every** silent `/auth/refresh` (`refresh OK`) was paired, in the same second, with a `refresh REUSE-DETECTED — revoking token family` and then a re-`login OK` — 6 family-burns in 7 days, 35 logins, the silent renewal never once keeping the session alive. Root cause: the refresh token is one-time-use *rotating*; when the access token expires the client silently rotates it, but two requests present the same token near-simultaneously (parallel dashboard calls / a second tab / a focus-refetch). The server rotated the first and treated the second — a now-consumed token — as token theft, whose response is to revoke the entire family → logout. The 30-day `refreshTokenMinutes` was irrelevant; the session died on the first renewal. Fixed on both sides (the user asked for belt-and-braces): **(server, `Packet.Node.Core`)** `RefreshTokenService` gains a reuse-leeway window (`DefaultReuseLeeway` = 10 s, constructor-injectable; `TimeSpan.Zero` = strict): a replay of a token rotation-consumed within the leeway, *while its family is still alive*, is taken as the legitimate client racing itself — it mints a fresh successor instead of burning the family. Outside the window, or against a logged-out / already-burned family, it is still the theft response. New `RefreshTokenRecord.RevokedUtc` (only the rotation consume stamps it; logout/expiry hard-revoke leaves it null = not leeway-eligible) + `IRefreshTokenStore.HasLiveToken` (guards the grace path so it can extend a live session but never resurrect a dead one); `Revoke` now carries the consume time; SQLite store gains a `revoked_utc` column with an in-place `ALTER TABLE` migration for upgraded nodes. **(client, `web/packetnet-ui`)** `lib/api.ts` serialises rotation across same-origin tabs with the Web Locks API (`pdn-auth-refresh`) and, after taking the lock, re-reads the token so a tab that lost the race adopts the winner's rotated token from localStorage instead of replaying the consumed one. Not an AX.25-library change → no `Ax25SessionQuirks`/ax25-ts parity obligation. Tests: `RefreshTokenServiceTests` (+benign-concurrent-replay, reuse-after-leeway-burns, no-resurrect-logged-out-family, zero-leeway-strict, negative-leeway-rejected), `SqliteRefreshTokenStoreTests` (+stamp/HasLiveToken/pre-leeway-DB migration), `AuthApiTests` integration flipped to assert the tolerated concurrent replay; full Node suite (776) + web build/vitest (22) green.

### 2026-06-12 — LinkBench: auto-scale the default T1V at `--baud 0`, and the zero-airtime "T1-storm" diagnosis

Closed the dig into the zero-airtime timer pathology the bench exposed, and it is **not an engine bug**. The "12 T1 expiries at one instant → DM death" symptom I'd previously described is no longer reproducible on main — that was the ax25spec#9 RC-ratchet-to-N2 → spurious DM, already fixed by `Ax25Spec9AckProgressResetsRc`. Re-investigated empirically against the real engine: (1) the "T1V collapses via the Select_T1 SRT IIR" hypothesis is **false** — `Srt`/`T1V` do not decay on clean transfers (the IIR doesn't sample on the piggybacked-ack path). (2) What `--baud 0` + loss still shows is purely a **T1V↔airtime mismatch**: the instant-delivery channel pairs with the 6 s spec-default T1V (sized for real ~1200-baud airtime + turnaround), so recovery latency = losses × T1V and any loss-heavy run times out paying a full 6 s per dropped frame/ack — expiries exactly one T1V apart (6.037 s, 12.039 s), a slow crawl, no clustering. At seed 7 / 30 % loss / 8 KB: k=1, k=4, k=7 all time out at T1V=6000 (2/25/54 retx), but k=1 at T1V=400/500 completes. The engine (pipelining, go-back-N, SREJ recovery) is fine; the bench was measuring the timer:airtime ratio. **Fix is bench-side ergonomics only:** when `--baud 0` (the default rung-1 channel) and no explicit `--t1` is given, LinkBench now auto-scales the default T1V to 500 ms (T2 to 250 ms) and prints a one-line notice; an explicit `--t1`/`--t2` is always respected, and modelled channels (`--baud N`, `--time-scale`) keep the real spec defaults. No engine/SDL/`Ax25SessionQuirks`/parity change. Verified: the previously-timing-out k=1/30 %/8 KB run now completes intact with a clean DISC; lossless rung-1 unaffected (still 3 MB/s). `tools/Packet.LinkBench` `RunConfig`/`Cli` + README/`--help`.

### 2026-06-12 — #214 loss recovery: confirmed code-done + committed a deterministic bulk-transfer-under-loss regression

Took #214 (the 10 kB-transfer-under-0–30 %-scripted-loss criterion) and found there is **no engine bug to fix** — both upstream blockers are already worked around in the current engine: ax25sdl#43 (`Enquiry_Response` F:=1) is wired via the `Enquiry_Response_F_1` subroutine alias (`SubroutineRegistry` sets `tx.Pending.PfBit = true`; a frame-by-frame trace confirms the responder replies `RR…F=1`), and ax25sdl#44 (`Invoke_Retransmission` multi-frame replay) works (go-back-N re-emits the whole window). `tools/Packet.LinkBench`'s `--loss`/`--srej` sweep over a modelled-airtime channel completes across the **full 0–30 % loss range, k=1–7, go-back-N and SREJ**. Distilled to a CI-deterministic regression: `DataLinkSrejUnderLossTests.Bulk_transfer_survives_scripted_loss_across_ring_wraps` ([Theory] over go-back-N/SREJ × k=2/4) drives a 24-frame transfer (3 mod-8 ring cycles) dropping the first transmission of every third frame, T1-paced, asserting every byte delivered exactly once in order across the V(R) wrap + the link ends Connected + fully acked. **Correction:** the k=1 stop-and-wait "stall" I'd earlier attributed to #214 is a **zero-airtime (`baud=0`) bench artifact** — instant delivery + real-clock T1 produces a T1-expiry storm racing RC→N2; it vanishes with any modelled airtime (k=1, 5–10 % loss: clean). #214 stays open only for the literal on-hardware bench run (un-skip `HardwareLoop10KBTransfer`); the §5.2 criterion note is refreshed. 934 Ax25 tests green (+4).
### 2026-06-12 — RHP↔AX.25 bridge frames an over-N1 byte-stream write into multiple I-frames on a v2.0 link (was: errCode 17, mail lost)

The live B2/multi-frame forwarding failure to the real GB7RDG (LinBPQ) was *not* a timing/ackmode/half-duplex problem — it was the bridge. `Ax25NodeConnection.WriteAsync` (the RHP↔AX.25 byte-stream sink) handed the whole application write straight to `Ax25Listener.SendData`. `SendData`→`SegmentationLayer.BuildSendRequests` treats its input as one SDU and (correctly, per the existing `Ax25ListenerSegmentationTests`) throws `InvalidOperationException` when the payload exceeds N1 and the v2.2 segmenter was never negotiated — which is every v2.0 peer (LinBPQ connects with plain SABM, no XID). `WriteAsync` caught only `ArgumentException`/`ObjectDisposedException`, so the exception escaped, surfaced from `RhpServer.HandleSendAsync` as errCode 17 "Not connected", and the BBS aborted the cycle and DISC'd — so any forwarded body, console reply or NET/ROM payload over ~256 bytes (e.g. a B2 object, or a long bulletin) was silently undeliverable to a v2.0 station. Diagnosed by decoding a lab pcap frame-by-frame: after the peer's `FS`, pdn sent `DISC` instead of any body I-frame; the 755-byte body produced **zero** frames on the wire. A connected-mode link is a byte stream — the data link frames it into N1-sized I-frames; v2.2 segmentation (PID 0x08) is a *different* feature for preserving a single oversized SDU's boundaries and is rightly XID-gated. The bug was the missing plain-chunking path between the two. **Fix:** when the segmenter isn't negotiated, `WriteAsync` slices the write into ≤N1 ordinary I-frames (`session.Context.N1`, PID 0xF0) preserving byte order; a negotiated v2.2 link still passes the whole buffer through to produce PID-0x08 segments. Node-host bridge only — the SDL/engine and `SegmentationLayer` are untouched and already correct, so **no `Ax25SessionQuirks`/`Ax25ParseOptions` flag and no ax25-ts parity obligation**, no config change. Test: `Ax25NodeConnectionSegmentationTests` (red→green — a 755-byte write becomes 3 ordered N(S)=0,1,2 I-frames reassembling to the input; a within-N1 write stays one I-frame). Full suite green. The matching pdn-bbs data-loss guard (don't mark a message Forwarded until its body is actually sent) shipped separately as m0lte/packet.net-bbs#8.
### 2026-06-12 — SREJ window-wrap fix: clamp k ≤ modulus/2 under Selective Repeat (ax25spec#13 quirk) — was silent payload corruption

`tools/Packet.LinkBench`'s SREJ sweep (the `--srej` flag, #391) found **silent payload corruption** under Selective Repeat: a bulk transfer with SREJ enabled, a large send window, and frame loss could complete with the exact right byte count but the data scrambled — the receiver delivering a stale stored I-frame from the previous ring cycle (one with the same N(S) exactly `modulus` frames back) in place of the live frame. The integrity diagnostic (#392) pinned the pattern (every wrong position p gets frame p−modulus's data) and a window-threshold sweep located the boundary exactly at the textbook Selective-Repeat limit **k ≤ modulus/2**: at mod-8, 5% loss, corruption appears at k≥5 and is absent at k≤4 (#393). Root cause: AX.25 lets k range to modulus−1 (fine for go-back-N, which buffers no out-of-order frames), the figures never enforce the tighter `2·W ≤ modulus` bound Selective Repeat needs, and `StoredReceivedIFrames` keys recovery state by the bare N(S) — so above mod/2 two in-flight frames can share an N(S) and collide. This is [packethacking/ax25spec#13](https://github.com/packethacking/ax25spec/issues/13).

Fix: new `Ax25SessionContext.EffectiveWindow` caps the enforced window (both the send-side `v_s_eq_v_a_plus_k` / `CanTransmitIFrame` outstanding-frame check **and** the receive-side #40 out-of-window-discard acceptance bound) at `modulus/2` whenever `SrejEnabled` is set — capping only one side was insufficient (the receiver still accepted into an ambiguous window-7 buffer; both ends must be bounded). Gated by the new `Ax25SessionQuirks.Ax25Spec13ClampSrejWindowToHalfModulus` (default on; off under `StrictlyFaithful`, reproducing the unsafe figure-literal behaviour for conformance study). A configured k above the cap is run at the safe window for the duration SREJ is in effect; the configured value is untouched and applies again on a go-back-N link. **Go-back-N (SREJ off) is never capped** — it legitimately tolerates k up to modulus−1; the mod-8 default k=4 = modulus/2 is exactly at the limit and unchanged. Verified: post-fix 0/10 corrupted at k=5/6/7 (was 2/8, 3/8, 1/8); SREJ still recovers selectively at the capped window; go-back-N k=7 unaffected; 930 Packet.Ax25 tests green (+10 `Ax25Spec13SrejWindowClampTests`). **Parity:** new `Ax25SessionQuirks` flag — the ax25-ts leg ships alongside.

### 2026-06-12 — TX-complete→T1: kiss.t1FromTxComplete runs T1 from the frame's ACKMODE TX-completion echo, not from enqueue
