The SDL figures start T1 the instant a frame is "sent" — which behind a buffering TNC means enqueued, so on a slow channel T1 absorbs the sender's own TX queue + airtime before it even starts bounding the peer's response: a k=4 window of 256-byte frames is ~8.5 s of air against the 6 s default T1V, and LinkBench rung 1b/2 measured that configuration self-destructing (130+ retransmits, duplicate-supervisory bursts, transfers that never complete). The new wiring closes the gap end-to-end: `Ax25ListenerOptions.RestartT1OnTxComplete` (default off) makes the listener transmit every T1-arming frame (I-frame, P=1 enquiry, SABM/SABME/DISC — never responses/acks) via `IKissModem.SendFrameWithAckAsync`, and when the TNC's 0x0C TX-completion echo lands it re-arms a still-running T1 to (now + T1V) via the new `ITimerScheduler.RearmIfRunning` (atomic against the SDL stopping T1, so an ack racing the echo can never resurrect a stopped watchdog; default-interface no-op so test schedulers don't care). The SDL itself is untouched — Start T1 still runs where the figures draw it; the seam only pushes the deadline out using a fact the figures never had. Echo loss leaves T1 as armed; a non-ACKMODE modem latches back to plain sends after one `NotSupportedException`.

`PacingKissModem.SendFrameWithAckAsync` is reworked to route THROUGH the pacing queue (previously a documented bypass nobody used): the frame transmits in strict arrival order with the port's fire-and-forget traffic and the caller's receipt resolves when its own echo lands — pacing and T1-wiring compose on one port. Config: per-port `kiss.t1FromTxComplete` (default false), construction-time like `ackMode` (ReconcilePlanner classifies a toggle as a port restart), threaded through KissParams/YAML/template/ChannelProfiles/PortSupervisor. Bench: `--t1-tx-complete on,off` sweep dimension. Measured effect (rung 1b, modeled 1200 baud half-duplex, DEFAULT T1V=6 s): k=4 and k=7 go from catastrophic failure to 0 retransmits / 0 dups / clean completion at full throughput — the k×airtime>T1 failure class is structurally gone rather than tuned around. Tests: scheduler re-arm semantics, pacing queue-routing + per-caller timeout faulting, listener deadline-extension / default-unchanged / graceful-degradation (FakeTimeProvider + a gated-echo LoopbackModem), config round-trip + planner classification. Full suite green. **Parity:** `RestartT1OnTxComplete` is a new `Ax25ListenerOptions` member — recorded as a reviewed exception in ax25-ts `parity-exceptions.json` (the echo is a TNC/node-host transport capability; no TS transport speaks ACKMODE yet).

### 2026-06-12 — Sustained bulk transfers over net-sim survive: RC-ratchet figure defect fixed (ax25spec#9 quirk), rung-2 bench results land

Rung 2 of the link bench (real net-sim AFSK1200, the pinned ackmode image from `docs/link-bench-plan.md` §7, scratch instance) found and fixed the sustained-transfer killer: 32 KiB bulk transfers died mid-transfer (~19 KiB, near-deterministically) with the sender spontaneously emitting DM while the receiver was still acking. The bench's `--trace` + transition journal pinned the path: under sustained load the sender enters TimerRecovery and never exits (V(S)≠V(A) at every F=1 checkpoint keeps it on the stay-branch), and that branch never resets RC — so ten T1 hiccups across 200 s of *progressing* transfer ratchet RC to N2 → `t21_t1_expiry_yes_no` → DL-ERROR I → DM → dead session. This is exactly the already-filed **packethacking/ax25spec#9** (the figures only reset RC on the fully-acked checkpoint; rax25 ships the equivalent fix). New session quirk **`Ax25Spec9AckProgressResetsRc`** (default on, off under `StrictlyFaithful`, house figure-defect conventions): a T1 expiry that follows V(A)-advancing progress clamps RC to 1 before the `RC = N2?` guard, so RC counts *consecutive* recovery failures and a genuinely dead link still exhausts N2. The clamp is deliberately at-expiry rather than zeroing RC eagerly at ack time: RC==0 doubles as `Select_T1`'s Karn sampling branch signal, and an eager zero fed retransmit-polluted samples into SRT/T1V (it measurably wedged the SREJ-under-loss suite mid-development). Tests: `Ax25Spec9RcResetQuirkTests` (4 paired tests incl. dead-link-still-dies and StrictlyFaithful-as-drawn); full suite green. **Parity:** new named flag on `Ax25SessionQuirks` — needs the ax25-ts leg (or a recorded parity exception) before interop CI passes.

With the quirk, **the goal scenario demonstrably works: 32 KiB sustained bulk transfer over net-sim with ackmode, intact payload, clean DISC, zero duplicate supervisories**, surviving 42 retransmit hiccups that pre-fix killed the session at the 10th. Other rung-2 findings (full numbers in `docs/link-bench-plan.md` §13): net-sim's 0x0C echo is **post-transmission** (mean RTT ~2 s ≈ real airtime — settling §7's open question: `PacingKissModem` genuinely paces, it is not a no-op); engine-default T1=6 s is broken at every k on AFSK1200 (33–64 % retransmit waste); the clean operating envelope is **k=2–3 with T1≈15–20 s** (~80 B/s, zero retransmits); above k≈3 the receiver's T2-delayed RR collides with the sender's window tail regardless of T1 — the carrier-sense (DCD, plan §8) gap quantified. The bench also grew `--trace` (merged frame + DL-signal + SDL-transition journal, the thing that made this diagnosable) and per-run SSIDs (frames draining through the slow channel from run N can't poison run N+1's handshake).

### 2026-06-11 — Ax25Session.PostEvent made thread-safe; AX.25 link bench (tools/Packet.LinkBench) lands and finds it

`Ax25Session.PostEvent`'s run-to-completion machinery (a plain `bool dispatching` + `Queue<Ax25Event> deferredEvents`) was only safe against *same-thread re-entrant* posts (the #327 case). But production posters genuinely race: the listener's inbound pump posts frame events, `SystemTimerScheduler` fires T1/T2/T3 expiry callbacks on TimeProvider timer threads, and upper-layer callers (`ConnectAsync` / `SendData`) post from whatever thread the app uses. Two threads could both observe `dispatching == false` and dispatch concurrently (corrupting `ctx.IFrameQueue` and `CurrentTrigger`), or one thread's deferred enqueue could race the dispatching thread's `finally { deferredEvents.Clear(); }` — a **silently lost event**. Fix: the whole dispatch loop now runs under a reentrant `lock (dispatchGate)` — same-thread re-entrant posts still fall through to the deferred queue exactly as before (Monitor is reentrant), only genuinely concurrent posters serialise. No SDL change, no options-surface change (parity unaffected); lock-order safety verified against `SystemTimerScheduler` (it invokes `onExpiry` outside its own gate). Regression tests: `PostEventConcurrencyTests` (hammer tests that crash inside `Queue<T>.Enqueue` and lose I-frames pre-fix, verified red-then-green). ax25-ts is single-threaded on the JS event loop, so no TS leg is needed.

Found by the new **AX.25 connected-mode link bench**, `tools/Packet.LinkBench` (plan: `docs/link-bench-plan.md`, rung-1 results in its §12): two `Ax25Listener` engines in one process over a pluggable channel (`InProcChannel` with modeled airtime/half-duplex/loss/ackmode-echo + a designed-but-unwired DCD seam per the coming KISS DCD extension; `AxudpChannel` UDP-loopback cross-check; `NetSimChannel` for rung 2), bulk connected-mode transfer with integrity check, sweepable k/T1/T2/paclen/ackmode/loss, and a per-run metrics table (throughput, retransmits, RR/REJ/SREJ split, #79 duplicate-supervisory count, window-stall time, ackmode echo RTTs). Pre-fix, 64 KiB ackmode-off bulk transfers stalled mid-transfer or crashed in `PostEvent` within seconds — the same instability class the lab chased for weeks, reproduced in milliseconds. Headline rung-1 results: lossless engine↔engine bulk transfer is clean (zero dups → #79 is **not** engine-intrinsic under clean conditions); throughput with default T2=3 s is T2-bound (~347 B/s on an *infinite-speed* channel); at modeled 1200 baud half-duplex, **k≥4 with default T1V=6 s self-destructs** (T1 starts at enqueue and expires mid-window — a k=4 window is ~8.5 s of airtime) and ackmode pacing alone does not fix it, confirming the §10 caveat that pacing without TX-complete→T1 wiring is not enough.

### 2026-06-11 — KISS ACKMODE host-side pacing: a kiss-tcp port can serialise its own transmissions onto the half-duplex channel (default-off)

The lab node blasts its outbound frames fire-and-forget — the `Ax25Listener` send sink (`Ax25Listener.cs:922`) discards the modem `SendFrameAsync` Task and returns immediately. On a shared half-duplex medium (net-sim's software-RF channel, or a real radio) that means the node can pile several of its own frames onto the air at once and collide with itself. The transport half of this feature (already on `feat/kiss-tcp-ackmode`) gave `KissTcpClient` a `SendFrameWithAckAsync` that sends a frame in the G8BPQ ACKMODE wrapper (KISS command `0x0C` + a 2-byte sequence tag) and awaits the TNC's TX-completion echo, returning an `AckModeReceipt` (or throwing `TimeoutException`); net-sim echoes that `0x0C` transparently, so the echo means "this frame has cleared the channel". This entry adds the host-side pacing that *uses* it.

**`PacingKissModem`** (`src/Packet.Node.Core/Transports/`) is a new `IKissModem` decorator: `SendFrameAsync` snapshots the caller's (reusable) buffer and drops it on an unbounded `Channel`, returning `Task.CompletedTask` — the fire-and-forget contract the SDL frame sinks depend on is preserved exactly. A single background pump drains the channel and sends each frame via the inner modem's `SendFrameWithAckAsync`, **awaiting the prior frame's TX-completion echo before pulling the next** — that await *is* the pacing, serialising the node's transmissions in order. A `TimeoutException` (echo never came in the pacing window, default 5 s — enough for a max-size frame to clear 1200 baud) or any other send fault is logged and the pump moves on, so one frame can never wedge the port; AX.25 T1 retransmits anything genuinely lost. `ReadFramesAsync` + the four `Set*Async` KISS setters + the explicit `SendFrameWithAckAsync` pass straight through to the inner. The decorator owns the inner modem and disposes it once (the standard chain, matching `ReconnectingKissModem`); `RunningPort.DisposeAsync` tears the whole wrap down.

**Config + wiring.** A new per-port `kiss.ackMode` bool (`KissParams.AckMode`, default `false`) threads through every layer `txTail` touches — the config record, the camel-case YAML read/write path, the System.Text.Json config-write binding (no custom DTO — it binds `NodeConfig` directly), `ChannelProfiles.OverlayKiss` (no profile sets it; the explicit per-port choice carries through), the first-start template comment, and the web TS client's `KissParams` type. `PortSupervisor.BringUpAsync` wraps the modem in `PacingKissModem` (after the `ReconnectingKissModem` wrap, before the `Ax25Listener` gets it) when the effective `ackMode` is on, unwrapped when off — so the listener is unchanged and paces transparently. Unlike the other KISS knobs `ackMode` is a *construction-time* choice (whether to wrap), not a live modem setting, so `ReconcilePlanner` classifies a toggle as a single-port **restart** (alongside transport/profile changes) rather than a no-op live KISS-apply — the change actually takes effect. Default-off preserves today's fire-and-forget behaviour byte-for-byte.

**Scope.** Host/transport + config layer only — no SDL state machine, no `Ax25ParseOptions`/`Ax25SessionQuirks`/`XidParseOptions`, no `Ax25Listener` options-surface change, so the ax25-ts parity check is untouched. Tests: pacing/serialisation (a gated fake inner proves frame N+1 starts only after frame N's ack is signalled, in order), timeout-resilience (a `TimeoutException` on one frame doesn't stall the next), pass-through (reads + setters reach inner), inner-disposal, plus config round-trip (`ackMode: true` parses + survives serialise→parse; absent → false) and the planner restart classification. 767 Node tests green (+9); full non-hardware/non-interop suite green.

### 2026-06-11 — T2 coalesced acks: the §6.7.1.2 acknowledge delay defers the LM-SEIZE grant — ends ack-per-frame and the stale-F=1 checkpoint rollback that killed sustained inbound transfers (#385)

Off-air capture (merged dual-listener) of the lab node receiving a sustained I-frame stream from a real LinBPQ at 1200 baud half duplex: the receive path emitted **one RR keyup per received I-frame** (five frames → five RRs at ~120 ms spacing), that TX occupancy left the half-duplex port deaf/queued while the peer streamed its next window, the peer's checkpoint poll (RR P=1) got an **F=1 answer carrying a stale V(R)**, and BPQ — treating the F=1 as authoritative — rolled back and retransmitted the whole window. Loop until retries exhausted → DISC; sessions died 60–90 s in, every time.

**Root cause.** The #327 fix grants LM-SEIZE *immediately* at both production construction sites (`Ax25Listener.BuildSession`, `Ax25Adapter`), and figc4.4 t23 clears Ack-Pending when the Enquiry-Response RR flushes — so every in-sequence I-frame finds ack-not-pending again and re-runs the seize→confirm→RR cycle. The v2.2 **prose** §6.7.1.2 describes exactly the missing piece — the T2 acknowledge delay, letting the TNC wait briefly for an outgoing frame to piggy-back (or coalesce) the acknowledgement — but the **figures** ack immediately and have no Start/Stop T2 verb (the #292 finding), and `ctx.T2` (default 3000 ms, per-port `T2Ms`) armed nothing.

**Fix — same documented-deviation pattern as #327/#292, runtime layer only, no SDL change.** (1) The construction-site grant is now **deferred by `ctx.T2`**: the first in-sequence I-frame of a burst arms a "T2" timer on the session scheduler (t26 requests the seize only while no ack is pending, so a running delay is never re-armed and the ack fires T2 after the *first* unacknowledged frame); the confirm at expiry emits **one cumulative RR** — `Enquiry_Response` reads `N(r) := V(r)` at confirm-dispatch time, so the ack always covers everything received. (2) The dispatcher's `ClearAcknowledgePending` verb now also **cancels the armed T2** — that verb is exactly the SDL's own "ack satisfied or superseded" chokepoint, run by every N(R)-bearing emission chain (the I-command piggyback paths, RR/RNR/REJ, the enquiry/poll responses, `Transmit_Enquiry`, `Clear_Exception_Conditions` on reset), so no redundant or stale-N(R) ack can follow; the SREJ chains deliberately do NOT run it (an SREJ's N(R) is not a cumulative ack in the figures' bookkeeping), so the delayed cumulative RR still flushes after an SREJ and the pinned REJ/SREJ recovery paths are untouched. (3) Poll integrity needed no engine change — `PostEvent` is run-to-completion and the F=1 `Enquiry_Response` reads live `ctx.VR`, so with the dribble gone the checkpoint answer is always current; the regression test pins it. **`T2Ms=0` restores ack-per-frame** (the figures-as-drawn wire shape); `NodeConfigValidator` now accepts 0. No new quirk/flag/listener option (ax25-ts parity inventory unchanged — the TS port should mirror the behaviour as its own #385 follow-up).

**Named deferral — no k/2 early-flush threshold.** Considered acking early once ≥k/2 frames are unacked so a saturating sender isn't ack-starved; deferred because the de-facto cohort (Linux ax25, direwolf, BPQ's RESPTIME) is pure-T2, the spec's own backstop already covers the stall (the sender's T1 poll is answered F=1 with current V(R) immediately), the receiver only knows its *own* k (not the sender's), and the threshold would re-add keyups in exactly the high-load regime the fix targets. Revisit with lab evidence if window-stall dead-air shows up; `T2Ms` is the per-port lever meanwhile (keep T2 well under the peer's FRACK/T1).

**Tests.** New `Ax25ListenerT2CoalescedAckTests` (red-first against main: 3/4 fail pre-fix): the marquee **burst-then-poll** transcript pin (5 I-frames + RR P=1 → wire is exactly [UA, RR F=1 N(R)=5], nothing before it, nothing after T2 expiry), 5-frame coalescing → ONE cumulative RR after T2 (FakeTimeProvider) + clean re-arm for the next burst, outgoing-I-frame piggyback cancels the pending delayed ack, and T2=0 → legacy per-frame. The #327 suites (`Ax25ListenerDelayedAckTests`, the adapter mirror in `Ax25AdapterTests`) updated to the coalesced model — same ack-always-reaches-the-wire guarantees, now asserted around the virtual clock, with the bounded-RR-count assertion tightened to exactly one. Conformance/V-series/SREJ suites untouched and green (the TwoStationHarness wires its own link-mux confirm, so the SDL-level regression net is independent of the construction-site grant policy). 909 Ax25 tests green; full default-filter suite green (Node 725, NetRom 276, Rhp2 184, properties 84, all others).
### 2026-06-11 — Persistent traffic log: every frame, every port, into a separate traffic.db

pdn now logs ALL of its traffic — every AX.25 frame sent or received, on every port — into its own SQLite database for troubleshooting, on by default. Motivation: diagnosing a live session-drop meant hand-building off-air KISS listeners because the node kept no durable frame history (the web monitor's ring is 250 frames, in-memory, gone on restart). Design: a new `traffic:` config block (`enabled` default TRUE, `path` default `traffic.db` beside pdn.db — deliberately a SEPARATE database so a huge or corrupt frame log can never threaten node state; `retentionDays` default 14; `maxMb` default 512 as a hard size cap). The writer (`TrafficLogService` in `Packet.Node.Core/Traffic/`) subscribes to the existing `NodeTelemetry` frame-trace stream — the same tap the SSE monitor rides, no second decode path — through a bounded hand-off queue: on overflow (a slow/stalled disk) traffic-log rows are dropped and counted (`droppedFrames`, surfaced in `GET /api/v1/status`'s new `traffic` block and in logs), so the radio path can never be back-pressured by the log. Batched transactional inserts into `SqliteTrafficStore` (WAL + incremental auto-vacuum, schema-on-open, degrade-on-fault — the house store discipline); one row per frame: UTC timestamp, port, rx/tx, source/dest, frame kind, N(S)/N(R)/P-F, control octet, PID, info length, and the raw frame bytes BLOB capped at 2 KB/frame, indexed on (ts) and (port, ts). A TimeProvider-driven prune (5-min cadence + once at startup) enforces retention + the size cap, re-reading `retentionDays`/`maxMb` from the live config each pass (a hot edit); `enabled`/`path` are startup-bound — restart-applies, documented in the config template. `MonitorEvent` gained additive `control`/`infoLength` fields (set by the factory — keeping the single-decode-path rule) so the log persists them without re-deriving offsets. Read surface: `GET /api/v1/traffic?port=&since=&until=&limit=` (read-gated, newest-first, default 250 / max 1000, empty when disabled) — documented in `docs/node-api.yaml`; no web-UI work in this slice. Packaging needs no change: the default path lands in the same StateDirectory (`/var/lib/packetnet`) postinst already creates. Tests: store round-trip incl. nullable I/S/U shapes + reopen durability, prune-by-age, prune-to-size (proportional oldest-first deletes + incremental vacuum make the cap a real bound), bounded-queue overflow (drops counted, never blocks), telemetry→store end-to-end, config validation + YAML/template round-trip (FsCheck arbitraries extended), and a WAF endpoint-shape suite (`TrafficApiTests`).

### 2026-06-11 — DAPPS bundle re-pinned to v0.34.0 — manifest now fetched from the release

DAPPS bundle re-pinned to v0.34.0 — manifest now fetched from the release (the interim copy deleted, completing the 5b design); the release carries `DAPPS_ENV_MANAGED` + the node-derived callsign with SSID probe-walk, so enabling dapps on a pdn node is now zero-config. Mechanics: packet-net/dapps v0.34.0 is the first release shipping `pdn-app.yaml` as a release asset (version-stamped to the tag — the packet-net/dapps#148 upstreaming landing), so build-deb.sh now pins FOUR assets (the three per-RID binaries + the manifest, each sha256-pinned) and fetches the manifest through the same version-keyed cache/verify/skip machinery as the binaries (`dapps_fetch`: a fresh download failing its pin hard-fails; unreachable with no valid cache warns and skips the dapps package entirely — all-or-nothing, never a binary without its manifest or vice versa); `packaging/dapps/pdn-app.yaml` is deleted as the 5b design always intended. `ShippedManifestsTests` re-pointed at the cached release asset (`artifacts/cache/dapps-v<pin>-pdn-app.yaml`, the path derived from the script pin so the cache key always tracks it): the dapps tests are now `[SkippableFact]`s (skip with "run scripts/build-deb.sh first" when the cache is absent), assert version 0.34.0 + the new `DAPPS_ENV_MANAGED=true` deployment-managed env mode, keep the manifest↔script version-drift guard, and the catalog test split into an unconditional examples/ (wall+lobby) leg + a skippable full-shipped-layout leg staging the cached manifest under a temp `dapps/` dir. Zero-config story: `DAPPS_ENV_MANAGED` makes the manifest's env deployment-managed (re-applied every start, badged read-only in the dashboard), and DAPPS derives its identity from the injected `PDN_NODE_CALLSIGN` as `<nodecall>-7`, probe-walking to the next free SSID on a duplicate-socket refusal — no more must-set-`DAPPS_CALLSIGN`-before-first-enable wrinkle (an explicit `DAPPS_CALLSIGN` override still wins). Deb 73.3 MB; 729 Node tests green (9 ShippedManifests).

### 2026-06-11 — DAPPS bundle re-pinned to v0.34.1 (gateway-proxied dashboard)

packet-net/dapps v0.34.1 honors `X-Forwarded-Prefix` (PathBase middleware + a ~60-site URL audit, packet-net/dapps#150) so its dashboard works behind pdn's app gateway instead of escaping the `/apps/dapps/` mount on every redirect/link/fetch; standalone access proven byte-identical. Four sha pins bumped; fresh-cache build verified; ShippedManifests tracks 0.34.1.

### 2026-06-11 — Panel: sessions survive browser restarts; gateway: X-Forwarded-Prefix for proxied apps

Two operator-experience fixes from live lab use. **(1) Sticky login:** the SPA kept its token pair in `sessionStorage`, which dies with the browser — so every visit to the panel demanded a fresh login regardless of the refresh token's 7-day life. The pair now lives in `localStorage`: the session survives restarts, the existing silent-renew path (401 → refresh → retry) picks it up cold, and the rotation + family-revocation semantics bound the risk of the longer-lived storage. Pair this with `management.auth.refreshTokenMinutes` for the wanted horizon (the lab runs 43200 = 30 days). **(2) The gateway now injects `X-Forwarded-Prefix: /apps/{id}`** (anti-spoofed like the X-Pdn-* set): a server-rendered app prefixes its absolute links/form actions/redirect Locations with it so they stay inside the proxied mount instead of escaping to pdn's root — found live when the BBS webmail's claim form POSTed to `/claim` and got pdn's 405. Contract documented in docs/app-gateway.md; the BBS consumes it in its own repo; DAPPS's dashboard has the same class of issue when driven through the gateway (its own follow-up).

### 2026-06-11 — App packages: PDN_NODE_CALLSIGN/PDN_NODE_ALIAS injected into supervised services

A one-line widening of the package contract (docs/app-packages.md § The manifest): the service supervisor now injects the node's own identity (`PDN_NODE_CALLSIGN`, plus `PDN_NODE_ALIAS` when set) alongside the existing `PDN_APP_*`/`PDN_RHP_*` variables — so an app can derive its on-air identity per Tom's convention that an app lives at an SSID of the node callsign (the first consumer: DAPPS, which gains a `<nodecall>-<DAPPS_SSID>` default in packet-net/dapps' config-env-precedence PR, removing the must-set-DAPPS_CALLSIGN-before-first-enable wrinkle).

### 2026-06-11 — App platform Slice 5b: DAPPS bundled with pdn — the flagship external package, public interfaces only

Tom's call: DAPPS is destined to be a fundamental pdn service, so it ships **with** pdn — but strictly through the public mechanisms ([`docs/app-packages.md`](app-packages.md) § Distribution): pdn's build **fetches the published release artifact** (packet-net/dapps cuts self-contained single-file binaries per RID), never builds from or vendors its source, and pdn never special-cases the app id. **The package:** `packaging/dapps/pdn-app.yaml` — a `service:` block running the staged binary with the rhpv2-bearer environment (`DAPPS_NODE_BEARER=rhpv2`, RHP 9000 loopback, web on 127.0.0.1:18080 matching the `ui:` upstream, MQTT moved off the well-known 1883, self-update disabled — the deb owns the binary), `capabilities: [network, web]`, **no session verb** (DAPPS is not a console app; it binds its own callsigns over RHPv2 exactly as the R-4 acceptance proved). **The staging:** build-deb pins `DAPPS_VERSION` v0.33.12 + per-RID sha256 hashes; artifacts cache under `artifacts/cache` (hash-verified hits skip the download); a downloaded file failing its pin **hard-fails the build**, an unreachable release with no valid cache warns and skips the package (the deb still builds — DAPPS is bundled, not load-bearing); all four paths functionally exercised. Deb size 39.8 → 73.4 MB (the 96.5 MB binary xz-compresses to a third). **Manifest upstreamed** as packet-net/dapps#148 (the interim copy in pdn is deleted once a dapps release ships its own as an asset). **Runtime notes:** DAPPS seeds config from `DAPPS_*` env **on first start only** (a fresh db) — the owner must supply `DAPPS_CALLSIGN` via the `apps:` override environment before first enable (`apps: [{id: dapps, enabled: true, environment: {DAPPS_CALLSIGN: M0XYZ-7}}]`); without it DAPPS runs benignly in setup-required placeholder mode (no crash-loop) and can be finished from the proxied dashboard; the db lands at the state-dir root (`/var/lib/packetnet/apps/dapps/dapps.db` — DAPPS only uses `data/` if it already exists); re-pinning a new DAPPS version means bumping the script pin + three hashes + the manifest version together (a CI test enforces the manifest↔script pair). +3 manifest tests (8 ShippedManifests green).

### 2026-06-11 — App platform Slice 5a: the app-package model — manifests, discovery, supervised services, owner toggles

The user-facing packaging/plugin model ([`docs/app-packages.md`](app-packages.md) — the contract; decisions Tom 2026-06-11: lifecycle = pdn-supervised with an external escape hatch, and the bundled apps must use the same mechanism as everyone else). **The model:** an app package is a directory carrying a `pdn-app.yaml` **authored by the app, not the node owner** (identity + `capabilities` + optional `session:`/`service:`/`ui:` blocks); pdn discovers packages under `/usr/share/packetnet/apps` (distro) then `/var/lib/packetnet/apps` (owner-installed; later root wins), re-scanned on every config apply; **discovered is not enabled** — a package stays off until the owner grants trust via an `apps: [{id, enabled}]` override (small per-app overrides: session verb, service environment merged owner-wins). **Catalog** (`AppPackageCatalog`, 50 tests): kebab-tolerant YAML binding, the validation ladder (manifest=1, id=dirname, block requirements, ui-upstream shape, verb collisions vs built-ins/inline/sibling packages, inline-id collisions), throw-free per-directory Error entries (broken packages surface in the UI, never vanish), EffectiveMatch/EffectiveEnvironment as the single merge authority. **Supervisor** (`AppServiceSupervisor`, 26 tests): enabled packages' `service:` daemons are pdn's to run — spawn via `setsid` process groups (the .NET `CreateNewProcessGroup` is Windows-only) with `PDN_APP_ID/DIR/STATE` + `PDN_RHP_HOST/PORT` env injection and package-dir-relative path resolution, stdout/stderr → `app:<id>` log categories, SIGTERM → 5 s grace → kill-tree, restart policies with 1 s→60 s backoff and a 5-in-5-min crash-loop breaker into `Faulted` (a plain reconcile never resurrects; RestartAsync or a spawn-fingerprint change does), `managed: external` observed-not-owned; reconciled at node start + every config apply (both planner paths) + on demand, disposed with the node. **Session/web union:** `ApplicationHost` resolves verbs from inline entries first then enabled packages; `PdnAppGateway` serves launcher tiles + the `/apps/{id}/*` proxy for package `ui:` blocks with identical identity-injection. **Surfaces** (15 + 11 tests): `GET /api/v1/apps/packages` (the inventory — source, enabled, service state/pid/detail, errors), enable/disable through the config-write seam (broken packages 409), restart; the Manage-apps section with status pills and the **capabilities confirm step at enable time** (the trust prompt made visible). **Packaging:** WALL (session + supervised `wall_web` + ui) and LOBBY (socket session + supervised daemon — replacing the lab's transient systemd unit) ship as real packages staged by build-deb with manifests parsed in CI (`ShippedManifestsTests`); `RuntimeDirectory=packetnet` added so the lobby socket can bind under `ProtectSystem=strict`; `packetnet.yaml` reframed with packages as the primary path. Default-off everywhere: a node with no `apps:` entries behaves exactly as before. +91 tests total; 725 Node + 22 web green; Release clean. Next: 5b — DAPPS bundled from its published release artifact via this exact mechanism.

### 2026-06-11 — RHPv2 R-5: field-notes/RHPTEST conformance sweep

Two primary sources landed upstream and re-graded the homework ([`docs/rhp2-server.md`](rhp2-server.md) — wire-fidelity rows 10–15 + footnote †, deviations D6–D9, the §pdn extensions section, the R-5 roadmap entry): rhp2lib's [protocol field notes](https://rhp2lib.pages.dev/protocol-field-notes/) and **RHPTEST** (`rhptest.c`, Paula Dowie G8PZT's own harness, asserted against XRouter **v505d**; ingested into rhp2lib-net 2026-06-11) — the first source of protocol *intent* beyond the white papers and the live binary. Method: every diff-verifiable claim was **probed against the live container first** (fresh connection per scenario; the alphabetic-SSID probe deliberately binds without connecting so it can never wedge the shared XRouter), then fixed/pinned/documented. **Four conformance bugs fixed in `RhpServer`/`Packet.Rhp2`:** **(1)** `seqno` was a server-wide counter whose first push was 1 — now **per RHP connection, starting at 0**, one counter across all push types (RHPTEST: first post-open push is `status seqno:0`, first `recv` is 1; confirmed live — a fresh connection's `status` query answers `"seqno":0`); **(2)** `send.data` absent vs empty were indistinguishable (DTO defaulted to `""`) — `Data` is now nullable, absence answers **12 "Missing data"** (the live wire's exact errText) while `"data":""` stays a legal zero-byte send; **(3)** an absent `handle` answered 3 — now **12 "Missing handle"** on every handle-bearing op (close/send/bind/listen/connect/status/sendto; RHPTEST: "3 is for handles that are well-formed but unknown"; all seven verified live), with nullable request/reply DTO handle fields so absence survives deserialization and parameter-error replies omit the handle echo like XRouter; **(4)** `send` on a **listener** answered 17 — now **16** per RHPTEST's "listener sockets reject everything but accept/close", split from the kept 17 for non-listening unconnected handles — and here the live wire **disagrees with RHPTEST**: 505c answers 17, so this is named deviation **D9** with both sides asserted explicitly in the diff (a 505d+ container bump surfaces it). **Pins and rows:** id-less requests — the spec says reply-only-on-error but the live wire **replies to everything** (id simply omitted), pdn matches (row 10); `bind` port `"0"` ≡ null = all ports (row 14); the accept push is now followed by the lifecycle's **child `status` Connected push** (row 15, was missing); alphabetic-SSID `bind`/`open` refused with a clean **6/7** where XRouter accepts then wedges in background SABM retries (**D7**, asserted both-sides in the diff); `open` without `local` stays permissive — an XRouter-ism, "NOT a requirement of the RHP protocol" per RHPTEST (**D8**, verified live: XRouter answers 6); admission posture documented (**D6**: XRouter trusts localhost/RFC1918 + USERPASS.SYS; pdn uses loopback-bind + `requireAuth` against the node user store); D1 refined (the silent-drop cliff is 8100–8200 bytes), D2 refined (the wedge answers **`authReply`** errCode 14 to every request type); passive `open` (deterministic 16) and Busy flow control (no queue-depth signal exists yet) join the deferral list by name; the RHPTEST-vs-live re-listen conflict (16 vs idempotent Ok) recorded as the row-9 footnote with the container version (image label **505c**) — pdn keeps matching the live wire. **Extension:** `hello`/`helloReply` capability discovery from the field-notes proposal (observation 4) — pdn is the first server to answer it: errCode 0 + `proto`/`impl`/`pfams`/`maxData`/`enc`; XRouter's unknown-type fallback (`helloReply` errCode 2, verified live) is exactly what makes it backwards-compatible, and the pre-auth gate stays uniform (hello → 14, still distinguishable from "not supported"). 184 Rhp2 (+39: codec absence/hello suite, seqno/absent-field/callsign/lifecycle server tests, 6 new raw-wire conformance pins) + **19 live wire-diff tests (+14)** green against the running container; full default gate + Release clean.

### 2026-06-11 — App platform Slice 4 (R-4): the conformance gate — live-XRouter wire diff + raw-wire suite + DAPPS acceptance (Slice 4 complete)

The homework gets marked by someone else ([`docs/rhp2-server.md`](rhp2-server.md) — status, wire-fidelity row 9, deviation D5, the R-4 roadmap entry). Three independent legs. **Leg A, `XRouterWireConformanceTests` (7 tests, sub-agent):** rhp2lib's `RealXRouterTests` assertion set re-pointed at pdn, every assertion against the **raw JSON text** off the frame (the typed sibling suites read case-insensitively, so they'd miss a casing regression every real client would feel): capital `errCode`/`errText` on every reply type, `type` as the first key on all 14 output paths, id-echo on replies vs seqno-never-id on pushes, unknown type → `{type}Reply` errCode 2, `accept.port` as a quoted string, the numeric error ladder (3/9/8/16/14), and Latin-1-not-base64 data (byte-exact through the codec; the base64 encoding asserted absent). No conformance bugs found — the suite pins the shape against regression. **Leg B, `XRouterRhpWireDiffTests` (5 tests, interop lane):** the same protocol scenarios driven against the **live XRouter** (`RHPPORT=9000` added to the docker fixture, exposed 127.0.0.1:8900) and an in-proc pdn side by side, shapes compared — the independent oracle, and it earned its keep immediately: **(1)** re-`listen` on an already-listening socket → XRouter answers **idempotent Ok**, pdn answered 9 — pdn corrected (wire-fidelity row 9; `RhpServer` + flipped unit test); **(2)** a **second socket** claiming the same callsign → XRouter answers Ok leaving accept routing ambiguous between two listeners — pdn deliberately keeps the deterministic 9 (**deviation D5**, per don't-reproduce-defects), with the divergence asserted on *both* sides in the diff so a change in either surfaces loudly. D4 (resolved-outcome openReply) revisited and stands. **Leg C, the acceptance headline — stock DAPPS against the deployed lab node:** DAPPS (`DAPPS_NODE_BEARER=rhpv2`, .NET-10 roll-forward, SSH-tunnelled to the lab's loopback 9000) → `RHP inbound: listener bound to M0LTE-7 on handle 103`; an independent raw-AX.25 python station over the net-sim channel (pdn-lab:8101) sent SABM to M0LTE-7 → **pdn answered UA as the RHP-bound callsign** → DAPPS logged `ACCEPT child=104 from M0TST-1` → its `DAPPSv1>` greeting arrived at the station as an I-frame over RF. The full chain — RHPv2 wire → multi-callsign engine → accept push → child-handle send → radio — proven live against the only complete independent implementation and the only real client. 145 Rhp2 (138+7) green; the diff tests run in the interop lane. **Slice 4 is complete**; Slice 5 (BBS/Chat/DAPPS on the platform) is next; shared-codec convergence with rhp2lib + Whatspac support remain the long-run follow-ups.

### 2026-06-11 — App platform Slice 4 (R-3): the RHPv2 passive half — multi-callsign engine + socket/bind/listen/accept

The load-bearing piece of the network plane ([`docs/rhp2-server.md`](rhp2-server.md), updated): the node now ANSWERS for callsigns an RHP client registers — DAPPS's *listener* path, completing the full DAPPS message surface in both directions. **Engine (`Ax25Listener`, the parity-guarded surface):** the session cache re-keys from remote-only to the **(local, remote) pair** (same remote can hold simultaneous links to MyCall and an app callsign; LRU and `SendData` re-keyed; 905 Ax25 tests green incl. 7 new), with **refcounted local aliases** (`AddLocalAlias`/`RemoveLocalAlias`) — the inbound filter widens to MyCall ∪ aliases ∪ live-session keys (an outbound alias link receives its replies with no registration; a deregistered alias stops NEW accepts but live sessions keep routing), inbound TEST to an alias is answered *as* the alias, and `ConnectAsync(remote, local)` originates from one. Per the discipline, the **reviewed parity exception shipped in ax25-ts FIRST** (`parity-exceptions.json` ax25-ts#58, merged before this PR; unused exceptions tolerated — verified) with the TS leg a named follow-up. **Supervisor:** `RegisterAppCallsign(callsign, port?, handler)` — registry + alias application to running listeners + re-application on port (re)start; `OnSessionAccepted` routes an app-callsign session to its handler (never the console; node callsign console untouched; unclaimed → DISC); the node's own callsign and duplicates are refused; `ResolveConnector(portId, localOverride)` + `Ax25OutboundConnector` gain alias origination. **Server:** the BSD lifecycle — `socket` (same family/mode ladder), `bind` (records callsign; null port = all ports, exactly DAPPS's shape), `listen` (registers with the engine via the gateway; second listen / cross-client duplicate → errCode 9), async **`accept` pushes** (listener handle + new `child` + caller callsign + arrival port as a STRING — the XRouter wire shape) with the child pumped like any stream handle; closing a listener stops listening (children independent); a dropped RHP connection releases its registrations (PWP-0222). Gateway `RegisterListener` maps port labels ↔ ids and translates the supervisor's port id back to the wire's 1-indexed label; outbound `open.local` may now be any valid callsign (the R-2 limitation lifted, doc updated). +20 tests across the three layers (7 engine multi-callsign, 8 server passive wire, 3 node-host end-to-end over the in-memory radio — an over-the-air connect to a registered callsign reaches the app with data both ways, console intact, node-call/duplicate refused) + the obsolete R-2 deferral pin removed. 905 Ax25 + 138 Rhp2 + 629 Node green; Release clean. Remaining: R-4 (XRouter conformance diff + DAPPS-against-pdn acceptance).

### 2026-06-10 — App platform Slice 4 (R-1+R-2): the RHPv2 server — wire codec + outbound host API

The network plane begins ([`docs/rhp2-server.md`](rhp2-server.md) — scope, oracle strategy, and the named-deviations table; design background in [`docs/app-extensibility.md`](app-extensibility.md)). **The homework problem named and handled:** rhp2lib (the reference client) is ours and has only ever been validated as far as getting DAPPS working against XRouter, so v1 scope = exactly the DAPPS-proven subset (`ax25`+`stream`, auth/open(Active)/send/close + async recv/close; socket/bind/listen = R-3, connect/sendto/status-query deferred — each replying a visible errCode 16, never dropped), and the oracle ladder is: golden wire fixtures captured from real XRouter → the MockRhpServer behavioural contract → **DAPPS-against-pdn** as acceptance → R-4's real-XRouter Testcontainers diff. **R-1, `Packet.Rhp2` (sub-agent, 112 tests):** an independent codec implementation byte-compatible with the reference — 2-byte BE length framing (clean-EOF null / zero-length legal / truncation throws / >0xFFFF rejected), the full 22-message catalogue with the XRouter casing traps (capital `errCode`/`errText` on every reply, lowercase `sendto`/`frametype`, the `"ConnectReply"` spec-typo tolerated on read), `type`-first emission, string-or-int `port` normalisation, Latin-1 (not base64) `data` encoding, error codes 0–17 incl. the XRouter-observed 17 "Not connected" — pinned by every golden fixture from the reference plus serialize→deserialize→re-serialize byte-identical across all types. **R-2, `Packet.Rhp2.Server` + host wiring (17 tests):** `RhpServer` (TCP loop; per-client dispatch with opens off-loop so a slow dial never blocks other handles; id-echo on replies vs seqno-no-id on pushes; unknown type → `{type}Reply` errCode 2; per-handle recv pumps chunked at 2 KB; server-initiated `close` push on peer drop; all handles die with their TCP connection per PWP-0222), `IRhpGateway`/`RhpGatewayException` (the engine seam owning the PWP error vocabulary), `SupervisorRhpGateway` in the host (1-indexed-config-order port labels, the claimed connect-out via new `PortSupervisor.ResolveConnector(portId)`, 90 s connect backstop), `RhpServerHostedService` (config-driven lifecycle, telnet-style restart on `rhp:` hot-reload, bind-clash never crashes the node), `rhp:` config block (default-off, loopback, `requireAuth` validated against the existing Argon2id user store — no second credential system) + validation + packaging. **Named deviations (full table in the doc):** D1 no 8 KB silent-drop, D2 no bad-auth connection-wedge, D3 handles owned by their creating connection (numbering stays global-style from 100), D4 `openReply` carries the resolved outcome (a `status` Connected push follows for shape-compat) — all defects-not-reproduced per Tom's call; every XRouter wire *contract* is matched. R-2 named limitation: `open.local` must be the node callsign (app-callsign origination = R-3's multi-callsign engine work, which also unblocks listening). 129 Rhp2 + 626 Node tests green; Release clean. Follow-up agreed: converge on a shared codec library with rhp2lib longer-term.

### 2026-06-10 — App platform Slice 2: the long-running-socket rung (`kind: socket`) + LOBBY

The "next rung" of the local-session seam ([`docs/app-extensibility.md`](app-extensibility.md), wire §6 of [`docs/app-local-session-wire.md`](app-local-session-wire.md)): an app can be a **long-running daemon listening on a Unix-domain socket** instead of a process spawned per connect — the node opens a fresh connection per session and bridges it over the **same `pdn-app/1` wire**, so the app can hold **shared in-memory state across users** and push unsolicited output (a presence list, a chat, a broadcast — which the spawn-per-connect floor structurally cannot do). New `ApplicationKind.Socket` + `ApplicationConfig.SocketPath`; `SocketApplication` connects the Unix socket, bridges, and closes on teardown (the daemon's `recv` → EOF tells it the user left); a daemon that isn't listening surfaces as `ApplicationStartException` (the host reports "unavailable"). The owner runs the daemon (the node does not manage its lifecycle — it only connects). **Refactor:** the load-bearing wire (header build + the two normalising pumps + teardown semantics) is extracted into a shared **`AppSessionBridge`** used by both transports — the process floor and the socket rung — so the wire is defined once; `ExternalProcessApplication` is reduced to spawn + stderr-drain + teardown over it. **Bug fixed by the refactor's tests:** the header write to a fast-exiting one-shot app (e.g. `/bin/echo`) raced its exit and threw `Broken pipe`, which the old code mis-treated as a start failure — the bridge now makes header delivery **best-effort** (a one-shot app that produced output and closed its input is forwarded, not killed); the only "unavailable" failure is the spawn/connect step. **LOBBY** ([`examples/lobby/`](../examples/lobby/)) is the worked example: a stdlib Python Unix-socket daemon where `WHO` lists who's connected right now and `SAY` broadcasts to every connected user live (thread-per-connection, lock-guarded shared registry, dead-peer-reaping broadcast). Packaging: the `.deb` ships `lobby.py`; a commented `kind: socket` block documents it. Default-off. +12 tests: config bind/validate (socket kind needs `socketPath`); the socket bridge via a stub Unix-socket echo daemon (header + line bridge + teardown + not-listening→`ApplicationStartException`); and a **cross-language end-to-end** with the real Python LOBBY — **two** C#-bridged sessions, a `SAY` from one reaching the other (the shared-state proof). 626 Node tests + full solution + Release clean. One fan-out sub-agent (LOBBY) against the locked socket contract; I owned the bridge refactor + the socket transport + integration.

### 2026-06-10 — Fix: app-gateway injected an empty `X-Pdn-User` (inbound-claim mapping)

Second lab live-verify catch on Slice 3: the proxied app saw `X-Pdn-User: ` (empty) for a genuinely-authenticated viewer (the WALL web view rendered "viewing anonymously" for a logged-in sysop), so an app couldn't tell *who* was viewing. Cause: the transformer read only `ClaimsIdentity.Name`, but the JWT bearer pipeline's inbound-claim mapping can surface the subject as the raw `sub` claim or the mapped `NameIdentifier` instead — so `Name` was null. Fix: extract the username with the same three-way fallback the auth APIs already use (`PrincipalUsername` in `PdnTotpApi`/`PdnWebAuthnApi`) — `Identity.Name ?? sub ?? NameIdentifier` — factored into `PdnAppGateway.AuthenticatedUsername`. Made `AppGatewayTransformer` internal + added unit tests (`AppGatewayTransformerTests`) that assert path-rebase, client-`X-Pdn-*` strip, and identity injection from a sub-only / NameIdentifier-only principal — directly covering the bug the integration test (auth-off → anonymous) couldn't. `PdnAppGateway` + the test.

### 2026-06-10 — Fix: app-gateway trailing-slash redirect loop (`/apps/{id}/` 302-looped)

Lab live-verify of Slice 3 caught a real bug the integration test missed: the convenience `/apps/{id}` → `/apps/{id}/` redirect route also matched the WITH-slash form (routing treats a trailing slash as insignificant), so `/apps/wall/` — the actual launcher URL — matched the redirect and 302-looped to itself (`Location: /apps/wall/`) instead of being proxied. The integration test only hit `/apps/wall/hello` (3 segments → unambiguously the catch-all), so it passed. Fix: **drop the redirect route** — apps are reached at `/apps/{id}/` (the launcher always emits the trailing slash, which the catch-all `/apps/{id}/{**rest}` handles with `rest=""`; a no-slash request falls through to the SPA). Added a regression test that GETs `/apps/wall/` and asserts a 200 forward (`path=/`), so the launcher URL itself is now covered. Also packages `examples/wall/wall_web.py` into the `.deb` (`build-deb.sh` — it was added to the repo in Slice 3 but not wired into the staging step). `PdnAppGateway` + `build-deb.sh` + the test.

### 2026-06-10 — App platform Slice 3: the app-gateway (human plane — launcher + reverse-proxy + auth shell)

The human plane of the app platform ([`docs/app-extensibility.md`](app-extensibility.md) + the new app-author contract [`docs/app-gateway.md`](app-gateway.md)): an app exposes a web UI *through* pdn without pdn knowing the app. pdn is a broker — manifest reader, reverse proxy, auth gateway. Config: `ApplicationConfig.Ui` (`AppUiConfig { Upstream (absolute http(s), validated), Name?, Icon? }`) — presence of a `ui` block is what puts an app in the launcher. New `PdnAppGateway` (`src/Packet.Node/Api`): `GET /api/v1/apps` (read-gated) returns the launcher tiles (`{id,name,icon,url:/apps/{id}/}`); `/apps/{id}/{**rest}` (read-gated, all methods via `MapMethods`) reverse-proxies to the app's loopback `ui.upstream` using YARP's `IHttpForwarder` (added `Yarp.ReverseProxy` to CPM — the direct-forwarding primitive, no config-model), via a custom `HttpTransformer` that **rebases the path** (strips `/apps/{id}`), **derives Host from the upstream** (not pdn's), **strips any client-supplied `X-Pdn-*`** header (anti-spoof), then **injects** `X-Pdn-User`/`X-Pdn-Scope` (from the validated principal) + `X-Pdn-Gateway: 1`. **Auth gateway:** the proxy route requires `read` scope (passes through when auth is off); since a browser navigation can't set an Authorization header, the JWT bearer pipeline (`OnMessageReceived`) also reads the token from an **HttpOnly `pdn_at` cookie** (SameSite=Strict, Secure-on-HTTPS, Path=/apps) that pdn sets at login / refresh / passkey-assert and clears at logout. Trust model (v1): the app binds loopback; only pdn reaches it; headers are unsigned (signing with an owner-provisioned per-app secret is a documented later hardening — `app-gateway.md` §Trust). Web: an **Apps launcher** screen (`/api/v1/apps` → a responsive tile grid linking to `/apps/{id}/`) + nav entry + a dynamic-lucide `AppIcon` (sub-agent). **WALL gained a web view** ([`examples/wall/wall_web.py`](../examples/wall/wall_web.py) — stdlib read-only page of the same wall the packet app writes; loopback-bound, relative-URL, reads `X-Pdn-User`), so WALL now exercises both planes; the `.deb`/config example carries the `ui:` block. **A real Host-forwarding bug was caught by the integration test** (pdn was forwarding its own Host to the upstream → a strict upstream 404s; fixed by deriving Host from the upstream URI). +14 tests (config bind/validate; a `WebApplicationFactory` + stub-`HttpListener` end-to-end proving path rebase, identity injection, client-header strip, unknown-app 404, and the launcher feed); 614 Node tests + SPA 11/11 + Release clean. Default-off — no `ui:` block, no launcher tile, no proxy. Two fan-out sub-agents (WALL web view + SPA launcher) against locked contracts; I owned the gateway/proxy/auth + integration.

### 2026-06-10 — App platform Slice 1: `INodeApplication` + the `applications:` registry + the `pdn-app/1` stdio wire + WALL (shipped, fully separated)

First code slice of the app-extensibility platform ([`docs/app-extensibility.md`](app-extensibility.md)), built end-to-end. New `Packet.Node.Core/Applications/`: `INodeApplication` (the one seam — `RunAsync(session, NodeAppContext)`), with the existing `NodeCommandService` now *implementing* it as **application #0** (explicit-interface delegate to its existing prompt loop — zero behaviour change, all 603 Node tests green); `ExternalProcessApplication` — the **`pdn-app/1` external-process stdio wire** ([`docs/app-local-session-wire.md`](app-local-session-wire.md)): spawn-per-connect, a newline-sanitised connect header (callsign/transport/port/args) to the child's stdin, then two pumps bridging the session ↔ the child's stdio with terminator normalisation (inbound user lines → a single `\n`; the child's `\n` → the transport's CR / CR-LF), stderr drained to the log, and unconditional teardown (close stdin → grace → kill the tree); `ApplicationHost`/`IApplicationHost` — resolves a verb against `NodeConfig.Applications` read **live** (so a hot edit applies to the next launch with no reconcile machinery, since each connect spawns fresh) and runs it totally (a spawn failure is reported to the user, never thrown). Config: `ApplicationConfig` + `ApplicationKind` on `NodeConfig.Applications` (YAML binds via a new `IReadOnlyList<ApplicationConfig>` type-map); validation rejects duplicate ids/verbs and — reusing `NodeCommandParser` as the source of truth — a `match` that shadows a built-in console verb. Dispatch: the console's `UnknownCommand` arm offers the verb to the host (built-ins always win first); a match launches the app over the session, then re-prompts on exit. Wired through all three inbound sites (AX.25 + NET/ROM via `PortSupervisor`, telnet via the host factory). **Separation is enforced, not just intended** (Tom's directive): the node links no app code; WALL is a separate-language (**Python**) out-of-process program ([`examples/wall/`](../examples/wall/), stdlib-only, ~120 lines of logic, 6 self-tests) so a code link is structurally impossible; an **architecture test** fails the build if `Packet.Node*` ever references an app project or hardcodes `wall.py`/`examples/wall`; and pdn's own config docs use a neutral placeholder, never naming WALL. Packaging: the `.deb` ships `wall.py` to `/usr/share/packetnet/apps/wall`, `postinst` creates the writable `/var/lib/packetnet/apps/wall` state dir, `Recommends: python3`, and a commented `applications:` block documents enabling it. Default-off everywhere — a node with no `applications:` entries behaves exactly as before. +31 app-platform tests (config/validation, the bridge via `/bin/cat` as an echo oracle, host resolve/run, console dispatch, the arch guardrails); 603 Node tests green; full solution + Release clean.

### 2026-06-10 — App-extensibility platform: design agreed (supersedes the in-proc `.dll`-plugin model)

The node UI is intrinsically an admin surface (status + config); packet comes to life through **applications**. Agreed the design for letting node owners build + expose apps easily, **without locking them to .NET** — captured in [`docs/app-extensibility.md`](app-extensibility.md). It **supersedes** the original Phase-9 sketch (`IApplicationModule` `.dll`s in an `AssemblyLoadContext`): out-of-process language-agnostic apps win on language reach, blast radius, independent release cadence, and interop.

Shape: two planes. **Packet plane**, two seams *by purpose* — a pdn-native **local-session** seam (the node hands an app the connected user's session; floor = stdio-per-connect; for local-only apps like WALL — no network, no interop story to preserve) + the **network** seam = **RHPv2**: pdn implements the RHPv2 server (`Packet.Rhp2.Server`) over its own AX.25/NET-ROM engine, so any [rhp2lib](https://rhp2lib.pages.dev/) app (DAPPS, BBS forwarding, chat linking) runs against pdn *and* XRouter unchanged — the ecosystem unlock. **Human plane** — a manifest + reverse-proxy + auth-gateway shell: an app registers a manifest, pdn renders a launcher tile + reverse-proxies `/apps/{id}/*` to the app's own web service with the authenticated identity injected, so an app's UI (webmail, chat client, DAPPS panel) surfaces *through* pdn without pdn knowing the app. The node owner owns trust (apps out-of-process + a capability manifest the owner approves; WASM sandbox is a later marketplace option, not v1). One `INodeApplication` abstraction; the console is app #0; the registry lives in `NodeConfig` (hot-reloaded like ports).

Validating cast: **WALL** (local session + tiny store, no network — the floor), **Chat** (local + BPQ-compatible inter-node linking), **BBS** (everything), **DAPPS** (network + admin UI). Slicing: (1) `INodeApplication` + registry + WALL packet-side → (2) external local-session wire + WALL as the worked example → (3) app-gateway → (4) `Packet.Rhp2.Server` validated against rhp2lib's mock/XRouter conformance suite → (5) BBS/Chat/DAPPS. WALL is the first build — shipped reference app **and** the worked example future docs are written around. Open questions (RHPv2 family coverage, conformance-oracle reuse, BPQ-chat wire-compat scope, spec-vs-wire deltas) tracked in the doc. Doc-only entry; the first code slice (WALL) follows.


### 2026-06-10 — ax25-ts parity restored to lib-v0.7.0 + a cross-repo drift guard (CI-enforced on both sides)

Tom: "ax25-ts should be at parity with pdn, what's missing?" — audited and found the heavyweight machinery in sync (all 8 session quirks, XID parse options, mod-128 + segmentation + MDL, LM-SEIZE grant, the #231 no-renumber fix) but the parse-options/listener surface behind: no `allowCommandFrameAsResponse` (#142) or `allowEmptyCallsignBase` flags (STRICT_PARSE couldn't reject either), no peer presets, the TEST/axping responder missing (a TS node answered an axping with a **DM** — live on-air divergence), and no listener-level parseOptions/quirks (#366's library half). **Parity restored** in ax25-ts#55: both flags (optional, absent = lenient — no consumer breaks), `BPQ_PARSE`/`XROUTER_PARSE`/`DIREWOLF_PARSE`, the TEST intercept (command → echoing response with F mirroring P; response absorbed) + `test()` factory + `sendTest()` initiator, and `Ax25ListenerOptions.parseOptions`/`quirks` used at the decode sites + session build. 13 paired tests; 1179 green. Stale ax25-ts#5 (retransmit renumbering) closed — already fixed there.

Tom: "devise and implement a method to ensure it doesn't drift." — **the parity drift guard**: ax25-ts `scripts/parity-check.mjs` extracts the named-flag / preset / listener-surface inventories from both repos (regex over a shallow sparse source clone — no build; Pascal↔camel + small alias maps like `SendUiAsync`→`sendUi`, `T1V`→`t1Ms`) and exits 1 on any gap not recorded as a *reviewed exception with a reason* in `scripts/parity-exceptions.json` (one starter: `UpdateSessionParameters`, the node-host live-reseed). Wired twice so drift fails CI on whichever side introduces it: a `parity` job in ax25-ts's ci.yml (vs packet.net main; needs a `PACKETNET_READ_TOKEN` fine-grained read PAT since this repo is private — warns-and-skips until the secret is added) and a step in this repo's interop.yml (vs the PR head + ax25-ts main — credential-free since ax25-ts is public, so this copy is always armed). Verified to pass on the current trees and to fail on a simulated new C# flag. CLAUDE.md (both repos) now documents the rule: a new named flag ships with its TS leg or a reviewed exception.


### 2026-06-10 — Release ledger: lib-v0.7.0 + node-v0.6.0 + the downstream cascade

The #366 merge triggered the release arc (every tag on green main; the merge commit's ci needed two reruns past the known runner-contention flakes — AXUDP node-to-node once, the console Bye-timeout once, both 5/5 locally — before tagging). **`lib-v0.7.0`** (the six NuGet packages): carries #366's listener `ParseOptions`/`Quirks`, the #142 strict response-SABM guard, and the §4.3.4.2 TEST/axping responder (#347/#348). **`node-v0.6.0`** (.debs amd64/arm64/armhf + SHA256SUMS, non-draft): the whole arc since 0.5.3 — web control panel + auth parts 1–4 (HTTPS, JWT+refresh+lockout, passkeys, over-RF sysop TOTP), ID beacons, monitor history, sysop console, axping, per-port compat profiles. **Leaf cascade**: axcall + packet-term-tui `Packet.*` pins 0.5.1 → 0.7.0 (PRs axcall#9 / packet-term-tui#14, both green against the new libs — additive API, no break) → both released **v0.2.9** (six-platform binaries, verified non-draft with assets). No `@packet-net/ax25` leg in *this* arc (no TS change in the window); the parity work above ships as ax25-ts 0.12.0 separately.


### 2026-06-10 — #366: per-port AX.25 compatibility profile — Ax25ParseOptions presets + session quirks reach the node config

Closes the gap the #142 work exposed: the leniency/quirk knobs existed in the library but the node host pinned every port to `Ax25ParseOptions.Lenient` (hardcoded at `Ax25Listener`'s parse sites) and `Ax25SessionQuirks.Default`, so nothing the strict/pragmatic audit documents was operator-reachable. Now `PortConfig` gains a **`compat:`** block — a preset name (`strict` | `lenient` | `bpq` | `xrouter` | `direwolf`), optional per-flag overrides (`allowEmptyCallsignBase` / `allowInfoOnSupervisoryFrames` / `allowCommandFrameAsResponse`, explicit-wins on top of the preset, mirroring how channel profiles overlay timers), and a `quirks:` selector (`default` | `strictly-faithful`). `Ax25CompatPresets` is the single name→preset authority the validator and `PortSupervisor` share, so a name validation accepted can never fail at bring-up. Library side: `Ax25ListenerOptions`/`Ax25SessionParameters` gain `ParseOptions` + `Quirks` and the listener actually uses them — the inbound pump parses every frame under the configured options (a rejected frame is dropped before trace/dispatch, so a Strict port is deaf to it end-to-end), the mod-128 re-parse uses the same options, and `BuildSession` seeds `ctx.Quirks`; the TX-trace parse stays deliberately Lenient (it renders our own strict-built frames for the monitor). A compat-only edit is a **hot** reconcile class (`ReconcilePlan.CompatChanged` → the same `UpdateSessionParameters` live reseed as the timer params): parse options apply from the next inbound frame, quirks seed the next-built session, existing sessions untouched. Web UI: a per-port "AX.25 compatibility" preset dropdown in the Ports editor (audit rationale as field help; YAML-set flag overrides/quirks carried through untouched, with a "customised" badge). Defaults preserve behaviour exactly (absent compat = lenient + default). Paired tests at the listener (Strict drops a response-SABM entirely — no session, no reply, no trace; Lenient connects on the same bytes; quirks seeding; live reseed gates the very next frame) + validator/planner/preview/round-trip coverage and a supervisor integration test (compat reseed preserves listener identity). Audit doc gains a "node surface" note. Closes #366.


### 2026-06-10 — #142: reject a C=Response SABM under Strict (the sweep's one code item)

The correctness sweep's only genuine library change. The classifier mapped `0x2F`/`0x6F` → SabmReceived/SabmeReceived from the control octet alone, so a SABM/SABME (or DISC) whose **address C-bits mark it a response** — malformed per §4.3.3.1 / §6.1.2, where SABM/SABME/DISC are *always* commands — was accepted and could open a session. Following the repo's "pragmatism is a named flag" discipline rather than a silent tightening: new **`Ax25ParseOptions.AllowCommandFrameAsResponse`** (default `true` = lenient, preserving current behaviour) which **`Strict` sets false**; `Ax25Frame.TryParse` then drops a command-only U-frame (SABM/SABME/DISC) that isn't `IsCommand` (`Destination.CrhBit && !Source.CrhBit`) at decode. The **default stays lenient on purpose**: a legacy AX.25 v1.x peer predates the v2.0 command/response C-bit encoding, so rejecting by default would break v1.x interop — exactly the "justified leniency" the audit doc wants. Added: the strict/pragmatic-audit row, a paired test (`Ax25FrameOptionsTests.Strict_Rejects_Sabm_With_Response_Cbits_Lenient_Accepts` + a well-formed-command control), and an updated note on the listener edge test (the listener keeps the lenient default for v1.x interop; strict is opt-in). 892 Ax25 + 42 Core tests green; warnings-as-errors clean. Closes #142.


### 2026-06-10 — Correctness sweep: tracker reconciliation + roadmap true-up

Stepping back after the auth arc, a correctness review of the open AX.25/runtime issues found the **issue tracker had drifted well behind the code** — most "open" conformance bugs were already fixed (by the conformance-harness + SP-010 work) but never closed. This pass verified each against green tests + the resolving commit and reconciled the tracker rather than re-implementing:

- **#231** (retransmits renumbered → no loss recovery) — fixed `cabd071` (PR #232); `DataLinkConnectedRetransmitTests` green. Closed.
- **#292** (half-duplex stall / per-port t1Ms no-op) — fixed `2b8c616` (PR #302, the establishment-param clobber class) + channel-profile plumbing; `HalfDuplexContentionTests` + `ChannelProfileIntegrationTests` green. Closed.
- **#239** (mod-128 connected-mode data transfer unimplemented — first I-frame threw) — the `RequireMod8`/`NotSupportedException` throw is gone; N(S)/N(R) extraction is mode-aware (7-bit under extended), and `Mod128LossRecoveryConformanceTests` + `Mod128EstablishmentConformanceTests` + `Ax25FrameExtendedControlTests` (34) are green — loss recovery exercises real extended I-frame data transfer. Closed.
- **#230** (Ax25Listener T2/T3 options silently ignored) — `Ax25Listener` now flows configured T2/T3 into `ActionDispatcher.T2Duration/T3Duration` (which the timers arm from). Closed.
- **#144** (figc4.1 t16 accepts SABME without a version_2_2 guard "though the runtime can't do mod-128") — premise no longer holds (mod-128 works, #239), so faithful-to-figc4.1 is correct. Closed.
- **#167 / #260 / #177** (build out the node host / SP-010 typed verbs / direwolf-reference harness) — all shipped. Closed.

**Roadmap true-up:** the §5 status table + per-phase headings + the document status header were badly stale (header read "Phase 2 in progress", 2026-05-17). Refreshed to reality: Phases 0–5 ✅; Phase 6 (AGW/RHPv2) is the next v1 capability; Phase 7 (distribution) is 🟡 (.deb + node-v* release done, apt-repo + signed one-click update pending); Phase 9 partly landed early (NET/ROM L3+L4 + INP3 cross-stack); a Phase 10 (hardware ecosystem & adaptive RF) row was added.

**Residual / new:** **#142** (a SABM with the C=Response bit is accepted; spec §4.3.3.1 says SABM is always a command) stays a scoped library follow-up — the classifier maps `0x2F`/`0x6F` → SabmReceived/SabmeReceived without a C-bit guard. Per the repo's "pragmatism is a named flag" rule it wants a strict-default classifier guard **plus a named listener escape-hatch** (a legacy AX.25 v1.x peer may not set the command bit the v2.2 way) + a strict/pragmatic-audit row + a paired test — a focused PR, not a silent tightening. **#363** (new) captures deep radio integration: per-frame RSSI → SNR via an `IRadioControl` abstraction with a Tait 8100/8200 CCDI driver, feeding the monitor + INP3 + the (deferred) link-tuner — Phase 10, and the measurement the closed-loop tuner needs.

No code changed in this entry beyond closing issues + this doc; the sweep's one code item (#142) is tracked separately.


### 2026-06-10 — Fix: passkey login showed the wrong identity (empty username → "node")

Found in live use on the lab (`pdn.m0lte.uk` on an iPhone): signing in with a passkey logged the panel in with an empty username — the user menu fell back to a "node" placeholder and the admin-gated Users nav disappeared, even though the JWT was correct (scope admin, `sub`=the real user). Root cause: the SPA set the session username from the **login form input**, which a password login has but a **passwordless / discoverable passkey assertion leaves blank** (the identity only emerges from the signed credential at the server). So `auth.login(..., username.trim(), ...)` passed `""`.

Fix — make the server authoritative: the node already resolves the account at every token-issue point, so `PdnAuthApi.LoginResponse` now carries `Username`, populated on `/auth/login`, `/auth/refresh` (from the rotated token's user), and the passkey `/auth/webauthn/assert/complete` (the credential's owner). The SPA's `LoginResult` gains `username`, and `login.tsx` uses `res.username` on BOTH the password and passkey paths instead of the typed box. Mock `mockTokens` carries it too. +2 assertions (login + refresh responses include the username). Behavioural fix to the shipped passkey flow; no new endpoints. Live-verified on the lab (passkey sign-in now shows the real user + keeps admin nav).


### 2026-06-10 — Auth part 4/4: over-RF sysop TOTP — the verification gate (SYSOP elevation + privileged commands)

The verification half of the final "nail auth" part, on top of the `TotpService` core and the enrolment half below. A connected operator can now authenticate **over the air** with a rolling one-time code and gain a time-limited elevated session that unlocks remote node administration. This is the security-critical piece the enrolment half deliberately left to the lead.

**The elevation handshake.** A console session (AX.25, NET/ROM L4, or telnet — all three console construction sites are wired) accepts `SYSOP <code>`; over AX.25/NET-ROM the callsign is implicit from the connection's `PeerId` (canonicalised through `Callsign.TryParse`) and the single argument is the code, while telnet (no callsign) uses `SYSOP <user> <code>`. The service resolves the account — `IUserStore.FindByCallsign` (AX.25) or `FindByUsername` (telnet) — and verifies the code with `TotpService.TryVerify(secret, code, lastAcceptedCounter, …)`. On success it **persists the accepted counter BEFORE granting** (`UpdateTotpCounter`): the single-use replay guard is only real once the high-water mark is durable, so if the counter can't be persisted the node refuses to elevate (no window where the same code re-elevates), and a replayed code (same 30 s window ⇒ same counter, now `== lastAccepted`) is rejected on the next attempt. On success the per-connection elevation state is set to `{ElevatedUntil = now + TTL, Scope = user.Scope}` where the TTL is `management.auth.sysopElevationMinutes` (default 15, validated `> 0`). The elevation state is a local in the command loop — **never shared between connections**, and every check rides the injected `TimeProvider` (no background timer; an elevation simply lapses).

**Privileged commands, gated + routed through the existing serialized seams.** `SESSIONS` (list) and `KICK <id>` require `operate`; `PORT <id> UP|DOWN` and `RELOAD` require `admin`. Each passes a single gate (`RequireElevatedAsync`: wired-for-sysop + auth-enabled + currently-elevated + `AuthScopes.Satisfies`) and then calls a new web-free `ISysopOperations` seam implemented by `HostSysopOperations`, which drives the **identical** paths the web control API uses — `NodeHostedService.RunExclusiveAsync` for session enumeration / kick (`PostEvent(DlDisconnectRequest)`), `IWritableConfigProvider.TryApply` for the port enable/disable (the reconcile worker brings it up/down), and `FileConfigProvider.Reload` for the conffile re-read. So RF admin can never race a config reconcile or a port bring-up; it is the same validated, serialized path reached over a different transport. Unelevated / under-scoped commands are refused with no side effect.

**Threading + default-off.** `TotpService` is DI-registered and (with `IUserStore`) injected into `NodeHostedService`, which assembles a `SysopContext` once at start and threads it into every per-connection `NodeConsoleEnvironment` (via `PortSupervisor`); a node missing either dependency, or with `management.auth.enabled` off, has no `SYSOP` capability at all — the command reports "not available" and the verbs are inert. The parser gained `SYSOP`/`SESSIONS`/`KICK`/`PORT`/`RELOAD` (total + never-throws, the fuzz contract preserved); `SYSOP`/`SESSIONS` require ≥2 chars so a bare `S` can never trigger the auth command. A DB-level partial `UNIQUE INDEX` on `user(callsign)` was added (hardening the enrolment half's callsign uniqueness against a TOCTOU + guaranteeing `FindByCallsign` is single-row). The audit log records the callsign/username + scope + outcome — **never the code**; every failure mode (unknown callsign, no credential, bad code, expired, under-scoped) returns the same generic message (no oracle).

**Deferred by design (named, not silent).** Free-form config `SET <path> <value>` and a full-node `RESTART` over RF are NOT built: editing a deep config tree char-by-char over a lossy 1200-baud link, and a restart that drops the operator's own RF session, each want their own focused design. The `ISysopOperations` seam is shaped so both slot in later without touching the elevation gate. The operationally meaningful RF-admin set (who's connected / kick / port up-down / reload) is complete.

**Verified.** `dotnet build -c Release` clean under warnings-as-errors (only pre-existing `tools/*.Spike` warnings); **548** Node tests green (+19: 9 `SysopElevationTests` end-to-end over the real `SqliteUserStore` + `TotpService` + a recording fake `ISysopOperations` — valid-code elevates + privileged command runs, replay rejected with the counter persisted, unelevated/expired/under-scoped denied, wrong code + unknown callsign generic-fail, auth-off unavailable, telnet user+code form; 10 parser tests incl. the bare-`S` ambiguity guard). The end-to-end live-on-the-lab check (enrol a TOTP via the web UI, connect over the net-sim RF path, `SYSOP <code>` → run a privileged command, confirm a replay is rejected) is the deploy step.


### 2026-06-10 — Auth part 4/4: over-RF sysop TOTP — the enrolment / identity half (store + endpoints + web UI)

Last of the four "nail auth" parts (TLS · refresh tokens · passkeys · **over-RF sysop TOTP**). This entry covers the **enrolment / identity half only** — provisioning, inspecting, and removing the rolling one-time code a sysop will present to elevate a session over a plain packet link (AX.25 has no authentication, so a single-use time-based code, not a static replayable password, is the right primitive — see `TotpService`'s remarks). The **verification half** — the console `SYSOP` command, the per-session elevation state, and the privileged-command gate — is a separate, security-critical piece tracked on its own; nothing in the `Console` namespace changed here. The `TotpService` RFC-6238 core (HMAC-SHA1, single-use replay guard, RFC-vector tests) was committed as the foundation ahead of this; this part builds the store/endpoint/UI around it.

**Store (Core, web-free, resilient).** `UserRecord` gains `Callsign` / `TotpSecret` (base32) / `LastTotpCounter`; `UserSummary` stays hash-free AND secret-free but now exposes a `HasTotp` bool + the `Callsign` (neither secret) so the UI can show enrolment state — the secret never leaves the store. The TOTP secret is stored **plaintext** in the 0640 packetnet-owned `pdn.db`, the same accepted decision as the JWT signing key: the db file (perms + OS-user ownership) is the trust boundary, and a secret the node must *verify* a presented code against (not merely hash-compare) cannot be one-way hashed — documented in a code comment. `SqliteUserStore` migrates **additively**: the existing `user` table gains the three columns via `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN`, guarded for idempotency (a `PRAGMA table_info(user)` check plus a duplicate-column catch — SQLite has no `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS`), all inside the same resilient try/catch as the rest of the store, so a pre-TOTP db opens fine and gains them. New `IUserStore` methods, each resilient (read→null, write→false): `FindByCallsign(callsign)` (case-insensitive — the over-RF lookup the gate will use), `SetTotpSecret(username, secret, callsign)` (binds + resets the replay counter; returns false to 409 if the callsign is already bound to a **different** user — callsign uniqueness), `ClearTotp(username)` (nulls secret/callsign/counter; true iff a row changed), and `UpdateTotpCounter(username, counter)` (persists the replay high-water mark; best-effort but returns **false on fault** so the gate knows it couldn't persist). `FindByUsername` / `List` / `Create` all round-trip the new columns.

**Pending-enrolment cache (Core, `TimeProvider`-driven, in-memory).** New `TotpEnrollmentCache` mirrors `WebAuthnChallengeCache` exactly: `begin` mints a fresh secret (`TotpService.GenerateSecret()`) and stashes it keyed to the enrolling user; `complete` **consumes** it. The same three non-skippable properties — server-generated (the client only types a code derived from our secret), single-use (`Take` is an atomic remove-and-return — a replay finds nothing), expiring (5-min TTL off the injected clock). A never-confirmed secret is therefore never persisted — the secret reaches `pdn.db` only once the user proves they hold a working authenticator.

**Endpoints (`PdnTotpApi.cs` + `Program.cs`).** Self-service, all **`read`-gated** (the floor for an authenticated user managing their own credential — same gate as the passkey register group), username always from the authenticated principal **never the body**, mapped under `/api/v1/auth/totp`: `POST …/enroll/begin` → mint secret + the `otpauth://` URI (issuer = the node's own callsign from `IConfigProvider`, account = the user's callsign-or-username), stash pending, return `{ secret, otpauthUri }` (shown once, NOT persisted); `POST …/enroll/complete {code, callsign}` → consume the pending secret (absent ⇒ 400 "no pending enrolment"), verify the typed code with `TryVerify(secret, code, lastAcceptedCounter: -1, …)`, on success `SetTotpSecret` (false ⇒ 409 callsign-in-use) then `UpdateTotpCounter` so the confirming code itself can't be replayed over RF, validate the callsign with `Packet.Core.Callsign.TryParse` (400 if malformed), generic 400 on verify failure; `GET …/enroll` → `{ enrolled, callsign }` (never the secret); `DELETE …/enroll` → `ClearTotp` + 204. New `AuthLog` lines (`LoggerMessage`, no secrets) for totp-enrolled / -enroll-failed / -cleared. The cache is always-registered + degrade-safe (the endpoint takes `TotpEnrollmentCache?` for parity with the other auth services). **Default-off contract:** always mapped, inert until used — a node with no TOTP enrolled behaves exactly as before, and with auth off the gated begin 409s (no authenticated "self" to enrol for), mirroring the passkey register group.

**Web (`users.tsx`, `lib/api.ts`, `lib/types.ts`).** The mock TOTP affordance is replaced with a real self-service "Over-RF sysop code (TOTP)" row on the signed-in user's OWN row (mirroring the live Passkeys row): "Enrol authenticator" → `enroll/begin`, render the `otpauth://` URI as a **QR code** (new dep **`qrcode.react`**, `QRCodeSVG`) plus the base32 secret as fallback text, a callsign input + a code input → "Confirm" → `enroll/complete`; the enrolled state (callsign + a "Remove" → `DELETE`) reflects `GET /enroll`. Other users' rows show a static self-service indicator. `api.ts` adds `totpSupported/totpState/totpEnrollBegin/totpEnrollComplete/totpRemove` + `types.ts` shapes (mirroring the webauthn ones). **Mock mode keeps building and does NOT fake the round trip** (`totpSupported()` is false in mock ⇒ a disabled/explanatory affordance — no faked begin/verify).

**Verified:** `dotnet build -c Release` clean under warnings-as-errors (Core + Node + tests); **517** Node tests green (+21: 7 `TotpEnrollmentCacheTests` single-use/expiry/user-binding/supersede/prune; 7 new `SqliteUserStoreTests` add-secret round-trip via FindByUsername + FindByCallsign, callsign uniqueness rejects a second user, re-enrol-own allowed, counter persist+reopen, ClearTotp idempotent, UserSummary HasTotp/Callsign-no-secret, an OLD pre-migration db still opens + gains the columns; 7 `TotpApiTests` mirroring `WebAuthnApiTests` plumbing — begin returns secret+uri, complete with the correct generated code persists + GET reflects enrolled, wrong code 400s + does NOT persist, single-use pending secret, delete clears, gated-401-without-token, auth-off no-regression — using `TotpService.ComputeCode` against a `FakeTimeProvider` pinned via `ConfigureTestServices`). The lone full-suite failure was the known `Ax25ConsoleIntegrationTests` CPU-oversubscription timing flake (infra, not code — passes solo in 171 ms; the change touches nothing in the Console / AX.25 path). Web: `npm run build` clean (tsc + vite) + 10/10 vitest. **Handed to the gate (task D):** `IUserStore.FindByCallsign` + `TotpService.TryVerify` + `IUserStore.UpdateTotpCounter` are ready for the console SYSOP authorization gate to consume.


### 2026-06-09 — Auth part 3/4: WebAuthn / passkeys (localhost-first, tier-1) — register + passwordless assert + credential management

Third of the four "nail auth" parts (TLS · refresh tokens · **passkeys** · over-RF sysop TOTP), extending the existing web-auth foundation.
