The login screen's previously-disabled "Continue with passkey" button now lights up and signs in passwordlessly; a signed-in user can enrol passkeys for themselves and manage them. **Scope is deliberately tier 1 (`localhost`) per [`docs/passkeys-lan-trust-pattern.md`](passkeys-lan-trust-pattern.md) §2/§4/§8:** the RP id and expected origin are made **configurable but default to `localhost`**, where origin and RP id coincide — the doc names that split as the single most error-prone part, so `localhost` is the zero-config default that works today over plain HTTP (a loopback secure context, no cert). The **distribution tiers (mDNS / ACME call-home / IP-encoded names / redirects) are NOT built** — §8's decision gate parks them; only the RP-id/origin knobs are exposed so a real-domain operator (doc §2a) can set them by hand. Passkeys **coexist** with password login.

**Credential store (Core, web-free, resilient).** New `IWebAuthnCredentialStore` + `SqliteWebAuthnCredentialStore` (`src/Packet.Node.Core/Auth/`) follow the same resilient pdn.db pattern as the user/refresh stores — WAL, a fresh pooled connection per call, `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`, every op wrapped so a fault degrades (read→null/empty, write→false) and never throws the node down. Table `webauthn_credential(credential_id BLOB PRIMARY KEY, username, public_key BLOB, sign_count INTEGER, cred_type, transports, aaguid BLOB, created_utc, last_used_utc)` + a `username` index. Methods `Add` / `GetByUser` / `GetByCredentialId` / `GetAllCredentialIds` (for `excludeCredentials` + a username-less assertion allow-list) / `UpdateSignCount` / `Delete(id, username)` (the username predicate is the ownership guard — you can only delete your OWN passkey). A stored COSE public key is not a secret (it can only verify, never sign), but is still scoped to the enrolling user. `WebAuthnCredentialRecord` carries the unsigned-32 `SignCount` round-tripped through SQLite's signed-64 INTEGER losslessly.

**The challenge cache is the security-critical piece** (`WebAuthnChallengeCache`, Core, `TimeProvider`-driven, in-memory): every `begin` mints a fresh server-side challenge inside the Fido2 options and stashes those exact options here; the matching `complete` **consumes** them and verifies the authenticator's signed challenge against the **server's** — never a client-echoed one. Three properties it enforces, none skippable: **server-generated** (the client only returns a signature over our challenge), **single-use** (`Take` is an atomic remove-and-return — a replay finds nothing), and **expiring** (5-min TTL off the injected clock — a stale ceremony can't be completed). **Key binding:** a registration is keyed to the enrolling **user** (`reg:<username>` — a pending enrolment for one user can't be completed as another); an assertion is keyed to a per-attempt random **session** handle (`assert:<id>` the begin call mints and complete echoes), because a passwordless / discoverable-credential assertion has no username yet — the identity only emerges from the signed credential at complete. A restart simply invalidates a half-finished ceremony (the browser retries) — nothing here is worth persisting.

**Fido2 wiring + config.** Uses the already-pinned **`Fido2.AspNet 4.0.0-beta.16`** (Fido2NetLib). The `IFido2` verifier is built **per-request** (`WebAuthnFido2Builder`) from the live config + the **actual serving origin** the browser used (request scheme+host+port), so the origin-vs-RP-id handling lives in one place: RP id is fixed (`management.auth.webAuthn.relyingPartyId`, default `localhost`), and the accepted origins are the operator's explicit `allowedOrigins` when set, else the request's own origin + loopback (what makes localhost zero-config). New `WebAuthnConfig { RelyingPartyId, RelyingPartyName, AllowedOrigins }` under `AuthConfig`, validated in `NodeConfigValidator` (RP id required + must be a domain, never an IP literal; each allowed origin an absolute http(s) origin) and YAML round-trips (an `IReadOnlyList<string>` type-mapping + value-based `WebAuthnConfig.Equals` so the empty-origins default survives serialise→parse). **Default-off contract:** the endpoints are always mapped, but a node is only usable via passkeys when `management.auth.enabled` is on AND a user has enrolled one — a node that never enrols behaves exactly as before.

**Endpoints** (`PdnWebAuthnApi.cs` + `Program.cs`). Registration is **gated** (`read` — any authenticated user may manage their own login) and takes the username from the **authenticated principal, never the body**, so a user can only enrol/manage for self: `POST …/register/begin` → `CredentialCreateOptions` (resident-key required ⇒ discoverable ⇒ username-less login later; challenge stashed under the user); `POST …/register/complete` → verify the attestation via `IFido2`, store the credential (+sign_count, aaguid, transports). Assertion is **always-open** (a passwordless login carries no bearer token): `POST …/assert/begin` (allows a username-less / discoverable assertion — an unknown username yields an empty allow-list, indistinguishable from "use a discoverable credential", so no existence oracle) → `AssertionOptions` + a per-attempt session id; `POST …/assert/complete` → verify the assertion against the stored public key, **enforce sign_count clone-detection** (reject when the new counter ≤ the stored one for authenticators that use counters > 0 — both via Fido2NetLib's `StoredSignatureCounter` and a belt-and-braces check of our own), and on success **issue the SAME `{token,expiresAt,scopes,refreshToken}` as `/auth/login`** (`JwtTokenService` + `RefreshTokenService`) + advance the counter + stamp last-used. `GET …/credentials` (the caller's own, never the key) + `DELETE …/credentials/{id}` (only the caller's own). A structured **audit log** (`AuthLog`, `LoggerMessage`) adds passkey registered/failed, assertion ok/failed, **clone-detected**, and deleted — username / IP / reason, never key material. All assert failures return the same generic 401 (no oracle for credential existence / clone / bad signature).

**Web (`@simplewebauthn/browser` 13).** `login.tsx`'s passkey button → `assert/begin` → `startAuthentication()` → `assert/complete` → `auth.login(...)` with the returned token+refresh pair (same as a password login). `users.tsx` gains a live Passkeys row on the signed-in user's OWN row: "Add passkey" → `register/begin` → `startRegistration()` → `register/complete`, plus a list with delete; other users' rows show a static "self-service" indicator. `lib/api.ts` webauthn methods + `lib/types.ts` shapes. The button enables only in a secure context (`window.isSecureContext` + the WebAuthn API present) — HTTPS or localhost — and a user-cancelled ceremony (`NotAllowedError`) is silent. **Mock mode stays building and does NOT fake a ceremony** (the button reports unsupported). Fido2 pulls `Microsoft.Bcl.Memory` 9.0.0 transitively (CVE-2026-26127); pinned the first patched 9.x (9.0.14) via CPM transitive pinning so warnings-as-errors (NU1903) stays clean.

**The headline verification — a real CDP virtual-authenticator E2E** (`scripts/passkey-e2e.sh` + `.mjs`, Docker-Playwright per `web/packetnet-ui/scripts/screenshot.sh`). One `node:22-bookworm-slim` container runs BOTH the self-contained pdn node AND chromium on **`http://localhost:8080`** (a secure context over plain HTTP — exactly the tier-1 case), enables auth + bootstraps an admin over the API, then adds a CDP **virtual authenticator** (`WebAuthn.addVirtualAuthenticator` — ctap2/internal/resident-key/UV) and drives the UI to **register a passkey** (real signed attestation the node's Fido2 verifier accepts) then **sign in with it passwordlessly** (real signed assertion), asserting the authenticated panel renders — a genuine ceremony end-to-end, not a stub.

**Integration (tier-3 readiness for the lab/iPhone path).** Reviewed + integrated the sub-agent branch with two changes so the configurable knobs are not just present but *exercised* for the real-domain case the lab actually needs (`pdn.m0lte.uk` reached from a phone on the WLAN, never localhost): (1) the origin-set computation was extracted from `WebAuthnFido2Builder.ForRequest` into a public `AcceptedOrigins(cfg, request)` and locked with `WebAuthnFido2BuilderTests` (7 tests) — the load-bearing property being that **when the operator pins `allowedOrigins`, ONLY those are trusted and a request arriving with a spoofed `Host` cannot widen the set**, plus serving-origin rendering (non-default port present, default port absent), the zero-config localhost default, trailing-slash trim, and multi-origin pinning; (2) corrected a stale doc comment that still said the register group was `operate`-gated — it is deliberately `read`-gated (the floor for an authenticated user managing their own login credential), matching `Program.cs`. No behavioural change to the shipped flow; the localhost E2E remains the proof of the live ceremony.

**Verified:** `dotnet build -c Release` clean under warnings-as-errors (only pre-existing `tools/*.Spike` warnings; none in `src/Packet.Node*`); **474** Node tests green (+40: credential-store add/get/get-all/update-count/owner-scoped-delete/dup-id/persist/degrade; challenge-cache single-use/expiry/user-binding/session-binding/supersede/prune/wrong-type; WebAuthn-config validator rules incl. RP-id-not-an-IP + absolute-origin pinning; the `WebAuthnFido2BuilderTests` tier split above; and `WebAuthnApiTests` plumbing — assert/begin issues distinct server challenges, assert/complete is single-use + generic-401, register/begin is gated + 409s when auth-off + reflects the principal's name, credentials list, auth-off no-regression). Web: `VITE_API_MODE=live` tsc+vite build clean + 10/10 vitest. The CDP-virtual-authenticator E2E passes (register → passwordless sign-in on localhost). Distribution tiers parked per the doc; the tier-3 real-cert/DNS lab wiring (`pdn.m0lte.uk` via Cloudflare DNS-01 into the existing `management.https` cert slot) is operational config, tracked separately.


### 2026-06-09 — Auth part 2/4: refresh-token rotation + reuse detection, silent access-token renewal, and login lockout

Second of the four "nail auth" parts (TLS · **refresh tokens** · passkeys · over-RF sysop TOTP), extending the existing web-auth foundation rather than reinventing it. The access JWT is now short-lived and paired with a long-lived **refresh token**, so a session survives an access-token expiry by silently rotating rather than forcing a re-login.

**The security core — opaque token, hash at rest, one-time-use rotation.** The refresh token handed to the client is a 256-bit CSPRNG value (`RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes(32)`, base64url) — unguessable, never derived from anything. Only its **SHA-256 hash is stored** (base64url), and lookups are by hash, exactly the way a password is never stored in clear. A plain (unsalted) SHA-256 is the right primitive here, deliberately *unlike* the Argon2id+salt used for passwords: the input is already 256 bits of CSPRNG entropy, so there is nothing to brute-force and a per-token salt would only break the by-hash lookup. New `IRefreshTokenStore` + `SqliteRefreshTokenStore` (`src/Packet.Node.Core/Auth/`) follow the same resilient pdn.db pattern as `SqliteUserStore` — WAL, a fresh pooled connection per call, `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` schema on the meta-row approach (no `PRAGMA user_version` fight with the routing store), and every op wrapped so a store fault degrades (lookup→null, write→false) and never throws the node down. Table `refresh_token(token_hash PRIMARY KEY, username, family, issued_utc, expires_utc, revoked)` + a `family` index. The rotation/reuse logic lives in a cohesive, web-free, `TimeProvider`-driven `RefreshTokenService` (unit-testable on `FakeTimeProvider`).

**The exact rotation + reuse-detection algorithm** (`RefreshTokenService.Rotate`, look up by hash): not-found → reject (`Invalid`); **already-revoked → revoke the ENTIRE family and reject (`ReuseDetected`)** — a replayed/consumed token is the theft signal, and burning the family bounds a stolen token to a single rotation; expired → revoke it + reject (`Expired`); valid → mark it revoked (one-time use), mint a successor **in the same family**, return the new pair (`Rotated`). A `family` is the chain of tokens descending from one login (its own random id). Login issues a token in a fresh family; logout (`RevokeFamily`) revokes the presented token's whole family; a refresh whose user was deleted out from under the session burns the family rather than mint for a ghost. All four reject outcomes are a generic 401 to the client (no oracle); the outcome only steers the audit log. The user's CURRENT scope is read fresh from the store on each refresh, so a scope change / deletion takes effect on the next renew, not only on re-login.

**Login hardening (lockout).** A new in-memory `LoginThrottle` (`src/Packet.Node.Core/Auth/`, `TimeProvider`-driven sliding window) counts failures keyed per-username AND per-source-IP; once **either** key crosses the threshold (default **5 failures within a 5-minute window**) the login is refused with **429** — checked BEFORE the user lookup / password verify, so a locked account or IP also stops burning Argon2 CPU and the lockout adds no user-existence oracle. The window self-heals (oldest in-window failure ages out → unlocked, no separate timer); a successful login resets both counters; memory is bounded (per-key history is trimmed on each touch, a global sweep runs past a soft cap) so a flood of distinct usernames/IPs can't leak. A structured **audit log** (`LoggerMessage` source-gen, `AuthLog`) emits one line per login success/failure, lockout, refresh success/rejection, reuse-detection, and logout — carrying username / source-IP / outcome but **never** a password, token, or token hash.

**Endpoints** (`PdnAuthApi.cs` + `Program.cs`): `/auth/login` success now returns `{token, expiresAt, scopes, refreshToken}` (issues the refresh token alongside the JWT); new always-open `POST /auth/refresh {refreshToken}` rotates → the same `LoginResponse` shape (new access + new refresh token), 401 on invalid/expired/reused; new always-open `POST /auth/logout {refreshToken}` revokes the family → 204 (idempotent — an unknown/blank token is still 204). The refresh store/service are gated on the signing key exactly like `JwtTokenService` and injected `[FromServices] RefreshTokenService?` so an unregistered service (degraded boot) resolves to null → 503 rather than aborting minimal-API startup (the same lesson as the part-1 degraded-boot fix). `LoginThrottle` is always-registered (pure in-memory + clock) but still injected optional. **Config:** `AuthConfig.RefreshTokenMinutes` (default 10080 = 7 days), validated positive and `> AccessTokenMinutes` when both set (a refresh token that didn't outlive its access token would defeat the silent renew).

**Web (`auth.tsx` / `api.ts` / `login.tsx` / `router.tsx` / `types.ts`).** Both tokens persist in `sessionStorage` under `pdn.session` (the shape gains `refreshToken`). `authFetch` on a 401 attempts ONE silent refresh and retries the original request once; it avoids the two classic hazards — **no infinite loop** (the `/auth/refresh` call itself is a *bare* fetch, never `authFetch`, so a 401 from refresh can't recurse into another refresh) and **no stampede** (a single shared in-flight refresh promise dedupes concurrent 401s — a burst of parallel requests all expiring at once triggers exactly one `/auth/refresh` and they all await it; the slot clears once it settles so a later 401 can refresh again). Only if the refresh fails does it dispatch `pdn:unauthorized` → logout. `logout` best-effort revokes server-side (`POST /auth/logout`) before clearing the session, wired via a registration hook (`setLogoutRevoker`) to avoid an `auth.tsx`↔`api.ts` import cycle. Mock mode keeps working (login/refresh synthesise a fresh synthetic pair each call so a mock rotation visibly rotates). Login surfaces 429 as a plain error.

**Verified:** `dotnet build -c Release` clean under warnings-as-errors (the only warnings are pre-existing in the `tools/*.Spike` scratch projects — none in `src/Packet.Node*`); **434** Node tests green (+35: `RefreshTokenServiceTests` — issue→rotate gives a new usable token + revokes the old, a rotated old token → reuse → family burned, a sibling-family is untouched, expired rejected, logout revokes the family, store-fault degrades-not-throws, hash is deterministic + not the plaintext; `SqliteRefreshTokenStoreTests` — insert/find-by-hash, single + whole-family revoke, prune-expired, persistence across reopen, broken-store degrade; `LoginThrottleTests` — N→lock, window-slide reset, success reset, per-username/per-IP independence, defaults 5/5min; `AuthApiTests` integration — login returns a refreshToken, refresh rotates (old → 401, reuse burns the successor), invalid/blank → 401, logout → 204 + revokes, 5 bad logins → 429 even with the right password, and refresh/logout exist + gated APIs serve tokenless with auth OFF; plus `NodeConfigValidatorTests` refresh>access rules). Web: `VITE_API_MODE=live` tsc+vite build clean + 10/10 vitest. No new package (SHA-256 / `RandomNumberGenerator` are in `System.Security.Cryptography`). Default-off auth contract preserved — with `management.auth.enabled:false` the refresh/logout endpoints are mapped but the gated APIs still serve tokenless exactly as before.

### 2026-06-09 — Candidate pattern captured: passkeys on a LAN-only node (trust + naming) — `docs/passkeys-lan-trust-pattern.md`

Captured the design for the open question the TLS work below (auth part 1/4) explicitly parked: a self-signed cert encrypts password/JWT traffic but does **not** give the *trusted* secure context WebAuthn needs, so how does a node people run on their own LANs — never internet-exposed — offer passkeys without making every client (phones included) install a root CA? New design doc [`docs/passkeys-lan-trust-pattern.md`](passkeys-lan-trust-pattern.md), **status: candidate — not adopted, nothing built**; it informs auth **part 3 (passkeys)**, not a locked decision. The two hard browser constraints it turns on: WebAuthn needs a secure context (trusted cert) **and** the RP ID must be a registrable domain, **never an IP** — so `https://<lan-ip>` can't do passkeys even with a valid cert. The actionable takeaways usable today: (a) **`localhost` is exempt from both**, so same-machine passkey dev needs zero infra — bind loopback, `rp.id = localhost`, build the `Fido2NetLib` register/verify flow there first (origin and RP ID coincide, avoiding the doc's most error-prone bit); (b) for the **lab box reached from a phone** (deployed `pdn`, iPhone on the WLAN — the realistic loop, where loopback doesn't apply), the manual single-operator path (doc §2a) is a **real wildcard cert via DNS-01** (Cloudflare + LE, an owned domain) dropped into the *existing* `management.https` bring-your-own-cert slot — **no new node code** — with `pdn.lab.<domain>` resolved to the box's LAN IP (split-horizon, or a grey-cloud A record); trusted on iOS with no profile install. `B + mDNS` is that same path *automated* for operators who don't control DNS. The distribution candidate is **`B + mDNS`**: a vendor domain + call-home ACME issues a real per-install wildcard cert (key stays on the box) behind a new `TlsCertificateProvider` impl; mDNS gives zero-config discovery (`.local`, since `.local` can't hold a public cert — hence a deliberate two-name split); a stable per-install subdomain is the RP ID so passkeys survive LAN-IP changes; an sslip.io-style label decodes the IP so there are no per-device DNS writes. Named residual risk: public-name→private-IP trips DNS-rebinding protection on some networks (mitigations in the doc). Alternatives (private-CA install, Tailscale `tsnet`, Cloudflare Tunnel, shared-key public certs) recorded with why-not. Doc-only — no code; supersedes nothing (the shipped self-signed TLS still earns its keep encrypting non-passkey traffic).

### 2026-06-09 — Auth part 1/4: optional HTTPS/TLS for the web control panel (self-signed-on-first-run or bring-your-own), DEFAULT-OFF

First of the four "nail auth" parts (TLS · refresh tokens · passkeys · over-RF sysop TOTP). The panel served plain HTTP, so the password + JWT crossed the LAN in clear; TLS encrypts that and is the prerequisite secure context for passkeys. New `management.https` (`HttpsConfig` under `ManagementConfig`): `Enabled` (default **false** — HTTP-only as before, no regression), `Bind`, `Port` (8443), `CertificatePath?` + `CertificatePassword?` (a PKCS#12), `GenerateSelfSignedOnMissing` (default true). A new web-free `TlsCertificateProvider` (`src/Packet.Node.Core/`) loads the supplied cert, else generates a self-signed one (`CertificateRequest`/`CreateSelfSigned`, RSA-2048/SHA-256, SAN = localhost + machine name + bind IP, 2-year, serverAuth EKU — .NET built-in, **no new package**) and persists it as a PKCS#12 (0600) beside the node state (`{stateDir}/certs/server.pfx`), reused across restarts; every failure path returns null + logs so the host just skips the HTTPS endpoint and HTTP keeps serving (TLS misconfig never takes the node down). `Program.cs` adds a second Kestrel `options.Listen(...).UseHttps(cert)` when enabled. `ManagementValidator` gates it (port range, bind non-empty, no http/https or telnet/https collision, cert path required when self-signed-gen is off). Packaging: `postinst` creates `/var/lib/packetnet/certs` (0750 packetnet); the conffile gets a commented `https:` block. Web: a TLS sub-section in **Config → Management** (toggle + bind/port + cert path, with the self-signed/secure-context caveat in the hint), wired through the existing config PUT path; the client's relative `/api/v1` base means HTTPS "just works" client-side. **Verified:** 399 Node tests (12 new — HttpsConfig validation + the cert provider generate/persist/reuse/regenerate/supplied/missing/disabled paths); UI `VITE_API_MODE=live` build + 10/10 vitest; **locally smoke-tested** — node with `https.enabled` serves `https://…/healthz` + `/api/v1/status` 200 (and HTTP still serves), the self-signed cert generated + persisted (0600, CN=callsign, 2-yr). Lab live-verify (`curl -k https://10.x.x.x:8443`) on deploy. Passkeys (part 3) need a *trusted* secure context, so over a LAN IP the self-signed cert must be trust-installed (or reach the node via localhost) — noted; self-signed still earns its keep encrypting password/token traffic.

### 2026-06-09 — Node ID beacons: a per-port periodic UI-frame presence broadcast, DEFAULT-OFF, hot-reloadable, wired end-to-end (config → host → wire + the live web tab)

The node can now transmit an ID beacon — a periodic connectionless AX.25 UI frame (dest the literal `BEACON` callsign, PID `0xF0`, info = the expanded beacon text) — on each port. **Default-OFF is the load-bearing contract:** `BeaconConfig.Enabled` and the per-port override default `false`, so a node that never configured a beacon arms no timer and transmits nothing — byte-for-byte as before beacons existed. **Config model** (`src/Packet.Node.Core/Configuration/NodeConfig.cs`): a system-default `NodeConfig.Beacon` (`BeaconConfig { Enabled=false, IntervalMinutes=30, Text="{node} pdn node" }`) plus an optional per-port `PortConfig.Beacon` (`PortBeaconConfig { Enabled, IntervalMinutes?, Text? }` — the `Enabled` flag is authoritative for the port; null interval/text inherit the default, resolved by the pure `EffectiveBeacon.Resolve`). It binds through the existing camel-case YAML + `IgnoreUnmatchedProperties` deserializer (no custom converter — it's pure bools/ints/strings, exactly like the `inp3:` block), round-trips through the config-write seam, and is readable via `GET /api/v1/config` / writable via `PUT /api/v1/config` (the records serialize straight to JSON). Validation (`NodeConfigValidator`): interval ≥ 1, non-empty text when enabled (per-field for the nullable override). **Text expansion** reuses the services-banner `{node}`/`{call}` logic, extracted into a shared `NodeTextTemplate` so the banner and the beacon can never drift (`{call}` = callsign, `{node}` = alias else callsign). **The scheduler** is a new `BeaconService` (`src/Packet.Node.Core/Beacons/`) modelled on `NodeTelemetry`'s attach/detach lifecycle + the NET/ROM NODES-origination send pattern: per-port `TimeProvider.CreateTimer` (so tests drive it on `FakeTimeProvider`), it sends through an `IBeaconChannel` seam (`ListenerBeaconChannel` adapts a live `Ax25Listener.SendUiAsync`; a fake satisfies it in tests), the async timer callback swallows+logs faults (never throws onto the timer, no unobserved Task) with a self-overlap guard so a wedged modem can't pile up sends, and it never needs the host's `RunExclusiveAsync` gate (UI sends only, never touches the port set). **Host wiring**: the supervisor attaches/detaches beacons at the same site as telemetry (`PortSupervisor.BringUpAsync`/`TearDownAsync`); `NodeHostedService` owns it (optional ctor param defaulting to a self-constructed instance, so existing direct-construction tests are unchanged) and DI-registers it as a singleton (`Program.cs` + `NodeServiceCollectionExtensions`). **Hot-reload** re-arms via `BeaconService.Reapply()`, which the host calls after *every* reconcile — including the no-op-plan path, because a beacon-only edit is invisible to the port-supervisor reconcile planner — so a changed interval/text/enabled takes effect live (each port re-resolves its effective beacon from the live config, exactly the way the console reads `ServicesConfig` live). **UI**: the Config → Beacons tab moved from mock data to the live `NodeConfig` (system default = `cfg.beacon`, per-port override = `cfg.ports[i].beacon`, written through the same dirty-set/reconcile-preview save path the other tabs use; mock mode still renders because `NODE_CONFIG` carries the beacon fields). **Verification:** 383 Node tests green (+30 beacon tests: scheduler on `FakeTimeProvider` — enabled fires once per interval to BEACON/0xF0/expanded-text, disabled is silent, override beats default for interval+text, field-by-field inherit, attach→detach stops, Reapply re-arms live + on/off; plus YAML round-trip, validation, the default-config-transmits-nothing no-regression assertion, and the `{node}`/`{call}` expansion); `dotnet build -c Release` clean under warnings-as-errors (the only warnings are pre-existing in the `tools/*.Spike` scratch projects); the web UI builds (`VITE_API_MODE=live`, tsc+vite) clean and 10/10 vitest. Live-verified on the lab after merge.

### 2026-06-09 — De-flake AxPinger: arm the per-probe timeout before the send (a genuine test/runtime race, not the infra flake)

`AxPingerTests` (new this session, with the connectionless TEST-ping work) intermittently hung in CI — `Mixed_success_and_loss_aggregates_correctly` timing out at ~30 s. This was a REAL race in `AxPinger.PingOnceAsync`, not the known box-oversubscription integration flake: the per-probe timeout (`Task.Delay(perPingTimeout, clock)`) was created AFTER `await channel.SendTestAsync(...)`, but the send is exactly what makes the probe observably "sent" (the unit test waits on `channel.Sent.Count == N`, then advances the `FakeTimeProvider`). Under load the run task could still be between "send returned" and "timeout armed" when the test advanced the clock — and an advance that lands before the `Task.Delay` timer is registered is simply lost (no timer to fire), so the timeout probe never completed and the whole run hung. Fix: create the timeout timer BEFORE the send (RTT still starts at the send, so timing is unaffected; the sub-µs gap is immaterial against a multi-second real timeout), which guarantees the timer is registered by the time the probe is observably sent — closing the interleaving structurally. Verified: the `AxPingerTests` ran 40/40 green under a full-core CPU-hog stress load (the condition that surfaced it in CI); 357 Node tests green overall. Pure timing-ordering fix in `src/Packet.Node.Core/Api/AxPinger.cs`; no behaviour change on the wire.

### 2026-06-09 — Monitor history: the live monitor bootstraps with recent frames instead of an empty table

The web monitor only ever showed frames that arrived *after* it was opened — a freshly-loaded monitor sat empty until the next frame on the air. `NodeTelemetry` now keeps a bounded ring of the most recent decoded `MonitorEvent`s (capacity 250, appended in `Observe` under a dedicated lock kept off the Interlocked counter path), exposed as `RecentFrames(limit)` (oldest→newest snapshot). A new read endpoint `GET /api/v1/monitor/recent?limit=N` (gated `read`, bounded to the ring) serves it, added to the locked OpenAPI in `docs/node-api.yaml`. The web client's `useFrameStream` hook seeds its table from `api.recentFrames(cap)` on mount, then live-streams `/events` as before — slotting the history *under* any live frames that arrived during the bootstrap fetch and deduping the overlap by `seq` (so a boundary frame present in both the ring snapshot and the live feed appears once, in the right order). The first-render guard on the monitor's smooth-prepend means the seeded rows render statically — no 250-frame animation storm — and the live prepend animation is unchanged. Mock mode seeds from `mock.seedFrames` so `npm run dev` still fills. 357 Node tests green (4 new `RecentFrames` tests: empty-before-any, last-N oldest-first, all-when-fewer + non-positive-limit clamp, bounded-to-capacity); UI `tsc`+`vite` clean + 10/10 vitest.

### 2026-06-09 — Web UI mobile polish: zoom-locked viewport + info tooltips portaled out of clipping containers

Two small fixes from a poke around the live panel on iPhone/Chrome. (1) **Mobile zoom locked** — the viewport meta gains `maximum-scale=1, user-scalable=no, viewport-fit=cover` and `body { touch-action: manipulation }`, so the control panel behaves like an app (no pinch / double-tap / input-focus zoom) rather than a document. (2) **Info `(i)` tooltips no longer render behind/clipped by their card** — the `Tooltip` primitive (`src/components/ui/index.tsx`, also used by `InfoHint`) was an absolutely-positioned descendant, so a hint in a table heading was clipped by the table's `overflow-hidden` Card → `overflow-x-auto` scroll box. It now renders its panel via `createPortal` into `document.body` with `position: fixed` computed from the trigger's rect (shown on hover OR focus = tap on touch), so it escapes every overflow/stacking ancestor; verified with a Docker-Playwright shot of a routes-table header hint sitting fully above the heading. UI-only; `tsc`+`vite` clean, 10/10 vitest. **Captured as follow-ups (not in this PR):** the Ports screen's "NinoTNC test frame received" banner can flash spuriously on a node with no NinoTNC (gate it to a real `nino-tnc`-port test-frame event), and the live monitor should bootstrap with a bounded ring buffer of recent frames replayed on connect instead of starting empty.

### 2026-06-09 — Packaged-node fix: the service can write its own config dir, and the host degrades (not aborts) when `pdn.db` is unwritable

Two robustness fixes for the `.deb`-packaged node, both surfaced while enabling auth on the lab.

**1 — config-write was broken on a packaged node.** First-run `/setup` (and, by the same path, `PUT /config` and all port CRUD) 500'd live with `System.IO.IOException: Read-only file system : '/etc/packetnet/packetnet.yaml.tmp'`: the management API rewrites its YAML conffile through an atomic write (a `.tmp` in the same dir, then a rename), but the service couldn't write `/etc/packetnet` — the systemd unit had `ProtectSystem=strict` with `ReadWritePaths=/var/lib/packetnet` only, and the dir was shipped `root:root 0755`. So the whole config-write reconcile seam (shipped in Slice-3 steps 2/3) silently didn't work on a real packaged node; the earlier "config-write live-verify" had run on the pre-`.deb` rsync deploy, where the config lived in a writable working dir. Fix: `packaging/packetnet.service` adds `/etc/packetnet` to `ReadWritePaths` (the sandbox half), and `packaging/postinst` `chown`s `/etc/packetnet` (dir, `0750`) + the conffile (`0640`) to the `packetnet` service user (the unix-perms half; hand edits as root still work). `scripts/deb-install-smoke.sh` now asserts both are owned by `packetnet`.

**2 — the host aborted at startup if `pdn.db` was unwritable, instead of degrading.** When the DB can't be opened the routing store and user store both degrade (warn + disable — "persistence is disabled" / "web auth is unavailable for this run"), which leaves `JwtTokenService` null and unregistered. But the `/auth/login` handler took a `JwtTokenService?` parameter, and minimal-API parameter inference can't classify an *unregistered* complex-type parameter — it threw `InvalidOperationException: Failure to infer one or more parameters … tokens | UNKNOWN` at startup and aborted the entire host (SIGABRT), contradicting the auth foundation's own documented "login → 503, but the node still boots" intent. Fix: mark the parameter `[FromServices] JwtTokenService?` so it binds as an *optional* service → resolves to null when unregistered → login returns 503, while liveness + the (auth-off) read API keep serving. This never triggers on a normal node (`pdn.db` lives in the writable `/var/lib/packetnet` StateDirectory) but hardens against a full/locked state dir, and it restores `deb-install-smoke.sh` (which boots the packaged binary from a read-only cwd, so `pdn.db` can't be created there).

**Verified.** Live on the lab (`root@pdn-lab`): after the redeploy, `POST /setup` → 200 (admin bootstrapped, the `gb7rdg` port + `M9YYY` identity preserved through the reconcile), then `management.auth.enabled` flipped true → tokenless `/status` 401, login → JWT, Bearer → 200, and the browser flow (login → authenticated dashboard → live monitor "streaming" via SSE `?access_token=`) all green. `deb-install-smoke.sh` PASS on debian-stable (4 sections incl. boot-from-read-only-cwd + both ownership assertions). New `Packet.Node.Tests` regression `Node_boots_and_degrades_to_503_when_the_db_is_unwritable` (boot with an unopenable `pdn.db` → `/healthz` 200 + `/status` 200 + `/auth/login` 503); **353** Node tests green; clean warnings-as-errors build. Packaging + host-only — no library/sibling-repo bearing.

### 2026-06-09 — Enable auth: SSE token-by-query + the full SPA auth wiring — the control panel now logs in (or runs open), verified end-to-end

Completes the enable-auth arc on top of [the foundation](#17-amendment-log) — the machinery shipped default-off last; this makes a node turn it on and the web UI work with it on. Two pieces, one backend + one UI.

**Backend (host) — the only server change.** `EventSource` cannot set an `Authorization` header, so the SSE endpoints accept the JWT as a `?access_token=` query parameter. `Program.cs` gains a `JwtBearerEvents.OnMessageReceived` that, *only* when no bearer header was supplied AND the path is one of the two SSE routes (`/api/v1/events` or `/api/v1/sessions/{id}/stream`), reads the token from `?access_token=`. Every other endpoint stays header-only; a bogus or expired query token 401s exactly like a bad header. That narrow, path-scoped fallback is the whole backend delta — the foundation already wired `AddJwtBearer` / `UseAuthentication` / the scope policies.

**UI (SPA, `web/packetnet-ui/`) — the mock gate becomes real.** `app/auth.tsx` is now a real session provider: token + username + scopes in `sessionStorage` (`pdn.session`), a `has(scope)` helper, and a listener for a `pdn:unauthorized` window event that logs out. `app/router.tsx` gates startup against the real endpoints — `GET /setup/state` → if `needsSetup`, show the setup wizard; else probe `GET /status` with any stored token → 200 enters the panel / 401 routes to login; **when auth is off the probe succeeds tokenless and the app enters anonymously** (an open node shows no login wall, preserving today's behaviour). `lib/api.ts` `authFetch` attaches the Bearer header and dispatches `pdn:unauthorized` on a 401 (an expired session bounces to login mid-use); the SSE helpers append `?access_token=` so the monitor + console streams authenticate. Real CRUD on `screens/{login,setup,users}.tsx` (the passkey button is visible-but-disabled — WebAuthn is a named follow-up), a user menu + logout in `components/layout/shell.tsx`, and light scope-gating hides operate-only actions from a read user. Mock mode keeps a synthetic session so `npm run dev` still runs offline.

**Verified end-to-end, locally, with auth ON** (temp conffile + a fresh `pdn.db`, curl against the running host): `setup/state`→`needsSetup:true`; `/status` tokenless → **401**; `POST /setup` creates the admin (scope `admin`); `setup/state`→`needsSetup:false`; login → a JWT; `/status`+Bearer → **200**; wrong-password and unknown-user logins are **both** a generic 401 (no user-existence oracle); and the new SSE path: `/events?access_token=<jwt>` → **200**, no-token → **401**, bogus-token → **401**, `Authorization: Bearer` header → **200** (the header path still works). A created `read`-scope user then proves the gates enforce: 200 on `/status` (read), **403** on `/ping` (operate), **403** on `/users` (admin). C# 352 `Packet.Node.Tests` green; the UI `VITE_API_MODE=live` build is clean (tsc+vite) and 10/10 vitest. **Lab rollout** rides the standing deploy: ship the `.deb` with auth still OFF in the lab conffile (the panel keeps serving open exactly as today), then a deliberate flip — bootstrap the admin through the setup wizard and set `management.auth.enabled: true` — and confirm the live login → authenticated panel + authenticated SSE streams (and a 401 without a token). **STILL DEFERRED (named):** WebAuthn/passkeys, TOTP/2FA, refresh tokens; whether the shipped config template's default should flip to auth-on; the node still binds `0.0.0.0` with no auth on the lab today (Tom cleared that for this stage). Host-only — no `@packet-net/ax25` / pico-node bearing.

### 2026-06-09 — Slice-3 backend auth foundation (Argon2id + HS256 JWT + scope gates), DEFAULT-OFF behind `management.auth.enabled`

The web control-API gets its authentication backbone — built, tested, and wired, but **enforcing nothing until the operator turns it on**. New config flag `management.auth.enabled` on a new `AuthConfig` under `ManagementConfig`, default **false**: with it off the API behaves byte-for-byte as before (the read/SSE/config/ports/sessions/ping endpoints + the SPA all serve unauthenticated), so turning auth on is a deliberate, reviewed step and never a silent regression for a running node. The machinery is always wired; only the per-endpoint scope check is conditional on the flag.

**Core (web-free, `Packet.Node.Core/Auth/`):** `SqliteUserStore` persists users + the JWT signing key to the same consolidated `pdn.db` the routing table uses (raw SQL + Dapper + WAL, fresh pooled connection per call, resilient — a fault logs and degrades, never crashes the node — mirroring `SqliteNetRomRoutingStore`; a `user` table + an `auth_meta` table, schema by a meta sentinel so it doesn't fight the routing store over `PRAGMA user_version`). `PasswordHasher` does Argon2id via the MIT-licensed `Konscious.Security.Cryptography.Argon2`, OWASP params (m=19 MiB, t=2, p=1), a 16-byte CSPRNG per-user salt, a 32-byte digest, stored as a self-describing PHC string (`$argon2id$v=19$m=…,t=…,p=…$salt$hash`) so verify reads the params back out, with `CryptographicOperations.FixedTimeEquals` on the digest. `JwtTokenService` issues + validates HS256 JWTs against a 256-bit `RandomNumberGenerator` key persisted in `pdn.db` (tokens survive a restart; never logged), `sub`=username + a single `scope` claim, ~1h lifetime, a `LifetimeValidator` driven by the injected `TimeProvider` (no wall-clock, §2.7). `AuthScopes` encodes the implication admin ⊃ operate ⊃ read by rank in the authorization check (one `scope` claim, not all-three-stuffed).

**Host (`Packet.Node/Api/`):** `PdnAuthApi` maps `POST /auth/login` (→ `{token,expiresAt,scopes}`/generic 401; unknown-user and bad-password are indistinguishable — same code path, the absent user is verified against a fixed decoy Argon2 hash so the cost is constant w.r.t. existence, same generic message), `GET /setup/state` (`{needsSetup}`, unauthenticated bootstrap probe), `POST /setup` (one-shot while zero users: creates the admin + applies identity/firstPort through the existing `IWritableConfigProvider.TryApply` seam — reused, not reinvented — 403 once an admin exists, min-password-length validated), and the admin-gated `/users` list/create/delete (hashes never returned; can't delete the last admin). The `read`/`operate`/`admin` policies + a flag-aware `ScopeRequirementHandler` (passes the requirement through unconditionally when auth is off; when on, requires an authenticated principal whose scope `Satisfies` the endpoint's) gate the existing groups: reads (`/status`,`/ports` GET,`/sessions` GET,`/netrom/routes`,`/config` GET,`/links`,`/log`,`/events`,`/config/raw` GET) → `read`; writes/actions (`PUT /config`(+raw), ports POST/PUT/DELETE+lifecycle, sessions POST/DELETE/send/stream, `/ping`) → `operate`; `/users` → `admin`. `/healthz`, `/setup/state`, `/auth/login`, `/setup`, and the SPA static files are always open.

`Packet.Node.Core` stays web-free (store + hashing + token model in Core; the `JwtBearer`/`UseAuthentication`/policy wiring + endpoints in the host). New packages via CPM: `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.JwtBearer` 10.0.8 (host) + `Microsoft.IdentityModel.Tokens` 8.5.0 (Core; `Konscious…Argon2` + `…JsonWebTokens` were already pinned). 28 new tests (Argon2 hash/verify/salted/malformed-safe, JWT issue/validate/tampered/expired/wrong-key, the user-store round-trip incl. unique-username + count + last-login + key persistence, the scope implication, the YAML auth round-trip, and the integration `AuthApiTests`: login good/bad/unknown-indistinguishable, the setup needs→bootstrap→not-needed→one-shot flow, **no-regression with auth off** — read+write reach the handler tokenless — and **enforced with auth on** — 401 no token / 403 read-token-on-operate / 200 sufficient, login+setup-state stay open). 349 `Packet.Node.Tests` green; clean Release build (warnings-are-errors). **DEFERRED (named, not built):** WebAuthn/passkeys, TOTP/2FA, refresh tokens, and ALL the UI wiring (the login/setup/users screens stay on their current mock gate — a later pass wires them to these endpoints). The human reviews the security before this is ever enabled.
### 2026-06-09 — Connectionless AX.25 TEST responder (axping answer side) — `Ax25Listener` now answers an inbound TEST; XRouter confirmed a TEST interop target

Closes the responder half of the connectionless TEST exchange (§4.3.4.2) — the initiator (`SendTestAsync` + `AxPinger`) shipped earlier this day; now our node answers an inbound TEST instead of dropping it. `Ax25Listener.DispatchInbound` gains a connectionless TEST intercept *before* any session routing (right after the `Destination == MyCall` address filter): a TEST frame addressed to us — `(Control & 0xEF) == 0xE3` — is handled link-independently and returns without ever entering the session machine. A TEST *command* (`IsCommand`) is answered by `RespondToTest`, which builds a TEST *response* via the strict `Ax25Frame.Test` factory (destination = the command's source, source = `MyCall`, info = the command's info echoed verbatim, `isCommand: false`, F bit mirroring the command's P bit), sends it on the modem, and traces it `Transmitted` — mirroring `SendUiAsync`/`SendTestAsync`. The send is fire-and-forget on a tracked `Task.Run` with a try/catch (the pump is sync `void`; the modem send is async — we don't block the pump and we don't leave an unobserved Task/ValueTask: CA2012-clean), and the echoed info is materialised before the async send because the inbound buffer is recycled once `DispatchInbound` returns. A TEST *response* arriving inbound (`!IsCommand`) is now **absorbed** here too rather than routed onward — its `FrameTraced` already fired upstream on the pump (so the `AxPinger` initiator still correlates it), and routing it into the transient Disconnected session only produced a spurious connectionless DM via the t05 catch-all (back at a station that just answered our probe). This is a small fix to the prior "TEST → t05 → DM" behaviour: TEST is connectionless and never belongs in the session machine; the stale dispatch/`ReclassifyForDisconnectedCatchAll` comments that listed TEST under the catch-all cluster were corrected. Additive, observation-safe (never touches a session's state), spec-faithful (outbound construction stays strict), warnings-as-errors clean. Tests: `Ax25ListenerTestResponderTests` (5, in `Packet.Ax25.Tests` over `LoopbackModem`) — command→echoing response with the right C/R + P↔F bits + verbatim info + no session created; empty-info echo; P=0→F=0; a TEST *response* inbound is received/traced but draws **no** reply (no ping-pong) and no session; a TEST command not addressed to us is ignored. `AxPingRoundTripIntegrationTests` (2, in `Packet.Node.Tests` over `InMemoryRadio.CreatePair()`) — A `AxPinger.RunAsync` → B (responder) gives 0% loss with non-null RTTs and no session on either side (the pdn↔pdn live-send proof we could not get on the lab because LinBPQ does not answer TEST); plus a no-responder→100%-loss case. **XRouter interop finding (empirical, docker stack):** unlike LinBPQ, **XRouter ANSWERS TEST** — an `Ax25Listener`+`AxPinger` probe against XRouter's NODECALL (PN0XRT) over net-sim returned 0% loss, all 3 probes echoed (RTT ≈ 2 s over the afsk1200 sim), confirmed across two runs. So TEST is a real interop target (XRouter + direwolf + the Linux kernel AX.25 stack implement it; LinBPQ does not). The probe is left as an Interop-tagged finding-recorder (`tests/Packet.Interop.Tests/Xrouter/XrouterTestFrameProbe.cs`, excluded from the default suite). C#-only (`src/Packet.Ax25` is the published core); the responder ships in the next `lib-v*` NuGet release. **Named follow-up:** ax25-ts (`@packet-net/ax25`) has the TEST initiator twin but not the responder — a TS-side parity item for `packet-net/ax25-ts`, not this repo.

### 2026-06-09 — The node `.deb` now ships the web UI; `build-deb.sh` gains a fast dev mode; `deploy-node.sh` installs a real `.deb`

Closes the gap where a clean-checkout release `.deb` shipped without the SPA the node serves. `scripts/build-deb.sh` now builds the web UI (`web/packetnet-ui`: `[ -d node_modules ] || npm ci` then `VITE_API_MODE=live npm run build`) before staging the `.deb` tree, and copies `dist/` into the package at `/opt/packetnet/app/wwwroot` (the node's `{ContentRoot}/wwwroot`), so the served control panel is now part of the packaged artifact — built by the same script GHA runs, not hand-staged. It also gains a `PDN_FAST` env knob: unset (the default, and what `publish-node.yml` uses for releases) keeps the full self-contained single-file ReadyToRun publish; `PDN_FAST=1` drops `-p:PublishReadyToRun=true`/`-p:PublishSingleFile=true`/`-p:IncludeNativeLibrariesForSelfExtract=true` (still `--self-contained true`) for a much faster publish at the cost of a slightly slower cold start — the dev-loop tradeoff. The publish flags are an array conditioned on `PDN_FAST`; the contents dump now also lists the `wwwroot` payload. `scripts/deploy-node.sh` is reworked to ship a real `.deb` instead of rsyncing a raw publish dir: it builds with `PDN_FAST=1 scripts/build-deb.sh linux-x64 <version>` (a per-build dev version `0.1.0+dev<UTCstamp>`, env-overridable via `PACKETNET_DEB_VERSION`, that sorts above the last release), `scp`s the `.deb` to the box, installs it with `dpkg -i --force-confold` (reinstalls regardless of version; keeps the lab's edited `/etc/packetnet/packetnet.yaml` conffile; falls back to `apt-get -f install` on unmet deps), removes the staged `.deb`, then restarts the unit explicitly and runs the existing `/healthz` + recent-logs liveness summary (`--skip-build` now deploys the most recent existing `artifacts/packetnet_*_amd64.deb`). `packaging/postinst` now `try-restart`s the unit on an upgrade (when `$2`, the old version, is non-empty — `enable --now` doesn't restart an already-running unit, so a real `apt upgrade` would otherwise keep running the old binary), guarded by the existing `-d /run/systemd/system` block and kept POSIX `sh`. `publish-node.yml` adds `actions/setup-node@v4` (node 22) after checkout so the publish job can run the UI build (no other workflow change; still `[self-hosted, Linux, X64]`). Verified: `PDN_FAST=1 scripts/build-deb.sh linux-x64` produces a `.deb` whose `dpkg-deb --contents` lists `wwwroot/index.html` + `wwwroot/assets/`, and `scripts/deb-install-smoke.sh` passes (installs + `/healthz` OK + purges) on Debian-stable + Ubuntu-LTS.

### 2026-06-09 — Slice-3 backend step 6: connectionless AX.25 TEST ping ("axping") — `POST /ping`

The ping tool goes live. A new `Ax25Listener.SendTestAsync` (the one library addition — a connectionless TEST command frame, P bit set, built via the existing `Ax25Frame.Test` factory and traced; not all nodes implement TEST, a non-responder simply never answers) + a pure `AxPinger` correlation core in `Packet.Node.Core/Api`: for each of N sequential probes it subscribes to the channel's `FrameTraced` BEFORE sending, sends a TEST command carrying a process-unique tag (`"PDNPING"` | per-run Guid | seq), and races the matching TEST response — `Received && (Control & 0xEF)==0xE3 && IsResponse && Source==target && Info==tag` — against a `TimeProvider`-driven per-ping timeout (RTT via `TimeProvider.GetElapsedTime`, no wall-clock §2.7). A minimal `IAxPingChannel` seam (MyCall + FrameTraced + SendTestAsync) makes the core unit-testable without a modem; `ListenerAxPingChannel` is the production adapter. `POST /api/v1/ping {station, portId, count}` (replaces the 501 in `PdnSessionsApi`; 400 bad station / 404 no running port; count clamped 1–20, 5s per-ping) → `PingResult {replies[{seq,rttMs,timeout}], minMs, avgMs, maxMs, lossPct}`. The web ping tool renders per-reply rtt/timeout + min/avg/max/loss, with a clear "no response — unreachable or TEST unsupported" state for 100% loss. The pinger only reads FrameTraced + calls the public send, so it runs OUTSIDE the `RunExclusiveAsync` gate. 314 Packet.Node.Tests green (AxPinger unit tests over a fake channel + a real-listener-over-InMemoryRadio echo end-to-end with a spec-compliant TEST responder, proving correlation + RTT; endpoint 400/404 WAF tests); UI tsc+vite + 10/10 vitest green. **Verified live on the lab: GB7RDG (LinBPQ) does NOT answer TEST — 5/5 timeout, 100% loss** — confirming TEST isn't universally implemented (Tom's flag); the feature reports that honestly rather than erroring. C#/node-host only (not a cross-stack parity item); the `SendTestAsync` public API ships in the next `lib-v*` NuGet release but the node uses the project ref. **Deferred (named):** a TEST *responder* — our node currently DMs an inbound TEST (the Disconnected catch-all) rather than echoing it; answering TEST at the listener's connectionless inbound path would make us spec-compliant + enable pdn↔pdn ping, a separate AX.25-lib inbound-path change.

### 2026-06-09 — Slice-3 backend step 5: the interactive sysop console (a connect-out's live output streamed to the web)

The web connect-out stops being blind. A new `SysopConsoleManager` (Packet.Node.Core/Console) adopts each operator-initiated `INodeConnection` (keyed by the session id `"{portId}:{peer}"`) instead of discarding it: a per-connection read pump is the sole reader of the connection, draining the peer's output into a bounded backlog tail and fanning it out to any number of SSE subscribers (bounded channels, drop-oldest on a slow browser); the pump ends — completing every subscriber — when the peer goes away or on close. New `GET /api/v1/sessions/{id}/stream` (text/event-stream): replays the backlog then streams live, each chunk an `event: output` whose `data:` is the chunk JSON-encoded as a string (so embedded CR/LF survive SSE's line framing), with a `TimeProvider`-driven heartbeat and clean teardown on client disconnect / peer-gone. `POST /sessions/{id}/send` now routes through the manager (`WriteAsync`) for a managed session and `DELETE` through `CloseAsync` (clean DISC); both fall back to the prior direct paths otherwise. The web Sessions console drawer subscribes to the stream (`EventSource`, `output` events → JSON.parse → append to a `whitespace-pre-wrap` scroll-to-bottom pane), echoes typed lines, sends via the existing send endpoint, and auto-opens on connect-out so the operator sees the remote's banner immediately. The manager is the concurrency core (one pump task per connection + the SSE fan-out + lifecycle, built by me); the endpoints + drawer were built by two parallel sub-agents against a fixed SSE contract. **Verified live on the lab against the real GB7RDG/LinBPQ node** (audio path, not RF): connect-out adopted the session, the stream/drawer replayed LinBPQ's banner ("Welcome to GB7RDG. Sysop M0LTE…"), and typing `N` returned LinBPQ's live NODES list (BASTOK:GB7BSK … WOODLY:GB7WOD) — a real bidirectional web↔LinBPQ console; disconnect sent the DISC. 303 Packet.Node.Tests green (new SSE-stream + manager backlog/replay tests); UI tsc+vite + 10/10 vitest green. **Deferred (unchanged):** ping (TEST send+correlate — folds into the link-tuner design), per-`portId` connect dial, beacons, auth.

### 2026-06-09 — Slice-3 backend step 4: direct-supervisor session actions (connect / disconnect / send) + port restart now works; ping deferred

The session actions and port restart go live on top of the concurrency seam (`NodeHostedService.RunExclusiveAsync` + `PortSupervisor.RestartPortAsync`, built separately). Every action that mutates the live port/session set runs under the host's exclusive gate — the same gate the reconcile worker holds — so a web action can never race a config reconcile or another action. Critical sections are kept short: the gate is held only to capture a listener/session reference or post one event; the one long-running operation, a connect-out dial awaiting SABM/UA, runs *outside* the gate (bounded by a `TimeProvider`-driven 30s ceiling linked with the request token — no wall-clock, §2.7). New `Packet.Node/Api/PdnSessionsApi.cs` (`MapPdnSessionsApi`): `POST /api/v1/sessions {target, portId?}` resolves the supervisor's connector (which already encodes callsign→AX.25-dial / alias→NET/ROM-route AND claims the dialled remote so no node-console is started against it), dials outside the gate, and returns the new session's `SessionInfo` (400 bad target, 404 no port, 502 refused/no-route, 504 timeout); `DELETE /sessions/{id}` finds the session by the `{portId}:{peer}` id (split on the first `:`, the convention `PdnReadApi.BuildSessions` mints) and posts `DlDisconnectRequest` → 204, else 404; `POST /sessions/{id}/send {line}` sends the line UTF-8 + trailing **CR** (the telnet console's CR-not-CRLF link discipline) via `Ax25Listener.SendData` → 202, else 404. The single-session→`SessionInfo` projection is factored out of `PdnReadApi.BuildSessions` into a shared `internal ProjectSession` (no drift). The lifecycle `restart` arm in `PdnPortsApi.cs` — previously a 501 stub — now drives `RestartPortAsync` under the gate: unknown id → 404, **disabled** port → 409 (bring it up first; documented choice), enabled port → its `PortStatus`. **v1 scope (named, not silent):** a web connect-out opens the session and surfaces it in `/sessions` — there is NO console-bridge / received-data stream (the monitor shows the frames; the Sessions drawer is send-only); `portId` is honoured as validation but the dial goes through the supervisor's *default*-resolved connector (a per-`portId` dial selector needs a per-port connector factory on the supervisor — a later step). **Ping is deferred to an explicit 501:** a connectionless TEST ping needs a public "send TEST command frame + correlate the TEST response" path on `Ax25Listener` that doesn't exist (it exposes connected-mode `SendData` + UI-frame `SendUiAsync`, no TEST send, and its modem is private); building it would widen the AX.25 library, out of scope for this endpoint pass — the endpoint returns 501 with a clear later-step message rather than half-building it. Web: `api.ts` gains `connectSession`/`disconnectSession`/`sendSessionLine`/`pingTarget` (+ a `PingResult` type + `PingUnavailable` for the 501); `portLifecycle` no longer special-cases restart (it works) and now maps a 409 — not 501 — to `PortLifecycleUnavailable`. The Sessions screen's connect-out + disconnect (row + drawer) + the drawer's send line are wired to the live API (reload `/sessions` after connect/disconnect, errors to a notice banner); the ping tool calls `/ping` in live mode and surfaces the 501 as a graceful "not available yet" warning (keeping its synthetic animation in mock mode). **Not live-tested against the radio** (per the brief — the human verifies connect/send/disconnect against the real GB7RDG/LinBPQ node); verified by build + tests: 299 `Packet.Node.Tests` green (new `SessionsApiTests` covering connect-no-port→404 / bad-target→400 / unknown-port→404 / disconnect-unknown→404 / send-unknown→404 / ping→501, new `SessionIdSplitTests` unit-testing the id split via a new `InternalsVisibleTo`, and `PortsApiTests` rewritten for restart 404/409/200); UI `tsc`+`vite` (live mode) + 10/10 vitest green. **Still deferred (named):** ping (the TEST send+correlate path — needs an AX.25-lib addition); the per-`portId` connect dial; the link-tuner / `tune` flow (task #113).

### 2026-06-09 — Slice-3 backend step 3: port management (add / edit / remove / enable-disable) over the config-write path

The Ports screen becomes functional — without any new node surface. Every port mutation is expressed as a candidate `NodeConfig` and persisted through the **same** `IWritableConfigProvider` write seam the config editor uses (validate → `ReconcilePreview` → `TryApply` → the existing serialized hot reconcile), so there is no direct supervisor action and no new concurrency surface. `Packet.Node/Api/PdnPortsApi.cs`: `POST /api/v1/ports` (append), `PUT /ports/{id}` (full-object replace, 404 on unknown id), `DELETE /ports/{id}` (404 on unknown id) — each returns a `ReconcileResult` / 422 `ValidationProblem` (a duplicate id is rejected naturally by the validator's unique-id rule); `POST /ports/{id}/lifecycle {up|down}` flips `Enabled` through the seam and projects the port's best-effort `PortStatus` (reconcile is async, so a just-enabled port may read `faulted`/`down` for an instant — honest, not a bug). The shared validate→apply is one private helper mirroring `PdnConfigApi`. Web: `api.ts` gains `addPort`/`editPort`/`removePort`/`portLifecycle` (+ a `PortLifecycleUnavailable` for the deferred restart); the Ports screen's add/edit/remove/enable-disable are wired to them and reload `/config`+`/ports` on success, with a notice banner for rejections. **Verified end-to-end on a running node**: add → reconcile "added and brought up" + GET lists it, edit persists the new transport port, down/up toggles `enabled`, dup add → 422, unknown id → 404, delete → empty. 282 `Packet.Node.Tests` green (new `PortsApiTests`); UI `tsc`+`vite` + 10/10 vitest green; full filtered solution suite green. The OpenAPI doc's port-edit verb was corrected `PATCH`→`PUT` (full-object replace, matching the implementation + UI). **Deferred (named):** lifecycle `restart` → an explicit 501 (a transient restart needs a serialized supervisor entry point — task #114, paired with the session connect/disconnect/send + ping actions); the link-tuner / `tune` flow is held for a design discussion (task #113 — net-sim tuneables); then auth.

### 2026-06-09 — Slice-3 backend step 2: config-write through the reconcile path (the editor can save edits to the live node)

The config editor stops being read-only — it now persists edits through the existing hot-reconcile path, with a server-authoritative preview and validate-before-apply. The seam: a new `IWritableConfigProvider : IConfigProvider` (`Validate(candidate)` dry-run + `TryApply(candidate, out errors)`) implemented by `FileConfigProvider` — kept separate from `IConfigProvider` so the read-only consumers + the two test fakes are untouched, registered as a second DI singleton on the same instance. `TryApply` validates, persists atomically (temp-write + rename), advances `Current`, and raises the same `OnChange` the file watcher does (so the reconcile is identical whether an edit arrived over the web or by hand-editing the file); a self-write echo-suppress (compare the exact bytes we wrote) stops the watcher double-applying. A `TransportConfigJsonConverter` gives STJ the polymorphic `kind`-discriminated deserialise a PUT body needs (transparent for the existing GET serialisation; registered via `ConfigureHttpJsonOptions`). `ReconcilePreviewBuilder` turns the field-level `ReconcilePlan` into the operator-facing `ReconcilePreview` — changes grouped by impact (apply-live · port-restart · node-reset), plain-language, never leaking a type name; the two areas the planner doesn't hot-apply are diffed directly and labelled honestly (cosmetic identity → live; the whole NET/ROM block → node-reset "applies after a node restart", not a fake "live"). `Packet.Node/Api/PdnConfigApi.cs`: `PUT /api/v1/config` (validate → 422 `ValidationProblem` on reject; `?dryRun=true` returns the preview without applying; else apply → `ReconcileResult`), `GET /config/raw` (text/plain YAML), `PUT /config/raw` (parse → same flow; malformed YAML is itself a 422). Web: `api.ts` gains `putConfig`/`getConfigRaw`/`putConfigRaw` (+ a typed `ConfigRejected` 422); the config screen's "Review & apply" now fetches the server dry-run preview into the modal and applies on confirm (rendering the node's own validation errors inline), and the Raw-YAML tab round-trips real YAML through `/config/raw` (replacing the client-side approximate serialiser). `Packet.Node.Core` stays web-free (seam + converter + preview builder in Core, endpoints in the host). **Verified end-to-end on a running node**: dry-run previews without persisting, apply persists (grid round-tripped on disk + reflected by GET), a blank callsign → 422 with the field errors, a NET/ROM edit → the node-reset "needs restart" note, raw YAML round-trips, malformed YAML → 422; the live config screen's reconcile modal renders the server preview (Docker-Playwright). 273 `Packet.Node.Tests` green (new `TransportConfigJsonConverterTests`, `FileConfigProviderWriteTests` incl. the echo-suppress, `ReconcilePreviewBuilderTests`, `ConfigWriteApiTests`); UI `tsc`+`vite` + 10/10 vitest green; full filtered solution suite green. **Deferred (named):** `PATCH /config` (the form PUTs the whole config), `GET /config/schema` + the field-metadata descriptor (the form has its impacts client-side), and the port CRUD + lifecycle + sessions/tune/ping actions (the next "actions" step). Then auth.

### 2026-06-09 — Slice-3 backend step 1b: the live `/events` SSE feed + frame/byte telemetry (the monitor, frames/sec + link stats go live)

The read API stops being a static snapshot — the node now streams every frame and counts traffic. New `Packet.Node.Core/Telemetry/NodeTelemetry.cs`: a singleton that taps each port's `Ax25Listener.FrameTraced` event (subscribed/unsubscribed through the port lifecycle, mirroring the NET/ROM attach — `PortSupervisor.AttachPort`/`DetachPort`) and maintains thread-safe per-port + per-(port,peer) counters (frames, info bytes, REJ/SREJ tallies, first-seen/last-activity) plus a bounded-channel fan-out of decoded `MonitorEvent`s. **Concurrency:** `FrameTraced` fires on listener *pump threads* (several at once with multiple ports), so every counter mutates via `Interlocked` over fields on a `ConcurrentDictionary` value, and a slow SSE consumer drops its oldest buffered frames (`BoundedChannelFullMode.DropOldest`) rather than back-pressuring a pump thread; `Observe` swallows+logs any fault so telemetry can never disturb a session (same discipline as the NET/ROM tap). New `Packet.Node.Core/Api/MonitorEvent.cs` is the serialisable frame projection + a pure `MonitorEventFactory` decode (control-byte classify → I/S/U + RR/RNR/REJ/SREJ/UI/SABM/…; N(S)/N(R)/PID/PID-name/P-F/command/summary/raw-as-int[] — `byte[]` would Base64, the UI hex-dumps a number array — matching the web client's `MonitorEvent` shape in `src/lib/types.ts`). `Packet.Node/Api/PdnEventsApi.cs` (`MapPdnEvents`) serves `GET /api/v1/events` as `text/event-stream` — `event: frame` + single-line camelCase `data:`, an initial `: connected` + a `TimeProvider`-driven 15s `: ping` heartbeat (no wall-clock, §2.7), un-buffered, tears down cleanly on `RequestAborted`. The read-API stubs are back-filled from telemetry: `PortStatus.framesIn/out`, `/links` (real REJ/SREJ + frame counts; `smoothedRttMs`/`retries` honestly stay 0 — they live in session T1/SRTT state, not derivable from the frame tap — with a comment, not a silent zero), and `SessionInfo.bytesIn/out`/`uptimeSeconds`/`lastActivity`. `Packet.Node.Core` stays web-free — telemetry + decode live in Core, the SSE endpoint in the host. The dashboard's **Frames/sec** metric was a `Math.random()` mock timer; rewired to a real 3-second rolling rate off the same SSE stream (`subscribeFrames`), so a live node shows its true rate, not noise. **Verified end-to-end on a running node** (GB7RDG, one kiss-tcp port fed by a local KISS-TCP traffic generator injecting UI frames + the node's own NODES broadcasts): `/api/v1/ports` shows live `framesIn 29/framesOut 4`, `/api/v1/links` a per-peer rollup (M0LTE, 2E0XYZ-7, G8PZT-7, G4APL-1 + the NODES broadcast TX), the `/events` SSE streams decoded `frame` events; **Docker-Playwright screenshots of the live node** render the monitor with real frames + the per-link stat strip and the dashboard Frames/sec showing the true measured rate (no page errors). 252 `Packet.Node.Tests` green (new `MonitorEventFactoryTests`, `NodeTelemetryTests`, `EventsSseTests` — the last drives a synthetic frame through the live host's telemetry and reads it back off the SSE stream); full filtered solution suite green. **Step 1a** (the read-first arc's screen rewiring — every screen's data source moved to the API client, the dashboard role-breakdown + per-port health computed live) shipped just before this. **Still ahead in Slice 3:** config-write/reconcile (PUT/PATCH `/config` through `IConfigProvider`/`ReconcilePlanner`), the action endpoints (port lifecycle, sessions connect/disconnect/send, beacons, tune, ping), then auth (Argon2id/JWT → WebAuthn/TOTP), then a self-hosted web-UI CI job + ship. The `/log` tail (dashboard "Recent activity") stays a named TODO — it's a journald/log-sink concern, not frame telemetry.

### 2026-06-09 — Slice-3 backend step 1: the node serves the UI + a live read API (`/api/v1` read endpoints + static-serve)

The web server stops being inert. The node now **serves the built web UI** and a **read-only `/api/v1`** projected from live node state. Pieces: `Packet.Node.Core/Api/ReadModels.cs` (the DTO contract — `NodeStatus`/`PortStatus`/`SessionInfo`/`LinkStats`/`LogLine`, camel-cased to match `docs/node-api.yaml` + the UI's types); `Packet.Node/Api/PdnReadApi.cs` (`MapPdnReadApi` — GET `/api/v1/{status,ports,sessions,netrom/routes,config,links,log}`, each projected from the now-singleton `NodeHostedService`'s `Supervisor`/`NetRom` handles + `IConfigProvider`, null-safe during boot, unauthenticated for now); a read-only `Ax25Listener.ActiveSessions` snapshot accessor (the only engine change). Static-serve: `UseDefaultFiles`/`UseStaticFiles`/`MapFallbackToFile("index.html")` with a `/api/{**rest}`→404 guard so unknown API paths don't get served the SPA. The endpoint mapping lives in the **host** project (`Packet.Node`, which has the Web SDK) — `Packet.Node.Core` stays web-free (embeddable). **Two real bugs caught by running it live (not just the WAF test):** (1) `WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args)` sets ContentRoot = CWD, but the packaged node's CWD is the StateDirectory (`/var/lib/packetnet`) while the binary+`wwwroot` are in `/opt/packetnet/app` → static-serve would look in the wrong place; fixed by pinning `ContentRootPath = AppContext.BaseDirectory`. (2) node uptime read 0 (the start timestamp initialised lazily on first request) → forced eager capture at `MapPdnReadApi` (startup). **Verified end-to-end on a running node** (temp config, `link-dn` axudp up / `uhf-2` kiss-tcp faulted): `GET /` serves the real `index.html`+assets, `/monitor` → 200 SPA fallback, `/api/v1/status` returns the live callsign+`portsUp 1/2`, `/api/v1/ports` shows the real up/faulted states; **Docker-Playwright screenshots of the live node** (container→host via `host-gateway`) render all 9 screens with no page errors, the dashboard showing the real station/port/NET-ROM state. 225 `Packet.Node.Tests` green (the slice-1 "inert web server" invariant test rewritten to assert the new read-API surface + the `/api/*` 404 guard; new `ReadApiTests`). The build step (UI `dist`→`wwwroot`, `VITE_API_MODE=live`) is wired into the publish path; `wwwroot` is generated/gitignored. **Honest interim state:** the UI's data flows are only *partly* live — screens that fetch via the API client are live, but several still import mock constants directly for some detail lists (e.g. the dashboard's port-rows + log tail), so the next sub-step (1a) is rewiring every screen's data source to the API client. **Still ahead in Slice 3:** the `/events` SSE feed + frame/byte counters (step 1b — makes the monitor + LinkStats live), then config-write/reconcile + the action endpoints, then auth.

### 2026-06-08 — Phase 5 web UI BUILT (the control panel) + the Slice-3 API contract LOCKED — `web/packetnet-ui/` + `docs/node-api.yaml`

The converged Claude Design handoff (`design_handoff_pdn_control_panel/`, dropped on this branch) is implemented as the real Phase-5 SPA at **`web/packetnet-ui/`** — Vite + React + TS + Tailwind + shadcn-style components (the locked §3/§5.5 stack), faithfully recreating all ten screens: login (passkey-first), first-run setup wizard, dashboard, **live monitor** (the marquee — per-link stat strip, filtered streaming frame table with the custom easeOutCubic rAF smooth-prepend, click-to-decode hex/ASCII view over the SSE `frame` feed), sessions (+ console drawer + sysop connect-out with route autocomplete), NET/ROM routes (dual quality **and** INP3-time metrics, column tooltips, per-row ping/connect), ports (profile-first editor, NinoTNC test-frame banner, plain-language disruption confirm), config editor (form-first + raw-YAML, reconcile preview grouping live/port-restart/node-reset, NET/ROM+INP3 guidance, beacons), users (Web-login Argon2id/passkeys + on-air TOTP enrolment), and the link-tuner + AX.25-ping tools. Built largely by a 5-agent fleet against a hand-built foundation (design tokens + primitives ported from the handoff `ui.jsx`; the `data.jsx` mock ported to a typed `src/lib/mock.ts` using the real record field names; a typed API client `src/lib/api.ts` with a `VITE_API_MODE` mock/live toggle + an SSE abstraction), then hand-integrated. **The §7 API is now LOCKED as OpenAPI 3.1 in `docs/node-api.yaml`** — derived from the typed client + the handoff; this is the contract the Slice-3 backend implements and the UI's `live` mode talks to (no screen change to switch). **Verification:** `tsc --noEmit` + `vite build` clean; **`vitest` render smoke test mounts every screen against the mock backend and asserts no runtime crash (10/10 green)** — this is the runtime gate in lieu of headless-browser screenshots, which are impossible on the dev LXC (the sandbox denies Chrome's network sockets; documented in the UI README). The app runs fully today against its mock data layer (`npm run dev`, log in with the passkey button). **Named follow-up (the legitimately-next slice, NOT silently dropped):** the **Slice-3 backend server** — the read endpoints + SSE + static-serve + auth (Argon2id/JWT, then WebAuthn/TOTP) + the config-write/reconcile + lifecycle/connect/tune/ping actions, per `docs/node-api.yaml`. Auth + writes are security-sensitive and intentionally not rushed unsupervised overnight; the UI + locked contract make them a clean, well-scoped next arc. Plan status: Phase 4 Slice 3 = contract locked + UI built, backend pending; Phase 5 = scaffolded + screens built (🟡).

### 2026-06-08 — Phase 4 Slice 2.5: node-UI design spike (UI-first, ahead of the Slice-3 API) — `docs/node-ui-design.md`

Before writing any Slice-3 (REST API + auth) endpoint code, a consumer-first design pass: the API should fall out of the operator's screens, not the reverse. New `docs/node-ui-design.md` is both the in-repo spec and a paste-ready brief for **Claude Design** (Anthropic's prompt-to-prototype tool, claude.ai). The division of labour: this repo/session holds the grounded spec (screen inventory + the *real* data shapes + the derived API contract), Claude Design turns it into interactive React/Tailwind/shadcn mockups (the locked Phase-5 stack, so they seed the real UI), and the agreed design folds back here to lock the API contract before implementation. The doc covers nine screens (first-run setup, login, dashboard, live monitor, sessions, NET/ROM routes, ports, config editor, users), each annotated with what it reads/writes/streams; surfaces ground-truth records (`NodeConfig` tree, `NetRomRoutingSnapshot`/`NetRomRoute`/`Inp3RouteMetric`, the `Ax25Listener.FrameTraced`→`MonitorEvent` source) plus the new read models Slice 3 must add (`NodeStatus`/`PortStatus`/`SessionInfo`/`LinkStats`); and sketches the §7 REST + SSE contract with auth scopes. Key design positions taken (open for the Claude Design pass to confirm, §9): **SSE not WebSocket** for the live feed; **form-first config editor with a raw-YAML escape hatch** (both ride the existing `IConfigProvider`/`ReconcilePlanner` path); v1 UI scope = observe + configure + basic session control (deep link-troubleshooting deferred to Phase 8). No API code yet — this is the design artifact Tom reviews + takes into Claude Design first. Slice 3 implementation follows once §7 is locked as an OpenAPI document.

### 2026-06-08 — INP3 lab demo (isolated docker) + Nodes-command INP3 surfacing — converged on real nodes (PR TBD)

The tangible capstone: INP3 proven **live on two real `Packet.Node` hosts** over a simulated RF channel, in an **isolated docker lab** (kept off the shared pdn-lab box so it can't disturb the concurrent pico-node hardware bring-up session). `docker/inp3lab/` — a net-sim `afsk1200` channel + two pdn containers (INP3 on, compressed probe/RIF intervals). The nodes discover each other via NODES, a `connect` raises the connected-mode interlink, the L3RTT probe/reflect loop measures the link, and RIFs propagate the time-routes. Verified output (the `N` console command on node A): `GB7BBB:GB7BBB: via GB7BBB(192,6) [inp3 1734ms/1h]` — the NODES quality pair `(192,6)` AND the **live-measured** INP3 target time `[inp3 1734ms/1h]` coexisting on one route (≈ the real 1200-baud half-duplex round-trip ÷ 2); node B symmetrically holds `GB7AAA … [inp3 1778ms/1h]`. **Console enhancement (shipped):** `NodeCommandService` now surfaces the INP3 metric in the `Nodes` route render — `[inp3 <targetTime>ms/<hops>h]` appended when a route carries one (the pdn analogue of the deferred pico-node Nodes-surfacing item; default-invisible for quality-only routes so existing output is unchanged). The `docker/inp3lab/` scaffolding (compose + net-sim topology + two node configs + Dockerfile + README) is committed as a reproducible asset; the self-contained `app/` publish output is gitignored (built before `compose up`). 220 `Packet.Node.Tests` green; whole `dotnet build` clean. This satisfies the I-5 lab-demo / deploy-standing-order item via an isolated docker lab rather than the shared box. (PR #341)

### 2026-06-08 — INP3 I-5 closed (on the our-fleet bar): failover-by-time end-to-end + BPQ/XRouter interop resolution (PR #340)

Closes INP3 slice I-5's deterministic + interop-investigation scope (the live two-INP3-pdn-node lab demo is the only residual — the deploy standing order, tracked separately).

- **Failover-by-time end-to-end** (`NetRomServiceInp3EndToEndTests`, the 5th test): a 5-node diamond where A reaches C two ways of unequal hop length — short `A→B→C` (2 hops) and long `A→D→E→C` (3 hops) — over the in-process Fleet harness. After convergence A holds C with BOTH next hops carrying their accumulated Σ-SNTT target times (via B = 0+50+10 at B then +50+10 at A = **120 ms / hop 2**; via D = … = **180 ms / hop 3**), and `Inp3RouteSelector.SelectActiveRoute(C, preferInp3Routes: true)` returns the via-B (lower-time) route while `false` falls back to best quality — the failover-by-time decision proven end-to-end over *real learned multi-hop time-routes*, not a fixture.
- **BPQ/XRouter interop resolved** (`docs/netrom-inp3-interop.md`): an evidence-based verdict replacing the plan's vague "best-effort BPQ/XRouter interop" bullet. Both peers implement INP3 (LinBPQ `PREFERINP3ROUTES`; XRouter author-labelled-experimental), but **a deterministic CI test is not feasible against the current docker stack for a structural reason**: INP3 is off in both fixtures, *and* the existing NET/ROM peer tests observe only connectionless NODES broadcasts whereas L3RTT/RIF ride a connected-mode PID-0xCF interlink that those tests don't open. The doc gives the one feasible path (reuse the `NetRomL4CircuitViaAxudp` interlink seam + the exact `bpq32.cfg` `PREFERINP3ROUTES=1` + interval delta), tiered assertions, and the recommendation to close I-5 on the our-fleet bar with external interop a named follow-up — plus a **double-gated `[SkippableFact]` skeleton** (`tests/Packet.Interop.Tests/Linbpq/NetRomInp3L3RttViaAxudp.cs`, gated on stack reachability AND a `PDN_INTEROP_BPQ_INP3` marker) that green-skips until the delta lands. No `interop.yml` / docker-config changes (deltas recommended in the doc for a deliberate follow-up).

Built by a 2-agent fleet (packet.net only — pico-node untouched while its firmware layout is being restructured), hand-verified: 220 `Packet.Node.Tests` green; the interop skeleton green-skips with the stack down; whole `dotnet build` clean. (PR #340)

### 2026-06-08 — INP3 slice I-5 (C# leg): our-fleet end-to-end convergence harness (PR #339)

The cross-stack INP3 implementation is complete (C# I-1..I-4 + host integration + forwarding-by-time, all merged; ax25-ts host-free cascade + live connector wiring published as `@packet-net/ax25` 0.11.0; pico-node `ax25-node-core` no_std cascade merged). I-5 is the validation phase: prove the fleet converges end-to-end + best-effort BPQ/XRouter interop. This entry lands the **C# deterministic end-to-end leg** — `NetRomServiceInp3EndToEndTests`: a `Fleet` of real `NetRomService` instances cross-wired in-process over one `FakeTimeProvider` and a hop-by-hop message wire (each node's `interlinkSendSinkForTest` enqueues; `DeliverRound` hands frames to the target's `IngestInterlinkForTest` as if from the sender — one hop per round, so a clock advance between rounds is a controllable RTT). Where `NetRomServiceInp3Tests` drives one node against a fake peer, this drives the **actual two-way loop between real nodes**.
