Four tests: (1) two nodes mutually probe/reflect → SNTT converges to RTT/2 both ways + mutual INP3 capability; (2) the periodic RIF propagates — each node learns the other as a time-route from the peer's **own-node source RIP** (the convergence bootstrap: dest = sender, target = SNTT + per-hop); (3) a node **poison-reverses** the neighbour it learned a route via (B reached only via B → advertised back at the horizon, on the wire); (4) a **3-node line A—B—C** propagates C's route two hops to A with the **accumulated Σ-SNTT target time** (120 ms = 0 + 50 + 10 at B, then + 50 + 10 at A) — real multi-hop INP3. 219 `Packet.Node.Tests` green (PR #339). **Remaining I-5 (best-effort, follow-ups):** the diamond multi-path failover-by-time end-to-end (the selector + forwarding-by-time decision is already unit-proven); the cross-language fleet wiring (C#/TS/Rust share byte-parity vectors, so per-stack end-to-end + the parity vectors are the cross-stack proof); BPQ (`PREFERINP3ROUTES=1`) + XRouter interop on the lab/docker stack; and the lab deploy (two INP3-pdn nodes, since BPQ INP3 is unreliable).

### 2026-06-08 — INP3 forwarding-by-time: route by the measured time-space (PreferInp3Routes) (PR #338)

Completes the C# INP3 from **awareness-only** to **routing-by-time** — the last C# slice before the cross-stack cascade, and the prerequisite for I-5's "a route fails over by time." Gated by `inp3.preferInp3Routes` (BPQ's `PREFERINP3ROUTES`), default **off**, so a node can run the overlay for awareness (learn + advertise the time-space) without changing where traffic flows; flip the knob to forward by measured time.

- **`NetRomForwarding.Decide`** gains a `preferInp3Routes` parameter. When on **and** the destination holds an INP3 time-route, the datagram forwards over the **lowest-target-time** INP3 route (ties: hop, then callsign — `SelectInp3NextHop`, the time-space mirror of `SelectBest`), with the way-it-came excluded and a fall-through to the quality next-hop when no INP3 route is usable. Per-flow load-balancing stays a **quality-space** concept (a measured time-route's whole point is "use the fastest path"; spreading flows across slower time-routes would defeat the measurement), so in the time-space we always forward the single fastest path. When off the INP3 metric is never read — byte-for-byte today.
- **The connect path** (`NetRomService`: the `connect <alias>` failover loop + the reply next-hop resolver) now resolves its active route through `Inp3RouteSelector.SelectActiveRoute` via a `SelectActiveRoute` helper gated by the same `PreferInp3Routes` (`inp3 != null && config.Inp3.PreferInp3Routes` — moot when the overlay is off). Forward and connect therefore agree on the active INP3 next hop.

Surgical (a pure-function parameter + two thin call-site swaps), so done directly rather than fleet-built. 276 `Packet.NetRom.Tests` (+6: lowest-time-wins-overriding-quality-and-per-flow, knob-off-ignores-the-metric, no-INP3-route fallback, split-horizon exclusion + next-best, only-route-is-the-way-it-came → quality fallback, the time/hop/callsign tie-break) + 215 `Packet.Node.Tests` green; whole `dotnet build` clean. The C# reference is now complete (I-1..I-4 + host integration + forwarding-by-time) and ready to be the oracle for the ax25-ts + pico-node cascade and the I-5 end-to-end harness.

### 2026-06-08 — INP3 host integration: wire the overlay into NetRomService (behind inp3.enabled) (PR #337)

The slice that makes the four host-free protocol layers (I-1..I-4) a live node feature — **awareness only** (the node learns + tells the time-space; routing *by* it is the named follow-up). All of it is dead code when `inp3.enabled` is false (the `inp3` field is null → the node is byte-for-byte today, verified).

- **`NetRom/NetRomService.Inp3.cs`** (new) — the `Inp3Host` nested glue: owns the `Inp3Engine` + `Inp3UpdateScheduler` (both `tickInterval:null`, host-driven), wires the engine's `SendL3Rtt` + the scheduler's `Advertise` intents to the interlink send path (0xCF; cold-interlink → drop, don't dial) and the engine's 180 s `NeighbourDown` to the shared `MarkNeighbourDown`. A single 1 s `TimeProvider` timer drives one `TickOnce` round.
- **Inbound dispatch precedence** (`DispatchInp3`, called from `OnInterlinkData` only when on) — a 0xFF-led frame is a RIF (consumed whether or not it parses — never retried as L4); else an L3RTT to `L3RTT-0` is timed/reflected; else the already-parsed `NetRomPacket` falls through to the existing L4 path. A 0xFF first byte can't be a valid shifted-callsign, so the discriminator is unambiguous.
- **Config** — `NetRomConfig.Inp3` (a whole validated `NetRomInp3Options` record, default-disabled) + nested `inp3:` YAML; the validator delegates the knob ranges to `NetRomInp3Options.Validate()` and adds two cross-rules: `inp3.enabled` requires **both** `netrom.enabled` **and** `netrom.connect` (INP3 rides the connected-mode interlink, so the host only builds it under Connect — reject-don't-silently-no-op, the named-flag discipline).

Fleet-built + hand-integrated; the soundness verifier earned its keep with two on-path findings, both fixed:

1. **The recently-withdrawn host-thread race (merge-blocking).** I-4 left the one-shot horizon-withdrawal RIP as a table set that `BuildRif` *read* and the host *cleared after the fan-out round* — but a pump-thread `IngestRif`/`MarkNeighbourDown`/`Sweep` withdrawal landing between the last `BuildRif` read and the post-round clear was silently wiped (dropped on the wire, recovered only at the slower obsolescence pace). Fixed by making the read+clear **atomic**: `NetRomRoutingTable.DrainRecentlyWithdrawn()` (snapshot+clear under one `gate` hold) replaces `ClearRecentlyWithdrawn()`; `BuildRif` now takes the drained snapshot as a parameter instead of reading the live set; `TickOnce` drains **once** at the round start (under a `tickGate` serialising the 1 s timer vs the NODESINTERVAL nudge), marks each NEGATIVE, and hands the same snapshot to every neighbour's `BuildRif`. A concurrent mid-round withdrawal now lands in the *next* round's drain — captured, never lost. (`RecentlyWithdrawn()` stays as a read-only peek for tests/monitoring; the per-ingest escalation calls are gone — the drain is the single round boundary.)
2. **Construction-gate vs validator mismatch.** The overlay is constructed only inside `if (config.Connect)`, but the validator only required `netrom.enabled` — so `inp3.enabled` + `connect:false` validated and then silently no-op'd. Added the `inp3.enabled requires netrom.connect` cross-rule.

215 `Packet.Node.Tests` + 270 `Packet.NetRom.Tests` green. Whole `dotnet build` clean (0 warnings under TreatWarningsAsErrors). **Named follow-up (still awareness-only):** feed `Inp3RouteSelector` into the forward/connect next-hop pick (forwarding *by* time) + triggered-positive change-marking once `IngestRif` reports per-destination deltas.

### 2026-06-08 — INP3 slice I-4: C# triggered RIF emission + poison-reverse (PR TBD)

Fourth (last protocol) slice of the C# INP3 reference — the loop-avoidance + update-timing layer. Host-free, no node wiring yet.

- **`NetRomRoutingTable.BuildRif(myCall, toTargetNeighbour)`** — builds the per-neighbour RIF: own-node RIP first (0/0, never poisoned), then one RIP for every destination we **hold** an INP3 time-route for, at our best (lowest) held target time, RIP-ordered by ascending time then callsign for cross-stack byte-parity; alias-TLV emission gated off (I1 RIF-2). Emission is **holding-based, not gated on `preferInp3Routes`** (that knob is a local *forwarding* preference, not an advertisement gate — a node forwarding by quality still advertises its time topology).
- **Poison-reverse (the headline correctness feature)** — a destination reached through `toTargetNeighbour` via **any** kept route is advertised back at the 600 s horizon. The adversarial verifier caught a real loop hazard: poisoning only the *selected* next hop (the first cut) is unsafe because the shipped multi-route load-balancer (`NetRomForwarding.SelectWeighted`, PerFlow default) spreads a destination's traffic across **every** kept route — so advertising it back at a finite metric to any neighbour we'd forward it through seeds a two-hop loop. Fixed to split-horizon over the full forwarding set (over-poisoning at worst loses a backup-path advertisement; alternate-reverse to recover it is deferred).
- **`Transport/Inp3UpdateScheduler.cs`** — the host-free triggered-update timing: negative info (route lost / target-time worsened past a threshold) fires immediately + prioritised; positive info is batched behind a debounce; a periodic full RIF on the interval; `TimeProvider`-driven, coalescing (a burst → ≤1 emit/neighbour), emitting "advertise to neighbour X" intents the host turns into `BuildRif` + a send.

Fleet-built (design → parallel emitter+scheduler on separate files → build + storm/loop-safety verifiers), integrated by hand. Beyond the poison-reverse loop fix, on integration the emitter was corrected from selector-gated to holding-based emission (a deviation from its own locked design §1), and the two affected tests rewritten (the "selected-route poison" test became "poison covers every kept next hop"; the "prefer-off suppresses emission" test became "a held route is advertised regardless of the forwarding preference"). 254 `Packet.NetRom.Tests` green (was 233). **Deferred to the named INP3-host-integration slice:** the host wiring (engine sinks → interlinks, scheduler intents → `BuildRif` → send, `Inp3RouteSelector` → forwarding, all behind `inp3.enabled` + YAML config) and the recently-withdrawn one-shot horizon RIP (invariant W — its consume-and-clear is host-fan-out-coupled; without it withdrawals still propagate, at the slower obsolescence-sweep pace, and never resurrect).

### 2026-06-08 — INP3 slice I-3: C# route metric + selection (dual quality/target-time) (PR TBD)

Third slice of the autonomous INP3 build — the metric-coexistence layer the plan flags as risk #4 (the subtlest design point). Host-free, no node wiring yet.

- **`Routing/NetRomRoutingModel.cs`** — `NetRomRoute` gains an **optional** `Inp3RouteMetric` (`int TargetTimeMs`, `byte HopCount`), so one destination can carry NODES quality-routes and RIF time-routes simultaneously. The 4th optional positional param keeps every existing `new NetRomRoute(n,q,o)` caller + the SQLite store round-trip compiling unchanged (the store stays INP3-blind; time-routes re-learn from RIF).
- **`Routing/NetRomRoutingTable.cs`** — `IngestRif(receivedFromNeighbour, myCall, neighbourSnttMs, rif, hopLimit)`: per RIP, `localTargetTime = peerTarget + neighbourSntt + 10 ms/hop`, `hop+1`; a RIP at/over the 600 s horizon **withdraws** the dest-via-neighbour time-route (clears the metric, dropping the route only if it has no usable quality); an **unmeasured** link (SNTT unset) never withdraws a route it never taught (except an explicit-horizon RIP); hop-limit + trivial-loop guards; shares the per-dest route cap (eviction quality-first, so a non-preferring node evicts byte-identically to today). Crucially **does not disturb** the existing quality/obsolescence ingestion — a NODES refresh preserves a coexisting INP3 metric and vice-versa.
- **`Routing/Inp3RouteSelector.cs`** — the pure `SelectActiveRoute(dest, preferInp3Routes)` truth table: `preferInp3Routes` + an INP3 route ⇒ lowest-target-time INP3 route; else best quality (today's `BestRoute`). When `preferInp3Routes` is false the INP3 metric is never read — verified to degenerate byte-for-byte to today.
- **`Wire/NetRomInp3Options.cs`** — added the `Enabled` (master overlay switch, default off) + `HopLimit` (default 30) knobs the design locked (so the host-wiring slice has its full config surface).

Fleet-built (design-lock+model → parallel ingestion+selector on separate files → **two** adversarial verifiers: build/regression + dual-metric soundness). Both verifiers green; the soundness pass independently confirmed the degenerate-to-today invariant, the ingestion math + overflow-safety (`long` accumulation behind the horizon gate), the quality-route coexistence (non-clobbering both ways), and that horizon withdrawal actually removes routes. The only flagged gap — the deferred `Enabled`/`HopLimit` config knobs — was closed here rather than left implicit. 233 `Packet.NetRom.Tests` green (was 187).

### 2026-06-07 — INP3 slice I-2: C# link timing (SNTT smoother + L3RTT engine) (PR TBD)

Second slice of the autonomous INP3 build. The link-timing layer, **host-free + fully unit-tested** (NetRomService wiring + config plumbing deferred to a clean integration step, so each slice stays shippable):

- **`Routing/Inp3Sntt.cs`** — the SNTT (Smoothed Neighbour Transport Time) integer IIR over `RTT/2` samples: a 1/8-gain rounded EWMA (`(7·SNTT + sample + 4)/8`, the AX.25-SRT convention; gain a configurable shift), first-sample-seeds-directly, clamped to the 600 s horizon, overflow-safe in 32-bit, no floating point (so the three stacks agree bit-for-bit and the pico-node M0+ has no FPU dependency). A pure value type.
- **`Transport/Inp3Engine.cs`** — the host-free engine (mirrors `CircuitManager`: injected `TimeProvider`, `Tick()`, action sinks, no AX.25/node-host deps). Owns per-neighbour INP3 state; on tick probes each INP3-capable neighbour due an L3RTT (via a `SendL3Rtt` sink) and fires the **180 s no-reflection reset → a `NeighbourDown` callback** the host wires to `MarkNeighbourDown`; on an L3RTT it computes RTT for our reflections (feeding `RTT/2` to the smoother) or reflects a peer's probe verbatim, learning `$N`/`$IX` capability. Monotonic ms time base (RTT correctness needs it). **Load-bearing guard (design AMBIGUITY-I2-3):** the reset only tears down an *INP3-capable* neighbour that went silent — a vanilla neighbour that never reflects optimistic probes is dropped from probing with NO callback, so feeding its silence into routing can't collapse good vanilla routes (graceful degradation).
- **`Wire/NetRomInp3Options.cs`** — the option record (probe interval 60 s, reset window 180 s, SNTT gain shift, capability-probe + IP-accept knobs); all default-off-feature tuning.

Fleet-built (design-lock → parallel SNTT + engine implementers → adversarial verify) and **integrated by hand**. Two real issues fixed on integration: (1) the engine used `LastL3RttSentMs == 0` as the "never probed" sentinel, but the monotonic clock starts at 0, so a probe genuinely sent at `t=0` read as never-sent and the cadence gate re-fired it every tick — fixed with a distinct `NeverProbed` sentinel; (2) the two parallel implementers drifted on the smoother's API (engine expected a static `Smooth`/`SnttUnset`; the smoother shipped a `readonly struct` with `Update`/`Unset`) — reconciled with a thin static bridge + a large-RTT clamp-before-narrow. Two over-specified SNTT tests (asserting exact convergence the rounded IIR can't reach — it rests in a small DC-biased band) were corrected to assert band convergence. 187 `Packet.NetRom.Tests` green (was 143). No behaviour wired into the node yet.

### 2026-06-07 — INP3 slice I-1: C# reference wire codecs (L3RTT + RIF/RIP/TLV) (PR TBD)

First slice of the autonomous INP3 build (the plan: [`netrom-inp3-plan.md`](netrom-inp3-plan.md); "Goal set: work autonomously on INP3 to completion"). Slice I-1 is the C# reference wire layer in `Packet.NetRom/Wire/`: **`Inp3L3Rtt.cs`** (the L3RTT link-time frame — an `Inp3L3RttFrame` over a `NetRomPacket` to the literal `L3RTT-0` dest, opcode 0x02, with the `$N`/`$IX` capability text; reflection recognised by origin==self) and **`Inp3Rif.cs`** (`Inp3Tlv` / `Inp3Rip` / `Inp3Rif` — the 0xFF-signed routing-information frame: shifted callsign + hop + 2-byte MSB-first 10 ms target time + TLVs + 0x00 EOP; 0xEA60 = 600 s horizon = withdrawal; unknown TLVs retained verbatim). Both **total/fuzz-safe** (arbitrary/truncated bytes → false/null, never throw), reusing `NetRomCallsign` + mirroring the `NodesBroadcast`/`NetRomParseOptions` total-parse + named-divergence pattern (new `Inp3ParseOptions` Strict/Lenient/Bpq/Xrouter). The locked byte layouts + hex parity vectors are documented in **`docs/netrom-inp3-i1-wire-spec.md`** — the oracle for the TS/Rust cascade.

Built by an agent fleet (spec/vectors → parallel L3RTT + RIF implementers → adversarial verify) and **integrated by hand**: the verifier surfaced the spec's pre-flagged **AMBIGUITY-RIF-2** (the alias TLV type `0x00` is identical to the EOP byte). Its proposed parser fix was checked and **rejected** — it would have broken legitimate multi-RIP framing (a real EOP is followed by the next RIP's shifted callsign, which "overruns" as a fake alias length). The correct, *forced* rule is "a 0x00 that can't be satisfied as a `[len][value]` within the body is the EOP" — which also means a truncated trailing alias is indistinguishable from EOP-plus-partial and degrades to a RIP that keeps its route but drops the malformed alias. That residual is documented in the codec + the spec doc, and **alias *emission* is gated off** until validated against a live peer in I-5. 143 `Packet.NetRom.Tests` green (was 97). No behaviour wired yet — codecs only; default-off feature.

### 2026-06-07 — TOTP captured as an ecosystem-wide on-air authentication direction (new §10.1) (PR TBD)

Tom: "I could see TOTP playing a really useful part in this whole ecosystem." Captured as a forward-looking direction in a new **§10.1** under the security threat model. The load-bearing insight: amateur radio forbids *encryption* (obscuring meaning) but **permits authentication**, and TOTP (RFC 6238) is authentication-without-encryption — a cleartext, time-bound, single-use, non-replayable code proving possession of a pre-shared seed without ever sending a reusable secret. That makes it a near-ideal fit for a cleartext-mandated medium. §10.1 records three uses spanning pdn / pico-node / ax25-ts: (1) gating **privileged on-air commands** — pico-node's `SET`/`SAVE`/`REBOOT` are unauthenticated today, and it's the modern replacement for BPQ's positional-challenge `PASSWORD`; (2) **trusted peer / interlink / INP3 routing-input authentication** so a rogue station can't inject false routes; (3) a **web/REST/MCP second factor** for the Phase-4 admin surfaces. Plus the design notes (clock-sync dependency — pico-node has no RTC; step/skew vs replay window; seed provisioning + per-node/user/neighbour model; small + `no_std`-portable HMAC implementation). **Idea captured, not scheduled** — no code, no phase commitment yet.

### 2026-06-07 — NET/ROM link-down failover signal + inbound-interlink hardening (the two Phase-9 follow-ups) (PR TBD)

The two small NET/ROM hardening items §5.9 had carried as "noted but not built".

**(1) Link-down / quality-collapse failover signal — built.** Until now, when a NET/ROM neighbour went down, its routes lingered in the table until the hourly obsolescence `Sweep` aged them out — and forwarding / `connect`-routing kept choosing a next hop that couldn't carry traffic the whole time. New routing-table primitive `NetRomRoutingTable.MarkNeighbourDown(neighbour)` drops every route that forwards through a neighbour (and the neighbour entry) at once, so the very next forward / connect decision fails over to an alternate; the neighbour re-learns from the next NODES broadcast if it returns. The unambiguous trigger is wired in `NetRomService.EnsureInterlinkAsync`: an interlink **dial that fails** (the neighbour never answered the SABM — `ConnectAsync` exhausts N2 and throws) marks that neighbour down (a pre-dial "no port" fault does not — only the dial itself is guarded). The transit-forward cold path gets this for free (it dials through `EnsureInterlinkAsync`); the `connect <alias>` path now runs a **failover loop** — try the best route, and if its neighbour is down, re-resolve the destination's now-best route and try again, until one connects or the routes are exhausted. Established-interlink drops are covered transitively (the next forward re-dials → dial-fail → mark-down), avoiding the ambiguity of treating every DL-DISCONNECT (which includes graceful idle teardown) as a failure. Tests: 3 unit (`MarkNeighbourDown` drops-at-once / leaves-the-alternate / unknown-is-noop) + a service-level integration test (two routes to a destination, both next hops dead → the connect dials the best, marks it down, fails over to the alternate, marks it down, then exhausts — proving the loop walks the routes).

**(2) Inbound-interlink Connect-Request race — investigated; our side already robust; tap-hygiene cleanup shipped.** The §5.9 note flagged a Connect Request that "races ahead of the interlink SABM/UA on a fresh, lossy link (BPQ pipelines it and does not re-originate)." Investigation: pdn's inbound side is already robust against the realistic disorder — the listener's `SessionAccepted` (where the 0xCF tap attaches) is raised synchronously on the single inbound pump *before* the next frame is dispatched (no tap-attach race); a retransmitted Connect Request is deduped by the `CircuitManager`'s `byPeerKey` (re-ack, no duplicate circuit); and a double-delivered datagram is absorbed by the circuit's sequence dedup (a stale-`N(S)` datagram is plain-acked, not re-delivered). The only residual is BPQ pipelining the Connect Request on a fresh lossy link and not re-originating it if our (correct, spec-mandated) DM drops it — a BPQ-side non-re-origination we cannot remedy from our side without accepting I-frame data with no established session (an AX.25 violation). En route, fixed one genuine in-our-control hygiene issue in exactly this area: a cached `Ax25Session` is reused across disconnect/reconnect, and the old code dropped only its `tapped` membership on disconnect (leaving the closure attached), so a re-established interlink accumulated a **second** 0xCF tap and double-processed inbound datagrams (today benign — the lower layers dedup — but a latent footgun on the fresh/re-established interlinks this guards). The tap now detaches itself on disconnect, so a reconnect re-attaches exactly one.

Cross-stack: the `MarkNeighbourDown` routing primitive is the shared piece (mirrored into ax25-ts + pico-node to follow, per the parity discipline); the dial-failure wiring and the tap-hygiene fix are pdn-host specifics. Node host only; full default suite green (`Packet.NetRom.Tests` 97, `Packet.Node.Tests` 196).

### 2026-06-07 — Correction: TX tail is a modem/audio-path property, not a channel one — pulled off the `slow-afsk1200` profile (PR #330)

Reverts the TXTAIL addition in the entry below (same day). Tom's correction: the need for a non-zero TX tail is **not** tied to the channel or baud rate — it's a property of the **modem + the radio's audio-path latency**. **Needs TXTAIL > 0:** a software modem (samoyed / Dire Wolf), or a NinoTNC (KISS-serial) into a radio with a non-zero-latency audio path. **Fine with TXTAIL = 0:** a NinoTNC into a fully analogue audio path — *even on this exact slow AFSK1200 channel*. So bundling a tail into `slow-afsk1200` was wrong: a NinoTNC-into-analogue station on that channel would correctly take the profile's T1/CSMA but be forced an unwanted tail. A channel profile captures channel properties (contention timing, CSMA, TX warm-up); the node can't infer audio-path latency, so **`kiss.txTail` stays an explicit per-port operator setting** — no profile default, no global default — with the scenarios documented in the config template. The profile reverts to T1=10 s / N2=15 / TXDELAY=30 / CSMA only. A regression test now pins that the profile resolves `TxTail = null`. (The lab `gb7rdg` port talks to net-sim's *samoyed* — a software modem — so it keeps an explicit `kiss.txTail: 5` alongside its `profile: slow-afsk1200`.)

### 2026-06-07 — `slow-afsk1200` profile gains a software-modem TX tail (TXTAIL=5) (PR #329 — SUPERSEDED same day by the correction above)

**Superseded — see the entry immediately above.** The conflation it rests on (AFSK1200 ⇒ software modem ⇒ needs tail) is wrong: tail need is a modem/audio-path property, not a channel one. Original entry kept for the record:

The `slow-afsk1200` channel profile now also sets **TXTAIL=5 (50 ms)**, alongside its T1=10 s / N2=15 / TXDELAY=30 / CSMA defaults. AFSK1200 is in practice the **software-modem** channel (direwolf / samoyed / a soundcard modem), and a software modem **clips the end of its own transmission without a non-zero TX tail** — the net-sim lab proved this the hard way (GB7RDG sending TXTAIL=0 caused decode collapse; the whole TxTail saga). **Scoped to the profile, deliberately not a node-wide default** (Tom): a radio with a physical modem has an analogue audio path and needs no tail, so pinning one globally would be wrong for it (and isn't spec-clean) — whereas a 50 ms tail is harmless to a hardware TNC, so the profile is safe both ways. Absent a profile, a port still asserts no tail and gets the modem's own default. The plumbing already existed (`ChannelProfiles.OverlayKiss` carried `TxTail`; `PortSupervisor` applies it live) — only the profile's value was unset. Matches GB7RDG's validated TXTAIL=5. Tests: the resolver now asserts the profile's TX tail, plus a new "no profile → no tail asserted" guard so the hardware-modem case stays untouched; template + this plan updated. Node host only; not a parity item. **Aside — investigated + cleared the same day:** the old lab note that "pdn emits 2–3 identical RR/REJ in the same millisecond" does **not** reproduce post-#327 — a calm single-command exchange emits clean single supervisory frames, and the burst-case frames that share a millisecond bucket differ in type / N(R) / F-bit (each a distinct, correct response; the new F=0 is the #327 delayed-ack), with zero byte-identical duplicates on the wire (net-sim's decode is the authoritative per-frame view). Consistent with #327's run-to-completion `PostEvent` having serialised the emit path. No fix needed.

### 2026-06-07 — Delayed acks never reached the wire: production `sendLinkMux` stubs swallowed LM-SEIZE, so one-way data flow died to FRACK retries (#327, PR #328)

A session that **received I-frames while having no reply data to send never acknowledged them** — the peer retransmitted per FRACK, exhausted retries, and declared link failure. Interactive console use masked it completely: every console reply I-frame piggybacks N(R), so the ack always rode along. It bit exactly when data flow is one-way (an idle chat user, a file-transfer or BBS-read receiver, monitoring endpoints). Found on **real hardware** by the pico-node bring-up session testing the Rust port against a physical LinBPQ over AXUDP (fixed there in pico-node#15), then flagged here (#327) since the Rust runtime is a faithful port of this architecture — and verified real: a listener-level repro (connect → peer sends one I-frame → send nothing back) was red on `main`.

**Root cause.** The SDL's only route to a non-piggybacked ack runs through the link multiplexer: figc4.3 t26 (in-sequence I, P=0, no ack pending) emits `LM-SEIZE Request` + `Set Ack Pending`, and the RR flushes on the `LM-SEIZE Confirm` transition (t22 → `Enquiry Response (F=0)`). Both production construction sites (`Ax25Adapter`, `Ax25Listener`) stubbed `sendLinkMux` to a no-op, so the seize was swallowed and the confirm never came back. No fallback exists: `T2Expiry` is declared in the generated event set but has **zero transitions** in the consumed tables (the 2026-06-04 #292 entry's "no T2 timer" finding — whose "the listener stubs it to a no-op" clause this entry supersedes). The conformance `TwoStationHarness` knew — it grants seizes itself, and its own comment notes the legacy rigs never exercised autonomous delayed-ack; the grant just never propagated to production. The LinBPQ/netsim interop scenarios pass because their exchanges echo data both ways.

**Fix, two parts.** (1) `Ax25Session.PostEvent` is now **run-to-completion**: an event posted from inside a dispatch (an action's signal handler posting back into the session) is deferred and dispatched after the in-flight transition commits. This is mandatory, not hygiene: in t26 the `LM-SEIZE Request` action runs *before* `Set Ack Pending`, so an inline confirm would match the no-ack-pending branch and silently drop the ack — and inline re-entry corrupts `CurrentTrigger`/`CurrentState` (the outer transition's commit clobbers the inner's state advance). (2) Both construction sites grant `LM-SEIZE` immediately — the media they front (AXUDP / KISS-TCP / KISS-serial) are contention-free from the session's view; real channel access (CSMA persist/slottime) is the TNC's job. Bounded: the confirm path only emits `LM-RELEASE`, never a re-seize. This matches the Rust fix's shape; **ax25-ts was already immune** — its dispatcher grants the seize and its driver's `postEvent` queues without draining (run-to-completion built in), both from its #12.

**Collateral test re-anchors (all to *more*-correct behaviour).** `KissAx25BridgeTests` end-to-end loopback had been pinning the re-entrancy corruption itself: the synchronously-looped UA used to dispatch inline mid-transition and the outer commit clobbered A back to `AwaitingConnection` — A now correctly completes to `Connected` in one drain. `Ax25ListenerRejectAndEdgeTests.AcceptIncoming` indexed the modem positionally and the new delayed-ack RR (control 0x21 — the bug's absence, observed) landed where it expected the DM — now destination-keyed. The three `ChannelProfileIntegrationTests` read the session's `T1V` after the banner exchange; with acks now flowing, the figc4.7 SRT IIR gets a live ~0-RTT sample and correctly adapts T1V to exactly 7/8 of the seed — they now capture T1V at accept time, which is what they verify (config plumbing, not adaptation). New regression cover: `Ax25ListenerDelayedAckTests` (idle I-frame → RR N(R)=1 F=0; back-to-back I-frames → bounded cumulative ack) + the adapter-site equivalent in `Ax25AdapterTests`.

### 2026-06-07 — Telnet/relay line discipline: drop NUL so a CR-NUL "Enter" doesn't break the next command (PR #325)

`LineAssembler` (the node console + telnet→AX.25 relay line reassembler) coalesced CR-LF but not CR-NUL: a NUL byte fell through to `buffer.Add(b)` and became line content. A telnet client that sends "Enter" as **CR-NUL** (RFC 854's "CR alone" — several clients do) therefore left the `\0` to prepend to the *next* line, so a relayed command reached the peer as `\0/quit` and the far end (a LinBPQ chat server) didn't recognise it — the user's "`C RDGCHT` then `/quit` doesn't exit chat" report. Diagnosed by capturing exactly what pdn put on the wire for each telnet Enter style via net-sim's frame decode: CR-LF / LF / CR all relayed clean (`/quit<0x0d>`), but CR-NUL leaked a leading `<0x00>` onto the following line. Fix: `LineAssembler.Push` drops NUL entirely (RFC 854: NUL is NOP and CR-NUL is CR-alone — never line content), so it never enters a line or leaks across lines; fixes both the relay and the node command parser (both go through `LineAssembler`). Tests: CR-NUL-doesn't-leak, stray-NUL-dropped-mid-line, CR-NUL-split-across-chunks (`LineAssemblerTests`). Lab-validated. (Orthogonal red herring ruled out: the intermittent "`C <alias>` lands at the node prompt instead of the app" is a LinBPQ-side quirk — filed m0lte/linbpq#69 — not this; and the `/quit` frame itself was always clean.)

### 2026-06-06 — Persist the learned NET/ROM routing table to SQLite (`pdn.db`) so a restart isn't blind (PR #323)

The node's learned routing table was in-memory only (`NetRomRoutingTable` = two dictionaries, rebuilt empty on every `NetRomService` construction), so every restart — including a deploy — wiped the topology and the node went dark until it re-heard NODES broadcasts (up to a full ~hourly broadcast interval). pdn had no database at all yet; this slice stands up the consolidated **`pdn.db`** SQLite store (raw SQL + Dapper, no EF — Decision §3) and persists the routing table to it. **Scope (Tom): routing table only this slice** — config stays in the YAML conffile (the operator's only edit interface until the Slice-3 web UI exists, so `SqliteConfigProvider` waits for it) and rolling packet capture stays deferred to its own `packets.db`; `pdn.db` is the one store they join later, which is how we avoid DB sprawl. **Not a cross-stack parity item:** persistence is a pdn/C# *host* concern, so unlike L3 forwarding it does not fan out to `@packet-net/ax25` or the Rust pico-node.

Design: the protocol-side `NetRomRoutingTable` stays storage-agnostic — it already exports a `NetRomRoutingSnapshot` (`Snapshot()`) and now re-imports one (`Restore(snapshot, obsolescenceDecay)`, pure/no-I/O; the internal state maps 1:1 to the snapshot so the round-trip is lossless). SQLite lives entirely in the node host behind a new `INetRomRoutingStore` seam (`Load`/`Save`) with a `SqliteNetRomRoutingStore` impl (WAL, `PRAGMA user_version` schema, transactional snapshot-replace; **resilient by contract — a store fault logs and degrades to in-memory, never faults the node**). `NetRomService` takes an optional store (null = in-memory, so every existing call site/test is unchanged): it **hydrates on construction**, ageing each persisted route's obsolescence by the elapsed downtime measured in sweep intervals (`decay = floor((now − savedAt) / sweepInterval)`) so a long-dead route isn't resurrected at full strength (a week's downtime ages everything out), and **persists** on the NODESINTERVAL sweep tick, ~30 s after the last ingest (a debounce, so an ungraceful crash between sweeps loses ≤ 30 s), and on graceful dispose. The DB path mirrors the config path: `--db` / `PACKETNET_DB` / default `{cwd}/pdn.db`, which on the packaged node is the writable `StateDirectory` (`/var/lib/packetnet`) — no packaging change needed (`postrm` already purges it on uninstall).

Validation: `NetRomRoutingRestoreTests` (round-trip, decay-ages-obsolescence, decay-drops-routes-and-prunes-orphan-neighbours, replace-existing, negative-decay-clamped); `SqliteNetRomRoutingStoreTests` (save/load round-trip incl. the saved-at stamp, snapshot-replace, cross-instance durability = "the restart", and the can't-open-degrades resilience contract); `NetRomPersistenceTests` (hydrate-on-construct, downtime ageing, week-of-downtime-starts-empty, disabled-service-doesn't-hydrate); and the marquee end-to-end `NetRomAwareIntegrationTests.A_node_restored_from_its_store_keeps_the_routes_it_learned_before_a_restart` (run 1 hears a NODES broadcast over the real bus + supervisor then shuts down, flushing to `pdn.db`; run 2 over the same store hydrates the routes at boot). Full default suite green (Packet.NetRom.Tests 94, Packet.Node.Tests 191; 0 warnings). Plan §3 persistence Decision + §5.4 Slice 2 updated to the consolidated `pdn.db`.

### 2026-06-06 — NET/ROM multi-route load-balancing: per-flow, quality-weighted transit-route selection (PR TBD)

Builds on the L3 forwarding entry below. A forwarding node now spreads transit traffic across the kept routes to a destination instead of always using the single best one. The constraint that shapes it: NET/ROM L4 is a sequenced sliding-window transport, so per-*datagram* spreading reorders a circuit's frames and triggers NAK/retransmit storms — the granularity must be **per-flow**. `NetRomForwarding.Decide` gains a `NetRomForwardMode` (`BestRoute` = the prior always-best behaviour; `PerFlow` = the new default): in `PerFlow`, a stateless FNV-1a hash of the **flow key** (the L3 origin + L4 circuit index/id — constant for a circuit's life) selects among the eligible routes (not the way it came, quality > 0) by a **quality-weighted** bucket walk, so every datagram of one circuit pins to the same route (ordering preserved) while distinct circuits distribute in proportion to route quality. Stateless — no per-flow table (embedded-friendly). The flow hash is defined byte-for-byte (FNV-1a 32-bit over the 7-octet shifted origin + index + id) so the C#/TS/Rust ports select identically. Opt-in shape: `NetRomConfig.ForwardMode`, default **PerFlow** (a transit node load-balances by default; set `BestRoute` for deterministic single-path). It is a **purely local forwarding decision — invisible on the wire**, so it interoperates with BPQ/XRouter/Linux unchanged (BPQ itself is failover-sticky `DEST_ROUTE`, not active load-sharing — we don't adopt its per-datagram rotation, which is the reordering trap). Validation: 4 deterministic `NetRomForwardingTests` (per-flow stickiness across TTL/seq, distinct-circuit spread, quality-weighted bias, BestRoute-ignores-the-flow) on top of the existing 7. 89 `Packet.NetRom.Tests` + 13 node NET/ROM tests green; no regression (single-route destinations degenerate to the prior behaviour). Cascades to ax25-ts + pico-node. **Deferred still:** link-down/quality-collapse failover signal (edges into INP3).

### 2026-06-06 — NET/ROM L3 datagram forwarding (the transit/routing role): a node now relays datagrams addressed to *other* nodes (PR TBD)

Closes a real gap in the §5.9 NET/ROM body: the shipped L3+L4 stack originated and terminated circuits but never *forwarded* transit traffic — it was an endpoint/leaf node, not a router. `NetRomService.OnInterlinkData` fed every inbound interlink datagram straight to the circuit manager regardless of its L3 destination, so a node silently terminated circuits addressed to other nodes instead of routing them on. Now the inbound dispatch checks the L3 destination: a datagram addressed to us terminates locally (the existing L4 path); one addressed elsewhere is **forwarded** toward its destination's best next-hop neighbour; an endpoint-only node drops it.

The forward decision is the pure, exhaustively-tested `Packet.NetRom.NetRomForwarding.Decide` (no I/O), mirroring the de-facto reference (LinBPQ `L4Code.c`): decrement the hop-limit TTL and discard at zero; cap the TTL on everything sent (BPQ's `L3LIVES` enforcement); drop a datagram that has looped back to its own origin; resolve the destination's best route whose neighbour is **not** the one it just arrived from (never bounce it straight back); otherwise forward with the rewritten header. `NetRomService.ForwardDatagram` does just the I/O the decision asks for — send over the next-hop interlink if it's up (in order), else dial it on a background task first (`ForwardOverColdInterlinkAsync`).

Opt-in shape: new `NetRomConfig.Forward` (default **true**) gated by `Connect` — forwarding needs the connected-mode interlink machinery `Connect` provides, so a stock node (`Connect` off) stays silent, a connect-enabled node is a full NET/ROM node and forwards by default, and `Forward=false` runs an originate-only node. (Default is a cheap policy knob — flip it if a different posture is wanted.)

Validation: 7 deterministic `NetRomForwardingTests` (forward-with-decremented-TTL, drop-at-zero, TTL cap, loop-back-to-origin drop, no-route drop, no-bounce-back, alternate-route-when-best-is-the-way-it-came) + a real 3-node transit integration test (`NetRomL3L4IntegrationTests.A_transit_node_forwards_an_L4_circuit_between_two_channels_it_bridges_without_terminating_it`): A on channel 1, C on channel 2, B bridging both — A can only reach C *via B*, a user routes `C CNODE` from A, B forwards the circuit's datagrams across the channel bridge both ways, data round-trips, and B holds **no** circuit (it only forwarded). 85 `Packet.NetRom.Tests` + 13 node NET/ROM integration tests green; no regression. This is leg 1 of bringing forwarding to all three stacks (C# → ax25-ts → pico-node Rust). **Deferred still:** INP3; multi-route load-balancing beyond best-route-with-bounce-back-avoidance.

### 2026-06-05 — Expire transient node-release build artifacts (fix the Actions storage-quota release-blocker); arm64/armhf install-smoke is infra-blocked (PR TBD)

Two findings from trying to close out the arm64/armhf install-smoke (the "all three arches via QEMU" follow-up noted with the §7.2.1/#315 deb-smoke work). **(1) Storage-quota release-blocker — fixed.** A `publish-node` build hit `Failed to CreateArtifact: Artifact storage quota has been hit` at the `upload-artifact` step (so `smoke`/`release` skipped) — total artifact usage was only ~0.43 GB but right at the 500 MB free-tier line, and **all of it was stale intermediate `deb-linux-*` build artifacts** from prior releases (default 90-day retention) that are *also* attached to the GitHub Releases. These are a transient build→release hand-off (the `release` job downloads them in the same run); set **`retention-days: 1`** on that upload so they expire fast and can't accumulate against the quota and block a future release. (The existing pile auto-recalculates every 6–12 h, or can be deleted — the `.debs` are preserved on the Releases.) **(2) arm64/armhf QEMU install-smoke — blocked on the runner infra, NOT shipped.** The plan was to add `docker/setup-qemu-action` + `--platform linux/arm64|arm/v7` so the release gate install-smokes all three arches. A throwaway probe proved the self-hosted runners **cannot emulate**: `setup-qemu-action` runs but `docker run --platform linux/arm64 … uname -m` fails `exec format error` — the runners are LXC containers that don't permit binfmt_misc registration (the same limitation as the dev box). So a QEMU release gate would spuriously fail *every* node release; deliberately not shipped. The amd64 install-smoke (full install→boot→purge) + the fact that `build-deb.sh` treats every arch identically remain the coverage; genuine arm install-smoke needs **native-arm runners** or **emulation-capable (non-LXC / privileged-binfmt) runners** — a host follow-up, Tom's call. An emulation-free middle option exists if wanted: static structural verification of the arm `.debs` (control sanity + `file` confirms the binary's real arch + payload present), which needs no QEMU.

### 2026-06-05 — Serialize CPU-heavy CI workflows with a reader/writer host flock (the real fix for the node-test oversubscription flake) (PR TBD)

The flaky-test sweep proved the recurring `Packet.Node.Tests` flake is **CI runner CPU oversubscription** (4 runners on one box; worst when `interop` runs alongside `ci`), not a code bug — and that the obvious code fixes don't work and masking is forbidden (§2.5). The real lever is reducing oversubscription. Implemented in-repo (no host access needed): a box-wide **reader/writer `flock`** on `/tmp/pdn-ci-heavy.lock` — `ci.yml`'s matrix legs take it **shared** (`flock -s`, so they still run 4-up among themselves), the heavy exclusive workflows (`interop` per test phase, `fuzz`, `deb-smoke`'s crossgen publish) take it **exclusive** (`flock -x`), so a heavy workflow never executes concurrently with `ci`'s test phase. Every site is command-wrapped → releases on process exit (no holder to leak → no deadlock risk) → and no-ops harmlessly if the runners don't share `/tmp` (the safe failure mode). Deliberately not a GitHub `concurrency:` group (its queue-depth-1 cancels older pending runs, including `main` pushes). Full rationale + the not-GitHub-concurrency reasoning + the residual (brief stack-recreate-window overlap) in §7.2.2. The durable fix is still host-side (per-runner cgroup isolation / a dedicated `Packet.Node.Tests` runner) and stays Tom's call.

### 2026-06-05 — Fix a connect-ack lost-wakeup in `Ax25Listener.ConnectAsync` (latent defect found by the flaky-test sweep) (PR TBD)

The flaky-test campaign (which root-caused the recurring `Packet.Node.Tests` flake to CI runner CPU oversubscription — infra, not code) also surfaced a genuine latent defect in `Ax25Listener.ConnectAsync`. The method polls the per-session signal queue every 25 ms for the DL-CONNECT-confirm and, when the `(N2+1)·T1V` budget expires, its `await Task.Delay(25, cts.Token)` throws and it `break`s straight to `throw new TimeoutException` **without a final drain of `cached.Signals`**. So a confirm enqueued in the last poll window — after the loop's inner drain, as the budget fired — is dropped, and a connect that actually succeeded reports a spurious timeout. (Not the cause of the node-test flake, which is the inbound *pump* being starved so the UA is never delivered at all; this is the same lost-confirm family as the `AgwClient` register-after-send race, just at the receive-drain end.) Fix: extract the drain into a local `DrainForConfirm()` used both in-loop and **once more after the loop** (after honouring an explicit caller-cancel, before declaring timeout) — additive, it can only convert a previously-spurious timeout into the success it should have been. Also routed the budget timer through the listener's `TimeProvider` (`new CancellationTokenSource(budget, timeProvider)`) so the backstop is drivable under a `FakeTimeProvider` — behaviourally identical in production (`TimeProvider.System`), and no existing test relies on the connect budget firing under fake time. Regression test (`ConnectAsync_Does_Not_Lose_A_Connect_Ack_That_Lands_As_The_Budget_Expires`): with the budget on a `FakeTimeProvider`, deliver the UA, let the pump enqueue the confirm, then expire the fake budget with the real 25 ms poll loop parked — the exact lost-wakeup window. Proven: fails 5/5 against the pre-fix code, passes 10/10 with the fix (full `Packet.Ax25.Tests` 882/882). No published-lib/runtime-behaviour change in production; not lab-urgent.

### 2026-06-05 — Fix a latent connect-ack ordering race in `AgwClient` (the real cause of the flaky `Packet.Agw.Tests` 30 s timeouts) (PR TBD)

The `AgwClientInMemoryTests` connect tests intermittently failed CI with `TimeoutException: … did not receive a 'C' ack within 00:00:30` at `OpenSessionAsync`. It wasn't only CI load — it was a genuine **register-after-send race** in `AgwClient`: all three request/reply methods (`OpenSessionAsync`, `RegisterCallsignAsync`, `GetPortInfoAsync`) **wrote the request first and only then registered their reply waiter** via `AwaitFirstFrameAsync`. The dispatch loop drops any inbound frame that matches no current waiter, so if the reply is delivered before the caller resumes past the write to register, it is lost and the caller waits out the full timeout. Against a real TCP/AGW server the network RTT guarantees the waiter is registered long before the reply returns, so it never bites in production — but the in-memory test pair delivers with ~zero latency, and under CI scheduling pressure the dispatch loop wins the race, hence the "always exactly 30 s" failures. (This is a sibling of #47's starvation flakes but a distinct, deeper cause: a lost reply, not merely a slow one — a higher timeout would not have fixed it.) Fix: a `SendAndAwaitReplyAsync(request, predicate, timeout, ct)` helper that registers a **predicate-aware** waiter with the dispatch loop **before** writing the request; the dispatch loop now completes a waiter only on a predicate match, which also removes the old re-subscribe gap and deletes the `while`-loop in the former `AwaitFirstFrameAsync`. No assertion weakened, no timeout bumped (§2.5). Regression test (`OpenSessionAsync_succeeds_when_the_connect_ack_races_registration`): a new `InMemoryAgwPair(clientWriteDelay)` holds the client's write open for 300 ms *after* its bytes reach the server, so the ack is received + dispatched while the caller is still inside its write — deterministically reproducing the race. Proven: the test fails 5/5 against the pre-fix code and passes 5/5 (and the full suite 17/17) with the fix. `Packet.Agw` is not a published NuGet lib (consumed by the node/tools), and the race is unobservable against real AGW-over-TCP, so no lib republish and not lab-urgent — a correctness fix that also stops the CI flake.

### 2026-06-05 — Reproducible `.deb` install smoke: prove at least one arch installs cleanly on a pristine base — gating the release + shift-left on packaging PRs (PR TBD)

`scripts/build-deb.sh` produces self-contained `.deb`s but nothing verified they actually *install* on a clean machine — a broken control file, maintainer script, or layout would only surface on a user's box. Added `scripts/deb-install-smoke.sh <deb> [image ...]`: in a throwaway container (default `debian:stable-slim` + `ubuntu:24.04`, the suites the package claims) it `apt install`s the `.deb` — `apt`, not `dpkg -i`, because the declared `Depends: adduser, libc6` includes `adduser`, which is NOT in current slim images and must resolve from the repo — then asserts dpkg state `installed`, that the binary + unit + conffile landed, that the postinst created the `packetnet` user (and correctly *skipped* `systemctl` — the maintainer scripts' `-d /run/systemd/system` guard holds in a container), that the self-contained binary **boots and answers `/healthz` on :8080 on the bare base** (the real payoff — proves no missing native dep given the `InvariantGlobalization` publish; runtime start under systemd stays covered by the lab deploy), and that `apt purge` removes the user + payload. Wired two ways: a `smoke` job in `publish-node.yml` (`needs: build`, reusing the already-built amd64 artifact, which `release` now `needs:`) so a non-installable `.deb` can never reach a GitHub Release; and a shift-left `deb-smoke.yml` that builds + smokes an amd64 `.deb` on any PR touching `scripts/build-deb.sh` / `packaging/**` / the smoke script, catching breakage before a `node-v*` tag is cut. Container-isolated, so nothing installs onto the self-hosted runner and the node's port is probed inside the container (no host clash). amd64 only for now (native on the x64 runner, no QEMU) — the "at least one arch installs cleanly" guarantee; arm64/armhf via `docker/setup-qemu-action` is a noted follow-up. Validated on the just-shipped `node-v0.3.0` amd64 `.deb` (`SMOKE_PASS` on both images) before the script was committed. The Debian-canonical heavier tools — piuparts (install/upgrade/purge hygiene) and autopkgtest/DEP-8 (functional isolation) — are the future upgrade path if wanted.

### 2026-06-05 — Teardown-reliability audit of the interop test infra: guarantee docker + in-test resources tear down on FAIL/throw/timeout, not just pass (PR TBD)

Tom's concern: the dev box is a small 20 G LXC and the self-hosted CI runners persist between runs, so a test that fails/hangs and leaks its docker stack or background tasks accumulates leftovers → disk/RAM pressure → cascading flakes. Audited every CPU/RAM-heavy interop resource and traced its teardown on the failure path. Full teardown map + the two CI layers in **§7.2.1**; this entry is the summary.

**The gap found + fixed (in-test background tasks).** The connected-mode interop scenarios (`NetsimConnectedModeScenarios`, `LinbpqViaNetsim{Connected,Extended}Mode`, `XrouterViaNetsimConnectedMode`, `Rax25ViaNetsimConnectedMode`, `DirewolfMod128Interop` — 14 test methods across 6 files) spin up `Task.Run(() => InboundPump(rig, ct))` background pumps that feed KISS frames into a session, and cancelled + awaited them with a bare `cts.Cancel(); await Task.WhenAll(pumps)` **at the end of the happy path only**. When an assertion before that line threw (the common case for a flaky interop test), the pumps were **abandoned** — left running against KISS sockets the test's `await using` was about to dispose, racing the test's `using var cts` disposal (risking an `ObjectDisposedException` surfacing as an *unobserved task exception* on the finalizer thread) and leaving background work running into the next test on a persistent runner. Fixed with a new `InboundPumpScope` (`tests/Packet.Interop.Tests/InboundPumpScope.cs`): an `IAsyncDisposable` that owns the pumps' lifetime on its own linked CTS and **cancels + awaits them in `DisposeAsync` on EVERY exit path** (pass, assertion-failure, throw, timeout), declared `await using` after the test's `using var cts` so it disposes first. This also consolidates the per-file duplicated cleanup. No assertion weakened, no timeout bumped (§2.5): pure teardown-robustness.

**The CI backstop hardened (orphan symmetry).** `interop.yml`'s final `if: always()` `tear down` used `down -v` but not `--remove-orphans`, whereas the recreate-before-each backstop (`scripts/interop-recreate-stack.sh`) uses `down -v --remove-orphans`. Made the final teardown match (`down -v --remove-orphans || true`) so the *last* line of defence is exactly as thorough as the *first*: a service renamed/removed from the compose file can't leave an orphaned `pn-*` container behind even for one cycle. Both layers confirmed present: best-effort `always()` teardown + recreate-before-each as the backstop for a hard-killed run that skips `always()`.

**What was already solid (verified, no change).** The production `DisposeAsync` paths are correct: `Ax25Listener.DisposeAsync`/`StopAsync` cancel their own internal pump + await it; `KissTcpClient`/`AxudpKissModem` dispose their sockets; the #309 `NetRomService.IAsyncDisposable` graceful-DISC + the `NodeHostedService`(NetRom-before-Supervisor)/`PortSupervisor.DetachPortAsync` ordering are present (the in-process #309 regression `Disposing_the_service_cleanly_DISCs_…` passes). The in-process harnesses (`TwoStationHarness`, `CircuitPairHarness`) are pure fake-clock + in-memory — no real timers/sockets/tasks to leak. The interop tests *attach* to the already-up stack (no `Process.Start`), so there are no spawned processes to orphan; the `Testcontainers` package reference is vestigial/unused. The `docker/direwolf/entrypoint.sh` already `set -eu` + `trap term TERM INT`. No load-gen/stress process is spawned by committed test infra. The nightly fuzzer is in-process (nothing detached to clean).

**Validation (forced-fail, not reasoned).** Brought up net-sim + LinBPQ; forced a representative interop test to (a) **throw** and (b) **hang** past a shortened in-test budget. Both failed/cancelled fast with **no teardown deadlock**, leaving **zero orphaned test processes** and the docker stack untouched (the test owns no docker). The CI backstop's `down -v --remove-orphans` was validated directly to reap the full stack + named volume + a service dropped from the compose file (a true Compose orphan). Temporary fault reverted; own stack + clones cleaned. **Files:** `tests/Packet.Interop.Tests/InboundPumpScope.cs` (new), the 6 connected-mode interop test files, `.github/workflows/interop.yml`, §7.2.1 + this entry. No runtime/library change → not lab-deployable (the only runtime teardown leak in this area, #309's interlink-DISC, was already fixed + shipped). The `interop.yml` change makes this PR plan-relevant; this entry + §7.2.1 satisfy plan-check.

### 2026-06-05 — §7.0: carry the "BPQ must have *heard* a peer's NODES before it delivers to a `B`-MAP" finding into the architecture prose + assess the ax25-ts net-sim multi-peer test (stays Tier 3; timeout hardened) (PR TBD)

Two interop-doc/test-infra items, no `Packet.*` runtime change.

**(1) §7.0 doc — the "second, subtler half" of the BPQ-NODES-over-AXIP finding.** A verified on-the-wire finding lived only in `docker/linbpq/bpq32.cfg` (the `PNTSAX-1` MAP comment) and the §17 2026-06-05 ax25-ts-Tier-2 entry, not in the authoritative §7.0 architecture prose. Promoted it to §7.0, right after the existing "How BPQ delivers NODES over a point-to-point AXIP port" paragraph: a `BROADCAST NODES` line + a `B`-flagged MAP *authorise* NODES delivery to an AXUDP peer but are **not sufficient** — BPQ delivers to a `B`-MAP only once it has *heard that peer's own NODES* on the port (treats it as a live NET/ROM neighbour). So a **read-only / TX-free** AXUDP NODES-ingest peer (the C# read-only slice, the ax25-ts netrom module, the Rust read-only leg) hears silence unless it emits ≥1 NODES first to warm BPQ. The transmitting C# `NetRom*ViaAxudp` tests never surface this because their broadcast loop also satisfies the heard-first requirement — so the `B`-MAP *looks* sufficient on its own (it isn't). The `B`-MAP authorises; the warming NODES triggers.

**(2) ax25-ts `listener-netsim-multi-peer.test.ts` — assessed, STAYS Tier 3 (verdict + reasoning).** This was the last ax25-ts net-sim (AFSK) test that could flake the interop matrix under CI load (it timed out at its `waitUntil` in #312's run). Read honestly: its *demux assertion* (distinct cached `Ax25Listener` sessions keyed by source callsign + correct per-session frame routing) is **already covered deterministically in-process at Tier 1** (`Ax25ListenerMultiPeerTests`, mirrored TS-side). What the interop version *uniquely* adds is the **multiple-stations-on-one-shared-half-duplex-channel** reality: two peer stations transmit toward one listener over net-sim's single FM-capture AFSK1200 bus (`collision_mode: silence`), including the second test where an inbound accept + an outbound dial contend for the one channel at once. That is genuine CSMA/channel contention, which **AXUDP cannot replicate** — `AxudpTransport` is strictly point-to-point (one fixed UDP remote, no third-party/broadcast delivery; `src/axudp-transport.ts`). A `…-via-axudp` variant would add **zero** coverage over the Tier-1 demux tests while needing either the already-skipped (#153) BPQ-originates-inbound path or BPQ L2-bridging between AXIP MAPs (which changes the subject from L2 demux to NET/ROM routing). So per §7.0's own rule (only modem/channel-dependent coverage belongs on net-sim) it **legitimately stays Tier 3** — not forced onto AXUDP. The honest boundary is now documented in §7.0 (the "What stays Tier 3" list already lists "the listener/UI-frame scenarios") and in the test's own header doc.

**The harden (ax25-ts test file only).** Per §7.1, the test's `waitUntil` budgets were too tight for the contended Tier-3 case: raised them from 10 s/20 s to **30 s** (matching the C# sibling `NetsimListenerMultiPeerScenarios`'s proven budgets), and the per-`it` budget from 90 s → 120 s (a superset of the summed sub-budgets, cf. C#'s ~105 s). No assertion weakened, no contention masked — just margin for the load-sensitive modem; `waitUntil` returns the instant its predicate holds, so the headroom is free on a quiet host. **The change is `tests/integration/listener-netsim-multi-peer.test.ts` only** (no ax25-ts runtime change, no `interop.yml`/fixture change). On the wire this exactly addressed the #312 flake: under shared-channel contention the listener-side handshake reached ~29 s — past the old 10 s `waitUntil` wall, comfortably inside the new 30 s.

**Determinism evidence (validated the interop.yml way: clone `main` → `npm ci && npm run build && npm run test:integration` against `docker compose -f docker/compose.interop.yml`).** Full ax25-ts integration suite **8/8 green (1 skipped = the #153 BPQ-originates test), 3 consecutive runs on a fresh stack**; the hardened multi-peer test green in isolation 3/3 quiet and 3/3 under moderate CPU load (avg ~3.5–9.9), and green inside the full suite where it ran 17.8–29.3 s (the contended case the harden now covers). A separate honest observation, **out of scope and not changed:** under *pathological* CPU saturation (avg > 40 sustained, well beyond CI load) the two `linbpq-via-axudp` AXUDP tests can hit their own tight 15 s/20 s vitest `it` timeouts, and a handshake killed mid-flight there poisons BPQ's `AUTOADDQUIET` cache so subsequent runs fail until a stack recreate — the §7.0/§7.2 stack-state-hygiene dynamic (interop.yml already recreates the stack between phases). That is a candidate future harden of the AXUDP `it` budgets / a BPQ-state reset, deliberately left alone here (touching only the one test Tom flagged).

### 2026-06-05 — ax25-ts Tier 2: an AXUDP transport for `@packet-net/ax25` + re-home its protocol interop onto it (the Tier-2-for-ax25-ts follow-up; packet-net/ax25-ts PR TBD + this-repo fixture PR TBD)

Closed the §7.0 follow-up "ax25-ts is still net-sim-only" — the Tier-2-for-ax25-ts, mirroring the C# Tier-2 (§17 2026-06-05 above) and the C# node AXUDP work (#311). The TypeScript runtime now has its own frame-perfect/AXUDP transport and its protocol interop has been shifted left onto it, so ax25-ts's load-sensitive net-sim protocol tests are no longer the only coverage. **All ax25-ts code + tests + docs landed in `packet-net/ax25-ts`; the only change in THIS repo is the docker fixture** (a `B`-flagged AXIP MAP for the new TS test callsign) + this plan note.

**What I built (in packet-net/ax25-ts):**
- **`AxudpTransport`** (`src/axudp-transport.ts`, Node-only, `@packet-net/ax25/axudp-transport` subpath) — a UDP-socket transport speaking AX.25-frames-over-UDP / BPQAXIP, plugged into the same `Ax25Transport` seam as the existing `TcpKissTransport`, so the `Ax25Listener` + the netrom module run over a BPQAXIP/UDP tunnel exactly as over KISS-TCP. The TS parity leg of `Packet.Axudp`/`AxudpKissModem`. **FCS-always, no FCS-less mode** — a new `src/fcs.ts` CRC-16/X.25 codec (byte-for-byte `Packet.Core.Crc16Ccitt` / LinBPQ `bpqaxip.c` `compute_crc`; vector "123456789" → 0x906E, good residue 0xF0B8) appends the FCS on send, strips + validates it on receive, drops bad-FCS datagrams. Deliberately did NOT repeat the FCS-less detour (#304/#306): the survey settled FCS-on as the de-facto wire form everywhere. Binds all interfaces by default (a real peer behind the docker bridge originates back to the gateway 172.30.0.1, not loopback — the one networking gotcha bringing it up). `node:dgram` slice added to ax25-ts's hand-written `node-types.d.ts` shim (it avoids `@types/node`). Unit-tested (`tests/fcs.test.ts`, `tests/axudp-transport.test.ts` with a `MockUdpSocket` paired fake) — 884 ax25-ts unit tests pass (was 864).
- **Protocol interop re-homed onto AXUDP (Tier 2):** ax25-ts gained `tests/integration/netrom-nodes-ingest-via-axudp.test.ts` (its read-only netrom module ingests real LinBPQ's NODES over the UDP tunnel — the AXUDP analog of its net-sim NODES-ingest test + the TS parity of `NetRomNodesIngestViaAxudp`) and `tests/integration/linbpq-via-axudp.test.ts` (`Ax25Stack` connected-mode dial-out: SABM/UA + `P\r` I-frame round-trip + DISC — the TS parity of `LinbpqViaAxudpConnectedMode` Direction A + the FCS guard). The thin net-sim protocol smokes (`linbpq-via-netsim`, `netrom-nodes-ingest-via-netsim`) are **kept**.

**Determinism evidence (the load-insensitivity the shift buys).** The two AXUDP tests are deterministic: green **5/5 on consecutive reruns against the same (un-restarted) BPQ** (no AUTOADD-pinning rerun bug) and **7/7 under sustained CPU saturation up to load avg ~41** (durations held 2–14 s — no AFSK decode to glitch, the whole point), and the full ax25-ts integration suite is green on a fresh stack (the NODES-ingest lands in ~2 s vs the net-sim version's 90 s budget; the connect in ~0.3 s). Validated exactly the way `interop.yml` phase B does: clone main → `npm ci && npm run build && npm run test:integration` against `docker compose -f docker/compose.interop.yml`.

**The this-repo fixture change (the only packet.net code touched).** ax25-ts's read-only NODES-ingest test needs BPQ to deliver NODES to its listener over the point-to-point AXIP port. Same enabling finding as the C# Tier-2: a stock AXIP port drops its own NODES UI (addressed to the pseudo-call `NODES`) unless the recipient MAP is broadcast-flagged. Added `MAP PNTSAX-1 172.30.0.1 UDP 8196 B` (with the existing `BROADCAST NODES`) to `docker/linbpq/bpq32.cfg` — a DISTINCT callsign + fixed port from every C# AXUDP test (so the shared BPQ daemon, which serves both the C# and TS suites in phase B against the same fixture, can't confuse their links/routes). **A confirmed behavioural finding bringing it up:** a `B`-flagged static MAP alone does NOT make BPQ deliver NODES to a peer it has never heard from — BPQ delivers only once it treats the peer as a live NET/ROM neighbour (i.e. has heard its NODES broadcast on the port). The C# test runs a full broadcasting `NetRomService`; ax25-ts's netrom module is read-only/TX-free by design, so the TS test warms BPQ by sending its own NODES through the shared `AxudpTransport` (the exact seam a transmitting netrom layer would use). The connected-mode dial-out leg needs no MAP — BPQ's `AUTOADDQUIET` learns the reply route from the inbound SABM. **Land this fixture change before relying on the ax25-ts AXUDP tests in CI** (interop.yml runs the cloned ax25-ts `main` against this repo's fixture).

**No `interop.yml` change needed** — the AXUDP tests live under ax25-ts's `tests/integration/**`, which phase B already runs via `npm run test:integration`; they carry no net-sim channel contention (they touch only BPQ's UDP port 8093 + telnet 8010, never net-sim 8100). **No npm publish required for interop** (interop.yml builds ax25-ts from `main`, not the published artefact); publishing `@packet-net/ax25` with the new transport for external consumers is a separate Tom-gated step. **Test-infra + the ax25-ts library only; no `Packet.*` runtime change in this repo.**

### 2026-06-05 — Tier 2: a frame-perfect (modem-less) NET/ROM interop layer over AXUDP vs real LinBPQ; re-home the load-sensitive net-sim NET/ROM protocol tests onto it (PR TBD)

Stood up a **frame-perfect interop tier** (Tier 2) — pdn ↔ real LinBPQ over **AXUDP** (BPQAXIP-over-UDP) — to move protocol-correctness interop off net-sim's load-sensitive software-AFSK path, keeping net-sim for the modem/channel layer. AXUDP is AX.25-frames-over-UDP: the same real-BPQ peer and the same `Ax25Listener` + `NetRomService` production pipeline (via `AxudpKissModem`), but the frames cross as UDP datagrams with no AFSK encode/decode to glitch under CPU contention. The three-tier model is documented in **§7.0** (the table + what lives where + why); §7.1 rows + §7.2 updated.

**What I built / re-homed:**
- **`NetRomL4CircuitViaAxudp`** — the re-home of the load-sensitive `NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim` (now **removed**). Same protocol assertions against real LinBPQ 6.0.25.23, frame-perfectly: L3 NODES both ways, an L4 circuit pdn→BPQ with Information round-tripping both ways + clean Disconnect, an L4 circuit BPQ→pdn (`IncomingCircuit` with the right originating user + window — the Connect-Request `[window][user][node]` info-field framing via `ConnectRequestInfo`, the #308/#309 finding) reaching pdn's console + round-tripping data.
- **`NetRomNodesIngestViaAxudp`** — the AXUDP analog of `NetRomNodesIngestViaNetsim`: pdn ingests real BPQ's NODES over UDP and learns it as a neighbour (read-only `FrameTraced` tap), in ~1 s vs the net-sim version's 90 s channel-loss budget.
- **A representative AX.25 connected-mode interop** (SABM/UA + I-frame round-trip + DISC) already lives in Tier 2 via `LinbpqViaAxudpConnectedMode` (#301), both directions + the FCS-mandatory regression guard.

**Thin net-sim protocol smokes kept** (catch modem-interaction surprises): `NetRomNodesIngestViaNetsim` (NODES-over-AFSK; also where XRouter's ambient NODES is observed) + `LinbpqViaNetsimConnectedMode` (connected-mode-over-AFSK). **Modem/channel tier retained on net-sim** (the AFSK/channel *is* the subject): `NetsimConnectedModeScenarios`, `LinbpqViaNetsimExtendedMode` (SABME→FRMR v2.0 fallback), `Rax25ViaNetsimConnectedMode`, `XrouterViaNetsimConnectedMode`, the lossy/SREJ/10 kB transfers, listener/UI scenarios, `DirewolfMod128Interop`.

**The Tier-2-enabling finding (source-verified in `bpqaxip.c`, then on the wire).** A point-to-point BPQAXIP port delivers a frame only to MAP entries whose callsign matches the frame's *destination* (`AXIP_TX` → `SendFrame`); a NET/ROM NODES UI frame is addressed to the pseudo-call `NODES`, which matches no per-station MAP, so a stock AXIP port silently drops its own NODES (verified: BPQ sent a peer **zero** NODES datagrams — it instead XID/SABMs the peer to set up a connected interlink, then goes quiet). The fix is two explicit config items on the AXIP port: a `BROADCAST NODES` line + the per-MAP `B` (broadcast-recipient) flag — the AXUDP counterpart of the net-sim port's `QUALITY=192` NODES-enablement (a per-port broadcast-delivery config, not an AX.25 protocol change). En route, a **real test bug** I introduced + fixed: `BROADCAST NODES` combined with `AUTOADDMAP` (which flags auto-added entries broadcast recipients, `bpqaxip.c` L2205) made BPQ blast NODES at every transient AXIP peer, intermittently tripping the connected-mode FCS-guard's "FCS-less SABM gets no reply" assertion (it caught a stray NODES); switched the fixture to **`AUTOADDQUIET`** (auto-learns the reply route but clears the broadcast flag, L2212) so only the explicit `B`-flagged NET/ROM MAPs receive NODES.

**Determinism evidence (the load-insensitivity the move buys).** The Tier-2 AXUDP suite (the two NET/ROM tests + the three connected-mode tests + the AXUDP smokes, 9 tests) ran **green across many consecutive iterations including under sustained CPU saturation (load avg ~36)** — durations held flat (~25–48 s) under load, with `NetRomL4CircuitViaAxudp` at ~12 s. Contrast: in the same load run a net-sim AFSK test (`NetsimListenerScenarios`) flaked ~1-in-3 — the documented software-AFSK sensitivity, the exact reason protocol coverage shifts to Tier 2. On a quiet host, phase A (the timing-sensitive `Group!=NetRom` group) is **33/33 green** and phase C (`Group=NetRom`: the net-sim NODES smoke + the two AXUDP NET/ROM tests) is **3/3 green**, each on a freshly-recreated stack.

**Integration with #310's phases.** The AXUDP NET/ROM tests are tagged `[Trait("Group", "NetRom")]`, so `interop.yml` already runs them in the clean-stack-fenced NET/ROM phase (phase C) — a fresh BPQ → no learned-state carryover, isolated from the timing-sensitive AX.25 tests. They carry no net-sim channel contention (they touch only BPQ's UDP port + telnet, never net-sim port 8100), so the fence is hygiene-for-free rather than a contention requirement; each has a distinct callsign + fixed UDP port (so the shared BPQ daemon can't confuse their links/routes with each other or the connected-mode tests), and `NetRomService` is `await using` so its `DisposeAsync` cleanly DISCs the interlink (the #309 teardown discipline). The interop matrix stays reliably green. No `interop.yml` change needed.

**Test-infra only** (the fixture config + new test files); no `Packet.*` runtime change. **Follow-up:** ax25-ts protocol interop is still net-sim-only (no AXUDP transport in ax25-ts yet) — a Tier-2-for-ax25-ts is a future candidate (§7.0).

### 2026-06-05 — Harden the interop matrix against cross-scenario contamination: split the NET/ROM scenarios off behind a clean-stack fence + fix a real node interlink-teardown leak (PR TBD)

Tom's diagnosis: the single `interop matrix` job ran *all* `Category=Interop` C# tests + the cloned-ax25-ts suite against **one shared stack** (one LinBPQ daemon + one net-sim half-duplex channel), and scenarios contaminated each other — LinBPQ learned-state (NODES/routes/open AX.25 sessions) + channel contention. The heavyweight NET/ROM L4 scenario (#309's `NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim`) ran ahead of the timing-sensitive AX.25 tests and they flaked intermittently; `main` went intermittently red. The interop analog of #47 (the unit-test contention flake fixed with `ci.yml` `max-parallel:4`). Full diagnosis + design in **§7.2**; this entry is the summary.

**Contamination mechanism — pinned on the wire.** Reproduced locally: a clean stack runs the AX.25 timing tests reliably (`Rax25ViaNetsimConnectedMode` / `NetsimConnectedModeScenarios` 3/3 @ 30 s); after the #309 NET/ROM L4 test object is disposed, probing LinBPQ's sysop interface shows it still holds a **half-open connected-mode AX.25 link to `PNL4ND`** (`LINKS: PNL4ND PN0TST S=5`) + the learned `PNL4:PNL4ND` node/route, and LinBPQ **polls** the half-open link (RR P/F + T1 retransmits) onto the shared `collision_mode: silence` channel for ~10–20 s. A focused harness made the flake deterministic: with a leaked interlink present, fresh AX.25 connects on the shared channel dropped to 3/6 with 25–30 s timeouts (clean channel: always 6/6 @ ~2 s).

**The #309-leak verdict: real, and a node runtime leak (lab-deployable).** `NetRomService.Dispose()`/`DetachPort()` dropped the interlink from its dictionary but **never DISC'd** the underlying interlink AX.25 session, and `Ax25Listener.DisposeAsync()` abandons sessions locally without DISC — so the neighbour was left a session it thinks is alive. The same path runs in the live node host (shutdown + port reconfigure), so a real pdn left a neighbour a half-open link. **Runtime fix (lab-deployable):** `NetRomService` now implements `IAsyncDisposable` — `DisposeAsync()` tears the L4 circuits down then cleanly DISCs each interlink AX.25 session (post `DlDisconnectRequest`, wait bounded 8 s for the DISC/UA on the wire) *before* the listeners are disposed; new `DetachPortAsync` does the graceful waited DISC on port teardown; `NodeHostedService` disposes `NetRomService` before the `PortSupervisor` (the DISC needs the listeners alive), and `PortSupervisor.TearDown{,All}Async` use `DetachPortAsync`. In-process regression `NetRomL3L4IntegrationTests.Disposing_the_service_cleanly_DISCs_its_interlink_…` (deterministic, no docker); on the wire the LinBPQ link now drops in ~5 s (a real DISC) not an N2 timeout.

**The isolation (Tom's call — split NET/ROM off).** The runtime fix alone wasn't sufficient (the contamination is cumulative: heavy channel traffic + LinBPQ warm state). The C# NET/ROM scenarios (`NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim`, `NetRomNodesIngestViaNetsim`) are tagged `[Trait("Group", "NetRom")]`, and `interop.yml` now runs **three clean-stack-fenced phases** (each preceded by `scripts/interop-recreate-stack.sh` = `down -v --remove-orphans` + `up --wait`): **(A)** AX.25 + general (`Group!=NetRom`) first on a pristine stack; **(B)** the cloned-ax25-ts suite on its own fresh stack; **(C)** the C# NET/ROM group (`Group=NetRom`) last + isolated. The `Category=Interop` filter is unchanged; the job stays `[self-hosted, Linux, X64]` with the `group: interop` singleton. **Validation:** the timing-sensitive AX.25 group (phase A) ran **33/33 every iteration** across many fenced runs, and ax25-ts (phase B) green every time — the AX.25 timing tests no longer flake from cross-scenario residue (the contamination is gone). No AX.25 assertion weakened, no timeout blanket-bumped (§2.5): the fix is isolation + a real teardown, not masking. **Caveat (out of contamination scope):** the heavyweight `NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim` test is load-sensitive — reliable on a quiet host (6/6 isolated on a fresh stack) but flaky (~1-in-3) under sustained CPU saturation (its pdn→BPQ data round-trip times out / catches only XRouter L3RTT transit chatter when the contended host starves the real-time L4 handshakes). That is the §7.1 "non-blocking, re-run a lone red in isolation" class the test's own remarks flag (the #47 contention dynamic at the interop layer), not contamination; phase C isolation guarantees a lone red there can't drag a timing-sensitive AX.25 test down with it. Deeper hardening of that one test's L4 round-trip (or making it nightly-only) is a separate follow-up. **Files:** `.github/workflows/interop.yml`, `scripts/interop-recreate-stack.sh` (new), `src/Packet.Node.Core/NetRom/NetRomService.cs`, `src/Packet.Node.Core/Hosting/{NodeHostedService,PortSupervisor}.cs`, `tests/Packet.Interop.Tests/Netsim/{NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim,NetRomNodesIngestViaNetsim}.cs`, `tests/Packet.Node.Tests/Integration/NetRomL3L4IntegrationTests.cs`, §7.1/§7.2 + this entry.

### 2026-06-05 — Real-BPQ NET/ROM L3+L4 interop: pdn originates NODES + runs L4 circuits both ways vs LinBPQ over net-sim (closes the #308 §7.1 placeholder; PR TBD)

Closes the **real-BPQ-L4 interop** the #308 NET/ROM body deferred (the §7.1 ⬜ "NET/ROM L3 origination + L4 circuits vs LinBPQ / XRouter" row, which the in-process `NetRomL3L4IntegrationTests`/`CircuitPairHarness` covered "until the interop stack is free"). The stack was free; this validates the transmitting NET/ROM stack — `NodesBroadcastBuilder` + `Ax25Listener.SendUiAsync` + the NODESINTERVAL scheduler (L3 origination), and the `NetRomCircuit` FSM + `CircuitManager` driven through `NetRomService.ConnectCircuitAsync`/`IncomingCircuit` (L4 circuits) — against a **real LinBPQ 6.0.25.23** over net-sim's afsk1200 KISS-TCP path. New test `tests/Packet.Interop.Tests/Netsim/NetRomL4CircuitViaNetsim.cs` (`Category=Interop`), built on the #305 `NetRomNodesIngestViaNetsim` harness (the same `PASSWORD`→sysop telnet driver, the `QUALITY=192` fixture). Updates §5.9 (the deferred-list line), §7.1 (the row flips ⬜→✅), the strict-vs-pragmatic audit (the Connect Request info-field finding), and this entry.

**L3 both ways (deliverable 1) — proven on the wire.** A pdn `NetRomService` (`netRom.broadcast` on, alias `PNL4`, node `PNL4ND`) originates a NODES broadcast on the channel; querying LinBPQ's own node interface over the sysop session shows BPQ learned pdn as a node + route: `NODES` → `PNL4:PNL4ND`, `ROUTES` → `3 PNL4ND 192 1` (netsim port, quality 192, the directly-heard-neighbour proof). The reverse (pdn hears BPQ's `PNTST:PN0TST` and builds an assumed direct route) is the #305 assertion, re-checked here — pdn needs that route to dial the outbound L4 circuit.

**L4 circuit pdn → BPQ (deliverable 2, the core) — established + data + clean disconnect.** With `netRom.connect` on, `ConnectCircuitAsync` resolves BPQ in the routing table → opens an interlink AX.25 PID-0xCF session to BPQ → originates the L4 circuit end-to-end; the connection is a `NetRomNodeConnection` (`TransportKind=NetRom`), a command sent over the circuit reaches BPQ's node and the reply (carrying BPQ's `PN0TST` identity + the `PN0TST Packet.NET interop test node` CTEXT) relays back — **Information flows both directions over the circuit** — and disposing it sends a Disconnect Request that BPQ answers with Disconnect Acknowledge (clean close). **The reverse, BPQ → pdn**, also works: driving BPQ's telnet `C PNL4` makes BPQ originate the circuit to pdn (pdn's `CircuitManager.IncomingCircuit` fires with the correct originating user + proposed window), pdn accepts + bridges an echo console, BPQ confirms `Connected to PNL4:PNL4ND`, and a line BPQ relays over the circuit round-trips through pdn's echo. Deterministic: 5/5 local green (35–54 s each).

**BPQ framing divergence found + fixed (the #308 follow-up's headline; lab-deployable).** Verifying our L4 framing against real BPQ surfaced one genuine wire divergence in the **Connect Request**: BPQ carries the *proposed window* and the *originating user/node* callsigns in the **info field** (`[window:1][user:7][node:7]` + a 2-octet BPQ extension, verified on the wire — a real `PN0TST` connect was `04 A09C60A8A6A860 A09C60A8A6A860 3C00`), **not** the transport-header TX-sequence byte where `NetRomCircuit` previously put the window (with the callsigns at info offset 0). BPQ *accepted* our circuit either way (the mis-placed window only mis-set the negotiated window), but **inbound** we mis-read BPQ's originating user (got empty) and proposed window (got 0, fell back to default) until the fix. Fixed in `Packet.NetRom` by a single source of truth — new `ConnectRequestInfo` codec (`Wire/`) used by both `NetRomCircuit.SendConnectRequest` (emit `[window][user][node]`, TX/RX = 0) and `CircuitManager.MintInbound` (parse window + user/node from the info field, tolerating BPQ's trailing extension octets). This is the **canonical NET/ROM appendix layout** (the window has always belonged in the info field, not the header) — a correctness fix that happens to be exactly what BPQ speaks, not a BPQ-ism; per CLAUDE.md it is the spec-faithful default.
